requirement: ETCS shall provide the driver with information to allow him to drive the train safely.
ASR_Explanation: This high-level functional requirement is too generic and doesn’t impose specific architectural constraints or patterns.

requirement: ETCS shall be able to supervise train and shunting movements.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of real-time, safety-critical supervision modules and integration interfaces within the ETCS architecture.

requirement: If the supervision is performed by a RBC it shall be possible to prevent movements of a traction unit in its area if not authorised by the RBC.
ASR_Explanation: Requires incorporating secure authorization checks and fail-safe movement blocking into the RBC-traction control architecture, impacting component interfaces and safety design.

requirement: ETCS is required to be functional to a maximum train speed of 500 km/h.
ASR_Explanation: This high-speed requirement drives architecture choices for real-time data processing, low-latency communication, sensor accuracy, and hardware performance.

requirement: The following definitions shall apply for the ETCS application levels: Level 0: ETCS active for limited train control function; trackside not fitted with any train control system or fitted with a train control system for which no STM is available onboard. Level 1: Basic track to train information via intermittent transmission media, e.g. balises. This information can be supported by infill, transmitted via balise, loop or radio. Level 2: Basic track to train and train to track information via continuous transmission media, i.e. radio. The train detection is provided by trackside. Level 3: Same as level 2 except that train integrity is provided by onboard and therefore trackside train detection is optional. Level STM (Specific Transmission Module): Track to train information provided by national system. Onboard functions provided by national system (STM) in co-operation with onboard ETCS.
ASR_Explanation: This defines core ETCS modes and communication interfaces, driving system decomposition, component boundaries and infrastructure design.

requirement: It shall be possible to implement one or more of the ETCS application levels on a line.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a modular, configurable architecture to support and integrate multiple ETCS application levels, impacting componentization and interfaces.

requirement: Trains equipped for ERTMS/ETCS application level 3 shall be able to run on lines equipped with ERTMS/ETCS application level 3, 2, 1 and 0, trains equipped for ERTMS/ETCS application level 2 shall be able to run on lines equipped with ERTMS/ETCS application level 2, 1 and 0 and trains equipped for ERTMS/ETCS application level 1 shall be able to run on lines equipped with ERTMS/ETCS application level 1 and 0.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the system architecture to support multi‐level ERTMS/ETCS protocol handling and backward‐compatible interoperability across application levels.

requirement: The current application level shall be indicated on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user-interface display requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The driver shall acknowledge the level transitions, if requested from trackside. If the driver does not acknowledge after the transition the brake shall be applied. If the driver acknowledges afterwards, the brake can be released.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the real-time safety architecture by requiring fail-safe braking control and driver-interaction monitoring for level transitions.

requirement: ETCS shall be compatible with existing national systems listed in the CCS TSI such that it does not interfere with the national systems and is not interfered with by the national systems.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s integration architecture and interface design to ensure interoperability and non-interference with existing national systems.

requirement: The ETCS trainborne equipment shall be capable of supervising the following operational states: Full Supervision operation, Partial Supervision operation (Staff Responsible operation, On Sight operation, Unfitted Line operation, Train Trip operation, Post Trip operation), National operation (STM), Tandem operation, Multiple operation, Shunting operation, Stand By operation, Reversing operation.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the core operational modes that will drive the state‐machine design, module partitioning, and safety-critical architecture.

requirement: Any transition which occurs while the train is moving shall in principle occur automatically.
ASR_Explanation: This is a functional detail about automatic transitions and does not impose major architectural constraints.

requirement: Transitions which occur while the train is stationary, shall be initiated automatically or manually as appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the state-management and control modules by requiring a flexible, event-driven architecture to handle both automatic and manual transitions.

requirement: If, as a result of an automatic transition, except for transitions to and from National Operation (STM), the responsibility for the driver increases, the ETCS shall seek an acknowledgement from the driver, whether the train is stationary or not.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior requirement with no broad implications for system architecture.

requirement: For transitions to and from National Operation (STM) the ETCS shall request, an acknowledgement by the driver.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor extension to the existing mode-transition logic and driver interface without impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: In case the transition has to be acknowledged and the driver fails to acknowledge as required, the ETCS shall initiate a brake application.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical failure-handling requirement drives the design of real-time driver-monitoring, fail-safe logic, and braking subsystem integration.

requirement: During the transition period between two operational states (including two different national operations) the supervision provided shall at least ensure the same protection provided by the least restrictive state.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s security architecture by mandating dynamic state-management and policy-enforcement mechanisms to ensure consistent protection levels during operational transitions.

requirement: If an ETCS equipped train passes a level transition to a line fitted with more than one level, the onboard shall switch to the highest level, according to the priority given by trackside, for which it is equipped.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates the onboard architecture to include dynamic ETCS level negotiation and switching logic with the trackside interface.

requirement: If an ETCS equipped train passes a level transition to one or more levels for which it is not equipped, ETCS shall initiate a brake application.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical brake application on level transitions impacts the real-time control and subsystem integration architecture.

requirement: The current operational status shall be indicated to the driver on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI display requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The ETCS on-board shall be capable of receiving National values from the trackside to adapt to National requirements.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a flexible interface and modular data-handling architecture to support country-specific parameter sets.

requirement: National values shall be applicable to a defined area.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s data model and configuration management by introducing region-specific rules and values handling.

requirement: Once received onboard the national values shall remain valid even if the onboard equipment is switched off.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a persistent storage solution (e.g., non-volatile memory or database) to retain national values across power cycles.

requirement: If the on-board has no valid national values for the current location, default values shall be used by the onboard equipment.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local data‐fallback rule for onboard value resolution and does not drive high‐level architectural structuring.

requirement: Default values shall be harmonised values, permanently stored in all ERTMS/ETCS on board equipment.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates persistent harmonised default value storage for ERTMS/ETCS onboard equipment, impacting configuration storage and consistency architecture.

requirement: At Start Up, the on board equipment shall perform an automatic self-test.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the startup sequence and necessitates a built‐in test/diagnostic subsystem in the system architecture.

requirement: The test shall require no action on the part of the driver.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional behavior (automated test execution) with no major impact on high-level system structure or quality trade-offs.

requirement: The DMI shall indicate the result of the self-test.
ASR_Explanation: It only affects the DMI’s UI behavior for displaying self-test feedback and does not drive architectural structure.

requirement: Train data shall be entered before the on-board ETCS equipment allows train movement.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical interlock requirement impacts the high-level data validation flow and module interaction within the onboard ETCS architecture.

requirement: The driver shall be able to select Train Data Entry on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific user interface function with no broad architectural impact.

requirement: Entering or overwriting data manually by the driver shall be possible but only when stationary.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates integrating vehicle motion detection and runtime state management to conditionally enable manual data entry only when stationary.

requirement: Train data may be entered automatically from a railway management system or from train memory.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the system’s integration architecture by defining data ingestion interfaces and protocols with the railway management system and onboard train memory.

requirement: The driver shall be able to consult train data when the train is stationary or moving.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the data acquisition and streaming architecture by requiring real-time access and caching to support both stationary and in-motion operation.

requirement: Current train data shall be stored (except at transition to shunting) in the ETCS equipment until the traction unit is not operative.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the ETCS data storage and state-management layers, including memory allocation and lifecycle handling of persisted train data.

requirement: Stored train data shall be offered to the driver to be confirmed when Data Entry starts.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI behavior requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system for Train Data Entry shall provide for the input of other data required by STMs connected to ETCS. This may require additional items, not required for ETCS, to be entered.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for an extensible data‐entry subsystem and flexible data model to support arbitrary STM-specific fields and interfaces.

requirement: The entry of driver identification and the selection of the language shall be possible.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI/interaction detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The change of driver identification during a journey or a Train Running Number shall be possible.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts dynamic identity management, session/state handling, and data integrity/security flows.

requirement: Following successful completion of Train Data Entry, the driver shall be able to perform shunting movements or train movements.
ASR_Explanation: This describes a user workflow requirement without imposing constraints on high-level system structure or key architectural decisions.

requirement: The following data may be entered manually by the driver or from train memory, or provided by external sources: Driver identification, Train identification (train number), STM ready for use, Data required for brake calculation, Maximum train speed, Train length, Status of air tight system, Type of electric power accepted, Data additional required for STM (if any), International train category, Train gauge, Maximum axle load of the train with a resolution of 0.5 t.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional data-input specification that affects the data model but does not impose cross-cutting architectural constraints.

requirement: If the onboard fails to contact the RBC when awakening the driver shall be asked to enter the RBC contact details.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized error‐handling and UI prompt requirement with no broad impact on system architecture.

requirement: An ETCS equipped traction unit shall be capable of being moved in Shunting without train data, track data or movement authority.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a dedicated shunting mode in the ETCS onboard architecture, impacting control modules and safety logic integration.

requirement: Transfer to Shunting on driver’s selection shall only be possible when stationary.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s state machine and safety-critical interlocks, impacting how modules integrate speed sensing and mode control.

requirement: To prevent unauthorised use of the function permission shall be obtained from the RBC if the train is operating under the control of the RBC.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the security architecture and the authorization interface between the train and the RBC.

requirement: Permission received shall be indicated to the driver.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level notification requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to manually select Shunting from Stand By operation, Full Supervision operation or Partial Supervision operation.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the system’s mode/state management architecture by mandating manual selection logic and interfaces for operational modes.

requirement: Automatic transfer to Shunting may be from Full Supervision operation and Partial Supervision operation status at any speed lower than or equal to the supervised shunting speed based on trackside information.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This requirement drives the design of the mode‐management subsystem and its integration with trackside information interfaces.

requirement: Before automatic transition to Shunting, ETCS shall request confirmation from the driver.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s control flow and HMI architecture by adding a driver-confirmation step into the safety-critical state machine.

requirement: ETCS shall supervise Shunting operation to a permitted national speed value.
ASR_Explanation: This functional requirement can be handled within the existing speed-supervision component via configuration and does not drive significant architectural change.

requirement: The supervised Shunting speed shall be indicated to the driver on request.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI/functional detail that doesn’t impose high-level architectural constraints.

requirement: It shall be possible to apply the train trip function, if the shunting movement passes a signal showing 'danger for shunting'.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical shunting override requirement impacts the design of the real-time interlocking and train-trip control architecture.

requirement: Exit from Shunting shall only be possible when the train is stationary.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional safety rule implemented in the control logic without major structural or technology impacts.

requirement: Exit from Shunting shall take place when the driver selects exit from shunting.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-triggered state transition with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Partial Supervision shall be selected either by the Driver, or by information received from track-to-train transmission.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates dual selection paths (driver interface and track-to-train communication), impacting component boundaries, interfaces, and control logic in the system architecture.

requirement: If acknowledgement is specified the driver shall acknowledge transfer from Full Supervision to Partial Supervision within 5 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: Imposes a real-time performance constraint requiring low-latency, time-bound inter-module communication and scheduling.

requirement: Partial Supervision shall be indicated on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI display detail for the DMI with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: In Partial Supervision the train shall be supervised according to train speed and distance data available.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architecture of the real-time data acquisition and processing modules for speed/distance-based supervision.

requirement: The train shall have the capability of being supervised to a ceiling speed.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architecture of the real-time speed supervision subsystem and its safety-critical control and communication interfaces.

requirement: This ceiling speed shall not be shown continually on the DMI but may be shown momentarily when selected by the driver.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor DMI presentation detail affecting only the UI display logic, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: The train shall leave Partial Supervision when the trainborne equipment is not operative any longer, when Shunting is selected or when Full Supervision is available.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior requirement for mode transitions and does not drive high-level architectural structure or quality attributes.

requirement: It shall be possible to order a train trip when passing a stop signal.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the safety-critical signalling and control architecture by requiring override and permission handling when passing stop signals.

requirement: Transferring to Full Supervision shall occur automatically when a movement authority and all other necessary information is received through track-to-train transmission.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the design of the communication and control architecture by mandating reliable track-to-train data handling and automatic safety-critical state transitions.

requirement: It shall be possible for the trackside to ask a driver for confirmation about the occupancy of the track ahead before sending a Full Supervision movement authority.
ASR_Explanation: This adds a new safety-critical driver interaction step, impacting the communication protocol and control-flow/state-machine architecture.

requirement: Full Supervision shall provide supervision of speed and distance.
ASR_Explanation: Drives a real-time, safety-critical control architecture requiring precise sensor integration and performance guarantees for speed and distance supervision.

requirement: The trainborne equipment shall remain in Full Supervision until the trainborne equipment is not active any longer, when Shunting is selected or when Partial Supervision information is received.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s state machine and mode-transition logic for supervision control.

requirement: The ETCS trainborne equipment shall be capable of being isolated.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s high-level decomposition and interfaces by requiring dedicated isolation modules and safety partitions.

requirement: When the ETCS trainborne equipment is isolated, the system shall not show any ETCS information other than the fact that the system is isolated.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical requirement mandates architectural support for isolation state detection and UI display gating across system components.

requirement: Isolation of the ETCS trainborne equipment shall disconnect the ETCS trainborne equipment from the vehicle braking system.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a safety-critical interface and subsystem separation in the overall architecture to isolate ETCS from the braking system.

requirement: The ETCS trainborne equipment shall be capable of receiving information from the national train control systems by means of the STM.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the communication subsystem’s architecture by requiring support for the STM protocol interface with national train control systems.

requirement: The DMI shall display or be compatible with information from national train control systems. This may mean displaying the information shown by the national system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s architecture by requiring defined interfaces, data handling modules, and protocol support for interoperability with national train control systems.

requirement: Unfitted operation shall be possible if ordered by trackside.
ASR_Explanation: Enabling unbraked (“unfitted”) operation on trackside command impacts the braking-control architecture and mode-management logic in the safety-critical train control system.

requirement: Unfitted operation shall be possible if selected by the driver at start up.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the system’s startup architecture by requiring configurable boot‐time modes and conditional initialization of key subsystems.

requirement: The on board shall supervise the train against a ceiling speed.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes safety‐critical real-time monitoring and control constraints, driving the need for a dedicated, fault-tolerant speed supervision subsystem.

requirement: The ceiling speed value for the unfitted operation is determined by the lower value out of Maximum train speed, National value for unfitted operation.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple domain logic rule for selecting the lower of two speed values and does not impose any cross-cutting architectural constraints.

requirement: The onboard shall be capable to switch to another ETCS status when transmitted from trackside.
ASR_Explanation: It defines critical state-machine behavior and interfaces with trackside systems, impacting the communication and safety-critical architecture.

requirement: The ETCS on-board shall be capable of receiving track description from the trackside.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the communication subsystem and data‐handling interfaces for reliable trackside message reception.

requirement: It shall be possible to send information on adhesion conditions from trackside.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of the communication and data‐processing architecture by requiring real-time transmission of trackside adhesion information.

requirement: It shall also be possible, to allow the driver to change the adhesion conditions; in this case information from trackside has priority.
ASR_Explanation: Requires designing a data arbitration layer to enforce trackside information priority over driver inputs.

requirement: The trackside shall be able to send information for the calculation of speed profiles.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines a critical external interface and real-time data flow for speed profile calculations, impacting the system’s communication architecture.

requirement: If track data at least to the location where the relevant movement authority ends are not available on-board, the movement authority shall be rejected.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the on-board data acquisition, storage and fault-handling architecture for safety-critical movement authority processing.

requirement: Track to train transmission shall provide the capability to send different speed profiles for specific train categories.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the communication subsystem and data model to support dynamic, category-specific speed profiles.

requirement: The ETCS trainborne equipment shall supervise the end of movement authority, if this information is available on-board.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces a real-time, safety-critical supervision function that impacts system decomposition, interfaces and timing constraints.

requirement: The target distance to be displayed on the DMI shall be based on the most restrictive braking curve.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the integration and data flow between the braking-curve computation module and the DMI, affecting component interfaces and real-time processing.

requirement: Together with the movement authority, the on board shall be able to receive one or more time-out(s) for certain sections of the movement authority, and shorten the movement authority accordingly when a time out expires.
ASR_Explanation: It requires architectural support for real-time timeout management and dynamic update of movement authority data flows.

requirement: Using train data and infrastructure data, braking curves shall be calculated taking into account the target information but not the location of vehicles occupying the track.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the integration and separation of data sources and algorithmic components, shaping module boundaries, data flows, and performance requirements.

requirement: The ceiling speed level for the movement authority shall be defined as data National Value.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement specifies a detailed data attribute definition and does not drive high-level architectural structure or module interactions.

requirement: Before entering an occupied track, a driver acknowledgement shall be requested.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes safety-critical HMI and control-logic integration, requiring architectural support for occupancy detection, event handling, and driver acknowledgement.

requirement: The train shall be supervised according to train speed data available.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a low-latency, reliable real-time data ingestion and processing architecture for safety-critical train supervision.

requirement: The train shall, as a minimum, be supervised to a ceiling speed; the supervised speed shall not be shown on the DMI unless selected by the driver.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives architectural boundaries by separating the safety-critical speed supervision module from the DMI display logic with a driver-selectable interface.

requirement: The target distance shall not be shown on the DMI unless selected by the driver.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI behavior requirement with no broad impact on system architecture.

requirement: On request of the RBC, the driver shall have the possibility to confirm that the track ahead of him until the end of the on sight section is clear.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of real-time RBC communication, track data processing, and driver‐interface integration.

requirement: ETCS shall collect all relevant information concerning train and line speed.
ASR_Explanation: This basic functional requirement for data collection does not impose high‐level structural or quality‐attribute constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: ETCS shall calculate the permitted speed for the train for all locations of the authorised movement.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a core real-time computation service requiring dedicated modules for location tracking, speed profile integration, and performance constraints.

requirement: This static train speed profile shall also respect maximum line speed and track speed and special speed levels for special classes of trains.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-level functional constraint on speed calculation that can be handled within the existing profile management component without altering the overall system architecture.

requirement: The ETCS trainborne equipment calculates the static train speed profile on the basis of infrastructure data and train data.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a core processing component and its data interfaces, driving module decomposition, data flow architecture and real-time performance constraints.

requirement: Based on all relevant data, the ETCS shall calculate an emergency braking curve and a service braking curve.
ASR_Explanation: This core real-time braking curve computation dictates system decomposition, performance budgeting, and hardware resource allocation.

requirement: It shall be possible to permit/inhibit the service brake intervention by trackside.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the safety-critical control architecture by requiring trackside-to-vehicle communication interfaces and brake intervention logic.

requirement: When changing to a lower speed level, the front end of the train shall respect the dynamic train speed profile.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the front-end control architecture by requiring real-time integration of dynamic speed profiles and enforcement of safety-critical timing constraints.

requirement: When changing to a higher speed level the rear end of the train shall respect the static train speed profile.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior requirement for speed control that doesn’t drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: It shall be possible to define certain locations (e.g. tunnels) where speed increase is related to the front of the train.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional extension affecting domain logic but not driving major architectural structure or quality attributes.

requirement: The braking curves shall ensure that the train complies with its speed requirements.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of a real-time braking control subsystem with precise algorithms and sensor-actuator integration to meet safety-critical performance constraints.

requirement: Where failure to apply the full service brake is detected the emergency brake shall stop the train in rear of the danger point.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a safety-critical failure detection and emergency braking control mechanism, impacting fault-tolerance, monitoring, and actuator integration architecture.

requirement: The release speed shall be calculated on board, based on either: safety distance and overlap, accuracy of odometry, deceleration performance of the train, etc.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the on‐board processing architecture by requiring a real‐time calculation module that integrates sensor data (odometry, braking performance, safety margins).

requirement: The release speed given from the trackside shall take priority over the release speed calculated on board.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s data‐flow and module design by mandating priority logic between the trackside communication interface and the onboard speed‐calculation component.

requirement: The release speed shall be indicated on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI display requirement with no major impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: If the release speed is calculated on board it shall ensure that the train will stop before reaching the danger point.
ASR_Explanation: It requires safety-critical real-time braking control and hardware integration, shaping the system’s timing, reliability, and safety architecture.

requirement: When the train is stationary or after a certain time (e.g. the time for 'route releasing' of the overlap, the release speed calculation shall be based on the distance to the danger point (if calculated on-board). The condition for this change shall be defined for each target as infrastructure data.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the core speed‐calculation module and infrastructure data interface by introducing configurable, condition‐based logic for release speed computation.

requirement: Each railway shall have the possibility of allowing a different release speed for every signal.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed configuration requirement affecting data modeling and UI, but it does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The ETCS trainborne equipment shall be able to determine the location of the entire train.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives key decisions on sensor integration, data fusion, real‐time processing, and system interfaces for train integrity and positioning.

requirement: On lines fitted with RBC, the ETCS trainborne equipment shall be able to transmit the location of the entire train to the RBC.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the communication subsystem and real-time data interfaces for train location reporting to the RBC.

requirement: The train location calculation shall take into account error of odometry.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement refines the internal logic of the location‐calculation module without driving changes to overall system structure or component boundaries.

requirement: Actual speed shall be indicated on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI display requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: There shall be no discrepancy between the speed shown to the driver and the speed used for supervision of movement authorities and speed limits.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a real-time, synchronized data‐flow architecture between the driver display and speed supervision modules to guarantee consistency.

requirement: The indication provided shall enable the driver to drive at the permitted speed without receiving a warning and without intervention of ETCS.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architectural design of the HMI and speed-monitoring subsystems to ensure correct indication and safety without ETCS intervention.

requirement: The driver shall know the distance to the next point defining the indicated braking curve and the permitted speed allowed. This shall be shown to the driver in a way that is understandable and logical.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the HMI design and real-time integration of braking-curve and location data services, impacting system architecture.

requirement: Visual and acoustic warnings to the driver about possible intervention from ETCS shall be given to enable the driver to react and avoid intervention.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the HMI and real-time alarm subsystem design, including integration with ETCS interfaces and safety-critical timing constraints.

requirement: The driver shall have the possibility to select the language, this does not concern non pre-defined texts sent from the trackside.
ASR_Explanation: Requires architectural support for internationalization, including resource bundle management and dynamic UI language switching.

requirement: A train shall be supervised to its static and dynamic train speed profiles.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the real-time, safety-critical speed supervision module affecting system decomposition, performance constraints, and hardware-software interfaces.

requirement: Within the braking curve area, a warning shall be given to the driver to enable him to react and avoid intervention from ETCS equipment at least 5 sec. before the intervention.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes hard real-time, safety-critical timing on braking-curve computation and driver-warning subsystems, driving system partitioning and scheduling architecture.

requirement: If the train or the shunting movement exceeds the permitted ceiling speed by a certain harmonised margin, the trainborne equipment shall execute a brake intervention until the actual speed does not exceed permitted speed; then the driver shall be able to release the brake.

requirement: The driver shall be able to release an ETCS emergency brake application when stationary.

requirement: If decided by a national value, the driver may release the ETCS emergency brake when the actual speed is below the permitted speed.
ASR_Explanation: It requires the brake control module to support configurable national parameters for emergency‐brake release logic, impacting the safety and configuration architecture.

requirement: To protect a traction unit from roll away and unwanted reverse movements the trainborne equipment shall monitor the direction of movement in relation to the permitted direction.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes safety-critical real-time sensor integration and control requirements, shaping the system’s motion monitoring architecture.

requirement: The trainborne equipment shall apply the emergency brake after a distance, defined by a national value, is travelled by the train.

requirement: The roll away/reverse movement intervention shall be indicated on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific UI display requirement for the DMI and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: When the traction unit has come to a standstill, the driver shall be able to release the emergency brake.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed brake-control function with no significant impact on system-wide architecture or quality-attribute allocations.

requirement: After releasing the emergency brake ETCS will provide the supervision appertaining when roll away protection was initiated.
ASR_Explanation: It requires integration and timing coordination between the emergency‐brake control and roll‐away supervision modules, impacting the system’s component architecture.

requirement: When using more than one traction unit this function shall be disabled in all but the leading traction unit.

requirement: All data entered, received or indicated to the driver shall be recorded onboard. All data shall be related to UTC (Universal Time Corrected) and a reference point.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates an onboard data-logging architecture with UTC time-synchronization and reference-point management, impacting storage and time-service design.

requirement: Information shall be recorded to an accuracy which enables a clear view of the functioning of ETCS and way the traction unit has been driven.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives data recording and storage architecture, impacting database design, time‐synchronization, throughput, and precision constraints.

requirement: Standardised output interfaces shall enable transmission of information recorded to other media for investigation.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of system interface layers and data export protocols, shaping module boundaries and integration points.

requirement: Data to enable investigation of accidents need only be stored for at least 24 hours, and shall be very detailed. Operational data to enable assessment of driver performance shall be stored for at least one week.

requirement: The following information shall be recorded: any transition of Level and of operational status, The driver’s confirmation of transition to shunting shall be recorded, train supervision data and information received from national train control systems, actual speed, full service brake intervention, emergency brake intervention, applying the train trip function, selection of the override control, override of the route suitability function, isolation of on board ETCS equipment, Data entered, received or indicated to the driver.

requirement: It shall be possible to use multiple traction units without isolating the ETCS trainborne equipment on traction unit(s) with an in-operative cab.

requirement: Information received shall not influence the traction unit(s) with in-operative cabs.

requirement: The train trip function shall be suppressed in traction unit(s) with in-operative cabs.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical requirement affects the high-level control architecture by mandating cab-state detection and conditional suppression of the trip function across subsystems.

requirement: It shall be possible to use tandem traction units without isolating the ETCS trainborne equipment on the tandem traction unit.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the overall system integration and interface design for ETCS equipment across multiple traction units.

requirement: The train trip function shall be suppressed on the tandem traction unit.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the traction control subsystem architecture by requiring function allocation and safety logic changes for the tandem traction unit.

requirement: The driver shall enter the driver ID.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic input functional requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to drive the train backwards in a supervised way (speed and distance) according to information received from trackside.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior that can be handled within existing control modules without driving major architectural changes.

requirement: The train speed shall be at or below a speed specified by a national value.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional constraint on allowable train speed that does not drive high-level architectural structure or technology selection.

requirement: The driver shall select an override control according to the permission received.

requirement: The override control shall be protected against inadvertent operation.

requirement: When the train passes the stop signal, the train trip function shall be suppressed.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical behavior requires real-time integration between the signal‐detection subsystem and train‐trip control, impacting system layering and interfaces.

requirement: Actual speed shall still be shown on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI display detail that maintains existing data flow to the DMI and does not drive architectural changes.

requirement: A special indication shall be shown on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This UI-specific indication is a minor presentation detail with no significant impact on system architecture.

requirement: The supervised speed shall not be shown on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation constraint affecting only the display layer with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The train shall be capable of receiving any track-to-train information intended and relevant for this train including movement authority.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the train’s communication subsystem and interfaces for handling diverse, safety-critical track-to-train messaging protocols.

requirement: If supervised by an RBC it shall be possible to command an emergency stop to all trains in a particular area or to a specific train.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of high-availability, low-latency command channels and safety-critical communication and failover mechanisms.

requirement: It shall be possible to command an immediate train stop.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the real-time safety-critical control architecture and hardware interface design for reliable emergency stopping.

requirement: It shall be possible to command a conditional emergency stop. If the train has already passed the location for the emergency stop the command shall be ignored.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes real-time geofencing and fail-safe requirements on the control system architecture.

requirement: When a train has received an emergency stop ETCS shall command the emergency brake.
ASR_Explanation: Specifies a critical real-time safety function requiring fault-tolerant, fail-safe architecture and tight hardware-software integration.

requirement: The emergency stop shall be indicated to the driver on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI functional requirement for the DMI, with no broad impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to prevent a train from entering a route for which it does not meet the required criteria.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a safety-critical route control subsystem with a dedicated validation component enforcing entry criteria.

requirement: Route unsuitability shall be indicated on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a UI presentation requirement for the existing DMI module with no major impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The driver shall be able to override the function when the train is stationary.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a new override mode requiring changes to the control-logic state machine and safety-interlock architecture.

requirement: After overriding this function the movement authority shall be re-established.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level functional behavior requirement affecting internal function logic rather than high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: When a traction unit passes a stop-signal the emergency brake shall be triggered.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the safety-critical real-time braking control and sensor-actuator integration architecture with fail-safe emergency response.

requirement: Operation of the train trip shall be indicated on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI functional detail for the DMI with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The emergency brake shall be applied until the traction unit is stationary.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical braking requirement drives the real-time control architecture and hardware–software interface design.

requirement: When the traction unit is stationary the driver shall be required to acknowledge the train trip condition. This acknowledgement will release the emergency brake.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical acknowledgment requirement drives the integration and interface design between the HMI, traction control, and emergency brake subsystems.

requirement: After the acknowledgement the driver shall be able to continue the movement.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level control flow detail that does not drive high-level architectural structure or technology choices.

requirement: After the acknowledgement the train shall be able to be driven backwards for a certain distance defined by national value.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the motion-control and safety architecture by introducing reverse-driving logic and associated national-distance constraints.

requirement: The ETCS trainborne equipment shall transmit its own train identification to the RBC.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level functional detail (message content) that does not drive major structural or quality‐attribute decisions.

requirement: The train running number shall consist of a maximum of 8 numeric digits.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple data‐validation constraint on a field’s format and does not drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: On demand, the position of the front end of the train at the time of the demand shall be indicated on the DMI. This shall be possible while the train is moving or stationary.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes real-time integration and data‐flow constraints between the train positioning subsystem and the DMI, affecting interface and performance design.

requirement: The ETCS on-board shall be capable of receiving information about pantograph and power supply from the track side.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of the communication interfaces, protocols, and hardware integration required for trackside data reception.

requirement: The ETCS trainborne equipment shall indicate on the DMI the information regarding pantograph and power supply.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional requirement for the DMI and does not impose system-wide architectural constraints.

requirement: The information regarding lowering and raising of the pantograph and opening/closing of the circuit breaker shall be provided separately and in combinations.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional interface requirement affecting data modeling but does not impose fundamental architectural constraints.

requirement: The ETCS on-board shall be capable of receiving information regarding air tightness from the trackside.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a new trackside-to-onboard communication interface for air-tightness data, impacting subsystem integration and interface design.

requirement: It shall be possible to send plain text messages from track to train.
ASR_Explanation: Defines a critical communication interface requiring architecture-level design of messaging protocols, network interfaces, and reliability/security mechanisms.

requirement: When the plain text message appears on the DMI, the driver shall be alerted.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI event handling requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The onboard equipment shall display plain text messages as received.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI functional requirement with no major implications on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The character set used shall support different languages.
ASR_Explanation: This necessitates adopting Unicode-based encoding and internationalization frameworks, impacting data storage, processing, and presentation layers.

requirement: It shall be possible to send fixed text messages from track to train.
ASR_Explanation: It influences the design of the communication subsystem, protocols, and interfaces for reliable track-to-train messaging.

requirement: Fixed text messages shall be provided in the language selected by the driver.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the internationalization architecture by requiring resource‐bundle management and dynamic language selection for text messages.

requirement: It shall be possible to send information regarding the inhibition of the following different types of brake: Regenerative brake, Eddy current brake, Magnetic shoe brake.
ASR_Explanation: It requires defining a common communication interface and data model for inhibition events across multiple brake subsystems, affecting the system’s integration and messaging architecture.

requirement: Information shall be shown on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the inclusion of a DMI/UI component, defining data interfaces, rendering framework and display hardware integration.

requirement: The ETCS on-board shall be capable of sending to the trackside train integrity information detected by a system outside ETCS.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a new external data‐exchange interface impacting the on‐board communication architecture and integration modules.

requirement: The driver shall be able to confirm the train integrity to the RBC manually. The confirmation requires the train to be stationary.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a driver HMI module, train‐state monitoring logic, and integration with the RBC messaging architecture.

requirement: The on board shall be capable of sending train data to the trackside after confirmation by the driver, or when entering the RBC area.
ASR_Explanation: It defines critical communication interfaces and event-driven triggers with the RBC, impacting the onboard system’s protocol, reliability, and integration architecture.

requirement: The following train data shall be sent from the on board to the trackside: Train running number, STM ready for use, train gauge, Max. axle load, status of air tight system, type of el. power accepted, international train category, max. train speed, train length.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the data exchange interface and communication architecture between onboard and trackside systems, impacting protocols, bandwidth, and real-time constraints.

requirement: It shall be possible to revoke a Movement Authority that has already been issued to a train in a co-operative way between RBC and train.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the communication protocol and state-management architecture between the RBC and onboard train systems for safe MA revocation.

requirement: The co-operative revocation of the MA shall be possible to a new target location, proposed from RBC.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts system architecture by introducing new inter-component communication and state-handling protocols between the RBC and MA management.

requirement: The new target location shall be checked for acceptance by the on board.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional check for onboard target acceptance and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: If a train cannot stop at the proposed new target location it shall reject the request and keep the old target location.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the architecture of the motion-control and target-management modules by introducing a validation and fallback mechanism for target updates.

requirement: The Reversing function shall only be possible in one active cab which is not closed at any time during the procedure.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a cross-cutting safety and control constraint requiring exclusive cab ownership and door-state verification, impacting the system’s hardware/software architecture.

requirement: Reversing shall be possible as defined by a value given with the MA.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior requirement and does not drive major architectural decomposition or high-level design choices.

requirement: The driver shall be able to use the Reversing function without needing to re-confirm the train data.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the system’s data persistence and control‐flow architecture by requiring retained train data and modified safety checks for the reversing function.

requirement: Reversing shall be supervised to a distance and speed set as National Values.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the real-time control and sensor supervision subsystems and their configuration management for national parameter values.

requirement: The distance supervised can be extended from the trackside.
ASR_Explanation: It requires extending sensor hardware, communication interfaces, and system boundaries to support greater trackside supervision distance.

requirement: Once the train starts reversing the MA shall be cancelled.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces new event-driven behavior in the train control state machine, impacting module interactions and safety-critical control flow.

requirement: The train shall be able to automatically pass from one RBC area to another without driver intervention.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the communication and handover mechanism between RBC areas to ensure seamless, safe train control without driver intervention.

requirement: If the train is equipped with two operational radios there shall be no performance penalty as a result of a transition from one RBC to another (train spacing and train speed).
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s high-availability and performance architecture by requiring seamless radio handover without affecting train spacing or speed.

requirement: If the train is equipped with only one operational radio, passing from one RBC to another shall still be possible but might result in a performance penalty.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It drives the design of the train’s communication subsystem by requiring a single-radio fallback mode that degrades performance, impacting availability and real-time handing.

requirement: In the event of a Transmission Failure the following reactions, shall be capable of being applied in accordance with a National Value: Option 1. The ETCS trainborne equipment shall immediately command the emergency brake. The failure shall be shown on the DMI. Option 2. The ETCS trainborne equipment shall immediately command the full service brake. The failure shall be shown on the DMI. Option 3. The train may proceed unrestricted to the end of its movement authority. The indication on the DMI shall remain, and the driver shall be informed about the loss of transmission.
ASR_Explanation: It defines critical fault-handling behaviors and module interactions (braking commands, DMI updates, safety logic) that shape the system’s architecture.

requirement: If there are failures of the trainborne equipment which compromise the safety of train supervision, the ETCS trainborne equipment shall immediately command the brake and bring the train to a stop.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical fail-safe requirement necessitates real-time fault detection and redundant braking control architectures to guarantee immediate train stoppage.

requirement: The occurrence of a failure shall be displayed on the DMI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI display requirement that doesn’t drive any high-level architectural structure or component interactions.

requirement: In ETCS with RBC this restriction on performance shall, if possible be transmitted to the RBC.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the ETCS–RBC communication interface and data model, requiring architectural changes to message definitions and handling.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall control the heating and cooling units that are defined as part of the THEMAS system. The definitions of the individual heating and cooling systems reside in an initialization file. The system shall read this file and the definitions shall be derived from the initialization data in the file.
ASR_Explanation: Reading initialization data for heating/cooling definitions is an implementation detail that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall use the information contained in the initialization data file to determine which heating and cooling units are part of the THEMAS system. There is one heating and cooling unit that corresponds to one thermostat in each of four quadrants on each of three floors of the office building.
ASR_Explanation: This FR only dictates how initialization data is parsed and mapped to HVAC units, affecting module implementation details rather than the overall architecture.

requirement: Each thermostat shall have a unique identifier by which that thermostat is identified in the THEMAS system. This procedure will load these definitions into the THEMAS software.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple data‐model requirement (a unique identifier) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Each quadrant of each floor shall have a thermostat which is to be used to provide temperature data to the THEMAS system. The initialization file shall contain a unique identifier for each thermostat that the system is to monitor. These identifiers shall be read from the initialization file and loaded into the THEMAS system during the initialization process.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional requirement about sensor configuration and initialization that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: There shall be a maximum number of heating or cooling units that can be on at any given time. This procedure loads the maximum number of concurrently running units allowed.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint impacts the system’s concurrency control and resource‐management design by requiring configurable limits and enforcement logic at the architectural level.

requirement: The maximum number of heating or cooling units that can run concurrently shall reside in an initialization file. The maximum number of concurrently running units shall be read from the initialization file and stored in the THEMAS system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple configuration detail with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The trigger value is used in combination with the current temperature to determine when a heating or cooling unit shall be turned on or off.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine control‐logic rule and does not drive high‐level architectural structure or design decisions.

requirement: The trigger values shall reside in an initialization file. This procedure shall read the initialization file and establish the trigger value from the data in that file.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level configuration implementation detail affecting a single module’s initialization logic, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall ensure the temperature reported by a given thermostat shall not exceed a maximum deviation value of 3 degrees Fahrenheit.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional constraint on thermostat behavior with minimal impact on high-level architectural design.

requirement: The overtemperature values shall reside in an initialization file. This procedure shall read the initialization file and establish the overtemperature value from the data in that file.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation detail (config file handling) with no broad impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall only respond to temperatures that are within a reasonable value.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local input validation constraint with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The valid temperature range value shall reside in an initialization file. This procedure shall read the initialization file and establish the valid temperature range from the data in it.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level configuration detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When the THEMAS system is initialized, it shall first turn off all the heating and cooling units. Then, it shall check all the thermostats and determine if any thermostat’s settings require a heating or cooling unit to be turned on back on.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional startup sequence that only affects module-level control flow, not high-level architectural structure.

requirement: This process shall first determine a known state of all the heating and cooling units by issuing a request to turn off all the units. It shall then read the current temperature values and current temperature settings of each thermostat. If the settings reflect a need for a heating or cooling unit to be turned on, the process shall issue a request to turn on the appropriate unit. This determination shall be made in accordance with the rules outlined in section 3.1.4.1 and 3.1.4.2.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional control sequence that does not impose new architectural constraints or quality-attribute requirements.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall only respond to temperatures from the thermostats that are within the specified valid range.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized input-validation rule that doesn’t drive high-level architectural choices.

requirement: A current temperature value that is received from an individual thermostat shall be compared to the valid temperature range values. If the current temperature value is strictly less than the lower value of the valid temperature range or if the received temperature value is strictly greater than the upper value of the valid temperature range, then the THEMAS system shall identify the current temperature value as an invalid temperature and shall output an invalid temperature status. Otherwise, the THEMAS system shall output a valid temperature status.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is simple input validation logic with no significant impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: A temperature setting value that is received from an individual thermostat shall be compared to the valid temperature range values. If the temperature setting value is strictly less than the lower value of the valid temperature range or if the temperature setting value is strictly greater than the upper value of the valid temperature range, then the THEMAS system shall identify the temperature setting as an invalid temperature and shall output an invalid temperature status. Otherwise, the THEMAS system shall realize the value for that thermostat’s temperature setting.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized input-validation rule with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall determine when a reported temperature or a changed temperature setting exceeds the limits set by the overtemperature values. Temperatures that exceed the overtemperature limits shall be reported as such. Temperatures that do not exceed these limits shall be output for subsequent processing.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data-processing function that does not drive architectural structure, patterns, or nonfunctional trade-offs.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall compare the reported temperature value to the temperature setting and detect when the temperature value exceeds the specified limits. If T < LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded. In this case this process shall output the condition of the temperature limit having been exceeded. If LO <= T <= UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level functional behavior (threshold check) that does not drive high‐level architectural structure or quality attributes.

requirement: When the current temperature value exceeds the current temperature setting by a pre-defined amount, the THEMAS system shall activate the appropriate heating or cooling unit.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic control rule with limited scope and no major impact on overall system structure.

requirement: There are two conditions for each individual thermostat that shall be tested for: 1) the thermostat’s settings are satisfied and 2) the thermostat’s temperature indicates it requires a heating or cooling unit to be turned on. If the thermostat’s current temperature setting is satisfied, the module shall output a request to turn off both the heating unit and the cooling unit. If the thermostat’s temperature indicates it requires a heating or cooling unit to be turned on, this module shall output a request to turn on the heating unit if LO <= T < LT or the cooling unit if UT < T <= UO.
ASR_Explanation: This is purely detailed control logic for a thermostat module and does not drive any high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall control each of the heating and cooling units that are defined for the system. The THEMAS system shall limit the number of heating or cooling units that may be running simultaneously.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the system’s resource management and concurrency control architecture by imposing limits on simultaneous unit operations.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall maintain the ON/OFF status of each heating and cooling unit. When a request to turn on or off a heating or cooling unit is received, the system shall determine if the request can be honored. If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue. If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is not running, this process will generate a request to turn on the requested unit. When a request to turn off a heating or cooling unit is received, this process shall check the queue of waiting heating and cooling requests. If the queue is not empty, this process shall remove one request from the LIFO queue and check the current state of the thermostat for which the queued request was made. If that thermostat still needs a heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a LIFO queuing mechanism and resource‐limit checks, affecting core scheduling, state management, and concurrency control architecture.

requirement: When a request for a heating unit or cooling to be turned is denied, an event shall be generated and the THEMAS system shall record that event. The information in these events will be used for creating statistical reports.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for an event-driven logging/reporting infrastructure, impacting choices for messaging, storage, and analytics components.

requirement: This procedure shall realize the thermostat and heating or cooling information and use this information to generate a specific system event. This system event shall consist of a description of the event type (a request denied event), a designation of the thermostat that made the request, and a designation of the heating or cooling unit that was not turned on.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement about event content, not impacting the high-level system architecture or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall control the heating and cooling units that are designated as part of the THEMAS system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for hardware integration layers and real-time control components in the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a request to turn on or off a heating or cooling unit is made, this process shall generate the appropriate request to carry out that request. This request shall include the designation of the heating or cooling unit and a flag to indicate whether that unit is to be turned on or off.
ASR_Explanation: This detailed functional behavior does not drive high-level architectural decisions or patterns.

requirement: Once a request to turn on or off a heating or cooling unit, the THEMAS system shall provide the necessary control signal for the unit. This control signal shall also provide an indication of the unit’s status at the requesting thermostat.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the design of inter-component communication and control interfaces for issuing signals and propagating device status.

requirement: This process shall recognize the values for the requested heating or cooling unit and the ON or OFF status that is being requested. The necessary signal to the heating or cooling unit will be generated. Since the interface to control the units has not been defined, the part of this process that will issue the signal is designated as TBD (to be determined). In order to provide an indication of the status of the heating or cooling unit back to the requesting thermostat, this procedure shall output the status information of the heating or cooling unit. In order to provide an operational history and statistical reports, this process shall generate an event each time a change is made to the status of a heating or cooling unit. This event shall contain the identification of the heating or cooling unit whose status is being changed.
ASR_Explanation: This FR introduces a TBD control interface and event-driven status reporting that will shape module boundaries, messaging infrastructure, and integration architecture.

requirement: There are two events that shall result in an alarm condition: 1) an invalid temperature value is reported from a thermostat, or 2) the reported temperature has exceeded the defined limits. This process shall determine which alarm event is to be generated.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the event-processing pipeline and alarm-handling modules by requiring fault detection, validation, and prioritized alarm generation.

requirement: When the THEMAS system detects a request for an alarm, this process shall detect which of the two alarms are being requested. If the system detects an invalid temperature, this process shall output a continuous series of alternating 500 Hz and 700 Hz beeps on the supervisor’s computer. Each beep shall have a three-quarter second duration. This series of beeps shall continue until the supervisor manually resets the alarm through the supervisor’s interface window. If the system detects a temperature limit has been exceeded, this process shall output a continuous series of alternating 1000 Hz and 1500 Hz beeps on the supervisor’s computer. Each beep shall have a one-half second duration. This series of beeps shall continue until the supervisor manually resets the alarm through the supervisor’s interface window. Each time an alarm is requested, an alarm event shall be recorded. This event shall be used to provide operational and statistical reports about the system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a real-time alarm management subsystem (audio output, asynchronous looping, manual reset UI integration, and event logging) which impacts overall system architecture.

requirement: For each event that is generated, the THEMAS system shall identify each event and generate the appropriate event data.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the core event-processing pipeline (identification, routing, and data generation) and dictates use of an event-driven architecture.

requirement: When an event occurs, the THEMAS system shall identify the event type and format an appropriate event message. The THEMAS system shall record each event by a description and the current system time for that event. This information shall be recorded in a Microsoft® Access® database that shall reside on the supervisor’s computer.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the choice of a local Microsoft Access database and its deployment location, impacting the data storage and deployment architecture.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall provide the supervisor a mechanism to change the temperature setting of any of the thermostats in the system.
ASR_Explanation: This basic functional requirement for changing thermostat settings does not drive any major architectural decisions or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The supervisor’s interface shall display the available thermostats and their individual current temperature settings. The supervisor shall be able to select one of the thermostats and select a value for the current temperature from a list of valid temperatures.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI functional requirement that does not impose significant architectural constraints or high-level design changes.

requirement: The THEMAS shall provide the ability for the supervisor to select between two different types of reports: 1) the operational history of the THEMAS system for the past twelve months and 2) a statistical summary for any selected month.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard reporting feature that can be implemented within existing data storage and reporting modules without driving major architectural changes.

requirement: The supervisor’s interface to the THEMAS system shall provide a mechanism to select between an operational history report or a statistical summary report. Either report shall consist of an ASCII file whose location and name shall be selectable by the operator. If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select all the events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file. If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection. After selecting one of the months, the system shall generate the ASCII report file. The statistical reports shall consist of the following statistics: 1. The percentage of time each heating and cooling unit was on for each day of that month. 2. For each thermometer, the ratio of the number of requests for a heating and cooling unit that was granted to the number of requests for a heating or cooling unit that was denied.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed reporting function that does not impose significant constraints on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The THEMAS system shall be designed to run on the Microsoft® Windows NT™ operating system. All the internal process communications shall be designed to operate on this operating system. Any communication with the thermostats and heating and cooling units shall be done through the interface to these units. These interfaces shall run on this operating system as well.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: Mandating Windows NT as the deployment platform drives major architectural decisions around OS‐specific IPC, driver support, runtime services and hardware interfacing.

requirement: The thermostat is capable of returning the current temperature and the current desired temperature setting to the THEMAS system. The thermostat is constantly returning these values with no real time delay in between the thermostat and the THEMAS system. The thermostat also has the capability of being set and controlled by a user of the THEMAS system. All data sent by the thermostat is in the correct format for the THEMAS system to use.
ASR_Explanation: This real-time, zero-latency data exchange requirement drives the choice of communication protocols, middleware and performance architecture.

requirement: It is assumed that the heating/cooling unit is incapable of returning its current off/on status to the THEMAS system. The heating/cooling unit has no real time delay when sending these statuses to the THEMAS system. The heating/cooling unit shall have the capability of being turned off and on by the supervisor of the THEMAS system.
ASR_Explanation: Imposes real-time device integration and control interface constraints, impacting the system’s communication and timing architecture.

requirement: The thermostats shall only provide temperature values and temperature settings. The heating and cooling units provide no feedback to the THEMAS system. When a signal is sent to a heating or cooling unit, no signal shall be available to allow the THEMAS system to determine if the signal sent to the unit was realized by the unit.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint on unidirectional communication and lack of feedback directly impacts the system’s interface design, error handling, and reliability architecture.

requirement: The system should provide warnings to the supervisor about faulty temperatures.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the real-time monitoring and alerting infrastructure, shaping event-driven design and reliability guarantees.

requirement: The displaying of the current status of the system to the supervisor should not contain excessive information which could confuse the supervisor.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level usability requirement and does not drive significant changes to the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: The system should provide information in the form of reports to the supervisor so that the system can be run efficiently.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a reporting subsystem with data aggregation, storage, scheduling, and presentation capabilities, impacting core architectural layers.

requirement: The SRS shall give a prototype of a simulated thermostat for verification and validation of THEMAS reliability.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates adding a simulation module and test harness for the thermostat prototype, affecting system modularity and interface design.

requirement: Any operation requiring the user to supply a file name shall allow the user either to select the files from a list derived from files in the currently open application or to key in a file name, a relative path name, or a complete path name.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the UI and file-handling architecture by mandating both a file-list picker component and flexible path-resolution/parsing logic.

requirement: For any operation where the user is prompted to select from a list, the user shall be able to cancel the operation.
