ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI interaction requirement and does not drive significant architectural decisions.

requirement: When collecting generated output files from HATS-SML, the HATS-GUI shall overwrite any file whose name is generated automatically. For example, given a transformation language program X.tlp, the file X.tlp.parsed is generated when the parser is executed. If X.tlp.parsed existed prior to executing the parser, it shall be overwritten without prompting the user.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level file I/O behavior requirement and does not drive changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: When saving or copying files, the HATS-GUI shall use the following sequence of actions: prompt the user for a file name, examine the contents of the application directory, prompt to overwrite if file exists, attempt to write using a temporary name, delete the previous file, rename the new file, and notify the user of any errors.
ASR_Explanation: This describes a low-level file-handling sequence (safe save pattern) and does not affect the overall system structure or architectural layers.

requirement: Output pretty-printed text files and SDTs shall only be displayed when the user requests.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple user-triggered display behavior with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The main interface shall be menu driven. A summary of the menus available is given in Table 5.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI design detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The File menu shall contain menu items related to manipulating individual files and for closing the HATS-GUI application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI requirement that does not drive or constrain the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Application menu shall contain items related to manipulating entire applications.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Configure menu shall contain items related to modifying the configuration of the application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional detail and does not affect the system’s high-level architectural structure or constraints.

requirement: The Run menu shall contain items related to executing the HATS-SML programs.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI detail for menu content and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The View menu shall contain items related to viewing HATS-SML output.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI enhancement (adding menu items) with no significant impact on system architecture.

requirement: The File/Select menu item shall provide an interface for selecting files within the currently open application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI functionality requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The File/Edit menu item shall provide an interface for starting an editor as an external process. The editor shall be started according to information in the application configuration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI-level functional detail that does not drive core architectural structure or major technology choices.

requirement: The File/Save-as menu item shall provide an interface for saving a previously selected file under a different name.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-level functional requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The File/Delete menu item shall provide an interface for deleting a selected file. The HATS-GUI shall prompt the user to confirm deletion prior to completing this action.
ASR_Explanation: Standard UI confirmation dialog requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The File/Exit menu item shall provide an interface for exiting the HATS-GUI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor GUI implementation detail with negligible impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Application/Select menu item shall provide an interface for selecting and opening an application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI-level functional requirement with minimal impact on high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The Application/Close menu item shall provide an interface for closing an application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic UI-level feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The Application/Save menu item shall provide an interface for saving application configuration information.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI feature with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Application/Save-as menu item shall provide an interface for saving an application and its associated files under a different name.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level file operation requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Application/Delete menu item shall provide an interface for deleting an application and all the files in the application directory. The HATS-GUI shall prompt the user to confirm deletion prior to completing this action.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI confirmation and file‐deletion function with no major architectural impact.

requirement: The Configure/Editors menu item shall provide an interface for the user to enter editor configuration information. This information shall be used when editors are started as processes.
ASR_Explanation: This UI-level feature for editor configuration and process invocation has minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Configure/Node Display menu item shall provide an interface for configuring the display of nodes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI configuration feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow the user to specify the number of levels of nodes to expand during SDT viewing.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor GUI feature that does not impose new constraints on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow the user to select the colors for types of displayed nodes in a displayed graph. At a minimum, the system shall provide eight colors (red, yellow, white, black, green, blue, orange, and violet) for displayed nodes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI feature (color selection for node types) that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow the user to select the shapes for types of displayed nodes in a displayed graph. At a minimum, the system shall provide two shapes, rectangles and ellipses, for displayed nodes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture and can be handled within existing UI components.

requirement: The Configure/File Associations menu item shall provide an interface for associating files in an application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level feature with minimal impact on high-level architecture or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The Configure/HATS-SML menu item shall provide an interface for the user to enter implementation-dependent information for starting HATS-SML processes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI functional requirement with no significant impact on high-level architectural structure or constraints.

requirement: The Run/Generate Parser menu item shall provide an interface for generating a parser program.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level feature with minimal impact on overall system structure or architectural decisions.

requirement: The Run/Parse Target menu item shall provide an interface for selecting and parsing target programs.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional detail without broad structural or quality-impacting implications.

requirement: The Run/Parse Program menu item shall provide an interface for selecting and parsing transformation language programs.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-specific functional requirement with limited impact on high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The Run/Execute Transforms menu item shall provide an interface for selecting and executing transformation language programs.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level command feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Run/Pretty-print menu item shall provide an interface for selecting SDTs and pretty-print style files and formatting the SDT according to the style.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific UI feature requirement without cross-cutting concerns or constraints that would affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The View/SDT menu item shall provide an interface for viewing, navigating through, and searching SDTs output from the execution of transformation programs.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-specific functional requirement that does not impose significant constraints on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The View/Text menu item shall provide an interface for viewing and searching pretty-printed text.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI presentation feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The View/Standard Error menu item shall provide an interface for viewing error strings collected from HATS-SML.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI feature that binds to an existing error‐collection service without impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: The View/Standard Output menu item shall provide an interface for viewing debugging information collected from HATS-SML.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a UI-level feature with negligible impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The View/Select Text menu item shall provide an interface for selecting pretty-printed text or text strings associated with SDT displays.
ASR_Explanation: It describes a specific UI feature without imposing cross-cutting architectural constraints.

requirement: The View/Copy Text menu item shall provide an interface for copying selected text into an operating system buffer.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI feature using standard OS clipboard APIs and does not affect the system’s overall architectural structure.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall interface with HATS-SML (version 2.0) in order to parse transformation language programs, execute transformation language programs, and create output SDTs, error messages, pretty-printed text, and parsed transformation language programs. The interface shall consist of starting HATS-SML programs and passing command line arguments and capturing data written to the standard output and standard error streams of the HATS-SML processes.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the inter-process communication and orchestration architecture for spawning HATS-SML, handling its CLI arguments, and capturing its output and errors.

requirement: The GUI shall allow the user to create, modify, copy, or delete applications or files regardless of the current status of HATS-SML.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates an asynchronous, decoupled UI–backend design (e.g., command queuing or state management) so CRUD actions work irrespective of HATS-SML’s current status.

requirement: If the HATS-GUI attempts to start a HATS-SML program and fails, the HATS-GUI shall notify the user by displaying the following message: “Unable to start HATS-SML.” The HATS-GUI shall provide any other details related to the failure that are available from the Host OS.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI-level error‐handling requirement and does not drive a major architectural decision or structure.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall be able to communicate with Sun Solaris, Linux, and Windows 2000.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the architecture by requiring a cross-platform abstraction layer and OS-specific communication modules.

requirement: Data sets shall be saved as files using file names with the appropriate extensions. See Table 3 for description of the files and extensions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level file naming convention requirement and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The user shall be able to initiate processes from an interface provided by the HATS-GUI. The HATS-GUI shall optionally pass command line arguments to the process. Command sequences for initiating processes are part of the application configuration.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of the GUI‐to‐backend invocation layer and configuration framework for process management.

requirement: After initiating processes for editing files, the HATS-GUI is not responsible for communications with the process other than passing command line arguments.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement enforces clear component boundaries and dictates the inter-process communication design by limiting the GUI’s responsibilities to process initiation only.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall interact with the HOST OS file services to perform the following tasks: create new files, delete existing files, open an existing file for read and/or write, read from an open file, write to an open file, close an open file.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates OS file service integration, requiring an abstraction layer and impacting system modularity, portability, and security.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall interact with the HOST OS to find files in directories. (This capability is necessary for the HATS-GUI to display file lists.)
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing a file‐system abstraction layer and OS integration component, impacting module decomposition and platform dependency.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall interact with the HOST OS to compare time stamps for files. Time stamps indicate the date and time of the most recent update to a file. These time stamps must be reported with a precision of one second or less.
ASR_Explanation: This file timestamp precision requirement is a low-level implementation detail that does not drive the system’s architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow a user to create an application minimally consisting of a lexical specification file, a grammar file, a user-defined functions file, and application configuration information. The user shall be able to name this application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional requirement with limited system-wide architectural impact.

requirement: All files associated with a given application shall be stored in one directory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level file organization constraint with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The directory in which application files are stored shall be named with the application name.
ASR_Explanation: This naming convention is a low‐level implementation detail with negligible impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: There shall be exactly one file describing the lexical specification of inputs associated with an application. It shall have the file extension .spec.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement is a low-level file naming and count constraint and does not affect the system’s high-level structure.

requirement: There shall be exactly one file describing the grammar of inputs associated with an application. It shall have the file extension .grm.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level naming and file-organization constraint that does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: There shall be exactly one file describing the interpretation of function symbols associated with an application. This file shall be written in ML and have the file extension .lib.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation and packaging constraint (file naming/technology) that does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall suggest a file name for the user whenever it prompts the user to enter a file name. The HATS-GUI shall suggest the same file name for files copied to a different directory. The HATS-GUI shall suggest a file name with a sequentially numbered extension for files copied to the same directory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level usability feature that doesn’t drive or alter the system’s high-level architectural structure.

requirement: An application shall have associated with it an application configuration. The application configuration elements are given in Table 6.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a cross-cutting configuration management subsystem that affects component initialization, deployment, and integration.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall display output written to the standard output and standard error streams in a tab-selectable window. Text in this window shall be cleared prior to executing individual HATS-SML programs, unless the programs are contained in a program script. In the case of a script, the window contents shall be cleared prior to executing the script.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level GUI behavior requirement with no significant impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The system shall open a new window when the user selects to display output.
ASR_Explanation: This is a narrow UI behavior requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Display windows opened by the system shall have buttons for closing the windows.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI detail that can be handled within existing window management components without affecting the overall architecture.

requirement: A navigation window shall be available.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI functionality that does not impose any significant architectural constraints.

requirement: Each active display window shall have a mouse cursor, which indicates the location of the pointing device in the window.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level UI detail affecting only the presentation layer, with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Each display window shall have a keyboard cursor to track the current location in the display.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI behavior requirement with minimal impact on high-level system architecture.

requirement: The keyboard cursor shall be movable by the arrow keys.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI input behavior that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The keyboard cursor shall be set to the location of the mouse cursor when the pointing device is clicked.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI event-handling detail with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the File/Select menu item, the HATS-GUI shall provide the user with a list of files in the currently open application and allow the user to select a file. This file becomes the currently selected file. If no application is currently open, the HATS-GUI will prompt the user to select and open an application, then prompt the user to select a file.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI workflow requirement that does not drive or constrain the overall software architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the File/Save As menu item, the HATS-GUI shall prompt the user to enter a new name. A new file shall be created in the currently open application with the newly entered file name. The contents of the selected file shall be written into the new file, and the new file shall become the currently selected file.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine GUI-level file operation with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the File/Delete menu item, the HATS-GUI shall prompt the user to confirm the deletion operation. If the user confirms, the currently selected file shall be deleted. No file shall be currently selected at the end of this operation.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional detail (confirmation dialog and deletion behavior) with no significant impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the File/Edit menu item, the HATS-GUI shall request that the operating system start a process and load that process with the editor specified by the application configuration. The name of a selected file shall be passed to the editor as a command line argument. The editor started by the HATS-GUI shall be the editor associated with the file in the application configuration, or if there is no editor associated with the file, then the editor associated with the file type of the file in the application configuration. If no editor is associated with either the file or the file type, then the default editor shall be used. If no default editor has been selected, then the HATS-GUI shall present a list of the configured editors and allow the user to select an editor. If no editors have been configured, then the HATS-GUI shall inform the user that an editor must be configured before starting an editor.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional flow for editor invocation and fallback logic, not a high-level architectural driver.

requirement: When a user selects the File/Exit menu option, the HATS-GUI shall request the host operating system to kill all currently active HATS-SML processes started by the HATS-GUI and halt the HATS-GUI process. If changes have been made to the configuration but have not been saved, the user shall be prompted to save the configuration. If the user agrees, the configuration shall be saved. If the user does not agree, the configuration shall not be saved.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local UI shutdown and configuration‐save behavior that does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: When a user selects the Application/Select menu item, the HATS-GUI shall display a list of directory names in the current working directory, allow a user to navigate the directory structure, and allow a user to select a previously created application. This application becomes the currently selected application.
ASR_Explanation: This UI navigation requirement is a standard functional detail that does not drive high-level architectural structure or major technical constraints.

requirement: When a user selects the Application/Close menu item, the HATS-GUI shall close the currently selected application. No application is currently selected at the end of this operation. If changes have been made to the configuration but have not been saved, the user shall be prompted to save the configuration. If the user agrees, the configuration shall be saved. If the user does not agree, the configuration shall not be saved.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level interaction flow and state check that does not impose changes on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the Application/Save menu item, the HATS-GUI shall save all application configuration information to nonvolatile storage.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement addresses routine configuration persistence and does not alter the system’s high-level architectural structure.

requirement: When a user selects the Application/Save As menu item, the HATS-GUI shall prompt the user for a new application name. The HATS-GUI shall create a new directory at the same level as the currently selected application. All files in the application directory, including all subdirectories, shall be copied to the new directory, and the new directory shall become the currently selected application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized file‐copy feature with no impact on the system’s high‐level architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the Application/Delete menu item, the HATS-GUI shall prompt the user to confirm the deletion operation. If the user confirms, all files in the application directory shall be deleted and the application directory itself shall be deleted. No application is currently selected at the end of this operation.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-driven file deletion workflow with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When deleting an application directory, if the application directory contains a subdirectory, the system shall confirm the deletion of the subdirectory with the user before continuing. If the user does not confirm the deletion of the subdirectory, the application director shall not be deleted.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed user interaction business rule for delete operations and does not impact system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the Configure menu options, the HATS-GUI shall provide a list of elements in the application configuration, allow the user to select an element, then prompt the user for a value for the configuration element. The application configuration elements are given in Section 3.2.1.1. This information becomes part of the application configuration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed GUI functional behavior with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the Run/Generate Parser menu option, the HATS-GUI shall generate the parser using the MakeParser HATS-SML program as described in Appendix D.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level UI integration requirement that does not affect the system’s overall architectural structure.

requirement: When a user selects the Run/Parse Target menu option, the HATS-GUI shall parse the selected target program by using the ParseTarget HATS-SML programs as described in Appendix D. If no target program is selected, the HATS-GUI shall first prompt the user to select a target program.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user-interface control flow requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the Run/Parse Program menu option, the HATS-GUI shall parse the selected transformation language program by using the ParseTlp HATS-SML programs as described in Appendix D. If no target program is selected, the HATS-GUI shall first prompt the user to select a transformation language program.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed GUI-level functional requirement with no broad impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the Run/Execute Transforms menu option, the HATS-GUI shall execute the ApplyTransformations HATS-SML programs as described in Appendix D. This process is further described in Section 3.2.3.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-driven functional requirement with no broad architectural impact.

requirement: When a user selects the Run/Pretty-print menu option, the HATS-GUI shall execute the Pretty-print HATS-SML programs as described in Appendix D. This process is further described in Section 3.2.3.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI functional requirement that does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the View/SDT menu option, the HATS-GUI shall prompt the user to select an SDT file in the current application. The file list shall include SDTs in the application. The viewing of outputs is detailed in Section 3.2.3.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional detail for file selection that does not drive the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the View/Text menu option, the HATS-GUI shall prompt a user to select a pretty-printed text file in the current application. The viewing of text outputs is detailed in Section 3.2.3.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed GUI interaction requirement and does not impose high-level architectural constraints.

requirement: When a user selects the View/Standard Error menu option, the HATS-GUI shall display the standard error screen showing all outputs written to the standard error stream since the most recent clearing of this data. The viewing of errors is detailed in Section 3.2.3.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a user selects the View/Standard Output menu option, the HATS-GUI shall display the standard error screen showing all outputs written to the standard output stream since the most recent clearing of this data. The viewing of output is detailed in Section 3.2.3.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI behavior requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When viewing an SDT, a single left mouse click on an expandable displayed node shall expand the node by displaying the node’s children or collapse the node by hiding the node’s children.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI interaction detail with no broad architectural impact.

requirement: When a displayed node is expanded, the number of levels of children added to the displayed graph shall be determined by the application configuration.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts configuration management and data retrieval layers, influencing performance and modular graph rendering.

requirement: When viewing an SDT, a single left mouse click on an already-expanded displayed node shall collapse the displayed node. When a displayed node is collapsed, all descendants of the node are removed from the displayed graph.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI interaction detail for collapsing graph nodes and does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall collect process exit codes from HATS-SML processes. Process exit codes of 0 indicate nominal completion of the process. Process exit codes other than 0 indicate failure.
ASR_Explanation: It defines required inter-process communication and error‐handling integration between the GUI and SML processes, shaping module interfaces and monitoring architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall allow the user to import a file into the selected application. Importing a file consists of copying the file into the application directory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized GUI feature with no broad impact on the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall use the protocol and program signatures listed in Appendix D when executing HATS-SML programs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines critical interface contracts and communication protocols, directly shaping the system’s modular structure and integration architecture.

requirement: To perform a transformation in an application, the HATS-GUI shall execute the following sequence of actions: confirm that the transformation language program exists, check that the parser file is younger than the grammar and lexical specification files and that the parser file was generated from the grammar and lexical specification files, check that a parsed version of the transformation language program exists and is younger than the parser file and the transformation language program file, start the FindTarget program using the transformation language program as input, for each file in the list check that a parsed version of the target program exists and is younger than the target parser file and the target program file, start the ApplyTransformation program in HATS-SML and pass the parsed transformation language program file name, the user defined library file name, and the output file name to the ApplyTransformation program, redirect the standard output and standard error streams and capture any text written to those streams.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed procedural flow for a single feature’s implementation and does not impose constraints on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow the user to continue working while transformations are taking place. Thus a user shall be able to edit files, view output, and transmit other problems to HATS-SML while waiting for HATS-SML to generate output.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates asynchronous processing and concurrency support, impacting the GUI’s threading model, communication layer, and overall architectural design.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow the user to execute a script file. A script file shall contain a linear sequence of HATS-SML programs. The HATS-GUI shall begin with the first command and execute the commands sequentially until either an error occurs or all commands in the script file have been executed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior of command execution logic and does not drive high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: Prior to starting the execution of a script file, the display screens for the standard error and the standard output shall be cleared.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level CLI/UI behavior that does not impact system structure or key architectural concerns.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow a user to select output files in the application to display.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level GUI interaction requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow a user to display selected output files. The manner in which the output is displayed shall depend on the type of the output.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI functionality requirement and does not drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The user shall be able to select an arbitrary set of displayed nodes in a displayed graph. Selected displayed nodes shall be indicated visually. The following methods shall be used to select displayed nodes: right-clicking the mouse cursor, moving the keyboard cursor and pressing enter, clicking and dragging the pointing device selection rectangle, holding the shift key while selecting nodes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI interaction requirement with no broad impact on system architecture.

requirement: The user shall be able to deselect all selected displayed nodes by clicking either the right or left mouse buttons while the mouse cursor is not pointed at any displayed nodes and the shift key is not depressed; or by pressing the escape key.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI interaction detail (event handling) with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When viewing an SDT, selecting nodes shall result in the highlight of all displayed nodes in the smallest well-formed tree of the selected nodes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI interaction requirement with no significant impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: When the system displays a navigation window, the user shall be able to select a current SDT location by clicking the mouse cursor in the navigation window. The center of the displayed graph will be the displayed node closest to the mouse cursor when the mouse is clicked.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level UI interaction detail that does not drive high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: When both an SDT and a pretty-printed text display corresponding to a single transformed target file are open, highlighting and cursor location motion shall be mirrored in the two windows. Thus, when a displayed node is selected in the SDT display, the corresponding text in the pretty-print window is highlighted. When the user scrolls to the end of the pretty-printed text display, the display of the SDT should also scroll to the corresponding displayed nodes.
ASR_Explanation: It requires establishing an event‐driven synchronization layer and mapping service between the SDT and pretty‐print components, impacting the UI architecture.

requirement: The user shall be able to resize windows dynamically. When displayed graph windows are resized, the HATS-GUI shall respond by utilizing the space available.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes architectural constraints on the GUI layer by requiring a responsive layout manager and dynamic resizing event propagation.

requirement: The display of an SDT shall also result in the display of text from leaf nodes in a text display area.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level UI functional detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The text displayed in the text display area shall correspond to the concatenation of the node labels (separated by spaces) of the leaf nodes of the displayed graph (in-order traversal, left to right). If a leaf displayed node corresponds to a non-terminal SDT node, the label in the text window shall be surrounded by pointed brackets (<>).
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI display behavior requirement affecting view logic, not driving high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall provide for the copying of text from the text display area to a system buffer available for pasting into other applications (such as an editor).
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI clipboard feature with minimal architectural impact, implementable via existing GUI toolkit functionality.

requirement: The user shall be able to display more than one SDT at a time. Each SDT shall be displayed in a separate window.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the UI architecture choice (e.g., MDI vs SDI), affecting window management, resource allocation, and concurrency handling.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall provide for the manipulation and display of SDTs.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward GUI function that does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: SDTs shall be displayed as directed acyclic graphs with nodes and edges. Nodes in the displayed graph correspond to nodes in the SDT. Edges in the displayed graph correspond to the parent-child relationship between nodes in the SDT.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the UI architecture by requiring integration of a DAG rendering/layout engine and corresponding data structures.

requirement: Initially, a displayed graph will have a single displayed node. That node will correspond to the root of the SDT.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI initialization detail and does not drive any major architectural concerns.

requirement: When a displayed node and its children are displayed simultaneously, the parent node shall be displayed higher than the children.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI rendering detail about node layout order and does not impact the system’s architectural structure or qualities.

requirement: All displayed children of a single node shall appear at the same height.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI layout detail with no substantial impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The user shall be able to control the display by choosing the color and shapes of displayed nodes based on node type. The HATS-GUI shall use the shape and color of displayed node types stored in the application configuration to display nodes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized GUI feature using existing configuration and rendering components and does not impose new architectural constraints.

requirement: The user shall be able to expand displayed nodes (provided it is possible to expand the selected nodes). The HATS-GUI shall change the graph display by adding the descendant nodes to the displayed graph (thus changing the expandable nodes to internal nodes).
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level feature for dynamic graph updates without broad architectural impacts.

requirement: The user shall be able to collapse displayed nodes. The HATS-GUI shall change the displayed graph by removing all descendants of the selected displayed nodes (thus changing the internal nodes to non-expandable leaf nodes).
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level feature for collapsing nodes in the view with no broad architectural impact.

requirement: The user shall be able to hide a set of selected displayed nodes. The HATS-GUI shall change the display by removing the selected nodes from the display and changing the parent and descendant nodes of the removed nodes to nodes with hidden descendants and parents, respectively.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-specific functional change impacting view rendering logic without altering the overall system architecture.

requirement: The user shall be able to issue an unhide instruction that reverses the hide instruction for all displayed nodes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-level feature that can be implemented within existing view components without affecting the overall architecture.

requirement: After issuing the unhide instruction, a user shall be able to issue a rehide instruction that reverses the unhide instruction. All displayed nodes hidden before the most recent unhide instruction shall become hidden.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local UI behavior requirement with no major impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow the user to expand the SDT by a predetermined number of node levels. The number of levels expanded is set in the application configuration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI functional detail with configurable behavior and does not drive architectural structure or major cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall provide the function to fully expand a sub-tree regardless of the node expansion level in the application configuration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized GUI feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The user shall be able to view multiple SDTs simultaneously.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the UI and data layer architecture by requiring support for concurrent data streams, dynamic layout management, and increased performance/scalability.

requirement: SDT displays shall also provide the user with a view of text associated with the leaves of the displayed sub-tree. This text shall be copy-and-pasteable in the host operating system. Thus, text displayed in a HATS-GUI window can be pasted into other applications such as text editors that may be running concurrently with the HATS-GUI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a GUI-level copy-paste feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: SDT displays shall provide the user with the option to refresh the display. When a display is refreshed, the current display is erased, the SDT file is read, and a new SDT display is generated. The new SDT display shall approximate the previous SDT display by expanding the SDT to the same number of nodes, if this is possible.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional requirement for display refresh behavior and does not impose architectural-level constraints.

requirement: If a displayed graph or a text display is too large to fit inside its window, the windows shall have scroll bars attached to them. Dragging scrollbar buttons shall scroll the display. Pressing the arrow keys of the keyboard shall also scroll the display.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI implementation detail about scrolling behavior and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: For text windows, the keyboard cursor location shall move according the following rules: left arrow key moves left, right arrow key moves right, up arrow key moves up, down arrow key moves down, with appropriate behavior at line boundaries.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI behavior requirement with no substantial impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: If keyboard cursor movement causes the keyboard cursor to move to text or graph nodes not currently displayed, the display will scroll the minimal amount to display the current cursor location.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI behavior detail (minimal scroll on cursor move) that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: For displayed graph windows, the keyboard cursor location shall move according the following rules: left arrow key moves to the next unhidden node to the left at the same level, right arrow key moves to the next unhidden node to the right at the same level, up arrow key moves to the closest ancestor that is not hidden, down arrow key moves to the leftmost, nearest descendant that is not hidden.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a detailed UI interaction rule affecting input handling in the graph viewer, not driving high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The pointing-device cursor shall track the motion of the pointing device. The keyboard cursor shall move to the text element or graph node closest to the pointing-device cursor when the pointing device is clicked.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI interaction detail for input handling and does not drive major architectural structure.

requirement: If the displayed graph of the entire SDT is too large for the window, a separate, small window with a compressed view of the SDT shall be displayed. The compressed view is called the navigation window display. In this navigation window, a representation of the entire SDT will be drawn. A small box will be displayed showing the part of the SDT currently displayed in the main tree display window.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces a new UI component with view synchronization and rendering considerations, impacting the front-end architecture and data flow between views.

requirement: The user shall be able to turn the navigation window on and off.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI toggle feature with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall provide the user the capability to search SDTs. The search criteria are described in Appendix E. The user shall enter a search pattern. The HATS-GUI shall search the SDT for a matching pattern. When a match is found, the HATS-GUI shall highlight the matching displayed nodes and center the display on the left-most, top-level displayed node in the matching pattern. The keyboard cursor is set to this node. If no matches are found, the HATS-GUI shall display a message stating “No Match Found.”
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI feature requirement with minimal impact on overall software architecture.

requirement: To initiate an SDT search, the user shall be able to use the following sequence of actions: select a set of nodes using the mouse cursor, press the Ctrl-Insert key combination, select the tree search option, HATS-GUI presents a prompt for entering the search pattern, user presses the Ctrl-Shift-Insert key combination, HATS-GUI fills the search pattern entry box with text concatenated from the nodes highlighted at the time of the Ctrl-Insert key press, user selects initiation of search.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement solely defines a UI interaction sequence and does not drive any architectural structure or major quality-attribute tradeoffs.

requirement: When searching for SDT patterns, search begins at the SDT node corresponding to the node at the current keyboard cursor location. This becomes the original starting location. Search continues top-to-bottom, left-to-right until the end of the SDT is encountered. When the end of the SDT is encountered, search will continue from the root of the SDT until arriving back at the starting location.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional algorithm specification for SDT node traversal and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall provide for repeating a search. Repeating a search shall find the next matching sub-tree starting from the current keyboard cursor location. Search terminates when either a matching sub-tree is found or the search arrives at the original starting location.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-specific functional detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall provide for repeating a search in the reverse direction. Searching in the reverse direction continues right-to-left, bottom-to-top. Search terminates when either a matching sub-tree is found or the search arrives at the original starting location.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level behavior enhancement within the existing search component and does not drive any major architectural changes.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall provide the user the capability to search the text display for text sub-strings. The search criteria are described in Appendix F. The user shall be prompted to enter a text string describing a string pattern. The HATS-GUI shall search the text for a matching pattern. When a match is found, the HATS-GUI shall highlight the matching text and center the display on the left-most element of the matching text. If no matches are found, the HATS-GUI shall display a message stating “No Match Found.”
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward GUI search feature with no major impact on overall architectural patterns or component structure.

requirement: When searching for text patterns, search begins at the current keyboard cursor location and continues left-to-right, top-to-bottom until the end of the text is encountered. When the end of the text is encountered, search will continue from the start of the text until arriving back at the starting location.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level search behavior detail with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The formatting of the displayed pretty-printed text shall be faithful to the formatting represented in the pretty-printed text file. No additional white space shall be inserted, and a fixed width font shall be used to display the text.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level UI rendering detail (font choice and whitespace handling) with minimal impact on overall system structure.

requirement: The user shall be able to select pretty-printed text files to display.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI/file‐handling feature with no significant impact on the overall architecture.

requirement: Selected pretty-printed text shall be displayed in a separate window.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a low‐level UI presentation detail that doesn’t impact the system’s high‐level architecture or core quality attributes.

requirement: Windows for pretty-printed text shall allow for scrolling left, right, up, and down if the text is too large to display in the window.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level functionality detail (scroll behavior) that does not drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall provide the user the capability to search the text display for text sub-strings. The search criteria are described in Appendix F. The user shall be prompted to enter a text string describing a string pattern. The HATS-GUI shall search the text for a matching pattern. When a match is found, the HATS-GUI shall highlight the matching text and center the display on the left-most element of the matching text. The keyboard cursor shall be set to this location. If no matches are found, the HATS-GUI shall display a message stating “No Match Found.”
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI-level functional requirement without broad implications for system architecture or structural design.

requirement: When searching for text patterns, search begins at the location of the keyboard cursor and continues left-to-right, top-to-bottom until the end of the text is encountered. When the end of the text is encountered, search will continue from the start of the text until arriving back at the starting location.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized search behavior requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall not halt as a result of any error messages received from HATS-SML.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architectural design of error handling and resilience patterns between the HATS-GUI and HATS-SML interfaces.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall collect error messages from HATS-SML in a single display area. This display area shall be initially clear, and shall contain all error messages generated by HATS-SML since the HATS-GUI was started. The display area shall be a window accessible via a window tab. The display shall list all error received from HATS-SML in the order received.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI feature for error display and does not impose significant architectural impacts.

requirement: Errors shall not be displayed until requested by the user.
ASR_Explanation: This UI-focused display behavior can be handled at the presentation layer without affecting the overall system architecture.

requirement: The user shall be able to clear the error window without restarting the HATS-GUI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI feature that doesn’t impact the system’s overall architectural structure.

requirement: When both an SDT and a pretty-printed text display corresponding to a single transformed target file are open, navigation in the SDT window shall result in navigation in the pretty-printed text window. Searching, selecting, and cursor location motion should be mirrored in the two windows. Specifically, when a node is selected in the displayed graph, the corresponding text in the pretty-print window is highlighted.
ASR_Explanation: It requires an event-driven synchronization layer between the two UI components, impacting component boundaries and communication architecture.

requirement: When both an SDT and a pretty-printed text display corresponding to a single transformed target file are open, navigation in the pretty-printed text window shall result in navigation in the SDT display. Selecting, and cursor location motion should be mirrored in the two windows. Specifically, when text is selected in the text window, the displayed nodes corresponding nodes in the SDT are highlighted.
ASR_Explanation: This is a view‐level synchronization requirement that can be handled within the existing UI event/model‐binding framework without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall display and SDT with 10,000 nodes in five seconds or less.
ASR_Explanation: This strict rendering performance requirement drives architectural choices in data structures, rendering pipelines, and resource allocation within the GUI.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall display 1,000,000 node SDTs in the same amount of time, plus or minus two seconds, as it takes to display a 10,000 node SDT.
ASR_Explanation: This performance requirement drives the need for a scalable, high‐performance rendering and data‐processing architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow a user to request transformations while HATS-SML is performing transformations or parsing.
ASR_Explanation: Requires asynchronous, non-blocking GUI-to-engine communication with concurrency support, impacting threading, queuing, and event-driven architecture.

requirement: The HATS-GUI shall allow a user to navigate through the display of an SDT with 10,000 nodes so that scrolling a screen width with any portion of the SDT displayed takes less than 3 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This performance requirement will drive GUI architecture choices (e.g., virtualization, lazy loading, efficient data structures) to ensure sub-3-second scrolling on large node sets.

requirement: The complexity of the text search algorithm shall not exceed O(n2) for n nodes.
ASR_Explanation: This performance constraint drives the choice of search algorithms and data structures, impacting system scalability and resource allocation.

requirement: The complexity of the tree search algorithm shall not exceed O(n2) for n nodes.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a performance constraint that will drive algorithm choice and overall system scalability decisions.

requirement: The complexity of the scrolling algorithms for text and displayed graphs shall not exceed O(n2) for n displayed nodes.
ASR_Explanation: This performance constraint drives architectural choices around rendering pipelines, data structures, and algorithm selection to ensure efficient scrolling.

requirement: The user shall not be required to log in or authenticate his/her identity when using the system.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the security and access-control architecture by removing authentication modules and altering session management and data protection.

requirement: The GUI shall run on Windows 2000, Sun Solaris 8, and Linux operating systems without modification of the Java code.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates using a pure-Java, OS-agnostic GUI framework and deployment strategy, affecting technology choices and platform abstraction layers.

requirement: The system shall be implemented in the Java 1.3 programming language.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying Java 1.3 dictates available libraries, platform features, and runtime behavior, directly influencing architectural choices.

requirement: One algorithm for displaying an SDT has been implemented and found to be unacceptable: Draw SDT into large virtual window with a smaller viewing area; Zoom in on portions of the tree; This algorithm results in excessive memory usage and slow performance. This algorithm shall not be used in the HATS-GUI.
ASR_Explanation: This prohibition imposes a performance and memory constraint on the GUI rendering architecture, driving the selection or design of an alternative tree‐drawing module.

requirement: KeePass must be downloaded and installed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level deployment instruction for a third-party tool and doesn’t impact the system’s architecture.

requirement: Master Password has no limits in length. A whole sentence can be used with more than 100 characters.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts storage schema, input buffering, and hashing performance, requiring adjustments to database columns and security processing.

requirement: Folder selected must be of type the database can read and that is “name”.kdb.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple input validation requirement for file selection with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: Databases must have different names or else the previous one will be replaced if selected.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data validation constraint (unique naming) at the schema level and does not affect the overall architectural structure.

requirement: There must be entries in the database in order for them to be printed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple functional validation rule with negligible impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: All data related to the word must be shown. For example if user types “abc” and abc is part of a password and of a username, both entries must be shown.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a full-text search/indexing architecture across multiple data domains, impacting database design, performance, and security.

requirement: A name is required in order for the new group/subgroup to be created.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple input validation rule for entity creation and does not affect the system’s high-level structure or design.

requirement: A subgroup cannot be created when no group is selected.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI/validation constraint with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A name is required in order for the group/subgroup to be renamed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple input validation requirement for renaming operations and does not impact the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: In order to find something in a group a word must be placed in the find field.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic UI functional requirement for a search field and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: An entry must belong to a group to be created.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple data integrity rule enforceable via a foreign-key or service-layer check with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: When the password field is completed the repeat password field must be completed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple form-level validation rule with no impact on high-level system structure or architectural decisions.

requirement: Password field and repeat password field must be identical.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI-level validation requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: An entry must be selected to be viewed or modified.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple CRUD interaction requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When the password field is changed the repeat password field must be changed and be identical with password field.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a simple form-level validation requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: An entry must be selected before it is duplicated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI-level validation rule with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: An entry must be selected to be deleted.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic CRUD operation requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: An internet connection and a browser are required in order to download new language translations.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the architecture by requiring internet connectivity and browser integration for translation downloads, impacting update module design, network handling and offline support.

requirement: The prefix “Auto-Type:” is required in front of each sequence.
ASR_Explanation: This naming convention requirement has minimal architectural impact and can be implemented within existing modules.

requirement: Sequence’s length must not be more than a line (59 characters).
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple input-validation constraint with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: If two auto-types are referred in one note field, only the first is used.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level parsing rule with minimal impact on overall system structure or component boundaries.

requirement: Only one database file is allowed in command line options.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level CLI parsing constraint with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: In case a space is found in the path, it must be enclosed into quotes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level input parsing detail for handling file paths, not affecting the system’s overall architectural structure.

requirement: If a master password is required to unlock the database, the database doesn’t open unless the password is entered.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the security architecture by necessitating an authentication mechanism, key management, and encryption integration for database access.

requirement: If a key file is required to unlock the database, the database doesn’t open unless the key file is present.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s security architecture by enforcing key‐file based encryption and startup key management integration.

requirement: If there is a composite key, both master password and key file are required.
ASR_Explanation: This 2-factor composite key requirement impacts the authentication and key-management architecture, dictating support for both password handling and secure key file storage.

requirement: In case of lost master password or key file, the database never unlocks again. There is no recovery.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates irreversible key management with no recovery paths, impacting the security and storage architecture.

requirement: There is no backdoor or key that unlocks all databases.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring distributed key management and per-database access controls rather than a universal backdoor key.

requirement: File formats are not specialized password database formats.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the data persistence architecture by disallowing specialized password database formats, impacting storage and security module design.

requirement: File formats only specify a low-level layout of stored data.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation detail of data storage formats and does not drive the system’s high-level structure or module interactions.

requirement: Global hot key cannot be changed.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint only affects the UI keybinding logic and doesn’t drive any major architectural decisions.

requirement: Title, username or URL cannot be changed in a TAN entry.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD constraint enforceable in business logic or database schema with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: When a TAN is used, it expires automatically and can never be used again.
ASR_Explanation: This is a functional detail for token lifecycle management and doesn’t affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: KeePass should run on Operating Systems: WINE, 32-bit MS Windows (95/98), 32-bit MS Windows (NT/2000/XP), All 32-bit MS Windows (95/98/NT/2000/XP), Win2K, WinXP, Microsoft Windows Server 2003.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes platform compatibility constraints requiring abstraction layers and portability considerations in the system architecture.

requirement: KeePass should run perfectly on older releases without any features limitations or data loss.
ASR_Explanation: It requires architectural provisions for backward-compatibility layers and careful dependency management to support older platforms without feature or data loss.

requirement: When a password is copied for any reason, it remains in the memory for only 10 seconds. After 10 seconds pass there is nothing to paste and you have to recopy again.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing a secure clipboard mechanism with timed memory eviction, impacting the system’s security and memory management architecture.

requirement: Not in all translations translated help files and tutorials are available.
ASR_Explanation: This is a content/localization completeness issue, not requiring changes to the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: KeePass uses Advanced Encryption Standard (AES / Rijndael) and Twofish algorithms with 128-bit block size and 256-bit key size for encryption.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying AES and Twofish with 128-bit block size and 256-bit keys directly impacts the security architecture, crypto module design, and performance constraints.

requirement: For the creation of the 256-bit key the Cipher uses, the Secure Hash Algorithm SHA-256 is used.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying SHA-256 for key generation dictates the cryptographic architecture, impacting security component design, performance, and compliance.

requirement: All the bytes needed for the Initialization Vector, the master key salt, etc are generated via pseudo-random sources.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the design by requiring integration of secure pseudo-random number generators and cryptographic components, defining trust boundaries and module interfaces.

requirement: When KeePass is active, all passwords are stored encrypted in process memory using the ARC4 encryption algorithm, using a random 12 bytes long key.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates an in-memory encryption layer, affecting crypto library selection, memory management design, and performance trade-offs.

requirement: KeePass is a small and light project so it does not need to be installed. All it takes is unpacking from the Zip package. It can be transferred also in a USB stick with no additional configuration needed.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the deployment architecture by mandating a portable, zero-install, self-contained packaging with file-based configuration and no external dependencies.

requirement: KeePass is a project that once uninstalled from a computer, leaves no trace behind. So there is no way passwords and other data in the database to be found later.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of secure storage, encryption, and uninstallation modules to ensure complete data erasure with no residual traces.

requirement: KeePass is developed under GNU General Public License version 2 or later and can be distributed under those terms.
ASR_Explanation: This licensing constraint will shape architectural decisions around third-party dependencies, linking strategies, and distribution packaging.

requirement: KeePass can be found and downloaded for free.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR concerns licensing/cost considerations rather than influencing system structure, design, or key architectural decisions.

requirement: When a USB which contains the database is removed from a computer while changes haven’t been completely saved, the database is damaged and cannot be opened. In this case the repair functionality can help by repairing KeePass database file from tools menu.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the storage subsystem architecture by requiring built-in journaling and a repair module for fault-tolerant database recovery.

requirement: In case the user forgets or loses the Master Password, the repair functionality won’t help.
ASR_Explanation: This is a policy-level constraint with minimal impact on system structure or architectural design.

requirement: In case the header of the database, which is the first few bytes, is corrupted, again the repair functionality won’t help.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the storage subsystem architecture by mandating header integrity checks and alternative recovery/fallback mechanisms.

requirement: To avoid data loss situations, backups can be done regularly.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s reliability and disaster‐recovery architecture by requiring backup scheduling and storage strategies.

requirement: The system must be flexible in supporting future requirements and future developments.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the system’s modifiability and extensibility, impacting architectural styles and componentization.

requirement: The system must be open in the sense that 'anyone shall be able to get information on anything from anywhere', once they have authorisation to the system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a global, secure access‐control and data‐delivery architecture supporting authorization, federation, and open APIs.

requirement: The system shall be adapted to individual users and services provided accordingly by means of configurations, set-ups etc.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a modular, configurable architecture with dynamic service adaptation to support per‐user customization.

requirement: The communication system shall be based on open and widely accepted methods with a high degree of interface possibilities.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the use of open standards and modular protocols, impacting integration layers and overall system interoperability.

requirement: The system shall be robust and reliable, but the system shall not be used for the safe and secure operation of the plant.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around fault tolerance, redundancy, and reliability mechanisms even though it excludes safety-critical operation.

requirement: Faults in the communication system shall not cause malfunction of an individual wind turbine.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the fault-tolerance architecture by requiring decoupled communication modules and local control redundancy in each turbine.

requirement: The system shall be designed in a way that faults of a sub-system interfere as little as possible with functions of the communication system as a whole.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architectural design for fault isolation and resilience, influencing subsystem boundaries and error-containment strategies.

requirement: In designing the system it shall be taken into account, that the physical environment at the plant typically has a wide span of temperature, moisture, salinity and vibration levels.
ASR_Explanation: It requires ruggedized hardware and environmental tolerance strategies, influencing key platform and enclosure architecture decisions.

requirement: Condition monitoring system provides status and analysis reports for components. The values shall be available for display on operator HMI as well as for storage (databases).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data flow architecture by defining real-time acquisition, storage services, and HMI integration across system layers.

requirement: Updating of values shall be selectable down to an interval of 1 sec.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes a real-time update frequency requirement affecting scheduling, performance and resource management in the system architecture.

requirement: All data must be stored in the plant controller for transmission on demand.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the on-device storage architecture and impacts controller memory sizing, data management, and transmission subsystem design.

requirement: Transfer of data from the buffers may be carried out off-line without synchronism with real-time.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates asynchronous, offline data‐transfer impacting buffer management, module decoupling, and scheduling within the system architecture.

requirement: The basic services of the communication system shall include: Connection establishment and release, Authentication, Identification of functional object and devices, Data access and transfer, Reliable communication over a network.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It defines core communication and security services (connection management, authentication, identification, data transfer, reliability) that shape the system’s protocol and component architecture.

requirement: Data can be transferred according to one of the following principles: Periodic data transfer, Data transfer on demand, Event driven (spontaneous) data transfer, Command transfer, Set point transfer.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates supporting multiple data transfer modes, impacting communication patterns, scheduling mechanisms, and interface design.

requirement: The following kinds of data need to be supported: Measurements/analogue data, Set points, Binary Signals/Status data, Binary control commands, Alarms, Events, Counters, Timers, Data structures, Time series data, Short text messages, Flat files.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of core data modeling, storage subsystems, and messaging infrastructure to support diverse real-time, batch, and file-based data types.

requirement: It should be possible to time stamp all data. Time stamped data shall be stamped with 'last updated date + time' (UTC time). The accuracy and resolution of the timestamp should be at least 10 ms.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts system-wide time synchronization and data model design to ensure consistent UTC timestamps with 10 ms resolution across all components.

requirement: All analogue measured values should have readable properties like 'signal quality' and 'scanning rate'.
ASR_Explanation: It requires extending the data acquisition interfaces and data model to carry metadata (signal quality, scanning rate), impacting component design and performance.

requirement: The averaging time and the measuring and averaging method should be documented for all data.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a documentation NFR for metric metadata and doesn’t materially affect the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: It should be possible to group both analogue and binary values so they can be read in a single transaction.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data access layer and transaction handling design by requiring support for mixed-type batch reads in a single operation.

requirement: Authentication, Data Integrity and Data Confidentiality are required for security.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates defining security architecture layers—authentication, encryption, integrity checks and key management—that shape component design and technology choices.

requirement: Server authentication shall ensure the client application that it is truly operating on the intended site. Client authentication ensures that an authorised client/operator is operating the equipment.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates the design of secure authentication mechanisms and trust infrastructures, directly shaping the system’s high-level security architecture.

requirement: The rights for each user to operate functions and to see data on different levels in the object hierarchy might be necessary to set.
ASR_Explanation: It demands a fine-grained access control framework that impacts security, identity management, and data access layer architecture.

requirement: Non-corruption of data transferred is necessary, i.e. the ability of a communication system to deliver data from its originator to its destination with an acceptable residual error rate.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the choice and design of communication protocols (error detection/correction, checksums) and influences system reliability and network architecture.

requirement: Data items transferred might need to be encrypted to prevent both malicious and false operation, as well as eavesdropping.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates integrating encryption protocols and key management into the communication architecture, impacting security and performance.

requirement: The response times of most operational functions and, therefore, of the related communication does not need to be much faster than one second (human time scale).
ASR_Explanation: It defines a performance budget that will drive architectural decisions around component design, communication protocols, and infrastructure capacity.

requirement: System management functions, which shall be available for the operators and control systems, are of low time critical nature. Delay in execution of these functions however should not be more than 2 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This 2 s latency bound is a performance constraint that will drive decisions on communication patterns, concurrency, and resource provisioning.

requirement: No functions regarding safety of persons shall be based on the communication system. No functions regarding safety of plant and electrical network shall be based on the communication system – all safety functions must be self-contained in the process or in the devices where systems interface and will trip automatically.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives a self-contained safety architecture with local trip logic, isolation, and redundancy independent of the communication layer.

requirement: In situations where the communication system is completely inaccessible, the plant may be forced to a shutdown by alternative means.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical fallback shutdown requirement drives the design of redundant communication paths and fail-safe control architecture.

requirement: The time critical functions shall use short messages with a high priority. Data-wise the messages shall be small and shall be transmitted with a minimum of delay.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the communication infrastructure by requiring low-latency, high-priority messaging protocols and QoS mechanisms.

requirement: Time critical functions must be based on fast and reliable transmission of a number of selected data types.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of network topology, communication protocols and middleware to meet strict latency and reliability constraints.

requirement: The overall transfer time for services in time critical functions shall not be more than 0.5 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes strict sub-0.5 s latency requirements that drive choices in system architecture, protocols, caching, and infrastructure.

requirement: Reliability in the sense that data can be retransmitted, reconstructed, or reprocessed if lost or inaccessible of some reason is essential.
ASR_Explanation: This non-functional requirement drives the design of fault-tolerance, replication, and data recovery mechanisms in the system architecture.

requirement: For most data it must be possible to restore information, including the sequence of events.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the choice of an event‐sourcing or audit‐log storage architecture to persist and replay data change sequences.
