requirement: The communication system shall include functionality to transfer stored data to central storage and processing after restoration of the communication.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It mandates a store-and-forward subsystem with offline buffering and sync logic, affecting data flow, reliability, and integration architecture.

requirement: To prevent interruptions in the data transfer, the communication system shall allow for redundant communication channels.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates architecture-level design of redundant communication paths and failover mechanisms to ensure uninterrupted data transfer.

requirement: Automatic procedures for detection of communication faults and for managing redundancy of system components shall be established.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of the system’s fault-tolerance and redundancy architecture, impacting communication layers and component replication strategies.

requirement: The physical transport media should possibly be redundant to a certain degree depending on the conditions at the specific plant.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the network infrastructure topology and fault-tolerance mechanisms by mandating redundant physical transport media.

requirement: There must be a way for existing plants to interface to a new communication system.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of integration layers, connectors, and communication protocols to support legacy plant interfaces.

requirement: The expected solution to interface systems using proprietary methods for communication, e.g. manufacturer-specified protocols or customer-specified protocols, to new communication systems is to use gateways.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the architectural design by mandating gateway components for protocol translation and system integration.

requirement: The interface to existing plants will provide a subset of the functions and data specified in this Specification. It should however as far as possible be able to present data on the same HMI and provide as many data as possible for the system databases.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the integration layer and adapter components for legacy plants, affecting HMI data presentation and database schema.

requirement: Each device or object shall be self-descriptive (generic part) and the system has to have a function to extract the information contained in the wind power plants objects.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s core data‐model and interface architecture by mandating self‐describing objects and a metadata extraction mechanism.

requirement: It shall be possible to issue an identify request and get a list of all objects in a wind power plant, their names and possibly a short description for each object.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data-retrieval function that does not impose any special architectural patterns or cross‐cutting concerns.

requirement: It should be possible to get the attributes and services for each object. The list should at minimum include, Name, Type/Kind, Unit, Time requirements, and possibly a short Description.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data‐retrieval requirement affecting the data model and API response fields but not the overall system architecture.

requirement: For the HMI such information shall be contained in the device using standard readable text, such as ASCII or Unicode (UTF16) (at least optional in the language of the operator).
ASR_Explanation: It mandates choosing and integrating text‐encoding and localization support (ASCII/UTF-16) in the HMI, affecting UI framework, data storage, and resource management.

requirement: At least for default naming a hierarchical name structure and an object data dictionary specialised for wind power plants should be used.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s core data model and metadata architecture by mandating a hierarchical naming scheme and specialized object dictionary.

requirement: During data transmission the message should at least include the following parts; Name, Value(s), Scan frequency, Time tag and Quality.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the mandatory message schema and metadata, driving the data model, serialization format, and communication protocol design.

requirement: Communication and objects in the wind power plant shall be object based.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating object-based communication drives the choice of middleware, shapes component interfaces, and impacts overall system integration and coupling.

requirement: The system shall be able to manage naming of objects and variables (measurements, etc) in a hierarchical naming system in several levels.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the core data model and naming conventions across system components, affecting storage, interfaces, and data retrieval architecture.

requirement: All analogue process values shall be accessible in standard SI-units or other physical units.
ASR_Explanation: It only imposes unit‐conversion logic in data processing components without driving any high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: Analogue values 'at the source' shall be available as real-time on-line instant data as well as time averaged values.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of a low-latency data acquisition and processing pipeline with real-time streaming and time-series aggregation components.

requirement: The values shall be available for display on operator HMI as well as for storage (databases).
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional requirement for UI display and data persistence and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: Updating of analogue on-line values shall be selectable down to an interval of 1 sec.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a performance and throughput constraint on data acquisition, processing, and network layers to support 1 s update intervals.

requirement: All averaged values must be stored in the plant controller for retransmission on demand.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the controller’s data storage and retrieval architecture, affecting memory allocation, persistence mechanisms, and communication interfaces.

requirement: For averaged values the accuracy of the start time of the period shall be better than 10 ms.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes tight timing accuracy requirements that will drive the design of clock synchronization, scheduling, and real‐time processing components.

requirement: Some process values are not required as measurements directly at the source. The values shall be accessible as processed data in a condensed and analysed format.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the design of the data processing pipeline, storage, and access layers to transform, condense, and serve analysed values.

requirement: Values for local functions could be sent as set points. A confirmation of the set point update is required.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the communication architecture by mandating reliable acknowledgment protocols for set-point updates between control modules.

requirement: All binary process values shall be accessible. Binary values shall be available as real-time on-line instant data.
ASR_Explanation: This real-time, on-line delivery requirement drives selection of low-latency data streaming, communication protocols, and scalable processing architecture.

requirement: The values shall be available for display on operator HMI as well as for storage (databases).
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional requirement for data display and storage with no significant impact on the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: The values shall be stored and displayed at level shift with the corresponding date and time tag.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data‐logging and display requirement that doesn’t drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: Updating of binary on-line values shall be selectable down to an interval of 1 sec.
ASR_Explanation: This 1 s update interval imposes a real-time performance constraint affecting scheduling, concurrency and resource provisioning in the system architecture.

requirement: A handshake procedure is required for all commands that start or stop a mechanical component, influence the status or operation mode of the wind turbine or change the software.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes protocol-level changes and safety-critical state management in the control system architecture.

requirement: All other control commands shall give a response with the result of the command.
ASR_Explanation: This is an implementation detail about command-response behavior and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The binary commands may also include activation and deactivation of programs and parameter changes.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the command-processing and module-lifecycle architecture for dynamic activation, deactivation, and parameter changes.

requirement: Operational alarms must be transmitted immediately after a triggering.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a low-latency, real-time notification requirement that drives choice of messaging middleware, network design, and timing constraints.

requirement: The alarms shall be available for display on operator HMI as well as for storage (databases).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the alarm event pipeline, impacting messaging, storage schema, and HMI integration architecture.

requirement: Operational events must be stored in an event log in the plant controller for transmission on demand.
ASR_Explanation: Requires a persistent event-log subsystem in the plant controller, impacting storage, retrieval and transmission architecture.

requirement: Counters shall be available for display on operator HMI as well as for storage (databases).
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the data flow and integration between the counter acquisition module, HMI interface, and persistence layer.

requirement: The values shall be stored with a corresponding date and time tag. Updating of counters shall be selectable down to an interval of 1 sec.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level data‐format and timing detail that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: All values must be stored in the plant controller for transmission on demand.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the controller’s data storage and memory allocation design to support on-demand transmission.

requirement: The timers make it possible to determine the time for the important states in the wind turbine, e.g. Generator on-time, Yawing time and Free to operate time. It should be possible to reset all the timers and the 'Reset date' shall be stored as a separate item.
ASR_Explanation: This is domain-level timer functionality and reset storage detail with no broad architectural implications beyond standard module and data-persistence design.

requirement: Data values can be grouped based on logical relationships between the data, as chronologically ordered data, as text etc.
ASR_Explanation: This is a functional detail about data grouping logic and does not impose significant high-level architectural constraints.

requirement: It should be possible to exchange text messages between the wind power plant and the control centre using standard readable text, such as ASCII or Unicode (UTF16).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the data encoding standards for inter-system communication, affecting protocol design and interoperability layers.

requirement: Typically files will be used for upload and download of programs etc.
ASR_Explanation: Standard file upload/download functionality doesn’t impose unique architectural constraints.

requirement: Selected analogue values shall be stored in FIFO-buffers. The sampling rate of analogue values shall be high enough to characterise events and to determine the cause of faults. A typical sampling rate could be 25 Hz.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the real-time data‐acquisition architecture, affecting FIFO buffer sizing, memory management, and scheduling to meet the 25 Hz throughput and latency requirements.

requirement: The size of the buffers for analogue values shall correspond to a time span starting at 1 minute before a triggering and ending at 1 minute after a triggering.
ASR_Explanation: This buffer sizing requirement drives memory allocation and real-time data‐flow design in the system’s signal processing architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to start a scanning of selected analogue values at a sampling rate up to 25 Hz and a selectable duration. The entire mentioned high rate scanning must be stored in the plant controller.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the real-time data acquisition and storage architecture, requiring buffering, throughput, and memory management design.

requirement: Transfer of data in the buffers shall be carried out without synchronism with real-time.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates architectural support for asynchronous data transfer, affecting buffer management, concurrency control, and overall data flow design.

requirement: All binary values must be stored in the plant controller for retransmission on demand.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the design of the controller’s internal storage and memory management for persistent data retrieval.

requirement: It shall be possible to start a scanning of selected binary values at a selectable duration. All the mentioned scanning must be stored in the plant controller.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes timing and persistent storage constraints that will drive the design of the scheduling, sampling, and data‐storage subsystems.

requirement: Alarms must be stored in an alarm log. All alarms must be stored in the plant controller for transmission on demand. The buffer depth shall be at least one year.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the storage architecture by requiring a persistent, high-capacity buffer and retrieval mechanism for year-long alarm retention.

requirement: The buffer depth of the event log shall be at least one year.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts storage architecture and data retention strategies, requiring scalable log management and provisioning.

requirement: The buffer size for every counter shall be at least 20 years of operation with 5000 full load hours per year.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives buffer capacity planning and resource allocation, impacting storage/memory architecture and long-term system reliability.

requirement: Transferring assets within the same department: database can be updated directly without any request.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s transaction, security, and workflow architecture by requiring direct database updates that bypass standard request processing.

requirement: Inter-department asset transfer: request must be approved by a Department Administrator (DA) group member and faculty group member unless it came from a higher level group.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the role-based access control and workflow engine, affecting module interactions and approval routing logic.

requirement: Inter-faculties asset transfer: request can be made by any authorised user and approved by faculty group or higher level.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s role-based access control and multi-level approval workflow architecture for cross-faculty asset transfers.

requirement: Transfer of assets outside university should be approved by the university group.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard business rule enforceable within the existing workflow and access‐control layers without altering the overall system architecture.

requirement: Any administrative level user or inventory user can edit an asset that belongs to its department; same for faculty or university user, if authorised.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing role-based, department-scoped access control and authorization mechanisms.

requirement: All fields of an edited asset can be modified except IDs.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a simple business rule enforcing ID immutability that can be handled within the existing data model and service layer without architectural changes.

requirement: A bulk entry file can be used to modify assets.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces bulk file parsing and batch update processing requirements that impact system performance, transactional integrity, and module design.

requirement: Any DA group member or authorised inventory group member can add assets owned by the department.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard role-based access control detail that does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: Any faculty member can add all related departments' inventory.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a cross-department RBAC and data-partitioning design to securely allow faculty inventory modifications.

requirement: Any university group member can add all assets in the inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s authorization architecture by requiring a role-based access control mechanism that allows all group members to add inventory assets.

requirement: A bulk entry can be used to add many assets.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts architecture by requiring scalable, high-throughput batch ingestion and transactional consistency mechanisms.

requirement: Request to borrow an asset or reserve a location can be made by any authorised user.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine authorization rule and does not impose substantial architectural constraints.

requirement: After creation, a request is pending, waiting to be approved by an administrative level user with the authority.
ASR_Explanation: This simple pending-to-approved workflow with an admin role check can be handled via existing state management and RBAC without altering the system architecture.

requirement: An inventory user should check returned asset and update inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation for updating inventory with no special architectural implications.

requirement: IT group members can create a new space and modify floor structure when they receive an exception request from any administrative level.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward CRUD and role-based permission requirement that can be handled within the existing system architecture.

requirement: Any administration level or authorised inventory group member can display all pending requests waiting for approval from this level and approve those requests.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard role‐based access control and UI/listing feature with no major impact on high‐level architecture.

requirement: When request is treated, user is notified by email.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing or integrating an email notification subsystem (e.g., SMTP or messaging service) and impacts the event-driven workflow.

requirement: Request is added to the waiting for execution list.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional detail about request queuing with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Inventory is updated when user receives requested asset.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine transactional update (CRUD) with no broad impact on system architecture.

requirement: Authentication is made by user name and a password for all users.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design and integration of the security subsystem, determining credential storage, hashing algorithms, and authentication framework choices.

requirement: Administrative level users work on administration computers.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces dedicated administrative workstations, influencing network segmentation and security boundary architecture.

requirement: Any administrative level user can delegate another user to execute some or all his authorized actions. This user acquires the role of inventory administrator.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces dynamic delegation in the RBAC system, impacting the authorization framework and security services.

requirement: Asset report by location must be generated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine reporting feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture beyond standard data retrieval and presentation.

requirement: Request report must be generated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional feature (report generation) that doesn’t impose new high-level architectural constraints.

requirement: User permission report must be generated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard reporting feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: It is mandatory that learning time is between 2 to 4 hours at maximum, because many tasks are delegated to working students.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint on onboarding time drives architectural decisions for UI/UX simplicity, modular design, and streamlined workflows to ensure rapid learnability.

requirement: Web interface should use clear and consistent terminology so that users with basic internet and office experience find the application easy to use.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation-level usability guideline and does not drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: The application should be available always during working hours. Any maintenance or backup operation should be conducted out of working time.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces a high-availability architecture impacting redundancy, failover mechanisms and maintenance‐window scheduling.

requirement: The application should be installed on any Microsoft or Unix platform.
ASR_Explanation: This cross-platform installation requirement drives architectural decisions around portability layers, runtime environments, and packaging strategies.

requirement: Web application should be available to run on browsers like IE, Firefox, Chrome, Opera, or Safari.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the choice and configuration of frontend frameworks, transpilers, and polyfills to ensure cross-browser compatibility.

requirement: All users are authenticated by user name and a password.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement influences the security architecture by mandating authentication services, credential storage, and user identity management.

requirement: Permissions are assigned to users according to their roles.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a security cross-cutting requirement that mandates a role-based authorization subsystem, affecting component boundaries and data access flows.

requirement: Only IT team members can access and maintain database servers locally.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates security architecture changes—implementing RBAC, local access controls, and network segmentation for database servers.

requirement: Query is killed if it takes more than 1 minute.
ASR_Explanation: Enforces a global query timeout mechanism impacting system design for resource management, cancellation handling, and performance tuning.

requirement: Backup operations are executed periodically.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the storage and scheduling components, resource allocation, and disaster‐recovery architecture.

requirement: It is important to design the system to facilitate future evolution and maintenance operations.
ASR_Explanation: This modifiability-focused NFR drives key architectural decisions on modularization, layering, and extensibility to ensure future evolution and maintenance.

requirement: Level 0 has the permission to create requests only.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional permission requirement within the authorization module and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Level 1 can control the assets and locations that he owns.
ASR_Explanation: Requires role-based access control design affecting the security framework, service layer, and data model.

requirement: Level 2 can control the assets and locations that he owns and get all level 1 permissions.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a role hierarchy and asset-based access control that will shape the authorization subsystem.

requirement: Level 3 can control the assets and locations that he owns and get all level 2 permissions.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the security architecture by requiring hierarchical role‐based access control with inheritance and asset‐ownership management.

requirement: Level 1, 2, and 3 can delegate some or all of their permissions to any user.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the authorization architecture by introducing dynamic permission delegation and related security, audit, and policy management concerns.

requirement: Level 4 has all possible permissions. Level 4 can create and assign permissions to new groups.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional requirement for role-based permissions and does not drive high-level architectural changes.

requirement: Department administrator has control on his department inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine role-based CRUD requirement with no major architectural constraints.

requirement: Faculty administrator has control on his faculty inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD permission requirement with minimal impact beyond existing role‐based access control and inventory modules.

requirement: University administrator and IT administrators have control on the entire inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This requires a role‐based access control layer for inventory management, impacting the system’s security and modular design.

requirement: IT administrators can create different categories of permission to help administrators delegate part of their tasks.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a dynamic role/permission management layer, impacting the security architecture, data model, and authorization services.

requirement: Request related permissions: create, list, show, edit, approval or rejection.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD and approval workflow requirement that doesn’t impose major architectural constraints.

requirement: Asset related permissions: create, list, show, edit and modify.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD permission requirement that can be handled within the existing authorization framework without major architectural changes.

requirement: Location related permissions: create, list, show, edit, delete.
ASR_Explanation: Standard CRUD for permissions has minimal impact on high-level architecture beyond routine RBAC implementation.

requirement: University related permissions: create, list, show, edit, delete.
ASR_Explanation: This is standard CRUD functionality with no special architectural impact beyond typical RESTful service patterns.

requirement: Search permissions: simple, advanced.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the security and search-service architecture by requiring a role‐based or policy‐driven permission model and corresponding data‐filtering mechanisms.

requirement: Reports related permissions: list, show.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the core authorization architecture by introducing report-specific permission checks and integration with the RBAC/ACL system.

requirement: User actions related permissions: list, show, edit and change permissions.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces cross-cutting security concerns, shaping the system’s authorization and access control architecture.

requirement: Audit related permissions: list, show.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD-level permission feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The product must be available on both Windows and Linux operating platforms.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the overall architecture by requiring cross-platform abstractions, portability layers, and aligned build/deployment pipelines.

requirement: The execution of the application must be kept on the server side.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the system’s deployment topology and impacts decisions on distribution, scalability, and security boundaries.

requirement: The solution must be produced in a timely manner – see time restrictions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a project schedule constraint rather than a system quality driver and does not affect architectural structure or design.

requirement: The product should run on a minimum of a mid-range personal computer.
ASR_Explanation: This hardware constraint drives architectural decisions around performance, resource utilization, and technology selection to ensure compatibility with mid-range PCs.

requirement: The product shall run with the minimum input tools of mouse and keyboard.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR only affects low-level UI input handling (mouse/keyboard event support) and doesn’t drive the overall system architecture.

requirement: The user must have a capability for creating tab-delimited text files.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level file‐format detail for data export and doesn’t drive core architectural structure.

requirement: The product shall plot the data points in a scientifically correct manner.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized functional requirement for plotting accuracy and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The grid axis should be labelled correctly according to the input from the data file.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI labeling requirement that does not drive high-level system structure or architectural decisions.

requirement: Data points should be coloured in accordance to the cluster number (contained in the data file).
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI presentation detail with no significant impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The product should be able to handle up to 2000 data points.
ASR_Explanation: This low-volume data handling requirement does not impose significant scalability or architectural design constraints.

requirement: A single click of the mouse over a data point should bring up the name of the data point.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI interaction detail with negligible impact on the overall architecture.

requirement: A double-click of the mouse over the data point should cause the application to display all the data point's details.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI event handling detail confined to the presentation layer and does not drive core architectural structure.

requirement: The product should allow multiple points to be clicked so that multiple names can be displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI interaction detail that doesn’t drive significant architectural decisions.

requirement: The product should allow the grid to be oriented by the user. Rotation, zoom and move functions should be employed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level rendering feature (rotation/zoom/pan) that does not drive high-level architectural structure or major quality attributes.

requirement: The data file should contain: a name for the data point, 3 parameters from which the data point is to be plotted, a single parameter to designate the colour of the point, and a description for the data point.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level data format specification that does not drive high‐level architectural structure or key design decisions.

requirement: The system should allow for the user to specify their own axis. The first row of the data file should contain the column headings.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data parsing/UI detail that doesn’t drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The points should be large enough to see and select.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI usability detail that does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The points should not be too big, so as to distort the overall pattern of the point spread.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐level styling constraint with no significant impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The axis should be clearly labelled and easily recognised after the grid has been oriented into a different position.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation detail and does not impose system-level architectural constraints.

requirement: The application should be coloured so that the screen shots can be printed out clearly (i.e., black on white background).
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI styling requirement confined to the presentation layer and does not drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: The application should be intuitive and not require any specialist training.
ASR_Explanation: This usability requirement drives UI architecture choices, front-end frameworks, and component design to ensure intuitive interactions.

requirement: The program should start within 30 seconds (depends on the number of data points that are to be plotted).
ASR_Explanation: This startup time constraint drives architectural decisions around data loading strategies, initialization sequencing, and performance optimization.

requirement: The interaction with the data points should have a delay of no longer than 2 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This 2-second latency constraint drives architectural decisions around data access patterns, caching, and optimized communication mechanisms.

requirement: The response to a change in the orientation should be fast enough to avoid interrupting the user's flow of thought.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes strict low-latency requirements on orientation change handling, impacting state management, event propagation, and rendering pipeline design.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide multiple communication channels between software models running on a host workstation and a device under test (DUT) represented as a structural hardware netlist running on a verification platform.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a core interface architecture requiring support for multiple concurrent communication channels between host software models and the DUT on the verification platform.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must present channel end points as message ports to the DUT model and as message port proxies to the software models.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the inter-component communication interfaces and proxies, shaping the system’s component structure and connector design.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must cater to the needs of both emulation end users and emulation suppliers.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the modular and extensible design of the SCE-MI interface to accommodate both end-user APIs and supplier integrations.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must allow software-based verification products to port to different emulators without being restricted by proprietary APIs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for an abstraction layer and adapter pattern to support pluggable emulator backends, impacting the system’s modular and integration architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must take full advantage of emulation performance.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives critical architectural decisions around high-throughput SCE-MI interface design and hardware–software integration to maximize emulation performance.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must avoid communication bottlenecks that could compromise emulator performance.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around interface design, concurrency, and buffering to ensure sufficient throughput and avoid performance bottlenecks.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must be message or transaction oriented rather than event oriented.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the core communication paradigm for the SCE-MI interface, affecting component interactions, messaging infrastructure, and transaction management architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must support multi-threaded environments such as SystemC and also function in single-threaded environments.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the concurrency architecture by requiring thread-safe abstractions and synchronization mechanisms for both multi- and single-threaded environments.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide a service loop function that can be called from an application to service its communication channels.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates a core event‐loop mechanism affecting the control flow, concurrency model, and component interaction architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must allow the service loop function to be called periodically from a dedicated thread in multi-threaded environments.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s concurrency architecture by mandating a dedicated thread and periodic scheduling for the SCE-MI service loop.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must allow the service loop function to be called at strategic points in single-threaded environments.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the concurrency and interface design by dictating how and when the service loop is integrated into a single‐threaded scheduling architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide a means to bridge untimed software models with RTL or gate level DUTs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the simulation architecture by mandating integration layers/transactors to bridge untimed software models with RTL/gate-level DUTs, shaping component boundaries and interfaces.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must not be used to bridge event-based or sub-cycle accurate simulation environments.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint impacts the simulation integration architecture by disallowing SCE-MI bridging between event-based and sub-cycle accurate environments.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface should ideally be used for bridging untimed simulation environments with cycle accurate simulation environments.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a SCE-MI bridging layer between untimed and cycle-accurate simulation environments, impacting module interfaces and integration.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide a set of hardware side interface macros: SceMiMessageInPort, SceMiMessageOutPort, SceMiClockControl, and SceMiClockPort.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level interface API detail that does not impact the system’s high-level architectural structure.

requirement: Each SceMiMessageInPort macro must be instantiated for each required message input channel, and each SceMiMessageOutPort macro for each output channel.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation detail about macro instantiation and does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: Each SceMiClockPort macro instance must be parametrized to fully characterize a particular clock.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s macro interface and parameterization architecture by mandating a flexible clock-port configuration mechanism.

requirement: Each SceMiClockPort macro must have at least one SceMiClockControl macro associated with it.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation constraint on macro usage and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The infrastructure linker must analyze the user-supplied bridge netlist to determine the number of transactors, input/output channels, channel widths, clocks, clock ratios, and clock duty cycles.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional specification affecting implementation logic rather than driving high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide C++ objects and methods for version discovery, parameter access, initialization and shutdown, message port proxy binding and callback registration, rendezvous operations, service loop polling, message send/receive, input ready callback dispatching, and error handling.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the core C++ API surface and component interactions, shaping module boundaries, lifecycle management, and messaging architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must also provide a C API for pure C applications.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing a C API layer affects the system’s interface design, language bindings, and ABI compatibility across components.

requirement: The SCE-MI hardware side interface macros must use a dual ready handshake protocol for message transfer.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates the low-level interface protocol, impacting synchronization, timing, and module coupling in the hardware communication architecture.

requirement: The SceMiMessageInPort macro must present messages arriving from the software side to the transactor using handshake signals and a data bus.
ASR_Explanation: Defines the handshake and data bus interface architecture for software-transactor communication.

requirement: The SceMiMessageInPort macro must have a parametrized PortWidth and PortName.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation detail for macro parameterization and does not impact the system’s overall architectural structure.

requirement: The SceMiMessageInPort macro must use ReceiveReady and TransmitReady signals to indicate readiness for data transfer.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level interface implementation detail for the SceMiMessageInPort handshake, not affecting overall system architecture.

requirement: The SceMiMessageInPort macro must support input ready propagation via callbacks to the software side when more input can be accepted.
ASR_Explanation: This changes the core hardware/software interface contract by introducing a callback-based input readiness propagation mechanism.

requirement: The SceMiMessageOutPort macro must allow transactors to send messages to the software side using a dual ready handshake.
ASR_Explanation: This is an implementation‐level detail of the simulation port’s handshake protocol and does not drive system‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The SceMiMessageOutPort macro must have a parametrized PortWidth, PortPriority, and PortName.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the interface architecture by introducing configurable port parameters that affect data throughput, prioritization, and module connectivity.

requirement: The SceMiClockPort macro must supply a controlled clock and reset to the DUT, parametrized by ClockNum, RatioNumerator, RatioDenominator, DutyHi, DutyLo, Phase, and ResetCycles.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of the simulation/testbench architecture by requiring a configurable clock and reset generation interface for the DUT.

requirement: The SceMiClockPort macro must ensure that each instance has a unique ClockNum.
ASR_Explanation: This is an implementation detail for ensuring unique IDs in the macro and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The SceMiClockPort macro must allow specification of clock ratios, duty cycles, and phase shifts.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional detail for a macro’s behavior and does not affect overall system architecture.

requirement: The SceMiClockPort macro must support don’t care duty cycles for posedge or negedge active clocks.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation detail of the SceMiClockPort macro and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The SceMiClockPort macro must provide a controlled reset signal with duration specified by ResetCycles.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation detail of the SceMiClockPort macro and does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The SceMiClockPort macro must align all controlled clocks on the first rising uclock edge following the trailing edge of the creset.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates critical clock synchronization behavior at reset release, impacting the hardware’s clock domain alignment architecture.

requirement: For every SceMiClockPort macro instance, there must be at least one corresponding SceMiClockControl macro instance.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces a structural coupling between SceMiClockPort and SceMiClockControl instances, impacting component composition and system connectivity.

requirement: The SceMiClockControl macro must provide uncontrolled clocks and resets, and handshakes for explicit control of both edges of the generated cclock.
ASR_Explanation: It defines fundamental clock/reset distribution and handshake protocols that shape the system’s synchronization architecture.

requirement: The SceMiClockControl macro must allow any transactor to stop all clocks in the system by de-asserting ReadyForCclock.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts global clock domain control and synchronization, requiring architectural provisions for safe stoppage and restart.

requirement: The SCE-MI infrastructure must generate a parameter file after analyzing the user-supplied netlist, containing all required parameters for binding hardware and software sides.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the core integration layer by mandating a parameter‐generation component in the netlist‐parsing and hardware/software binding architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must provide an API for accessing and overriding parameters from the parameter file.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the core configuration management layer and its interfaces, impacting module boundaries and parameter injection mechanisms.

requirement: All SCE-MI required parameters must be read-only and automatically determined by the infrastructure linker.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement constrains the component interface and linker infrastructure design by enforcing read-only, auto-configured parameters.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must provide a singleton object representing the interface, with methods for version discovery, initialization, shutdown, message port proxy binding, and service loop execution.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level design pattern choice (singleton interface object) and doesn’t affect high-level system architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must provide error handling via SceMiEC structures and optional error handler callbacks.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces a cross-cutting error‐handling framework that affects module interfaces, control flow, and callback integration.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must provide info/warning handling via SceMiIC structures and optional info handler callbacks.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the API and event‐handling architecture by requiring structured info/warning delivery and optional callback management.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must provide memory allocation semantics such that objects constructed by the user are deleted by the user, and objects constructed by the API are deleted by the API.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines ownership and lifecycle management policies for objects, impacting the memory management architecture and API/module boundaries.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must allow message input and output port proxies to be bound by transactor and port names, with optional callback registration.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the proxy binding mechanism and callback registration interface, affecting the core communication and module interaction architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must allow replacement of port binding (callbacks and context) after initial binding.
ASR_Explanation: Requires a dynamic port binding/unbinding mechanism, impacting component interaction and lifecycle management.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must provide a service loop function that yields CPU time to the SCE-MI and dispatches input ready and receive callbacks.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the core concurrency and event‐dispatch architecture by defining the service loop and callback scheduling model.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must provide a message data object for transferring message payloads, supporting bit and word access, and cycle stamping.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines the core messaging data model and API with bit/word access and cycle stamping, directly shaping the system’s messaging architecture and performance characteristics.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must provide receive callbacks for output port proxies, which are required and must process data quickly.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the communication and concurrency architecture, mandating real-time callback mechanisms and thread management for low-latency data processing.

requirement: The SCE-MI software side must provide C API functions paralleling the C++ API for all major operations.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates designing parallel C/C++ API layers, impacting module interfaces, layering, and language‐interoperability architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must support abstraction bridges that allow untimed software models to interact with cycle accurate hardware models.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates designing key abstraction bridges and communication protocols between untimed software and cycle-accurate hardware models, impacting the core system architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must allow any transactor to instruct the infrastructure to stop the controlled clock and freeze controlled time.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the clock management architecture by mandating a control interface for halting and freezing the system clock and time domain.

requirement: All transactors must be notified by the SCE-MI infrastructure when the controlled clock is stopped.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates an infrastructure‐level event notification mechanism affecting component interfaces and synchronization architecture.

requirement: Any transactor must function correctly if controlled time is stopped due to operations of another transactor.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes core concurrency and timing control requirements on the transaction management architecture to support pausing and resuming operations safely.

requirement: A transactor may need to stop the controlled clock when performing operations involving decomposition or composition of transactions.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the clock‐control and transaction‐management subsystems by introducing pause/resume logic for transaction composition/decomposition.

requirement: The DUT must always be clocked by one or more controlled clocks that are controlled by one or more transactors.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the testbench’s clocking and transactor architecture, impacting synchronization and component interface design.

requirement: All transactors must be clocked by a free running uncontrolled clock provided by the SCE-MI hardware side infrastructure.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes an external free-running clock domain requiring specific clock distribution, synchronization, and CDC interface architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must allow the infrastructure linker to provide default values for implementation-specific parameters, minimizing the need for user overrides.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the interface and configuration architecture by dictating default parameter handling and override mechanisms in SCE-MI.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must allow the infrastructure linker to generate a parameter file that can be read by the software side for proper binding.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional interface detail (file-based parameter binding) that doesn’t drive major architectural structure or patterns.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide a means for software models to bind to message port proxies using transactor and port names.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a core interface and binding mechanism that impacts the system’s modular integration and communication architecture.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide a mechanism for input ready and receive callbacks to be registered and dispatched appropriately.
ASR_Explanation: This callback registration requirement concerns a specific interface behavior and does not drive the system’s high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must allow for the replacement of callback and context information on port proxies after binding.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the communication layer’s design by requiring dynamic reconfiguration support for port proxies and callback management at runtime.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide accessors for message port and clock parameters, including names, widths, priorities, ratios, duty cycles, phases, and reset cycles.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the public interface and data exchange contract of the SCE-MI module, impacting API design and module interactions.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must ensure that all message input and output port proxies are destroyed automatically when the interface is shut down.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level resource cleanup requirement affecting interface lifecycle implementation rather than high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide a means for cycle stamping, tagging messages with the number of elapsed counts of the fastest controlled clock.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement influences the SCE-MI interface architecture by defining message formats, timestamp integration, and clock-domain synchronization mechanisms.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must allow for both polling and blocking modes in the service loop function, depending on application needs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the concurrency and control-flow architecture by mandating flexible service-loop modes (polling vs. blocking).

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide a means for error and info messages to be logged or displayed to the console by the application.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a cross-cutting logging subsystem requirement that will affect component interfaces, error-handling flows, and framework integration.

requirement: The SCE-MI interface must provide a means for the application to override parameter values before initialization, but not after.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the system’s initialization lifecycle and configuration management architecture by requiring a pre-init override mechanism and post-init lock-down.

requirement: The system shall support point-to-point voice calls between any two call parties.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the real-time communication layer (signaling, media streaming, codecs, QoS and NAT traversal).

requirement: Such point-to-point calls shall allow both parties to talk simultaneously.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates full-duplex media streaming support, influencing the design of communication protocols, concurrency handling, and audio processing architecture.

requirement: The system shall allow a user to make public emergency point-to-point voice calls.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives low-latency, high-availability voice streaming and telephony integration architectural considerations.

requirement: The system shall support broadcast voice calls.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates real-time voice‐streaming infrastructure (MCU/SFU, QoS, scalability) and thus shapes core communication and network architecture.

requirement: The composition of call groups shall be able to be modified within the network. A single user shall be able to be a member of one or more call groups.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the architecture by necessitating a dynamic group‐management service and real‐time membership update mechanisms across the network.

requirement: The local area over which broadcast calls shall be implemented shall be able to be modified within the network.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the network topology design by requiring dynamic broadcast domain reconfiguration capabilities in the system.

requirement: It shall only be possible for the user who initiated the call to talk, other users can only listen.
ASR_Explanation: This requires adding a floor‐control mechanism in the conferencing/media layer to enforce a single active speaker per call.

requirement: The system shall support group voice calls.
ASR_Explanation: Supporting group voice calls drives the design of real-time media handling, session management, network protocols, and scalability infrastructure.

requirement: The composition of call groups shall be able to be modified within the network. A single user shall be able to be a member of one or more call groups.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the architecture by requiring a dynamic, distributed group‐membership service and supporting protocols for updating call‐group compositions in real time.

requirement: The local area over which group calls are implemented shall be able to be modified within the network.
ASR_Explanation: It requires dynamic network‐level configuration of group call zones, impacting the design of control, distribution, and scalability components.

requirement: It shall be possible for controllers to speak at any time during a group call.
ASR_Explanation: This requires real-time floor‐control and priority audio routing, impacting the design of the media subsystem and control protocols.

requirement: A mechanism shall be provided by the system to arbitrate between those users wishing to speak within the group call.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the real-time communication architecture by adding a floor‐control mechanism for session management and arbitration.

requirement: The system shall support multi-party voice communications between up to six different parties.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for real-time media mixing, concurrency and bandwidth management, shaping the choice of media-server topology and network architecture.

requirement: Any of the parties involved in a multi-party voice call shall be able to talk simultaneously.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates real-time, full-duplex multi-user audio mixing and streaming, impacting media server design and network protocol choices.

requirement: The network shall support point-to-point data communications.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement for network operations and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The network shall support data rates of at least 2.4 kbit/s.
ASR_Explanation: This data rate is a very low baseline that won’t drive major architectural trade-offs or design patterns.

requirement: If the text message facility is implemented, it shall not interfere with the ability of users to make or receive high priority voice or data calls.
ASR_Explanation: It requires architectural support for resource prioritization and isolation (QoS/scheduling) to ensure text messaging doesn’t degrade high-priority call handling.

requirement: Where fax functionality is provided, it shall be possible to interrupt the fax to make or receive a high priority voice or data call.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces call preemption and priority management, affecting the design of the telephony/fax subsystem’s resource allocation and concurrency control.

requirement: Where ERTMS/ETCS level 2 or 3 is implemented, the network shall be capable of supporting data communications for that train control system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the network architecture by imposing specific bandwidth, latency, redundancy, and QoS constraints for real-time train control communications.

requirement: It shall be possible for the equipment to display the identity of the called or calling party in the form of a standard telephone number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI-level functional requirement that does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to display the identity of the called or calling party as a textual description of their function.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s architecture by requiring integration with an identity management service and corresponding UI components to fetch and render dynamic user descriptions.

requirement: The network shall provide a mechanism whereby calls may be assigned one of a number of different priority levels.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of priority-based queuing, QoS mechanisms, and resource allocation in the network architecture.

requirement: This mechanism shall allow calls with a higher assigned priority to override (pre-empt) existing calls of a lower priority.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces a priority-based preemption mechanism affecting the core call-handling and resource-scheduling architecture.

requirement: Pre-empted calls will be discontinued and the new call of a higher priority shall be connected instead.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the call-control architecture by requiring priority-based session scheduling and resource preemption mechanisms.

requirement: The group of users who may access the facilities of the EIRENE network shall be limited.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates an access‐control mechanism, impacting the security architecture and user management components.

requirement: Any user who is not within the list of allowed EIRENE users shall not be able to gain access to any of the functions and services provided by the network.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a cross-cutting authentication/authorization layer in the system architecture to enforce user access control.

requirement: It shall be possible for an incoming call or data message for one user to be forwarded to another user using functionality provided by the network.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of call-control and routing subsystems, requiring network-level forwarding logic and dynamic user-specific routing rules.

requirement: In the case of voice calls, it shall be possible for the user who is attempting to forward a call to converse with the intended recipient prior to forwarding.
ASR_Explanation: Requires changes to the call-control and media-routing architecture to support a pre-forward consultation session before completing the forward.

requirement: The network shall allow the user to temporarily exit from an existing call by putting the call on hold.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine call-control function with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible for the user to re-join the call which is on hold at any time.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine call control feature impacting session state handling but not driving any new high-level architectural patterns.

requirement: The network shall provide the ability to inform a user, who is involved in an existing call, of attempts by other users to contact them.
ASR_Explanation: It requires changes to the call control and session management architecture to handle concurrent call events and deliver real-time user notifications.

requirement: It shall be possible, using network management or maintenance facilities, to prevent individual users from making or receiving calls to/from certain numbers or networks.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the call-control and policy-enforcement architecture by requiring dynamic blocking rules, management interfaces, and real-time routing adjustments.

requirement: The EIRENE network shall also provide support for the following railway specific services: functional addressing including registration/deregistration, location dependent addressing, shunting mode, Railway emergency calls.
ASR_Explanation: It shapes the core network architecture by requiring specialized subsystems for functional/location addressing, mode management, and emergency call handling.

requirement: The network shall support all EIRENE-compliant mobiles.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the network’s protocol stack and interoperability interfaces to ensure EIRENE compliance.

requirement: The land-based part of the system shall provide communications for mobiles when stationary and when travelling at speeds up to the maximum allowable line speed or 500 km/h, whichever is the lower.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of the wireless communication architecture—radio technology, handover strategy, antenna placement, and network topology to support up to 500 km/h.

requirement: The required call set-up times shall be achieved in 95% of cases.
ASR_Explanation: This performance NFR drives architectural decisions around component placement, communication protocols, and resource provisioning to meet strict latency targets.

requirement: Call set-up times for 99% of cases shall not be more than 1.5 times the required call set-up time.
ASR_Explanation: This strict performance requirement will drive architectural decisions around network design, resource allocation, load balancing, and real-time processing to meet latency targets.

requirement: Set-up times shall include the time required for any translation of functional numbers internal to the EIRENE network.
ASR_Explanation: This performance constraint impacts the system’s architectural design by requiring inclusion and optimization of internal number-translation components within the setup-time latency budget.

requirement: It shall be possible to determine the area over which the call takes place by one, or a combination, of the following: the location of the call initiator, the identity of the group being called, a prefix to the group identity specifying the call area.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of location-based routing, group identity resolution, and prefix‐based area determination components.

requirement: Any group or broadcast calls initiated in a given location shall be broadcast over an associated area based on the location of the call originator, and also to any fixed network numbers associated with the originating location.
ASR_Explanation: It requires architecture-level support for location-based call routing, broadcast distribution topology, and integration with fixed network endpoints.

requirement: Mobiles configured for reception of railway emergency calls entering into a call area where a railway emergency call is ongoing shall automatically join this call.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the call control and signalling architecture by mandating dynamic group‐call joining and real‐time resource allocation in the mobile network.

requirement: Cab Radios configured for reception of a call to all drivers in the same area entering an area where a call to all drivers in the same area is ongoing shall automatically join this call unless involved in a higher priority call or involved in a call of the same priority.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates dynamic location-based group call subscription and priority-aware call handling, impacting the design of call management, context-awareness, and messaging infrastructure.

requirement: It shall be possible to operate all mobiles in the frequency bands around 900 MHz, allocated for use by the railways, and in the public GSM networks.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the system’s hardware/software split and radio‐interface architecture by mandating multi-band transceiver and GSM network integration.

requirement: Mobile equipment shall function correctly when travelling at speeds from 0 km/h to 500 km/h.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes strict real-time performance and hardware/sensor constraints across a wide speed range, driving key architectural decisions.

requirement: All design, testing and installation of EIRENE mobile radio equipment shall comply with the quality procedures defined in ISO 9001.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement addresses quality management processes under ISO 9001 without imposing direct architectural design or structural constraints.

requirement: Any pressure pulses caused by vehicles passing, entering or leaving tunnels shall not affect the normal operation of EIRENE mobile equipment.
ASR_Explanation: Imposes environmental robustness constraints requiring architectural measures in hardware design and software to mitigate tunnel‐induced pressure pulses.

requirement: The design of EIRENE mobiles shall comply with the relevant European Norms for protection against physical hazards.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a mechanical safety compliance requirement that doesn’t drive high-level software or system architectural decisions.

requirement: All EIRENE mobile equipment shall be capable of being operated on board rolling stock.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes hardware and network architecture constraints to ensure ruggedized, interoperable operation onboard rolling stock.

requirement: The batteries to be used in the mobile equipment shall have sufficient capacity to support typical railway use.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the hardware and power-management architecture by requiring sufficient battery capacity to meet operational runtime demands.

requirement: Train-mounted equipment including the Cab radio terminal equipment, MMI and antenna shall comply with all specifications in section 4.3 and all of those defined in this section.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes stringent compliance constraints on hardware components and interfaces, directly influencing system architecture and subsystem integration.

requirement: The driver man-machine interface shall comprise the following components: display, control panel, loudspeaker, handset with Push-To-Talk button.
ASR_Explanation: It defines core HMI subsystem components, impacting hardware/software module boundaries and overall system integration architecture.

requirement: Radio equipment installed in a driver’s Cab shall not obstruct the driver’s vision or otherwise hinder the safe driving of the train.
ASR_Explanation: This is an ergonomic and safety placement requirement for hardware installation, not impacting the software or system architecture.

requirement: The driver shall be able to adjust the brightness of buttons, indicator lights and displays according to the ambient lighting in the cab.
ASR_Explanation: It requires integrating ambient light sensing, control algorithms, and hardware driver/ UI modules, impacting the system’s HMI and hardware interface architecture.

requirement: The driver shall be able to adjust the contrast of the display.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized display feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The emergency call button shall be red and shall be protected against accidental use.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level UI/hardware design detail without significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Any displays shall be clearly readable from a normal driver’s position, assuming a normal reading distance.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI readability constraint affecting display hardware and layout rather than driving core system architecture.

requirement: Displayed characters shall have a minimum height of 5mm.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI layout detail that doesn’t impact overall system structure or architectural design.

requirement: The standard set of audible and visual indications specified in the CENELEC Standard for the Driver-Machine Interface for ERTMS shall be implemented in the Cab radio system.
ASR_Explanation: Implementing CENELEC-compliant audible and visual indications drives high-level design of the HMI subsystem, hardware interfaces, and safety-critical partitioning.

requirement: Cab radios shall provide a means of preventing tampering.
ASR_Explanation: This security requirement drives the hardware/software architecture to include built-in tamper detection, secure boot, and encryption measures.

requirement: If contact with the mobile radio network is lost, then the Cab radio shall give an audible and visual indication.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the overall architecture by requiring integrated network-loss detection, real-time state management, and coordinated audio/visual notification subsystems.

requirement: If such an interface is implemented, it shall be capable of being connected to a device complying with UIC fiche 6413 concerning automatic vigilance devices used in international traffic.
ASR_Explanation: This standard-based interface requirement drives the architecture of communication layers and hardware abstraction for international compatibility.

requirement: The activation of the driver safety device shall automatically trigger the Cab radio to send a data message.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple functional integration between the safety device and radio, not imposing system-wide architectural or non-functional constraints.

requirement: DSD alarm information shall be transmitted to the primary controller.
ASR_Explanation: Specifies a critical inter-component messaging interface, impacting network protocols and reliability in the system architecture.

requirement: The Cab radio shall be able to provide the following information to a train-borne recorder or other equipment via a standardised interface: activation of Cab radio emergency call button, receipt of incoming Railway emergency call, termination of outgoing or incoming Railway emergency call, driver safety device alarm message transmission, details of confirmation of Railway emergency call.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines critical external interface and messaging protocols that will drive the system’s integration and module decomposition.

requirement: On-train mobiles shall provide a standardised interface to ERTMS/ETCS.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the system’s interface and integration architecture with the safety-critical ERTMS/ETCS subsystem.

requirement: The General purpose radio shall be capable of use as a standard telephone, such that the user is able to call any valid number subject to call restrictions enforced by the network.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces telephony call handling and network integration requirements that will impact system modules for protocol support, call management, and user interface.

requirement: The facility for a given General purpose radio to send a Railway emergency call shall be determined as a management function.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a management-layer component and control interfaces for emergency call capability, affecting system modularity and control-plane architecture.

requirement: A special MMI action shall be required for a General purpose radio user to send a Railway emergency call.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a dedicated UI workflow and call-routing logic, affecting the MMI subsystem and communication service architecture.

requirement: It shall not be possible for any General purpose radio user to send a Railway emergency call by dialling a short code or telephone number.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the call-routing and access-control architecture by mandating user-specific dialing restrictions.

requirement: An audible and visual indication of an incoming call shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI/UX feature that doesn’t drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Where available, the functional identity of the calling party shall be displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level display requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A short audible and visual indication of the incoming call shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This UI/UX notification requirement does not affect high-level architectural decisions or require specific architectural patterns.

requirement: The group identity of the voice group call (VBS or VGCS) shall be displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation-layer requirement affecting only the UI/display logic with no broad architectural impact.

requirement: If the call is a broadcast call, the user shall be informed that he cannot speak as part of the broadcast call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX notification detail and does not impose significant changes on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: If the call is a group call, the user has to request permission to speak during the call by using the Push-To-Talk (PTT) function.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of real-time voice channel control, permission arbitration logic, and stateful session management.

requirement: In a group call, a visual indication shall be provided to remind the user of the need to use the PTT function.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI enhancement that does not drive significant architectural changes or affect core system structure.

requirement: In a group call, an audible and visual indication of whether the request was successful shall be provided. The user may then talk whilst the PTT function is used.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the real-time media subsystem and UI architecture by requiring concurrent audio stream mixing with visual/audible feedback integration.

requirement: If the user moves out of the group or broadcast call area whilst the call is in progress, an audible and visual indication of the loss of call shall be provided to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the call management architecture by introducing real-time location monitoring and notification services for loss-of-call events.

requirement: Reception of this call (if implemented) shall proceed as for a standard group or broadcast call except that a distinctive audible and visual indication shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This solely affects the UI/notification component’s behavior and does not introduce or alter any core architectural structures or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The radio shall provide a means for the user to terminate calls which he is authorised to terminate (ie all calls except broadcast or group calls not initiated by the user).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only affects local call control logic for authorized terminations and doesn’t drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The radio shall have an on/off switch. Switching the radio ‘on’ shall initiate the following: transitory lighting of any display, controls or indicator lights; automatic self-testing; if the self-test fails a visible and audible indication shall be provided; connection to a mobile network; if connection is successful, the name of the network shall be displayed and an audible confirmation of successful connection shall be given; if connection is not successful, an audible and visual indication shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed startup sequence requirement for the power-on behavior, not a cross-cutting architectural concern.

requirement: Switching the radio off shall cause the following: termination of all calls under the control of (ie initiated by) the radio; disconnection of the radio from the network; clearing the display and inhibition of functions.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the shutdown workflow across call control, network interface, and UI modules, impacting overall system architecture.

requirement: When switched off, the radio shall retain any settings and numbers which are stored at the time the radio is switched off.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It mandates a non-volatile storage mechanism to persist user settings across power cycles, impacting hardware/software design.

requirement: Prompts and messages shall be capable of being displayed in at least 10 languages (each railway will choose which languages will be implemented).
ASR_Explanation: It mandates an internationalization/localization framework (resource bundles, locale handling and dynamic language loading) impacting core system design.

requirement: The user shall be able to select his preferred language for the display; this setting shall be retained by the radio.
ASR_Explanation: This requires an internationalization framework and persistent storage mechanism, impacting UI resource management and configuration architecture.
