requirement: When the radio is first powered on, the language that appears on the display shall be the default one set by the national railway.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only affects default UI language configuration and does not drive significant architectural structure or pattern decisions.

requirement: Where more than one mobile radio network is available for use by a mobile, the selection of the network to use shall be automatic.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a network‐management subsystem and decision logic in the connectivity architecture.

requirement: When the mobile changes network automatically, a visual and audible indication shall be given.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only affects the notification/UI layer for network-change events and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: It shall be possible for a user to override the automatic network selection manually. This function shall not be available during calls.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI/logic feature with limited scope and does not drive major architectural changes.

requirement: It shall be possible to register and deregister a functional number in the following way: Initiated by the user and transmitted to the ground: user enters functional number which is transmitted to the network.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine user-to-network transaction requirement and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Where a change of functional number is required, it shall be possible for the user to initiate the change.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward user-interface function with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible for the user to find and display stored numbers and their information.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic CRUD/display requirement with no major implications for the overall architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible for the user to store and/or overwrite numbers and their details in the radio.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic CRUD feature with minimal impact, relying on existing storage components rather than driving new architectural decisions.

requirement: If the ability to initiate Railway emergency calls is implemented in the radio, accidental initiation of a Railway emergency call shall be prevented.
ASR_Explanation: Preventing accidental emergency calls requires architectural changes to the input handling and call-initiation subsystem, including safety locks or confirmation flows.

requirement: A visual and audible indication shall be given when the remaining capacity of the battery is such as to enable only 10 minutes of full duplex call time.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a localized battery-monitoring and UI notification requirement that doesn’t drive fundamental architectural structure.

requirement: A network service availability indication shall be provided to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the inclusion of a health‐check/monitoring subsystem and its integration with the UI layer for real-time network status reporting.

requirement: A visual indication shall be provided to the user if the network service is no longer available.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a cross-cutting concern for network status monitoring and UI notification, affecting module interactions and error-handling patterns.

requirement: The Operational radio shall be capable of use as a standard telephone, such that the user is able to call any valid number subject to call restrictions enforced by the network.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s architecture by requiring integration of telephony protocols and network interfaces for PSTN call handling with enforced call restrictions.

requirement: It shall be possible for the user of an Operational radio to establish a call with each of the following types of controller (who are not permanently included in a shunting group) with a minimum number of actions being required: primary controller; secondary controller; power supply controller.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed call‐flow/UI functional requirement with limited impact on high-level architectural structure.

requirement: Once an appropriate number has been obtained, the radio shall attempt to establish a call to this number with railway operation priority.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates prioritized call setup and QoS enforcement in the communication subsystem, impacting scheduling and protocol design.

requirement: An audible and visual indication shall be provided to the user that the call is proceeding.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user interface feedback requirement and does not drive high-level architectural structure or component partitioning.

requirement: When the call is connected to the controller, an audible and visual indication shall be provided to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/audio feedback requirement with minimal impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The functional identity of the connected party, if available, shall be displayed to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This only affects the UI presentation and simple identity lookup, without driving major architectural changes.

requirement: Where available, an alphanumeric description of the identity of the connected party shall be displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI display requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: If the system is not able to connect the call, an audible and visual indication shall be provided to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level error‐handling detail with negligible impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The Railway emergency call shall be able to be initiated using a single red button.
ASR_Explanation: This single-button trigger is a UI/hardware detail with minimal impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: If the emergency call function is disabled, a visual indication and short audible indication shall be given when: the radio is switched on; the emergency button is pushed (in this case nothing else will happen).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the system’s event-handling and UI/audio subsystems, requiring architectural provisions for disabled-state notifications.

requirement: An audible and visual indication of an incoming call shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI/UX feature that does not drive significant architectural decisions.

requirement: Where available, the functional identity of the calling party shall be displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level display requirement that can be implemented with minor changes and does not drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: A short audible and visual indication of the incoming call shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This simple UI/audio notification feature has minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The group identity of the voice group call (VBS or VGCS) shall be displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation detail and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: If the call is a broadcast call, the user shall be informed that he cannot speak as part of the broadcast call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI behavior notification requirement with no significant impact on system-level architecture.

requirement: If the call is a group call, the user has to request permission to speak during the call by using the Push-To-Talk (PTT) function.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces real-time signaling and media stream control changes for permission management in group calls.

requirement: In a group call, a visual indication shall be provided to remind the user of the need to use the PTT function.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level enhancement that does not impact core architectural structures or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: In a group call, an audible and visual indication of whether the request was successful shall be provided. The user may then talk whilst the PTT function is used.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX feedback detail for the PTT feature and does not drive significant architectural structure or major cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: If the user moves out of the group or broadcast call area whilst the call is in progress, an audible and visual indication of the loss of call shall be provided to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces real-time coverage monitoring and cross-module event notification, impacting the system’s communication, location services, and UI integration.

requirement: Reception of this call shall proceed as for a standard group or broadcast call except that a distinctive audible and visual indication shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation-layer UI/UX enhancement (audible/visual indicators) that can be implemented within the existing call reception flow without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: The radio shall provide a means for the user to terminate calls which he is authorised to terminate (ie all calls except broadcast or group calls not initiated by the user).
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine call‐control feature that can be handled within existing modules without altering the overall system architecture.

requirement: If shunting mode is implemented, a means to enter and leave shunting mode shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific functional detail about mode transitions and does not drive major architectural patterns or cross‐cutting concerns.

requirement: If shunting mode is implemented, the functionality to enter or leave shunting mode shall not be available whilst there are on-going calls involving the Operational radio.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes cross-cutting constraints on the system state machine and call-management modules to coordinate mode transitions with ongoing calls.

requirement: If shunting mode is implemented, the link assurance tone shall be provided via the loudspeaker.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional detail routing an existing tone to the loudspeaker without requiring new architectural components or structural changes.

requirement: When switched off, the radio shall retain any settings and numbers which are stored at the time the radio is switched off.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a persistent storage mechanism for retaining configuration across power cycles, impacting memory and hardware integration architecture.

requirement: Prompts and messages shall be capable of being displayed in at least 10 languages (each railway will choose which languages will be implemented).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s internationalization/localization architecture, resource management, and UI adaptability.

requirement: The user shall be able to select his preferred language for the display; this setting shall be retained by the radio.
ASR_Explanation: It requires an internationalization/localization framework and persistent settings storage, impacting the UI and configuration architecture.

requirement: When the radio is first powered on, the language that appears on the display shall be the default one set by the national railway.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI configuration requirement that does not drive any major architectural decisions.

requirement: Where more than one mobile radio network is available for use by a mobile, the selection of the network to use shall be automatic.
ASR_Explanation: This requires adding dynamic network scanning and selection components, impacting the system’s interface layer and decision-making architecture.

requirement: When the mobile changes network automatically, a visual and audible indication shall be given.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI/UX notification requirement that can be implemented within existing event-handling and presentation components without redefining the system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible for a user to override the automatic network selection manually. This function shall not be available during calls.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the design of the network management subsystem and its integration with the call-control module to enforce UI restrictions.

requirement: It shall be possible to register and deregister a functional number in the following way: Initiated by the user and transmitted to the ground: user enters functional number which is transmitted to the network.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard user-driven register/deregister flow with minimal impact on system structure, performance, or other architectural concerns.

requirement: Where a change of functional number is required, it shall be possible for the user to initiate the change.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI-driven functional requirement with no significant impact on high-level system structure.

requirement: It shall be possible for the user to find and display stored numbers and their information.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic CRUD feature with minimal impact on high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: It shall be possible for the user to store and/or overwrite numbers and their details in the radio.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward CRUD feature that does not drive major architectural decisions or structural changes.

requirement: The Operational radio shall be of a rugged design suitable for use in the railway environment by operational railway personnel.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement concerns hardware ruggedization and mechanical design without impacting the software or system architecture.

requirement: Controls shall be designed such that they are suitable for use by people wearing gloves.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific ergonomic UI constraint (control sizing and sensitivity) without broad impact on the overall software architecture.

requirement: Controls shall be designed for use in a wide range of conditions, eg splash proof and suitable for viewing in direct sunlight and in darkness.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key hardware and UI architecture decisions—enclosure design, materials, and display technology must support environmental resilience and visibility.

requirement: It shall be possible to change the battery without losing data stored in the radio.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the memory and power-management architecture (e.g., non-volatile storage or backup power) to preserve data across battery changes.

requirement: Operational radios shall be equipped with rechargeable batteries capable of providing a minimum of eight hours operation from a single charge based on the following cycle: point-to-point calls 20%; group calls 60%; standby 20%.
ASR_Explanation: This battery‐life requirement drives the system’s power‐management architecture and hardware/software partitioning to ensure the eight‐hour operational profile.

requirement: The display shall be capable of displaying at least 6 lines, each of 20 characters.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes constraints on the display subsystem’s resolution and layout, affecting hardware selection and UI architecture.

requirement: The emergency button shall be designed to avoid accidental use.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives local UI/mechanical design for accidental‐push prevention and doesn’t impose system‐wide structural changes.

requirement: If shunting mode is implemented, the link assurance button shall be designed to avoid accidental use.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI/UX safety control requirement to prevent accidental button presses and does not impact the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: A dedicated Push-To-Talk button shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI/hardware detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A visual and audible alarm shall be given when the remaining capacity of the battery is such as to enable only 10 minutes of full duplex call time.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of the power‐management subsystem, hardware interfaces and real‐time alarm/event handling components.

requirement: A visual indication shall be provided to the user if the network service is no longer available.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level error‐handling detail and does not require changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: The EIRENE system shall enable users to originate and receive calls by functional number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic functional requirement for call handling and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Each mobile shall be identified by a unique telephone number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic data-modeling constraint (unique identifier) with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Every on-train function shall be identified by a unique standard number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level data identification requirement affecting numbering schemes but not the overall system architecture.

requirement: Every on-engine/coach function shall be identified by a unique standard number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a design‐level naming and traceability constraint that does not drive the overall system architecture.

requirement: Every shunting team number shall be based on an association of: service area identifier; shunting team identifier.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data‐format constraint (team number composition) affecting schema and validation logic but not driving high-level architectural structure.

requirement: Every maintenance team number shall be based on an association of: service area identifier; type of maintenance team (speciality code); maintenance team identifier.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data‐format/naming convention detail and does not drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: Every controller number shall be based on an association of: controller location; controller identifier.
ASR_Explanation: This numbering convention is an implementation detail of the data model and does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The numbering for other teams shall be treated in the same way as maintenance teams.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a minor extension of the existing numbering scheme and has no major impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Group call service areas shall be freely configurable within the operational responsibility of each railway network.
ASR_Explanation: Requires support for dynamic, per-network configuration of service area boundaries, impacting data modeling, configuration management, and network module design.

requirement: Telephone numbers can be defined on a national basis, but codes for certain functions shall be used on an international basis in order to allow interoperability.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of the numbering/addressing subsystem and interoperability layer to handle national versus international dialing schemes.

requirement: For certain functions, standardised telephone numbers shall be implemented. These functions are: Route call to most appropriate ERTMS/ETCS RBC; Railway emergency call; Route call to primary controller; Route call to secondary controller; Route call to power supply controller; Public emergency call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional mapping requirement that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: In network boundary areas, the pre-defined local area shall be allocated on a bilateral basis.
ASR_Explanation: It influences the network architecture by dictating how boundary-area resources are partitioned and managed on a bilateral basis.

requirement: Authorised users within the EIRENE network shall be able to receive calls from calling parties outside the EIRENE network.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing external gateway integration with the EIRENE network, including boundary security controls and protocol interoperability.

requirement: Five levels of priority shall be defined: Railway emergency; control-command (safety); public emergency and group calls between drivers in the same area; railway operation; railway information.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It dictates priority handling rules that drive the design of the messaging subsystem, real-time scheduler, and QoS architecture.

requirement: In order to provide interoperability, priorities shall be allocated consistently across all EIRENE networks.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the network communication architecture by enforcing uniform QoS and priority handling across all EIRENE interoperability interfaces.

requirement: The lowest priority ongoing call shall be pre-empted before that of a higher priority.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the call management architecture by requiring priority-based scheduling and real-time preemption logic.

requirement: Any implementation of such call restrictions shall not affect international interoperability.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of call‐routing and gateway components to ensure restrictions comply with international interoperability protocols.

requirement: A mobile may be a member of a number of groups. It shall be possible to ‘activate’ or ‘deactivate’ the mobile’s subscription to these groups.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine membership state management requirement that doesn’t impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: Activating a group on the mobile shall allow a user to receive a call from that group.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized functional feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Deactivating a group on the mobile shall prevent a user receiving calls from that group.
ASR_Explanation: This is a business-logic change (adding a call-filter rule) without requiring structural or cross-cutting architectural modifications.

requirement: Cab radios will be members of a number of standard groups: Railway emergency call; ‘all train drivers’; shunting team.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the communications architecture by requiring group membership management and multicast message routing across cab radios.

requirement: All mobiles with Railway emergency group call subscription(s) shall be prevented from deactivating the emergency group(s) whilst operational.
ASR_Explanation: It requires enforcing subscription locks at the device and network service-control layers, impacting the overall system and firmware architecture.

requirement: Authorised networks shall be listed (or automatically selected if automatic selection has been enabled) in the following order: home EIRENE network; ‘foreign’ EIRENE networks; public networks.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates prioritized network-selection logic and auto-selection behavior, impacting the design of the network-management component.

requirement: Where EIRENE facilities are not available within the currently selected network, the user shall be given a visible indication.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of a facility-availability monitoring service and UI notification mechanism, impacting component interactions and layering.

requirement: The access matrix defines which subscribers are able to contact which other subscribers within the EIRENE network.
ASR_Explanation: Defines critical access control policies that impact the design of security, routing, and communication modules.

requirement: An addressing scheme shall be provided which permits users to be identified by numbers corresponding to their functional roles rather than by numbers tied to the terminal equipment that they are using.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of a role-based naming and routing subsystem, decoupling user identities from physical endpoint addressing.

requirement: It shall be possible to assign up to a minimum of 3 functional numbers to an EIRENE user at any one time.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data‐level cardinality constraint on the user entity (number of functional numbers) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Only one EIRENE user shall be assigned to a given functional number at any one time.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint will drive the data model and concurrency control design, requiring unique-key enforcement and transaction management for functional number assignments.

requirement: A user shall be able to set up a functional number on one network, and cancel the number from another network.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for cross-network integration and orchestration components to manage provisioning and cancellation flows.

requirement: The functional number shall remain valid as a user roams from one network to another.
ASR_Explanation: It demands a cross-network session management and handover infrastructure, substantially shaping the system’s architecture.

requirement: The functional addressing scheme shall be independent of specific configurations of mobile and terminal equipment.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a hardware‐agnostic addressing abstraction layer, affecting module boundaries and interface definitions.

requirement: It shall be possible to call EIRENE users by functional numbers from a wide range of terminals (EIRENE and non-EIRENE).
ASR_Explanation: It defines interoperability requirements for supporting calls from heterogeneous (EIRENE and non-EIRENE) terminals, impacting interface and communication layer design.

requirement: Functional numbers must be unique within the domain of operation. Since the number must be independent of networks, each number must be unique across all networks.
ASR_Explanation: Enforces a globally consistent unique identifier scheme, impacting the system’s distribution, data consistency, and identifier-generation architecture.

requirement: The functional number shall consist of numeric characters only.
ASR_Explanation: The requirement only dictates input validation rules and does not affect the system’s high-level structure or quality attributes.

requirement: The functional identity of the called user shall be presented to the user initiating a call and the functional identity of the initiator shall be presented to the user receiving a call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional requirement affecting call session presentation logic without driving major architectural decisions.

requirement: For broadcast and group voice communications, the functional identity provided shall be that of the broadcast or group identity.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized identity propagation detail for voice communications and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: If the user initiating the call has more than one functional identity, the user shall be able to select, prior to call establishment, which functional identity is presented to the receiving user.
ASR_Explanation: This requires extending the call‐setup and identity‐management architecture to support selecting and propagating a chosen functional identity.

requirement: In the case of Cab radio, when a train number is assigned as a functional identity, this shall take priority over other Cab radio functional identities, and shall be the functional identity for the Cab radio to be displayed to other users.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local business rule for identity resolution in the Cab radio display logic and does not drive changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: The functional identity shall be presented to the user in a form which can be readily understood (eg ‘driver of train abcd’ rather than ‘abcd01’ or ‘xyz shunting team 3’ rather than ‘xyz03’).
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level formatting requirement for label presentation and does not affect the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: The functional addressing scheme shall be supported by a straightforward procedure for registration of functional numbers. This procedure shall be carried out by the user on commencement of the functional role.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional procedure that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The functional number registration facility shall be supported by all EIRENE user equipment.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: It shall be possible to register up to ten functional numbers to items of equipment physically connected to the Cab radio within 30 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes strict performance and concurrency constraints on the registration module and its hardware communication.

requirement: The registration procedure shall provide a means to prevent mis-allocation of functional numbers during registration.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a routine business‐rule validation in the registration module and doesn’t drive high‐level architectural changes.

requirement: Duplicate functional numbers (eg two trains with the same train number) shall be prevented.
ASR_Explanation: Preventing duplicate train numbers is a data‐integrity constraint handled by the database schema and does not drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The system shall provide a means to recover consistent data sets following a system failure during which functional addressing facilities are lost. During this recovery period, the system shall not permit the use of unverified functional numbers.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architecture for fault tolerance and ensures mechanisms for data recovery and address verification are in place.

requirement: The functional addressing scheme shall be supported by a straightforward procedure for deregistration of functional numbers. This procedure shall be carried out by the user at the end of the functional role.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts user-driven resource cleanup implementation but does not drive or alter the overall architectural structure.

requirement: The functional number deregistration facility shall be supported by all EIRENE user equipment.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional feature requirement and does not impose cross-cutting or structural constraints on the overall system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to deregister up to ten functional numbers to items of equipment physically connected to the Cab radio within 30 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This performance requirement imposes real-time throughput and concurrency constraints on the cab radio interface design.

requirement: A given set of users shall also be allowed to: deregister a functional number which is no longer valid but which has not been deregistered by the user; deregister, by overriding, another user of the same type; deregister, with one action, all functional numbers associated with the same mobile.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the authorization model and transaction design for bulk deregistration and overriding user actions.

requirement: An EIRENE mobile shall remove the displayed functional number and provide an indication to the user that deregistration has taken place.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only concerns a UI change (removing a number and indicating deregistration) and does not affect the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: To allow roaming between EIRENE networks, the system shall support a procedure for the re-registration of functional numbers after selection of a new network.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the network communication and state-management architecture by requiring support for roaming re-registration procedures across EIRENE networks.

requirement: This procedure shall be carried out without manual intervention.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s automation and orchestration architecture by requiring no-human intervention, affecting scheduling, error-handling, and integration design.

requirement: After automatic re-registration is performed, the new registration details shall be displayed to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement has minimal architectural impact, affecting only the UI presentation layer rather than core system structure.

requirement: Location dependent addressing shall be provided to route calls for a given function to a destination number that is dependent upon the user’s location.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the call-routing subsystem by requiring integration with location services and dynamic routing logic.

requirement: The functions to which calls shall be routed based upon the location of the mobile shall include: Primary controller; Secondary controller; Power supply controller; Train management centre.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines core call-routing responsibilities and impacts module decomposition and communication architecture between controllers.

requirement: The correspondence between the locations and the destination of the call shall be easily re-configurable to support dynamic changes in controller area boundaries.
ASR_Explanation: It requires runtime reconfiguration of controller area mappings, impacting module interfaces, service routing, and configuration management.

requirement: When operating with location dependent addressing, no manual action shall be required to update the system when a mobile moves between locations.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for an automated, event‐driven addressing and mobility management subsystem, affecting the overall network and configuration architecture.

requirement: The location dependent addressing scheme shall be available to all mobiles.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the network architecture by requiring a dynamic, location-based address allocation and routing service for all mobile devices.

requirement: As a minimum, the location information used by the EIRENE system shall be derived from that available from the network itself.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the location services integration and imposes dependencies on the network infrastructure layer.

requirement: A standard data interface between the mobile and the application module shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a critical module boundary and communication protocol that will shape the system’s layering, interface contracts, and integration architecture.

requirement: The maximum length of a message segment shall be 96 characters. A message can include several segments.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the messaging protocol and buffer design by requiring segmentation and reassembly logic.

requirement: The text message facility shall not interfere with the ability of users to use the radio.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes isolation and concurrency constraints on the system architecture to ensure messaging modules do not degrade or block radio functionality.

requirement: The type of Railway emergency call initiated shall be determined automatically, based upon the mode of operation of the radio.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior that can be implemented within the existing call-handling module without altering the high-level architecture.

requirement: If the mobile is in shunting mode, the emergency call button shall initiate a shunting emergency call, otherwise the call shall be a Train emergency call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional behavior change within the existing emergency call subsystem and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The Train emergency call shall be sent to all drivers and controller(s) within an area, which is pre-defined to meet operational requirements.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a real-time, location-based broadcast subsystem and messaging infrastructure for emergency calls.

requirement: The Shunting emergency call shall be sent to all users involved in shunting operations in the shunting area.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of a real-time, location-based group notification system for emergency alerts.

requirement: The Shunting emergency call shall automatically take priority over the link assurance signal.
ASR_Explanation: It requires implementing a real-time priority queuing mechanism in the communication subsystem, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: A Railway emergency call shall be able to be initiated by using a simple MMI action.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the design of the human-machine interface and its integration with the safety-critical telephony subsystem to ensure reliable, low-latency emergency call initiation.

requirement: A connection of Railway emergency priority shall be established to a pre-determined set of receiving mobiles and controller(s).
ASR_Explanation: It imposes real-time, priority-based communication requirements that will drive the design of the network and message-routing architecture.

requirement: If the system is not able to connect the call, the system shall automatically keep trying to connect the call for 30 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local retry behavior detail for call connection logic and does not impose new high-level architectural constraints.

requirement: During this period the user shall be provided with an audible and visual indication that the system is trying to connect the call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation-layer UI feedback requirement that can be implemented without major architectural changes.

requirement: After the 30 second period, if the connection was unsuccessful, the system shall provide another audible and visual indication that it was unable to connect the call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/feedback NFR impacting user interaction timing but does not drive major architectural structure or component design.

requirement: An audible indication of 5 seconds shall be provided to originating and receiving users that the emergency function has been activated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/audio subsystem detail for user feedback and does not drive major architectural structure or components.

requirement: A continuous visual indication that the emergency function has been activated shall be provided at the originating and all receiving terminals.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s architecture by requiring a distributed signaling and UI framework to propagate and maintain continuous visual state across all terminals.

requirement: In the event that a train enters the affected area after the warning stage is complete, the same audible and visual indications shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a real-time safety requirement for continuous event detection and unified audio/visual notification, affecting the design of the monitoring and notification subsystems.

requirement: A speech connection shall be established immediately following the warning tone, to allow the originator of the emergency call, to give information concerning the nature of the emergency.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the call control and real-time communication subsystems by mandating low-latency audio link establishment immediately after the warning tone.

requirement: The information shall be received by the same set of users who received the warning tone.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates tracking and filtering recipients for selective message delivery, impacting the messaging and user-context architecture.

requirement: A Railway emergency call may only be terminated by: the originator of the call; a controller participating in the call; the network following a (nationally determined) period of no speech.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts call-control/session-management architecture by requiring role-based termination logic and voice-activity–detection timers.

requirement: If the radio moves out of the area whilst the emergency call is in progress, an audible and visual indication of the loss of the call shall be provided to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of connectivity monitoring and real‐time notification components, impacting component interfaces and event handling.

requirement: Authorised EIRENE mobiles shall be able to receive a Railway emergency call at any time while the mobile is powered up.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes always-on, real-time emergency call availability, affecting connectivity management, resource allocation, and high-availability design.

requirement: For Railway emergency calls initiated by a mobile, the controller’s display will indicate the functional identity of the originating mobile, which includes the following: the train number, if available; the engine number, if no train number is available.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized functional display requirement with no major implications for the overall system architecture.

requirement: The confirmation shall be generated automatically without input from the user.
ASR_Explanation: It requires adding an asynchronous event-driven component or workflow to trigger and generate confirmations without user input, impacting system design.

requirement: The confirmation message shall commence at the end of the call or if the radio moves out of the call area.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior handled within existing call-control and radio-coverage modules and does not drive high-level architectural changes.

requirement: If the radio loses contact with the network, the mechanism shall commence as soon as possible on regaining communications, for up to a maximum of 5 minutes without achieving contact.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the network fault‐tolerance and reconnection subsystem, including state management, timers, and retry logic.

requirement: For Railway emergency calls initiated by a mobile, the automatic confirmation message of the initiating mobile shall contain: the time at call establishment; the time at clear down; the functional number of the call originator; the train number and engine number of the call originator, if a train.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a detailed functional spec for message content formatting and doesn’t drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: For Railway emergency calls received by a mobile, the automatic confirmation message of the receiving mobile shall contain: the time at which the call was first received; the time at which the call was lost (or terminated); the group identity of the sender; the functional number of the recipient; the train number and engine number of the recipient, if a train.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the design of the call‐confirmation message schema and forces integration with time‐stamping, user/group registry, and train/engine data services.

requirement: The confirmation message shall be received at the central location with a 99% probability of success within 5 minutes of call termination.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of reliable message delivery, retry mechanisms, and monitoring to meet the 99% success within a 5-minute window.

requirement: The performance of the confirmation procedure shall not be dependent upon the current use of the mobile and shall not overload the network.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives architectural decisions around offline-capable workflows, local processing, and network load management.

requirement: The shunting call shall be a group call and shall have ‘Railway operation’ priority.

requirement: The shunting communication shall be protected from unintentional and unauthorised access.

requirement: The link assurance signal shall be used to provide an audible indication to all group members. For the driver, this indicates that the radio link is operational.

requirement: It shall be possible for any member of the shunting group (including a driver) to transmit a shunting emergency call to all shunting groups in the area.

requirement: It shall be possible for all shunting drivers and shunting group members to receive a shunting emergency call from any equipment capable of taking part in shunting communications. The shunting emergency call shall cause the audible link assurance signal to be interrupted.

requirement: If a user, other than the shunting leader, attempts to make another call whilst being an active member of a shunting group, the attempt shall be ignored.

requirement: If the radio is used by the team leader for a shunting group call, switching the radio off shall result in the termination of the shunting group call.

requirement: A shunting group shall be able to consist of the following mobile members: the shunting leader; the shunting driver, who may remain fixed or may change one or more times during a working period; up to three shunting team members.

requirement: A controller shall be able to be associated permanently or temporarily with the shunting group.

requirement: It shall only be possible for the shunting leader to communicate with a person external to the ongoing shunting communication. requirement: The shunting leader shall then be able to allow the external user to join the shunting communication.

requirement: The link assurance signal shall consist of an intermittent audio tone (between 800 and 850Hz). The tone shall be of one second duration, followed by an interval of two seconds.

requirement: Operational radios used for shunting shall be able to be switched between the following two operating modes by a maintenance function: Mode A: the link assurance signal shall be interrupted, but not cancelled, when the initiator of the link assurance signal presses the PTT to speak. requirement: The link assurance signal shall be deactivated upon receipt of a shunting emergency call.

requirement: All members of the shunting group, other than the initiator of the link assurance signal, shall be unable to speak during the transmission of the link assurance signal. However, they shall be able to initiate a shunting emergency call at any time.

requirement: It shall be possible to modify a shunting group’s composition during a working period. The process for acceptance (or rejection) of new members, and for admission and removal of temporary members, shall be simple.

requirement: In a given area, a radio set shall be capable of operation in any of the shunting groups in use in that area, subject to any user access restrictions.

requirement: Implementation of direct mode is optional. Where the facility is provided, the following requirements are mandatory: a range of up to at least 2000m in open terrain between a direct mode transmitter and receiver; a voice only capability that supports the use of the link assurance signal; ‘open channel’ mode of operation such that all users employing direct mode receive transmissions when in range of the transmitting user; it is sufficient that the user may talk or listen only, but not both. The ability to talk shall be achieved through use of the Push-To-Talk function; a minimum of one channel is to be available for use of direct mode facilities; if more than one channel is provided, there must be a function allowing the user to manually select the channel; where equipment provides both normal and direct mode capability, the user shall be able to switch between the two modes in a straightforward manner (but not by accident); the user may only select direct mode when the normal mobile telephony services are not available; controls shall be simple to use, eg: direct mode on/off switch; Push-To-Talk function; channel selection; volume control; battery warning for handportables; battery life for handportables operating in direct mode shall be a minimum of 8 hours based on the following cycle: 20% transmit; 20% receive; 60% stand-by; when direct mode is used as a fall-back, an audible and visual indication shall be provided of normal mobile telephony services returning.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the hardware–software architecture by dictating radio subsystem capabilities, power management, UI/UX for mode switching, and fallback notification handling.

requirement: The system shall provide a way to evaluate the information extracted from the P3P privacy policy against the information extracted from the user’s privacy preferences. The system will provide users an unambiguous way to evaluate the information extracted from the privacy policy against the information extracted from the user’s privacy preferences. During the evaluation, following scenarios need to be dealt with: If the data-usage goals information mined from the P3P policy match those from the user's privacy preferences corresponding to both acceptance and rejection of a policy, then a conflict should be detected and a notification requiring user's intervention raised. If the goals data-usage information mined from the P3P policy match those from the user's privacy preferences corresponding to either accepting or rejecting a policy, the policy should be accepted or rejected respectively. If the goals data-usage information mined from the P3P policy does not match those from the user’s privacy preferences corresponding to either acceptance or and rejection of a policy, then again a conflict should be detected and a notification requiring user’s intervention raised.

requirement: The system must give stable and reproducible results.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architectural choices around deterministic algorithms, state management, caching, and versioning to ensure consistent, repeatable outputs.

requirement: The system should be able to cover more than one contact point per country depending on the administrative organisation adopted by each country and able to work in a multi hierarchical environment. This is no longer the case since everybody agrees upon having a single point of contact per Member State (even though the Member State is organized with several Card Issuing Authorities – up to the Member State to manage its own organisation).
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the data and domain model for contact points and hierarchy, affecting database schema, service interfaces and routing logic.

requirement: There will be no restriction in time or place for the use of the software built from the specifications produced under this contract.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for a globally distributed, always-on architecture (multi-region deployment, offline/online support) to ensure unrestricted time/place access.

requirement: The system must be able to establish and keep the dialog with the Members systems despite the various technical environments and technologies used on their sites.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for a flexible integration layer with protocol adapters, impacting the system’s overall integration architecture and technology choices.

requirement: The system will be designed so that background tasks can continue while the user performs foreground tasks.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the concurrency architecture by requiring asynchronous processing, thread management, and resource isolation between background and foreground tasks.

requirement: The system will be used 24x7 by operators under pressure to produce results rapidly. The system must respond rapidly to user requests irrespective of any background tasks. Such high-availability (24x7) is also required from the Member States systems to ensure acceptable response time (less than 1 minute) to enforcement authorities requests.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives a high-availability, low-latency architecture with redundancy, load-balancing and prioritized request handling.

requirement: The system should be able to support other types of message structure to cover f.i. a future driving licence network and correlated activities.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the core messaging architecture by requiring a flexible, pluggable pipeline for new message schemas and validation.

requirement: The system must be maintainable and extensible.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around modularization, coupling, layering, and extension points to ensure the system remains maintainable and extensible.

requirement: The system must be designed so that it can migrate to upgraded hardware or new versions of the operating systems involved.
ASR_Explanation: This drives architectural decisions for hardware/OS abstraction layers and modular portability to support seamless migrations.

requirement: The system must be able to migrate to other type of network than the one proposed by TESTA-II.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing network-agnostic migration requires a pluggable network abstraction layer, impacting core modularity and integration interfaces.

requirement: The system must provide solutions/rules regarding data encoding problems such as supporting different character sets, name truncation rules, name matching in case of misspelling,...
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of data processing pipelines and services by requiring specific encoding/decoding mechanisms and fuzzy name-matching algorithms across the architecture.

requirement: The system must be designed and documented with the expectation that its operational lifetime will be many years.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architecture for long-term maintainability, extensibility, and comprehensive documentation standards.

requirement: Each Member of this network will organise its data about smart card holders with no constraints or recommendations on operating system and/or technology used. The system will be able to dialog with these environments or specify a generic interface to dialog with the Member’s applications.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a technology-agnostic integration layer or generic interface design to enable interoperability with heterogeneous Member environments.

requirement: The system must use the network facilities supplied by the TESTA-II network.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the network integration and communication architecture by requiring compliance with TESTA-II protocols and infrastructure.

requirement: The algorithms in the software will be based on existing techniques and no research will be required to develop new algorithms under this contract.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint affects development effort but does not impose structural, interface, or quality-attribute decisions that impact the system architecture.

requirement: Most of the functionality of the new software shall depend on pre-existing or commercially available software.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes major integration and dependency constraints, driving component selection, coupling strategies, and vendor lock-in considerations in the architecture.

requirement: The system must process requests for checking driver’s issued card coming from a Card Issuing Authority (CIA), for both single driver (online mode) or several drivers (batch mode), including for enforcers during road checks.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s architecture by driving design decisions around external integration, real-time vs batch processing, scalability, and throughput.

requirement: The system must process requests for checking the status of a tachograph card based on its card number, for both administrative and enforcement purposes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD‐style operation with no unique architectural constraints beyond standard request handling and authorization.

requirement: The system must process requests for declaring the modification of the status of a card, including lost, stolen, confiscated, suspended, malfunctioning, in exchange, exchanged, surrendered, handed over, as per allowed status codes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard domain-level functional requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system must process requests to warn a Member State that a card has been issued to a driver who showed a foreign driving license, and the Member State should store that information (issued card number associated to the driving license number) in its own local data store.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the distributed messaging and local datastore integration needed for cross–Member State data replication and consistency.

requirement: The system must provide a service to compute and return Phonex search keys for given surname and first names, to be used by Member States when issuing a new card.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward domain logic function (name-to-key conversion) with no significant impact on system-wide architecture.

requirement: The system must provide a service to transliterate given surname, first names, place of birth, and driving license number to US/Ascii (ISO 646 IRV) from Latin or Greek, and return the transliterated values.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized data‐transformation requirement (transliteration service) that impacts implementation details but not the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system must enable the TCN Administrator to create a new CIA Administrator account in the Active Directory for a CIA Administrator using the Microsoft Management Console Active Directory Users and Computers (MMC).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s security architecture and integration layer by mandating Active Directory account provisioning via MMC.

requirement: The system must enable the TCN Administrator to reset in the Active Directory the password of a CIA Administrator using the Microsoft Management Console Active Directory Users and Computers (MMC).
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional detail for password management and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The system must transfer all expired TACHOnet transactions (completed or after timeout), store them, and generate usage statistics for the TCN Administrator and every CIA Administrator, including various statistics and consolidated lists as described.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data flow and storage architecture by introducing a transaction transfer pipeline, archival storage, and analytics/reporting components.

requirement: The system must allow the TCN Administrator and every CIA Administrator to browse, via a secure Web interface, the usage statistics reports, including dynamic reports and download in different formats (XML, Excel).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives key architectural choices for the secure web UI, role‐based security layer, reporting engine, and multi‐format export subsystem.

requirement: The system must log as-is every message sent or received by TACHOnet in the tracking database, and provide a system for archiving old messages.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s logging and storage architecture by requiring high-throughput message persistence, archival workflows, and retention management.

requirement: The system must monitor the whole TACHOnet system using Microsoft Operations Manager (MOM), raising events when particular problems occur, and allow the TCN Administrator to manage alerts.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces a cross-cutting monitoring and alerting subsystem requiring integration with MOM, impacting infrastructure and component interaction design.

requirement: The system must provide tools or procedures for the TCN Administrator to manage the TACHOnet configuration of a Member State CIA, including adding, editing, and removing a Member State CIA, and updating configuration as needed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD configuration management requirement with no special architectural implications beyond typical administration functionality.

requirement: The PMS will support access via both command line and web browser (following W3C XHTML-standards).
ASR_Explanation: It drives a multi‐client architecture requiring both a command‐line interface layer and an XHTML‐compliant web UI, impacting technology choices and interface design.

requirement: The PMS will manage a database with bibliographical information and allow saving the entire text of a publication in pdf and postscript.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts data storage and retrieval architecture by requiring accommodation of large binary documents (PDF/PS) alongside bibliographic metadata.

requirement: The PMS will automatically retrieve bibliographical information (like author, title, etc.) from the complete text of a publication and allow manual correction if the information is incorrect or insufficient.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the introduction of a text‐processing/extraction component and impacts system modularity and external integration.

requirement: The system will explicitly recognize authors and allow users to manually indicate that references like 'D. Vermeir' and 'Dirk Vermeir' refer to the same author.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for an author identity‐resolution and alias‐management subsystem, impacting data models, service APIs, and the UI.

requirement: The system will provide the possibility to receive a 'bulk update' (importing many publications or abstracts at once, e.g., through BibTeX files).
ASR_Explanation: This FR impacts the architecture by requiring a scalable bulk‐ingest pipeline with BibTeX parsing, throughput optimization, and error/retry handling.

requirement: The PMS will support access control, including accessing the contents of a publication if the full text or only the abstract is available.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design and integration of security/authorization services, impacting overall system architecture.

requirement: The PMS will rely on a database and a web server, accessible by web browser and command line.
ASR_Explanation: It defines core components (database, web server) and interfaces (browser, CLI), driving the overall client-server architecture and technology stack.

requirement: Accessing the PMS from outside the VUB-network is only possible for logged in users.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the system’s security and network access architecture by mandating authentication enforcement at the external boundary.

requirement: Users not logged in and/or not connected to the VUB-network will only have Guest privileges.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security and access‐control architecture by requiring network‐based user detection and guest role enforcement.

requirement: To log in, a user must register an account by providing first and last name, password, and a valid e-mail address. Upon acceptance, the user receives a validation mail to confirm registration.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates the addition of an identity management component with secure credential storage and integration with an external email service for asynchronous verification.

requirement: Any user can search for publications by title, author(s), institute, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives architectural decisions around search engine integration, indexing strategies, and scalability for efficient query performance.

requirement: Users can specify that different names refer to the same author or institute.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the data model and service architecture by requiring an alias/identity resolution mechanism and related storage and lookup processes.

requirement: The system contains an interface with a MySQL-Database.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying a MySQL interface constrains the data layer architecture and impacts choices for database connectivity, ORM and deployment.

requirement: The web server is responsible for executing the PMS, sending queries to the database, and generating the user interface for each user.
ASR_Explanation: It defines key responsibilities and interactions for the web server component, impacting system decomposition and component communication patterns.

requirement: Users can access the PMS from any OS using a web browser connected to the PMS web server. The PMS must be accessible for users with Linux OS and text-based web browsers (e.g., Links2).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the presentation tier architecture by enforcing support for text-based browsers and minimal client-side dependencies.

requirement: Administrators can access the system by command line from within the VUB-network.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the network and security architecture by requiring an internal‐only CLI access channel and associated authentication/deployment constraints.

requirement: If an error occurs during a request, the user should receive an error message stating the cause, and the error should be logged to notify Administrators.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a cross-cutting error-handling and centralized logging mechanism for user feedback and administrator notifications.

requirement: The PMS web interface will comply with the W3C XHTML 1.0 Transitional standard.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐layer coding standard for markup validity and does not drive high‐level architectural structure or component interactions.

requirement: Communication between the PMS (webserver) and users will be via HTTP using TCP/IP port 80.
ASR_Explanation: Defines the network protocol and port configuration, impacting firewall rules, load balancing, and security architecture.

requirement: There is no specific limit on the usage of memory by the PMS. Publications will have a maximum file size (TBD).
ASR_Explanation: This unfettered memory usage and TBD file size constraint will drive memory management, caching, and infrastructure provisioning for scalability.

requirement: A user must be logged in (and registered) or connected to the VUB-network to use (parts of) the PMS functionality.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the security architecture by requiring integrated authentication/authorization services and network‐based access controls.

requirement: The PMS will be easy to install, requiring Ruby 1.8 (or later) and will run under a Linux-based environment.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes platform and runtime dependency constraints that directly affect deployment architecture and environment compatibility.

requirement: The PMS will use a hierarchical user structure: Guest, VUB-Network User, Member, Publisher, Moderator, Administrator. Each higher level inherits all functions of the preceding one.
ASR_Explanation: Defines a role-based access control hierarchy with inheritance, impacting the security, authorization, and data-model architecture.

requirement: A Guest can register an account by filling in a form.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard user registration feature with no significant architectural impact beyond basic CRUD functionality.

requirement: A Guest can log into the PMS if they have a valid account.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard authentication function and does not impose major architectural constraints.

requirement: A Guest must validate a new account via a validation mail after registration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine user-management function (email-based account validation) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A Guest can retrieve their password if they have a valid account and are not logged in.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts security and integration architecture by requiring secure token generation, storage/validation and external email service support.

requirement: A VUB-Network User can search for publications using a form with multiple input fields (keywords, authors, dates).
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data‐retrieval feature that doesn’t by itself drive any specific architectural choices.

requirement: A VUB-Network User can perform advanced searches by specifying keywords and/or publications that refer to it.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design and integration of the search/indexing infrastructure and data access layer for performance, scalability, and metadata modeling.

requirement: A VUB-Network User can order search results by title, author(s), and year of publication.
ASR_Explanation: This sorting feature can be implemented within existing query and presentation layers without altering the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: A VUB-Network User can export and download search results in html, pdf, and ps formats.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement about export formats that can be addressed within existing modular components without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: A VUB-Network User can search for publications by similarity (same author, mentioned in results, same institute).
ASR_Explanation: It mandates integration of a search/indexing subsystem and data model extensions to support similarity queries, impacting overall architecture.

requirement: A Member can change their account details (password, e-mail, etc.) and request an account upgrade.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine user-management feature (CRUD operations) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A Member can log off the PMS; if connected to the VUB network, redirected to search page; otherwise, to login page.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user flow/UI navigation detail with no significant impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: A Publisher can upload publications (pdf, ps) following bibTEX or RIS format. The PMS will automatically retrieve bibliographical information and allow manual correction.
ASR_Explanation: This FR impacts the architecture by requiring file ingestion and storage subsystems, integration with parsing/extraction services (BibTeX/RIS), and a metadata validation workflow.

requirement: A Publisher can specify that different names refer to the same author or institute during upload.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces an alias management/entity‐resolution service impacting the data model, service layer, and upload workflow architecture.

requirement: A Publisher can edit only the publications they uploaded.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard authorization rule managed within the application’s existing access-control layer without requiring architectural changes.

requirement: A Publisher can bulk upload publications (e.g., through bibTEX files).
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a scalable file ingestion and parsing pipeline with asynchronous processing for large bibTEX uploads.

requirement: A Moderator can upgrade a Member (of their department) to Publisher or downgrade a Publisher to Member.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard RBAC operation within the existing security/permissions subsystem and doesn’t drive major architectural changes.

requirement: A Moderator can change Group Permissions for groups under their jurisdiction.
ASR_Explanation: This standard RBAC feature doesn’t alter the overall system architecture or require new architectural components.

requirement: A Moderator can edit information about authors.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine role‐based CRUD permission and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: A Moderator can edit or overwrite bibliographical details of any publication owned by their department/group.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD permission requirement impacting business logic, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: An Administrator has access to the Control Panel to edit user accounts (upgrade, downgrade, delete).
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD function under existing role‐based access control with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: An Administrator can create and delete any user of the PMS.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard user‐management CRUD operation with no major architectural impact beyond existing authentication/authorization modules.

requirement: An Administrator can create and delete any group of the PMS.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation within the existing user/role management module with no significant architectural impact.

requirement: An Administrator can change the User Level of any user to any other User Level.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine user-management feature with no unique constraints on the overall system architecture.

requirement: An Administrator can edit or overwrite bibliographical details of any publication.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation with role-based access control that can be handled within the existing application layer without major architectural changes.

requirement: The PMS may be extended to support additional formats (e.g., MS WORD).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for an extensible document‐processing pipeline or plugin/adapter architecture to support new file formats.

requirement: The PMS may allow viewing a list of documents that refer to a publication in the result page.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI/data‐retrieval feature with minimal impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The PMS may automatically recognize that different names refer to the same author or institute.
ASR_Explanation: This requires integrating automated entity resolution/disambiguation services, impacting the system’s data model and service architecture.

requirement: If a bibTEX file with only abstracts and referring URLs is uploaded, the PMS should be able to retrieve the full texts through those URLs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for an external resource-fetching integration layer (HTTP client, error handling, caching, security) and thus impacts the overall system architecture.

requirement: The PMS may be extended to upload audio and video files (e.g., mp3, mpeg) that are recognized automatically.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system architecture by requiring new media ingestion, processing pipelines, storage accommodations, and integration with audio/video recognition services.

requirement: The design of the PMS should be modular to simplify expansions and replacements.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces a modular, component-based architecture to support maintainability, extensibility, and easy replacement.

requirement: The PMS must be able to run on Linux, specifically the Wilma web server.
ASR_Explanation: This platform and server constraint directly impacts deployment architecture, runtime environment, and choice of middleware components.

requirement: The web interface will be attractive, simple, and standard (W3C XHTML).
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐level UI guideline (markup validity and aesthetics) that influences front-end implementation but not high-level architecture.

requirement: All functions and options should be available in the least amount of clicks while maintaining structure.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX usability guideline about navigation flow and doesn’t impose core architectural constraints.

requirement: The programming language used will be Ruby.
ASR_Explanation: Choosing Ruby constrains the technology stack and influences framework compatibility, performance characteristics, and long-term maintainability.

requirement: The code will be documented using RDoc.
ASR_Explanation: Using RDoc for documentation does not affect the system’s overall architecture or structural design.

requirement: Git will be used for configuration management.
ASR_Explanation: This is a development tooling/process decision and does not influence the software’s structural or runtime architecture.

requirement: The use of frameworks is not allowed.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint forces custom implementation of all infrastructure and patterns instead of using standard frameworks, significantly affecting system complexity, maintainability, and development effort.

requirement: Only free software can be used for tools and the end product. Use of certain libraries and aids is justified in the SCMP.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the technology stack to open-source tools and libraries, impacting component selection and integration strategies.

requirement: The PMS should be easily installable (details TBD).
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the deployment architecture by driving installer design, packaging strategy, and platform support considerations.

requirement: Users must have a web browser supporting W3C XHTML and a TCP/IP connection to the web server.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint on browser standards and TCP/IP connectivity dictates front-end technology choices and network deployment architecture.

requirement: HTTP traffic must be possible on the network connection between server and client.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying HTTP traffic dictates network topology, protocol stacks, firewall rules and security configurations at the architecture level.

requirement: Command line users have access to the server shell and all Administrator privileges.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the security and access-control architecture, requiring design of privileged access management, authentication, and authorization mechanisms.

requirement: All data (static and dynamic information, statistics, publications, administrator-settings, etc.) will be saved in the database.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data persistence requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The database must remain consistent at all times (no pointers to deleted records, no redundant information, etc.).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around database technology, schema design, transaction management, and consistency models.

requirement: The database must handle several requests at once via SQL statements.
ASR_Explanation: This concurrency requirement drives database selection, connection pooling, and scalability strategies, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: A backup structure is required to save all data in case of server or disk problems.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s data persistence architecture by necessitating backup, replication, and disaster-recovery components.

requirement: The PMS web interface must conform to W3C XHTML 1.0 Transitional standard.
ASR_Explanation: This is an implementation‐level markup standard that affects coding guidelines rather than high-level architectural design.

requirement: The implementation must comply with the Coding Conventions of SE1.
ASR_Explanation: This is a code-level style guideline that affects consistency and maintainability but does not impact the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: The implementation must be object-oriented.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level design constraint (coding paradigm) and does not affect the system’s high-level architectural structure.

requirement: Design diagrams must follow the UML standard.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates only a documentation notation standard and does not influence the system’s structure, behavior, or quality attributes.

requirement: The PMS should not experience software faults; a spiral model with 2 iterations will be used to minimize bugs.
ASR_Explanation: This is a development process quality requirement (use of a spiral model), not a constraint that drives the system’s architectural design.

requirement: The PMS will be available at all times from any computer (fulfilling assumptions and dependencies), except when Wilma is not available.
ASR_Explanation: This availability requirement drives architectural decisions around redundancy, failover, and global access infrastructure.

requirement: In case of a corrupted database, the Administrator should replace it with the last available backup.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates backup and restore mechanisms that shape the database disaster‐recovery and high‐availability architecture.

requirement: During downtime, the PMS should display an error stating it is temporarily down.
