ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level maintenance notification requirement and does not impose significant changes on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Every user account is protected with a personal password; only the specific user can log in and change personal information.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a secure authentication and authorization subsystem with password management and user identity controls, impacting the core security architecture.

requirement: User passwords must be encrypted when submitted and stored in the database; only the PMS can access these passwords.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces security architecture concerns around encryption algorithms, key management, secure data transport, and storage boundaries.

requirement: Direct access to the database is only allowed for Administrators using the CLI.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the security and data-access architecture by enforcing role-based access control and a dedicated CLI pathway for administrator database connections.

requirement: User levels must always be respected regarding editing, deleting, and uploading publications and their bibliographical details.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a cross-cutting authorization architecture (e.g., RBAC) to enforce user-level permissions on editing, deleting, and uploading publications.

requirement: The database should be maintained by the Administrator(s).
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational role assignment for database maintenance and does not affect the system’s architectural design.

requirement: The PMS should be maintained by Administrators globally; Moderators can maintain at the departmental level.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a hierarchical role-based access control and data segregation architecture to enforce global versus departmental maintenance scopes.

requirement: The PMS is developed and tested to run on Wilma (Linux-based), but may be relocated to other OSes if necessary software is available and adjustments are made.
ASR_Explanation: This OS portability NFR drives architectural decisions around abstraction layers, platform-specific adapters, and dependency management for cross‐platform support.

requirement: Log in to the system from the internet: users must be registered and have a username and password to log in from outside the VUB-network.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of externally accessible authentication services and associated network/security architecture.

requirement: Log in to the system via the VUB-network: users can log in or perform a search immediately if connected to the VUB-network.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement enforces network-specific access control, impacting the system’s network topology and authentication design.

requirement: Register for an account: users must provide username, password, name, email, university, and department; system checks if username exists; confirmation email sent to Administrator.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward user registration function (CRUD and basic email notification) with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Log off: users can log off and are redirected appropriately based on network connection.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard session management and navigation detail with minimal impact on overall architecture.

requirement: Change password: users can request a new password if forgotten; must have a valid username; confirmation email sent to Administrator.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine user-management feature with standard email integration that doesn’t drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Search for publications: users can search by keyword, author, period, and advanced criteria; results list provided.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the choice of search/indexing technology, database schema design and caching strategies to meet performance and scalability requirements.

requirement: View the results of a search: users can view search results with title and main author, and navigate through result pages.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level display and pagination feature with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: View publication details: users can view detailed information (title, authors, date, abstract, references) of a selected publication.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a standard CRUD read operation with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: View detailed information for multiple publications: users can select multiple publications to view details at once.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional feature that doesn’t drive major architectural decisions beyond standard data retrieval and UI handling.

requirement: Download a single publication: users can download the full text of a selected publication from results or detail view.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-level feature with minimal impact on high-level system architecture.

requirement: Download multiple publications: users can download all publications in a search result at once.
ASR_Explanation: Bulk download triggers the need for a streaming/compression service and resource scaling to handle large payloads efficiently.

requirement: Consult publication references: users can view references cited in a publication from results or detail view.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI/DB feature (displaying linked records) with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Consult references for multiple publications: users can view all references cited in multiple publications at once.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data‐aggregation feature that can be implemented within the existing retrieval and presentation layers without major structural changes.

requirement: Display the full text of a publication: users can view the full text if available in the system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward content‐retrieval/display feature fitting into existing storage and UI layers without requiring new architectural changes.

requirement: View details of a reference: users can access detailed view of a cited reference if present in the system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐retrieval and presentation feature with no major architectural impact.

requirement: Export the references cited in a publication (BIB format): users can download references in bib format.
ASR_Explanation: This can be implemented as an extension to the existing export module without impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: Export the references cited in a publication (RIS format): users can download references in RIS format.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward file‐export feature that can be implemented within an existing module without altering the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: View authors: users can view information about an author by clicking their name in search results.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI feature (display author details) with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Editing personal data: users can edit their personal data (password, university, department) via Edit Settings.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation on user profile data that can be handled within the existing user-management module without architectural changes.

requirement: Access to your own publications: users can directly access all publications they have uploaded.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic CRUD access control feature with standard authorization requirements and minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Edit your own publications: users can modify information of their own publications.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD operation with basic access control and does not require major architectural changes.

requirement: Upload publications: users can add a publication in supported formats (pdf, ps, odt); system retrieves and allows editing of publication details.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the file ingestion/storage subsystem and metadata extraction/editing workflows for supported document formats.

requirement: Search users: Moderators and Administrators can search for users by criteria and view results.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine user‐filtering feature relying on standard database queries and doesn’t drive significant architectural changes.

requirement: Edit user settings: Moderators and Administrators can change settings of selected users from search results.
ASR_Explanation: Only adds role-based permission checks to the existing user management module without requiring new architectural components.

requirement: Create User: Administrators can create new users by providing details; system checks for existing username.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD operation with simple validation and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Delete User: Administrators can delete users by username; system checks for existence.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Create Group: Administrators can create new groups by providing group details; system checks for existing groupname.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation with no significant impact on high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Search Group: Administrators can search for groups by details and view results.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD‐style search feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Delete Group: Administrators can delete groups if empty; system checks for existence and emptiness.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation with simple existence and emptiness checks that can be implemented within the existing service layer without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: Edit group settings: Administrators can change settings of selected groups from search results.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine administrative CRUD feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Edit system configuration settings: Administrators can access the configuration panel to change and confirm system settings.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard administrative CRUD feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Citizens can register their complaints with police and then based on the evidence, facts and following investigation, police shall take the complaint forward.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional workflow requirement and does not impose any significant architectural constraints.

requirement: The Registration module acts as an interface between the police and citizens and it eases the approach, interaction and information exchange between police and complainants.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional feature description and does not impose specific architectural constraints or patterns.

requirement: The Investigation module of the CCTNS facilitates the investigation process and introduces operational efficiencies by automating most of the tasks that take place after initial entries are made during Registration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional workflow requirement with no direct implications on cross-cutting architectural decisions.

requirement: The Prosecution module of the CCTNS aids interfacing with courts by providing a platform to record entries of the court interactions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional feature for recording court interactions that does not impose system-wide architectural constraints.

requirement: The Search module of the CCTNS gives police personnel the ability to execute a basic or advanced search on cases, including searching for a particular person, type of crime, modus operandi, property, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the overall system architecture by necessitating search indexing, query processing components, and performance/scalability considerations.

requirement: The Search module gives the user the ability to customize the results view by criminal/accused or by cases.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level customization of search result filtering that doesn’t require changes to the core system architecture.

requirement: The Search module enables police to execute different types of queries such as monthly reporting, RTI related, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional feature and does not impose significant architectural constraints or require major design changes.

requirement: The Citizen Interface module acts as a conduit for information exchange between citizens and police units/personnel.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional decomposition defining a module boundary without imposing major structural or quality-attribute constraints.

requirement: Citizens can use the Citizen Interface module to get information or acknowledgements from police.
ASR_Explanation: This basic functional requirement doesn’t drive any major architectural decisions regarding system structure, performance, or non-functional constraints.

requirement: Police can use the Citizen Interface module to respond to citizens with very little turnaround time.
ASR_Explanation: This low turnaround-time requirement imposes architectural constraints on system latency, scalability, and communication patterns.

requirement: The Navigation module provides role-based landing pages which help in navigating through the CCTNS application and shows information such as cases assigned, alerts, pending tasks, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s navigation architecture by introducing role‐based page composition and RBAC integration for dynamic landing pages.

requirement: The Configuration module helps keep the application configured according to the states’ requirements and keeps data elements/rules up to date.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing a dynamic, extensible configuration and rule‐management subsystem for state-specific customization impacts module boundaries, data models, and integration patterns.

requirement: With proper configuration, information such as act and sections, state specific data, castes, tribes, property information, etc. can be created, updated, and deleted.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD feature for administrative data that doesn’t impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: The solution should provide detailed context-sensitive help material for all the possible actions and scenarios on all user interfaces in the application.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the overall system architecture by requiring a modular help framework, content management services, and UI integration for context-sensitive assistance.

requirement: The help should be accessible to the users both in the offline and online mode.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts architecture by requiring offline-capable content storage, caching, and sync mechanisms for the help system.

requirement: The solution should provide an interface for the user to log any defects or enhancement requests on the application and track thereafter.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement pertains to a standard CRUD user interface for defect tracking with minimal or no impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The solution should send alerts (e.g., email, SMS) to the user if the user chooses to whenever any action has been taken on the alert.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a scalable, decoupled notification subsystem with event-driven messaging and third-party gateway integration.

requirement: The solution should enable the user to track the submitted defect or enhancement request.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard tracking feature with no major architectural constraints beyond typical CRUD and UI updates.

requirement: The solution should enable the help-desk user to view the reports on the submitted defects or enhancement requests category-wise, status-wise, and age-wise.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It drives the design of data storage/indexing and the reporting engine to efficiently support aggregated, filtered defect/enhancement views.

requirement: The support solution should be accessible to the users both from within the application and also outside the application through a browser interface.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the architecture to include both embedded in-app components and standalone browser-based interfaces, impacting UI layers, service exposure, and security boundaries.

requirement: The System must keep an unalterable audit trail capable of automatically capturing and storing information about all actions (create/read/update/delete) taken upon the critical entities (case, suspect, property, etc.) in the system, the user initiating and/or carrying out the action, the date and time of the event, and administrative parameters.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of a tamper-proof, append-only audit logging subsystem and storage architecture to meet security and compliance requirements.

requirement: The audit trail data cannot be modified in any way or deleted by any user; it may be subject to re-department and copying to removable media if required, so long as its contents remain unchanged.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates designing a tamper-proof, append-only audit storage subsystem with strict access controls and secure export mechanisms.

requirement: Once the audit trail functionality has been activated, the System must track events without manual intervention, and store in the audit trail information about them.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a cross-cutting concern demanding an event-driven auditing component and persistent storage integration.

requirement: The System must maintain the audit trail for as long as required, which will be at least for the life of the case to which it refers.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of long-term data storage and archival mechanisms for audit trails to meet retention and compliance needs.

requirement: The System must ensure that audit trail data is available for inspection on request, so that a specific event can be identified and all related data made accessible, and that this can be achieved by authorised external personnel who have little or no familiarity with the system.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of the audit-log storage, indexing, access-control and retrieval interfaces (including external‐facing APIs and security boundaries).

requirement: The System must be able to export audit trails for specified cases (without affecting the audit trail stored by the System).
ASR_Explanation: Requires designing a separate read-only export mechanism and data‐access layer to ensure audit trail immutability, performance, and security.

requirement: The System must be able to capture and store violations (i.e. a user’s attempts to access a case to which he is denied access), and attempted violations, of access control mechanisms.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of a secure, scalable auditing subsystem and storage architecture for logging access-control violations.

requirement: The System must at a minimum be able to provide reports for actions on cases organised by case, by user, and in chronological sequence.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the data model, indexing strategies, and reporting infrastructure to efficiently support case-, user-, and time-based queries.

requirement: The System should be able to provide reports for actions on cases organised by workstation and (where technically appropriate) by network address.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It drives the design of the logging, data‐store schema and indexing strategy to capture and report actions by workstation and network address.

requirement: The System must allow the user to limit access to cases to specified users or user groups.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring integrated access-control mechanisms (e.g., RBAC/ACL) and data-model changes to enforce case-level permissions.

requirement: The system should provide for role-based control for the functionality within the system.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring an authorization framework and access control modules to enforce role-based permissions.

requirement: The System must allow a user to be a member of more than one group.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-level detail (a many-to-many user-group relationship) affecting the data model but not the overall software architecture.

requirement: The System must allow only admin-users to set up user profiles and allocate users to groups.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s security architecture by mandating role-based access control for user and group management.

requirement: The System should allow a user to stipulate which other users or groups can access cases.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s security and authorization architecture by requiring a flexible access control model (e.g., RBAC/ABAC) and enforcement mechanisms.

requirement: The System must allow changes to security attributes for groups or users (such as access rights, security level, privileges, password allocation and management) to be made only by super-user.
ASR_Explanation: Enforces privileged-role constraints affecting the design of authentication/authorization modules and security architecture (e.g., RBAC, super-user management).

requirement: If a user requests access to, or searches for, a case which he does not have the right to access, the System must provide one of the following responses (selectable at configuration time): display title and metadata; display the existence of a case but not its title or other metadata; do not display any case information or indicate its existence in any way.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives architectural decisions for configurable access-control enforcement, policy-based response handling, and UI/data-layer separation to prevent unauthorized information exposure.

requirement: If a user performs a quick or advanced search, the System must never include in the search result list any record which the user does not have the right to access.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the overall security and data-access architecture by enforcing authorization checks within the search pipeline.

requirement: If the System allows users to make unauthorised attempts to access cases, it must log these in the audit trail.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces a cross-cutting security and auditing requirement that mandates a centralized audit-logging service and impacts the logging/infrastructure design.

requirement: Any access to cases, and all other activities involving the cases and related documents or data should also need to be stored in the audit trail to ensure legal admissibility and to assist in data recovery.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates a system-wide audit logging architecture affecting data storage, security controls, and performance.

requirement: All error messages produced by the System must be meaningful, so that they can be appropriately acted upon by the users who are likely to see them. Ideally, each error message will be accompanied by explanatory text and an indication of the action(s) which the user can take in response to the error.
ASR_Explanation: This is a usability-focused non-functional requirement about error message content and doesn’t impose high-level structural or design constraints.

requirement: The System must employ a single set of user interface rules, or a small number of sets to provide a familiar and common look and feel for the application.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the choice and design of the front‐end architecture and component framework to enforce a unified UI design system.

requirement: The System must be able to display several entities (cases, suspects) simultaneously.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The interfaces must be made customizable or user-configurable to the extent possible. Such configurations must be saved in the user profile.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a dynamic UI configuration framework and persistent user-profile storage, impacting modularity and data architecture.

requirement: The System user interface must be suitable for users with special needs; that is, compatible with specialist software that may be used and with appropriate interface guidelines.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the selection of UI frameworks and architectural patterns to ensure compatibility with assistive technologies and accessibility guidelines.

requirement: The System must provide End User and Administrator functions which are easy to use and intuitive throughout.
ASR_Explanation: This generic usability goal is too vague to drive architectural decisions or impact the overall system structure.

requirement: The System must allow persistent defaults for data entry where desirable. These defaults should include: user-definable values; values same as previous item; values derived from context, e.g. date, file reference, user identifier.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the persistence and context-aware service layers, requiring design of configurable defaults storage, context propagation, and stateful UI support.

requirement: Frequently-executed System transactions must be designed so that they can be completed with a small number of interactions (e.g. mouse clicks).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR influences the overall UI and backend interaction architecture by requiring streamlined workflows, minimized round trips, and possibly client-side caching or state management.

requirement: Where the System employs a graphical user interface, it must allow users to customise it. Customisation should include, but need not be limited to menu contents, layout of screens, use of function keys, on-screen colours, fonts and font sizes.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a modular, extensible UI framework with runtime configuration and theming support.

requirement: The user interfaces should be designed to make them user-intuitive.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR guides UI/UX design details without imposing constraints on the system’s overall architectural structure.

requirement: The user interfaces of the system should comply with Standard ISO 9241.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the choice of UI frameworks, design patterns, and component architecture to ensure compliance with ISO 9241 usability standards.

requirement: ICT accessibility: ISO 9241-20 shall be the standard for guidance on ICT accessibility. Application user interfaces to meet its requirements and recommendations. Software accessibility ISO 9241-171 shall be the standard for guidance on software accessibility. User interfaces should meet its requirements and recommendations. Content accessibility WCAG 1.0 shall be the standard used for guidance on content accessibility. The application logo to be available on all pages as a link to the home page.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the selection and integration of accessibility frameworks, UI architectures, and compliance verification mechanisms as cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: All non-text media objects, such as graphical images or video, should be provided with alternative equivalent textual descriptions and/or with equivalent text-based functionality.
ASR_Explanation: This is an accessibility/content guideline affecting UI implementation and authoring processes rather than the overall system architecture.

requirement: Navigation should be designed to help users understand where they are, where they have been and where they can go next. General guidance on achieving self-descriptiveness is given in ISO 9241-110.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the high-level UI/navigation architecture by defining routing, state management and breadcrumb structures.

requirement: Each presentation segment (page or window) should provide the user with a clear and sufficient indication of where he or she is in the navigation structure and of the current segment position with respect to the overall structure.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level requirement for breadcrumb navigation in the presentation layer and does not drive high-level architectural changes.

requirement: Alternative access paths for navigating to a specific unit of content should be offered to support different navigation strategies.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of navigation and content‐routing services to support multiple access paths and discovery strategies.

requirement: The number of navigation steps needed to reach a certain piece of content should be minimized as long as different mental models, navigation strategies and tasks of the user are taken into account.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a flexible, context-aware navigation architecture (user modeling, dynamic routing/UI) to support multiple mental models.

requirement: Splash screens should be avoided unless they provide useful content or feedback about the application state to the user. If a splash screen is used, a navigation option to skip it should be offered.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX guideline with minimal impact on high-level architecture, only affecting the startup screen navigation.

requirement: Additional windows such as new browser windows or pop-up windows should only be opened if this supports the user’s task.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI usability guideline with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Vertical scrolling should be minimized. This may be done by placing important information at the top and providing links to information that is further down the page. Horizontal scrolling should be avoided wherever possible.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX layout guideline with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: User interfaces should be designed to allow activation of controls by a variety of input devices. The ability to choose between different input devices for activating controls such as links, fields and buttons is important both for users who prefer a certain input mode, mobile users and users with disabilities.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the UI architecture by requiring an input abstraction layer and accessibility support for multiple input modalities.

requirement: User interfaces should be designed to be as robust as possible in the face of changing technology.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for UI abstraction and modular architecture to ensure maintainability and adaptability to evolving technologies.

requirement: Application pages should be designed and implemented so that there are acceptable opening times and download times for the expected range of technical contexts of use.
ASR_Explanation: This performance NFR drives architectural decisions around caching, content delivery, framework choices, and infrastructure scaling.

requirement: Potential user errors as well as the effort needed to recover from errors should be minimized.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system architecture by mandating robust error‐handling, input validation, and UI design to prevent mistakes and streamline recovery.

requirement: The content of error messages shown on the pages or special error pages should clearly state the reason why the error occurred and, if possible, actions the user can take to resolve the error.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX guideline for error messaging and does not drive core architectural structure or significant design decisions.

requirement: When designing user interfaces for use by diverse groups, input and output of information elements such as currency, units of measurement, temperatures, date and time, phone numbers, address or postal codes should be designed so that they are usable.
ASR_Explanation: It requires an internationalization-aware UI architecture with modular, locale-specific formatting and validation components.

requirement: Text should be able to be resized by the user, using functions provided by the user agent or other appropriate means.
ASR_Explanation: It concerns UI styling/accessibility via user-agent features and does not alter system structure or component interactions.

requirement: The quality of textual content with respect to spelling and grammar should be sufficient so as not to impede readability.
ASR_Explanation: This content-quality NFR doesn’t drive software structure or technology choices but can be managed by editorial processes or tools.

requirement: The reading and understanding of the textual content on the screen should be supported by suitable means, including the use of short sentences, the division of the text into shorter chunks or the presentation of content items in the form of bullet points.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local UI/UX presentation guideline and does not drive the overall system architecture.

requirement: Fast skimming of text should be supported by the provision of clear links, bulleted lists, highlighted keywords, logical headings, and short phrases and sentences.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation guideline (styling and content layout) that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Text presented on the pages should be readable taking into account the expected display characteristics and spatial arrangement.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐layer usability requirement affecting styling and layout, not the core system architecture.

requirement: Within-page links should be clearly distinguishable from other links that lead to a different page.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI styling guideline with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Text pages containing large proportions of links should be formatted so that the presence of links does not impede the readability of the text.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI styling guideline affecting presentation layer formatting rather than impacting core architectural structure or components.

requirement: Navigation links — particularly links representing the main navigation structure — should be labelled with terms that are familiar to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX labeling guideline with no impact on system architecture or structural design.

requirement: The target or purpose of a link should be directly indicated by its label, avoiding generic labels such as “go” or “click here” except where the purpose of the link is clear from its context on the page or the labels have commonly understood semantics in the particular application domain.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level accessibility/usability guideline and does not drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: Links that open new browser windows or pop-up windows should be clearly marked.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI accessibility/consistency guideline with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Navigation links should be clearly distinguishable from controls activating some action.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a UI style guideline affecting presentation details without driving high-level architectural structure or component boundaries.

requirement: If a document is either too long, dispersed over several pages or in a specific layout that is not suitable for online reading, a printer-friendly version of the document should be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only impacts the presentation layer (alternate view/template rendering) without driving changes to core system architecture.

requirement: “White space” on a page should be used in such a way that it does not impair the visual skimming of the page.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI styling guideline with no significant impact on system structure, interfaces, or architectural decisions.

requirement: The length of a page should be selected so as to support the primary purpose and use of the page.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI design guideline affecting presentation layer details, not driving high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Colour should be used with care, taking into account human capabilities and restrictions in perceiving colour, and not as the only means of conveying information.
ASR_Explanation: This affects the UI architecture by requiring built-in accessibility/theming support for color contrast checks and alternative information channels.

requirement: If frames are used, care should be taken to avoid possible problems, for example, those involving the use of the back button, bookmarking of pages, or scrolling of information.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI implementation guideline around frame usage and does not drive or constrain the overall system architecture.

requirement: If frames are used, an alternative way of presenting relevant information without frames should be provided. When style sheets and/or frames are turned off it should be possible for the user to read and understand the page; alternatively, the user should be provided with an equivalent alternative text-only page.
ASR_Explanation: It requires the presentation architecture to support dual rendering modes (frames vs. non‐frames/text‐only) and content negotiation for accessibility.

requirement: Pages should be designed using consistent layout schemes, supporting the user in finding similar information at the same position on different pages.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation-layer consistency guideline that does not drive architectural structure or technology choices.

requirement: Page titles should be placed in a consistent location on the different pages.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐layer consistency requirement affecting UI layout without significant architectural impact.

requirement: When designing application pages, the general principles of human perception should be taken into account. The International Standards mentioned below shall be consulted for guidance.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR will influence the UI architecture, driving choices of frameworks, component designs, and theming to ensure compliance with human perception standards.

requirement: Each page should contain a link leading to the home page of the application or to a landmark page that is easy to recognize for the user.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX guideline handled at the presentation layer without affecting overall system structure or component boundaries.

requirement: A separate navigation overview such as a site map should be provided for application showing the structure of the site in an overview form.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX navigation feature that doesn’t drive major architectural structure or quality-attribute decisions.

requirement: If navigation components (or overviews) are shown in conjunction with associated content, consistency between the navigation component and the content shown should be maintained by indicating in the navigation component (e.g. highlighting) the topic currently visible in the content area.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI consistency guideline affecting component behavior but not the overall system architecture.

requirement: Navigation components should be placed consistently on the pages or in the framesets in the pages of the application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI design consistency guideline that affects implementation details rather than driving core architectural structure.

requirement: Adapting the content and the navigation of a user interface to individual users or user groups can be a useful mechanism for providing information that is of interest to the users and for making access to relevant information more efficient.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the architecture by requiring a personalization engine, dynamic UI rendering infrastructure, and user profile management components.

requirement: When providing different access paths or navigation structures for different user groups, the tasks and information needs of these user groups should be taken into consideration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX design guideline for navigation customization and does not drive core architectural structure or component boundaries.

requirement: It should be made evident to the user when individualization and/or adaptation are used.
ASR_Explanation: This transparency requirement affects primarily the presentation layer (UI/UX) without driving major structural or systemic architectural decisions.

requirement: If predefined user profiles or user-specified profiles are used for individualizing or adapting content, the profile currently used should be made evident.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level usability requirement affecting presentation of user profiles without altering core architectural design.

requirement: If user-specified profiles are used, users should be able to see, modify and delete that profile on demand.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD requirement for user profiles that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The user interfaces of the system should follow the guidelines specified under www.usability.gov
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation-layer design guideline and does not drive core architectural structure or technology choices.

requirement: The System must be available to users from <xx:00> to <xx:00> on <all weekdays/xxx days per year>.
ASR_Explanation: This availability window requirement impacts the system’s high-availability architecture, redundancy, monitoring, and failover design.

requirement: The planned downtime for the System must not exceed <xx> hours per <rolling three month period>.
ASR_Explanation: This availability constraint drives the high-availability architecture, requiring redundancy, failover mechanisms and monitoring strategies.

requirement: Unplanned downtime for the System must not exceed <xx hours/minutes> per <rolling three month period>.
ASR_Explanation: This availability target drives architecture choices around redundancy, failover, monitoring, and capacity planning.

requirement: The number of incidents of unplanned downtime for the System must not exceed <x> per <rolling three month period>.
ASR_Explanation: This availability target drives the design of redundancy, failover, monitoring, and fault‐tolerance mechanisms in the system architecture.

requirement: In the event of any software or hardware failure, it must be possible to restore the System (with inline synchronization) within no more than <xx> hours.
ASR_Explanation: This RTO-driven recovery requirement dictates the system’s redundancy, failover, backup, and synchronization architecture.

requirement: The System must provide adequate response times for commonly performed functions under both standard and peak conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This performance requirement will drive architectural decisions around scalability, resource allocation, caching, and load-balancing strategies.

requirement: The System must be able to perform a simple search within 5-8 seconds and an advanced search (multiple search criteria) within 10-15 seconds regardless of the storage capacity or number of cases in the system.
ASR_Explanation: This performance SLA drives key architectural decisions around indexing, caching, data partitioning, and infrastructure scaling to ensure consistent search response times.

requirement: The System must be able to retrieve and display within 5-8 seconds the case which has been accessed within the previous 2 months, regardless of storage capacity or number of cases in the system.
ASR_Explanation: This scale-bound performance requirement drives critical decisions around storage indexing, caching, and data partitioning strategies.

requirement: The System must be able to retrieve and display within 20 seconds the case which has not been accessed within the previous 2 months, regardless of storage capacity or number of cases in the system.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes strict scalability and performance constraints on data storage and retrieval architecture requiring indexing, partitioning, and caching optimizations to guarantee 20-second access regardless of data volume.

requirement: The System must be scalable and must not have any features which would preclude use in small or large police stations, with varying numbers of cases handled.
ASR_Explanation: This scalability requirement drives key architectural decisions around modular design, resource allocation, and elasticity to support varying deployment sizes and workloads.

requirement: The system should support multilingual interface.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces internationalization and localization infrastructure impacting the UI, resource management, and integration layers.

requirement: The system should be designed in manner that operational data is not lost in case of any failure of equipment or communication network.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for fault-tolerant, redundant data storage and failover mechanisms to ensure data durability across equipment or network failures.

requirement: The system should work even in an offline mode with the critical functionality.
ASR_Explanation: It requires an offline-first architecture with local data storage, synchronization and fallback mechanisms affecting storage and sync design.

requirement: The system should be designed to have satisfactory performance even in Police Stations connected on low-bandwidth.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around data transfer protocols, compression, caching, and offline synchronization to ensure acceptable performance on low-bandwidth links.

requirement: The system should be implemented using Service Oriented Architecture (SOA) and have a modular design.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR prescribes the architectural style (SOA) and modular decomposition, directly shaping service boundaries, integration patterns, and system scalability.

requirement: The system should be developed on Open Standards.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains technology selection and impacts system interoperability and architectural design choices.

requirement: The system should be built on a common User Access and Authentication Service to ensure Single-Sign on for the end-user.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates a centralized authentication/SSO infrastructure, impacting security architecture, integration patterns, and service boundaries.

requirement: The system should be developed for a centralized deployment and maintenance.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates a centralized hosting topology, impacting infrastructure design, scalability, availability, and maintenance.

requirement: The system should be developed to be deployed in a 3-tier datacenter architecture.
ASR_Explanation: Specifies a 3-tier deployment topology, directly impacting component distribution, infrastructure design, and network segmentation.

requirement: The system should be designed to have a n-tier architecture with the presentation logic separated from the business logic that is again separated from the data-access logic.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates a three-tier separation of concerns, shaping module boundaries, deployment topology, and overall system scalability.

requirement: The system should be extensible to provide access to the interfaces through PDA’s and mobile data terminals.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for a modular, platform-agnostic interface layer and communication protocols to support varied PDA and mobile terminal clients.

requirement: The system should adopt standardized formats and common metadata elements.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of data models, APIs, and integration layers by enforcing standardized schemas and metadata for interoperability and extensibility.

requirement: The system should be designed for access through browser-based systems and must impose minimal requirements on the client device.
ASR_Explanation: This drives a thin-client, browser-based architecture and influences technology choices for cross-browser compatibility and minimal client requirements.

requirement: The system must support multiple types of communication services for remote access.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing an extensible communication layer with protocol abstraction to support multiple remote access services.

requirement: The system should have capability to support public access to a subset of data and functionality.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the security and access‐control architecture by defining authentication/authorization boundaries and data‐partitioning logic.

requirement: The system should support multi-tier authentication where required.
ASR_Explanation: It influences the security architecture by requiring design of multi-tier authentication modules, trust boundaries, and integration with identity management.

requirement: The system should support SSL encrypted connections.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architectural decisions around secure communication layers, certificate management, and encryption protocol support.

requirement: The system should support secure virtual private network connections.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the network/security architecture by requiring VPN protocol support, encryption key management, and secure connectivity design.

requirement: The system should use HTTPS as the communication protocol, i.e., HTTP over an encrypted secure socket layer (SSL).

requirement: The system should run on multiple browsers.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates selection and structuring of UI frameworks and components to ensure consistent cross-browser compatibility.

requirement: The system should support selective encryption of the stored data.
ASR_Explanation: Selective encryption impacts the storage architecture, key management strategy, and performance/security trade-offs.

requirement: The system should ensure secure transmission of data over the network and utilize SSL and 2-way digital signatures.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the selection and design of the secure communication layer, including SSL/TLS protocol handling, certificate management, and cryptographic service integration.

requirement: The system should ensure high standards of security and access control through: Prevent cross-site scripting, Validate the incoming data/user request, Encode the incoming data/user request, Prevent SQL Injection, Utilize parameterized queries, Sanitize the user-inputs, Validate the data both at the client and server, Do not allow hard delete and perform only soft tagging the row for deletion.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the overall security architecture, affecting input-validation pipelines, encoding modules, parameterized data access and soft-delete data modeling.

requirement: The system should ensure high scalability and performance through: Use of cache for storing frequent data, Use of AJAX based technology to improve user experience, Leverage Asynchronous HTTP socket capabilities of web server for scalability and performance, Host all the static content (documents, images) on the web server, The search results should be fetched from the database in batches of 10 or 20 maximum as configured within the application, Display of records on the screen in batches/paged manner, The search should fetch only the fields that need to be displayed to the user. Only when the user clicks on a particular record to view its further details should a query be fired to fetch the additional details for this particular record only, A hierarchical cache should be configured and used for caching of results of most frequently used searches, Database Indexes should be applied on the key columns used for searching.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives critical architectural decisions around caching strategies, asynchronous HTTP handling, data paging, and database indexing to achieve the required scalability and performance.

requirement: Allow agents to join and leave multicast group.

requirement: Allow agents to choose to encrypt or not to encrypt message.

requirement: Security – we provide some basic mechanisms for security such as message encryption. However, there is no guarantee that the others cannot decrypt the encrypted messages.

requirement: Multicast Protocol – in order to send multicast message, network environment such as router, network card and operating systems must support multicast protocol.

requirement: Broadcast Message – in many network, only system administrator is allowed to send broadcast message.

requirement: We assume that each agent knows the address of destinating agents in order to send unicast message.

requirement: We assume that each agent has enough knowledge to decide the best way to communicate with the other agents.

requirement: We assume that each agent knows the multicast address in order to send multicast message.

requirement: Ability for the user to access e-mail archive.

requirement: Ability for the user to use wireless service with GIS.

requirement: Ability for the user to provide fax service integrated into e-mail.

requirement: Ability for the user to integrate telephone messaging (VOIP) into e-mail and vice/versa.

requirement: Ability for the user to provide List Server option for internal and external publishing of information (report subscriptions).

requirement: Ability for the agency admin to integrate directory services with other complimentary external systems for a unified client experience.

requirement: Ability for the agency admin to provide RSS Feeds.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide a people section outlining recent CS graduates and more experienced professionals in CS and related careers.

requirement: The Get Real website shall profile women computer scientists and engineers as people who work every day to solve problems and make the world a better, cleaner, safer place.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide resources for women in computer science, including links to organizations and contact lists.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide a wide range of pre-college technology programs and courses for high school students.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide a list of focused questions and answers with links inside the Get Real site, including topics such as money, people, and colleges.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide a list of articles outside the Get Real site, including '50 best jobs in America', 'Dream Jobs 2007', etc.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide recommended curricula for high school students interested in CS and related careers, with pointers to recommendations from OUS campuses and other university sources.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide pointers to ACM recommended curricula for high school CS students.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide at least two kinds of searches: 1) internal search of the Get Real site, and 2) external search of CS related outside sites such as OUS campuses and articles.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide a listing with links to ACM, IEEE, and key subgroups such as SIGGRAPH, including pointers to student sections of these organizations.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide selected articles from the chancellor’s office daily communications distribution, fed to RSS subscribers on the Get Real site.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide a FAQ section where students can get fast feedback from the Oregon University System on questions asked in a Q and A format.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide a Grad or Prof BLOG on CS careers.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional feature (blogging/CRUD) with minimal impact on the overall software architecture.

requirement: The Get Real website shall provide information for students in medicine, law, business, and other professions about the value of having a CS minor.

requirement: The Get Real site should work and be tested against IE, Firefox and Netscape.

requirement: The Get Real site should be hosted on a server that can provide adequate response time.

requirement: The Get Real site should have copyright and other security measures consistent with the OUS site.

requirement: There is a need to track and evaluate the 'hits' and time spent on the student-focused website over time, and make content, design, and navigation changes as needed based on evaluation of hits.
ASR_Explanation: It requires integrating analytics instrumentation, data collection, storage, and reporting infrastructure, impacting the system’s monitoring and data pipeline architecture.

requirement: Web design conventions should be consistent with the standards and conventions used on the OUS site.

requirement: Changes to version 2 should include design changes to all existing pages to shorten up paragraphs and add bullets and charts where possible to accommodate faster page reading and shorter reader attention span.

requirement: The Get Real website should use more white space, pictures, color, photos, diagrams, and charts to break up text and make the site attractive.
ASR_Explanation: This is a cosmetic UI styling preference with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Get Real website should break information into more readable chunks to avoid 'walls of text'.

requirement: The Get Real website should maximize Google and other search engine hits by being part of the OUS domain.

requirement: The Get Real website is constrained by space, bandwidth for video streaming, and limited availability of Chancellor’s Office resources.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint drives key architecture decisions around efficient storage, streaming optimization (e.g., CDNs and compression), and limited resource utilization.

requirement: Mashbot will allow the use of external authentication modules for user login. Mashbot will also provide an internal authentication mechanism in case an external module is unavailable.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of a pluggable authentication subsystem with fallback, impacting security boundaries, interfaces, and module orchestration.

requirement: Mashbot has an interface for a web client which processes user commands to interact with campaigns.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines a key client‐to‐server interface affecting API design, communication patterns, and campaign management integration.

requirement: Mashbot has an interface for publishing content to social networks, as well as for aggregating social network data.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the external integration layer, impacting API/connector architecture, security, scalability, and data ingestion patterns.

requirement: Mashbot has an interface to a database which allows for the storage and retrieval of data related to accounts and campaigns.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD data‐persistence requirement with minimal impact on overall architecture.

requirement: The user interface consists of a web front end with tabs to separate the various workflow areas. To create content, the user is provided with a calendar scheduling tool and a content editor. Additionally, there is a monitoring dashboard which gives the user a view on responses to the content in any given campaign. Finally, there is an explore view that gives the user a portal with which they can keep tabs on topics of interest in social media.
ASR_Explanation: This is a front-end UI layout specification that doesn’t impose significant constraints on overall system architecture.

requirement: The Mashbot web client runs on any computer hardware capable of connecting to the Internet, running a modern HTTP 1.1 web browser, includes a keyboard and a pointing device, and includes writable volatile storage.
ASR_Explanation: This is a generic environment compatibility constraint and does not drive major architectural structure or technology choices.

requirement: The Mashbot server runs on any computer hardware capable of connecting to the Internet as a server, interfacing with modern database software, and running a modern suite of networking software.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint defines the deployment environment and platform compatibility, impacting hardware, OS, database, and networking architecture decisions.

requirement: Mashbot authenticates users against its internal user database.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the security and authentication subsystem’s design, database integration, and cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: Mashbot interfaces with the users web browser and expects that it is capable of HTTP 1.1 and HTML 4.0.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the communication layer and browser compatibility, driving choices for HTTP handling and HTML rendering support in the architecture.

requirement: Mashbot software interfaces with an existing database software.
ASR_Explanation: Interfaces with an existing database dictates the system’s integration layer and data‐access architecture.

requirement: The Mashbot server runs on an operating system that supports serving dynamic web pages using encryption and is capable of communicating with the Internet.
ASR_Explanation: It influences the choice of operating system, security (encryption/TLS) support, and network connectivity, shaping deployment and security architecture.

requirement: Communication between the web client and the server software is facilitated by common network protocols. Data will be encrypted using TLS and HTTP/1.1. The use of these protocols requires the ability for the systems to communicate using TCP/IP network stacks. The Mashbot system sends emails using SMTP.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying TLS/HTTP/1.1, TCP/IP, and SMTP protocols directly shapes the communication layer and security architecture of the system.

requirement: The Mashbot server requires no greater than 1 gigabyte of RAM memory. The web client requires no more than the minimum memory a modern desktop computer commonly has, approximately 256 megabytes of RAM.
ASR_Explanation: This tight memory footprint constraint will drive decisions on technology choices, component design, and resource‐management strategies.

requirement: Mashbot should be able to schedule content for various services to be published concurrently.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing a concurrent scheduling and orchestration layer to handle multi-service publishing reliably and scalably.

requirement: Mashbot should be able to view and compare historical metrics of campaigns.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing a historical metrics storage and analytics layer, impacting data retention, retrieval, and reporting architecture.

requirement: Mashbot should be able to view/create replies to content.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD feature for user-generated content with minimal impact on the overall architecture.

requirement: Mashbot should be able to maintain users and approvers of content.
ASR_Explanation: It requires implementing user and approver management with role-based access control, impacting the system’s security and component structure.

requirement: Mashbot should be able to set up keyword alerts for watched services.
ASR_Explanation: This adds an event-driven alerting pipeline requiring subscription management, real-time keyword filtering, and notification infrastructure.

requirement: The external email system is to provide a messaging service from Mashbot to the Mashbot users.
ASR_Explanation: This integration drives architectural decisions around external system interfaces, message delivery reliability, security, and scalability.

requirement: The input is generated automatically by the Mashbot system using settings configurable by the System Administrator.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a configurable, automated input‐generation component, impacting module boundaries and configuration management architecture.

requirement: The output is in the form of an e-mail to an e-mail account, but it does not return any sort of message to Mashbot.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates asynchronous email delivery without a callback to Mashbot, affecting integration patterns, messaging flow, and error-handling architecture.

requirement: The format uses SMTP protocol. The actual messages are provided by Mashbot.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates the messaging integration architecture by mandating SMTP support and a Mashbot provider interface.

requirement: The system categorizes users on the basis of roles and privileges. Within these roles, the system also categorizes users based on the roles that they have within individual products. These are referred to as user account roles.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s cross‐cutting RBAC/security architecture, data models, and authorization services.

requirement: Mashbot Campaigns supports the following account roles: Contributor, Approver, Publisher.
ASR_Explanation: Defines required roles and permissions, impacting the design of the authorization architecture and RBAC implementation.

requirement: A user may possess more than one role.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the RBAC architecture and data model by requiring a many-to-many user-role relationship in the identity management subsystem.

requirement: Roles reflect actions that can be performed by a user.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of the system’s authorization architecture by requiring a role-based access control module and related security components.

requirement: Roles can be assigned to a user account for individual products.
ASR_Explanation: This affects the authorization architecture by requiring per-product role-based access control and corresponding changes to identity/data models.

requirement: Contributors may create new content, import existing content into the system, edit content, and delete it. They may also submit these actions for approval.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces requirements for a content management workflow and role-based access control that will impact system modularity, service boundaries, and data versioning.

requirement: Approvers can approve actions performed by contributors.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine role-based permission feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Publishers may schedule or immediately initiate actions put forth by contributors and those approved by approvers.
ASR_Explanation: Scheduling versus immediate execution drives the need for a workflow/scheduling engine and asynchronous task management components.

requirement: New user accounts can be created.
ASR_Explanation: This basic CRUD capability for user management does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: The system may contain any number of user accounts.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the need for a scalable user data storage architecture (e.g., sharding/partitioning and capacity planning).

requirement: Certain pieces of information are required to create new accounts: Username, Password, Name, Email Address, Group Membership, User Account Type.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the core user domain model and impacts database schema, validation logic, and security architecture.

requirement: The system allows users to modify their accounts once created.
ASR_Explanation: This basic CRUD operation doesn’t drive high-level structural decisions or non-functional constraints.

requirement: The system requires that a user have logged in before modifications can be made.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the inclusion of authentication/authorization layers and identity management services, impacting the security architecture.

requirement: The following user account information is modifiable by all user types: Password, Email Address, Name.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the design of authentication/authorization and user‐management services to enforce proper access control.

requirement: The system allows user accounts to be deactivated.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only affects the user account lifecycle management module without significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system denies users who have been deactivated from accessing the system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard access‐control check that can be handled within the existing authentication/authorization module without major architectural changes.

requirement: If an account has any history associated with it, it can only be deactivated and not deleted.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a soft-delete data model and retention policy enforcement affecting database schema and archival mechanisms.

requirement: If an account has no history associated with it, it can be deleted from the system.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the data model and lifecycle management, requiring mechanisms for safe deletion, cascade handling, and data retention policies.

requirement: A disabled account can be undisabled.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic CRUD-level account state transition with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: It is possible to disable all accounts except for the System Administrator account.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the security and user-management architecture by requiring a global account-disable feature with an override for the System Administrator.

requirement: The system gives users the ability to reset their password.
ASR_Explanation: It influences the authentication architecture by requiring secure token management, email service integration, and related security controls.

requirement: Individual passwords can be reset.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a common authentication feature that can be implemented within existing security modules without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: The system only allows users to change their own passwords.
ASR_Explanation: This security constraint mandates user-level authorization controls in the authentication/authorization architecture to enforce strict access boundaries for password changes.

requirement: A campaign has the following components: Name, Pieces of content, Schedule, User/Group Permissions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic domain data-model requirement with minimal impact on overall architecture.

requirement: A schedule is a mapping of times to publishing actions. It may contain any actions a publisher can perform, and these actions are performed at the associated time.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces a scheduling component and timed job-execution pipeline, affecting overall component structure and integration.

requirement: A piece of content may take the following forms: Text, Image, Audio, Video.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s storage, processing, and delivery architecture by requiring support for multiple media types.

requirement: Mashbot will allow for the association of Mashbot accounts with external service accounts.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the identity‐federation and security architecture, impacting authentication flows, data models, and integration interfaces.

requirement: Mashbot will provide an interface for authenticating a user account to an external service account.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the security and integration architecture by requiring design decisions around authentication protocols, external service interfaces, and credential management.

requirement: Mashbot will provide a standardized method of interacting with external service accounts.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the integration layer’s abstraction and connector framework, impacting module boundaries and external service interface design.

requirement: The system encrypts data over a direct connection between the web client and the server.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the communication architecture by mandating TLS/SSL implementation, certificate management, and secure channel establishment.

requirement: The system allows for the backup of the data system to local or remote non-volatile storage systems. Incremental backups should not create outages and full backups do not interfere with user interaction for more than 10 minutes.
ASR_Explanation: This backup availability and performance constraint drives the design of the storage/backup subsystem, including asynchronous incremental snapshots and scheduling.

requirement: The system is configurable to e-mail warnings about multiple failed login attempts from the same user. These warnings are sent to the System Administrator.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine alerting feature using standard email/configuration mechanisms and doesn’t drive major architectural changes.

requirement: The system allows only valid users to log into the system. A valid user is one which has not been disabled or deleted and exists on the system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement shapes the authentication and user management architecture, impacting security controls and identity verification flows.

requirement: To log onto the system, a user provides their valid username and password.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard authentication functional requirement that doesn’t impose unique architectural constraints beyond typical login handling.

requirement: The system uses the configured authentication module. The authentication method is configurable by the System Administrator.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the overall security architecture by requiring a pluggable authentication module with configurable strategies and dynamic loading.

requirement: The system allows the System Administrator to configure a timeout after which a user is automatically logged out of Mashbot.
ASR_Explanation: This is a configurable security setting that can be implemented within the existing session management component without altering overall system architecture.

requirement: The user interface will consist of a tabbed navigation bar that unifies the sitewide navigation, as well as module specific navigation as needed. Tabs: Dashboard, Create, Schedule, Explore.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI design detail and does not drive core architectural structure or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The dashboard consists of graphs and charts to give the user a quick view on how their campaigns are performing. Metrics include: Clickthrough rate, Page Views, Number of Comments. Service plugins can define additional specialized metrics to track.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a scalable, extensible plugin architecture and real-time data aggregation and visualization components.

requirement: A panel is available to give more information on each metric as it is selected.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI interaction detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: This view allows users to create campaigns and fill them with content. This view is also used when users need to edit an existing campaign.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD UI requirement with no significant impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: A user can add content via the add content button near the top of the view. This will prompt the user to select the content type, which is populated by the services the user currently has access to via credentials stored in the system. This will create a section in the main part of the view that allows the user to add individual elements of that content type, each individually scheduled. Each row line item will allow the user to schedule, edit, or delete that content type.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI interaction requirement and does not impose new architectural constraints or structural changes.

requirement: Users can drag items from the left hand bucket of content to the calendar to schedule content. The content will receive a default golive time of 12am on the day the content is dragged to. If the user desires a different time, he may click the content in the schedule and assign the proper time.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI interaction detail (drag-and-drop with default time setting) that does not affect the system’s high-level structure or non-functional architecture.

requirement: The calendar allows the user to manage when content goes live, and gives a visual representation of the actions taken on content. It is clear when content goes live and when (if) it is deleted. The user can page from month to month to schedule content to any day desired.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI scheduling feature that doesn’t drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: This view allows users to get a pulse on what information social media contains. This includes the ability to set up monitored searches for services that support keyword search via api. This also will aggregate data gathered from comments on content that has been published as part of a given campaign.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the overall system architecture by driving the design of external API integrations and a scalable data ingestion and aggregation pipeline.

requirement: A user should be able to register a new account.

requirement: A user should be able to log in.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard user authentication feature with no broad architectural implications beyond common implementation patterns.

requirement: A member should be able to log out.

requirement: A member should have a profile.

requirement: A member should be able to modify their profile.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation with minimal impact on high-level system architecture.

requirement: A user's email should be verified when registering a new account.

requirement: An admin should be able to modify accounts.

requirement: An admin should be able to suspend accounts.

requirement: An admin should be able to delete accounts.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation for admin users and does not drive any major architectural changes.

requirement: A member should be able to monitor trending topics regarding their campaign.

requirement: A member should be able to monitor facebook groups related to campaigns.

requirement: A member should be able to view @replies to tweets related to their campaigns.

requirement: A member should be able to see responses to blog posts related to the campaign.

requirement: An admin should be able to perform user account actions in bulk.

requirement: An admin should be able to see all campaigns.

requirement: An admin should be able to delete any campaign.

requirement: An admin should be able to edit all campaigns.

requirement: Mashbot campaigns should allow multiple users to collaborate on a campaign.

requirement: Mashbot campaigns should support multiple users.

requirement: A member should be able to store authentication for supported services.

requirement: A member should be able to add additional services to an existing campaign.

requirement: A member should be able to delete individual campaign elements.

requirement: Members should have hierarchical permissions.

requirement: A campaign should have workflow approval process.

requirement: A member should be able to 'unpublish' a campaign.

requirement: A member should be able to delete a campaign.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A member should be able to schedule events in bulk.

requirement: A member should be able to schedule 'live dates' for individual events.

requirement: A member should be able to delete existing content from supported services.
