requirement: A member should be able to see the content they have published in all supported services.

requirement: A member should be able to push to Facebook in a campaign.

requirement: A member should be able to post to a blog in a campaign.

requirement: A member should be able to post to Twitter in a campaign.

requirement: A member should be able to create a new campaign.

requirement: A member should get notified when activity occurs in a campaign they're working on.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine notification feature that can be implemented within the existing service layer without altering the core architecture.

requirement: CU2 will be responsible for the design and developing of GOG that will provide Catalogue, Main Database (MDB) and final data (in the sense of statistically equivalent to the final mission data) data without the use GASS telemetry, GIBIS or the main ESAC database.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates a standalone data ingestion and storage architecture, impacting how databases, catalogs, and data pipelines are designed and integrated.

requirement: GOG will be fully integrated in the Gaia Simulator project. That is, it will use as other the other simulators the Gaia Simu library and the Gaia Tools library.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates key component dependencies and integration points with the Gaia Simu and Gaia Tools libraries, impacting the system’s modular architecture.

requirement: For source generation GOG has 2 main options available: Universe Model from GaiaSimu or an Object Input Catalogue.
ASR_Explanation: This choice dictates the design and interfaces of the source‐generation subsystem, impacting module structure and data‐ingestion architecture.

requirement: GOG has a source by source processing mechanism.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates a per-source processing pipeline architecture, impacting componentization, data flow orchestration, and scalability.

requirement: GOG must consider noise effects. For noise generation those effects should be user-selectable (they can be added or not and the values should be given by users). We classify noises in two groups: 1. Instrumental noises: noises that come from observation process such as: on board detection and tracking or observation dead-time 2. Reduction noise: noise added to the data when removing instrumental noise.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a modular, configurable noise-processing pipeline and impacts how the system manages plug-in noise generators and user-defined parameters.

requirement: GOG I/O Catalogue format is driven by [AD3]. Main Database format is defined in [AD1].
ASR_Explanation: It imposes critical data format constraints that drive the design of integration interfaces and the database schema.

requirement: GOG must give the same result for identical input configuration parameters.
ASR_Explanation: Ensuring deterministic results for identical inputs drives architectural decisions around stateless processing, consistent algorithms, and reproducible data handling.

requirement: GOG must provide a way to be run from a command line or a shell script.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the system’s interface and deployment architecture by requiring a command-line interface, affecting module boundaries and automation.

requirement: GOG should be able to run in a cluster or grid environments.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the deployment architecture by requiring support for distributed clustering, node coordination, and fault tolerance.

requirement: GOG is built as a standalone java application that could be invoked from a command line and deployed as a jar file.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the deployment and packaging strategy as a standalone CLI Java JAR, impacting module structure and runtime environment.

requirement: GOG is designed to run on either in a single thread mode or on grid environments.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives core architectural decisions around concurrency models and distributed grid deployment.

requirement: GOG runs on LINUX, UNIX and MAC computers (minimum). GOG results are platform independent.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This cross-platform portability NFR drives architectural decisions on OS abstraction layers, technology stack, and testing strategy for Linux, Unix, and macOS support.

requirement: GOG will have to generate data of a considerable amount of astrosources whose number could be a significant part of the expected final catalogue (20%).
ASR_Explanation: This impacts system scalability and storage architecture by requiring capacity planning for generating and managing large-scale synthetic astrosource data.

requirement: GOG must be able to process one complete source processing on 2 CPU seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This performance constraint drives architecture choices around concurrency, hardware provisioning, and optimization strategies.

requirement: GOG initialisation Module should be able to read information of the input sources. Options are to use the Universe Model or provided user sources.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the architecture by requiring an extensible initialization component and abstract input-source adapters for the Universe Model versus user-provided data.

requirement: The GOG catalogue module must include information on the universe model: Galaxy model, Sky Background model, Extinction Law model, DMS model.
ASR_Explanation: It defines core domain models (Galaxy, Sky Background, Extinction Law, DMS) that shape data schema, module interfaces and extensibility requirements.

requirement: GOG initialisation module must include information on the instrument model: The selected scanning law, Dead time, Noise models (including detection noise).
ASR_Explanation: It defines core initialization data and interfaces for instrument configuration, impacting module boundaries and configuration management.

requirement: The GOG catalogue module must include information on the reduction model: Instrument effects subtraction node bias subtraction noises PPE residuals noise, Scientific content extraction noise, subtraction of close sources, second FoV contamination noise, background subtraction.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates integrating multiple specialized data‐reduction nodes and noise‐correction steps, shaping the modular pipeline design and performance architecture.

requirement: GOG initialisation module will define the output specification output parameters (spectrum RPBP/RVS, astrometry, Vr, physical parameters and flags) epoch and/or combined number of epochs.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional specification for a single module’s behavior and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Determine for epoch data the astrometric parameter (with instrumental and reduction errors): transit times and field angles in each CCD, mean transit time and field angle for the transit. Gate activation and signal loss due to window cutoff must be taken into account.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed domain‐specific calculation requirement and does not impose structural or quality‐attribute constraints on the overall architecture.

requirement: Determine the epoch data photometry (with instrumental and reduction errors): Gmag (for each CCD and average for the transit), GRP, GBP, GRVS. Gate activation and signal loss due to window cutoff must be taken into account.
ASR_Explanation: This functional requirement drives the design of specialized photometry pipeline components (error propagation, gate handling, window cutoff) and thus affects system modularization and data-flow architecture.

requirement: Determine the epoch radial velocity (with instrumental and reduction errors).
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized data-processing function that doesn’t drive major architectural structures.

requirement: Determine the epoch flags.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Determine the epoch physical parameters (Av, Teff, logg, FeH).
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a domain-specific functional computation that does not drive major structural or non‐functional architectural decisions.

requirement: Compute the astrometric combined data using all previous transits: Compute parallax and proper motion errors, Determine the covariance matrix, Determine estimated errors for the astrometric parameters at the mean epoch.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of a high-performance numerical processing pipeline and influences module boundaries, data flow, and resource allocation for covariance and error computations.

requirement: Determine the combined data photometry: mean G, GRP, GBP, GRVS.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-specific data aggregation function (mean photometry) with no broad impact on system structure, scalability, or other architectural concerns.

requirement: Determine the mean radial velocity.
ASR_Explanation: This simple computational requirement does not impose constraints on the system’s structure or key quality attributes.

requirement: Determine the final flags (variability, non-single star).
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific business logic rule for flag determination and does not drive any architectural or design-level decisions.

requirement: Determine the mean physical parameters (classification, Av, Teff, logg, FeH).
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data-processing function with no major impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Noise consideration on: Flux noise (Poisson noise, total detection noise, ...), CCD combination noise for (RVS), Wavelength calibration, Multi-transit combination noise (only for combined data) should be added.
ASR_Explanation: It requires adding complex noise modeling and calibration across multiple processing stages, impacting the data-pipeline architecture, module interfaces, and performance.

requirement: The universe catalog output data of the simulation has to follow the universe model ICD document. Note: GaiaSimu library will allow to write and read this format.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the system’s data model, interface contracts, and integration with the GaiaSimu library, impacting module boundaries and serialization components.

requirement: GOG output should be driven by the MDB data model. The output will be used to distribute extracts to the DPC’s.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes alignment of GOG output with the MDB data model, affecting component interfaces and the distribution pipeline architecture.

requirement: The APAF system shall acquire from ESOC the telemetry data of the ASPERA-3 Experiment and Mars Express Orbit/Attitude.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for external interface and data ingestion pipeline design to integrate real-time telemetry from ESOC.

requirement: The APAF system shall process all ASPERA-3 science data into IDFS data sets.
ASR_Explanation: This core data processing requirement drives the architecture of the ingestion‐to‐IDFS conversion pipeline, affecting component interfaces, scalability, and throughput.

requirement: The APAF system shall process the engineering and ancillary information necessary for calibration and science validation into IDFS data sets.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-specific functional requirement defining data processing behavior without imposing system‐level architectural constraints.

requirement: Intermediate files of cleaned-up ASPERA-3 and MEX OA telemetry shall be generated in the event that cleaned-up telemetry is not provided by ESOC.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces a fallback data‐processing module and storage handling for cleaned‐up telemetry, impacting the overall data ingestion and pipeline architecture.

requirement: The ASPERA-3 and MEX OA telemetry data shall be stored on a local SwRI archive.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of the data storage layer and local archive infrastructure, impacting data management, integration, and capacity planning.

requirement: The ASPERA-3 and MEX OA IDFS data sets shall be stored on a local SwRI archive.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates on-premises data storage constraints, impacting the system’s infrastructure and data architecture design.

requirement: Any APAF-generated intermediate files of ASPERA-3 and MEX OA cleaned-up telemetry shall be stored on a local SwRI archive.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the design of the system’s storage and data persistence layer, including integration with the local SwRI archive infrastructure.

requirement: Web-based displays of the most current ASPERA-3 data shall be provided for public view.
ASR_Explanation: This FR drives architectural decisions around real-time data ingestion, caching strategies, and scalable web‐service delivery for public visualization.

requirement: Web-based displays defined by the ASPERA-3 team shall be provided where any available ASPERA-3 data (as opposed to just the most current) can be used for science analysis.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the data storage and retrieval architecture by mandating historical data indexing, scalable storage, and flexible query/display capabilities.

requirement: The web-based displays defined by the ASPERA-3 team to be used for science analysis shall be password protected until the ASPERA-3 data is made public.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates integrating an authentication and authorization layer into the system’s architecture for password‐protected access.

requirement: The APAF ground data system shall have built-in error handling.
ASR_Explanation: Built-in error handling is a cross-cutting concern that drives the choice of architectural patterns and fault-tolerance mechanisms.

requirement: ASPERA-3 and MEX OA IDFS data and any APAF-generated intermediate files of ASPERA-3 and MEX OA cleaned-up telemetry shall be provided to all ASPERA-3 Co-I’s.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates a scalable, secure data distribution and access-control architecture for delivering telemetry and intermediate files to all Co-Investigators.

requirement: IDFS data access software developed by SwRI shall be made available to the ASPERA-3 Co-I’s.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine distribution requirement for making the software available and does not impose architectural constraints or drive high-level design decisions.

requirement: Science analysis software developed by SwRI to analyze IDFS-formatted data shall be made available to the ASPERA-3 Co-I’s.
ASR_Explanation: This is a deployment/distribution requirement and does not drive core architectural structure or technology choices.

requirement: The APAF system web server shall be password protected where appropriate to allow only pertinent ASPERA-3 team members access.
ASR_Explanation: This security requirement drives the authentication/authorization framework and overall security architecture of the web server.

requirement: There shall be a UNIX-based APAF system development computer.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint dictates the target operating system platform, directly impacting technology selection and system architecture.

requirement: There shall be a UNIX-based APAF system production computer with an archival system.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates the operating system platform and archival subsystem integration, directly impacting the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: There shall be a web server for the APAF system.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates a server-side component, driving choices in technology stack, deployment architecture, and scalability strategy.

requirement: There shall be a network connection between SwRI and NISN.
ASR_Explanation: It defines external network connectivity constraints impacting network topology, security zoning, and performance architecture.

requirement: SwRI shall provide APAF system maintenance.
ASR_Explanation: This is a contractual/operational support requirement and does not drive system architectural decisions.

requirement: SwRI shall provide software support for the APAF system.
ASR_Explanation: This operational support requirement addresses maintenance processes rather than driving system structure or design constraints.

requirement: ASPERA-3 IDFS data that are electronically distributed shall be provided to the ASPERA-3 Co-I’s within 24 hours of acquiring ASPERA-3 telemetry as long as the transmission and processing are error-free.
ASR_Explanation: This 24-hour delivery SLA drives the design of the data processing pipeline, scheduling, and error-handling architecture to meet the timeliness requirement.

requirement: MEX OA IDFS data that are electronically distributed shall be provided to the ASPERA-3 Co-I’s within 24 hours of acquiring MEX OA telemetry as long as the transmission and processing are error-free.
ASR_Explanation: This 24-hour delivery SLA drives the design of the data ingestion, processing pipeline, error-handling, and distribution infrastructure.

requirement: Any APAF-generated intermediate files of ASPERA-3 and MEX OA cleaned-up telemetry that are electronically distributed shall be provided to the ASPERA-3 Co-I’s within 24 hours of acquiring ASPERA-3 and MEX OA telemetry as long as the transmission and processing are error-free.
ASR_Explanation: This 24-hour delivery latency requirement imposes a service-level constraint that will drive the design of the data processing pipeline, scheduling, and distribution components.

requirement: ASPERA-3 IDFS data and MEX OA IDFS data shall be provided to NASA PDS.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s integration architecture by mandating support for NASA PDS interfaces, data formats, and delivery pipelines.

requirement: ASPERA-3 data shall be provided to NASA PDS in PDS-compliant form.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates adherence to NASA PDS data standards, impacting the architecture of the data pipeline, formatting, and integration components.

requirement: ASPERA-3 data shall be calibrated and validated prior to depositing in the NASA PDS.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates integration of calibration and validation processing stages into the data pipeline before archiving, affecting the system’s dataflow architecture.

requirement: ASPERA-3 data shall be provided to NASA PDS no later than 6 months after acquisition.
ASR_Explanation: This six-month delivery constraint drives the design of data processing and archival pipelines, storage capacity, and throughput to ensure timely NASA PDS ingestion.

requirement: SwRI shall provide IDFS data processing algorithms to IRF.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the creation of a core data‐processing component and its integration interface with IRF, impacting system structure and data flow.

requirement: Science analysis software shall be integrated into the NASA approved data repository.
ASR_Explanation: Integration with the NASA data repository dictates the design of the data flow and interface layers and affects compliance and security architecture.

requirement: How the ASPERA-3 and MEX OA data and exactly what data sets are to be distributed to each Co-I shall be determined by the needs and resources available six months prior to MEX launch. The distribution mechanisms shall be clearly defined/described in the APAF Operation Procedures Document.
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational planning requirement for procedural documentation rather than a constraint that drives system architectural design.

requirement: Measure integral ENA fluxes in the energy range of 0.1 to 60 keV.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional measurement requirement and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Measure mass/energy resolved neutral hydrogen and oxygen atom spectra in the energy range of 0.1 to 100 keV.
ASR_Explanation: This domain-level measurement function doesn’t impose structural or quality constraints that drive the overall system architecture.

requirement: Measure ion energy/mass/angle resolved spectra in the energy range of 0.001 to 40 keV; 1-1e6 atomic mass units per unit charge (amu/q); with 4pi steradian coverage.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives key architectural decisions for sensor subsystems, high-throughput data processing, and full-angle coverage integration.

requirement: Measure the electron spectra in the energy range of 0.001 to 20 keV, with 4pi steradian coverage.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of a 4π sensor array and the associated high-throughput data acquisition and processing architecture to cover 0.001–20 keV.

requirement: Measure ions and electrons in the energy range of 0.001 to 40 keV; 1-1e6 amu/q.
ASR_Explanation: This measurement range dictates sensor selection and data acquisition architecture, impacting hardware interfaces and signal processing design.

requirement: Data processing software shall meet the delivery requirements of the IRF in order to support the launch date and operation of the ESA Mars Express mission.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes strict delivery and integration deadlines that drive architectural decisions for modularization, testing, and deployment pipelines.

requirement: SwRI shall develop the ASPERA-3 data reduction system and software to be used during instrument integration, test, calibration and mission operations for the full ASPERA-3 experiment.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines the system’s scope and lifecycle contexts (integration, test, calibration, operations), directly impacting high-level architectural boundaries and design.

requirement: SwRI shall provide processing algorithms to IRF in preparation for the mission operations and data analysis phase of Mars Express on a schedule to satisfy the mission needs per agreement with IRF.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a schedule‐driven delivery commitment rather than a technical constraint or quality attribute that would shape the system architecture.

requirement: The ASPERA-3 MO Project shall develop a project data management plan to address the total activity associated with the flow of science data, including planning, acquisition, processing, data product generation and validation, archiving, and preservation.
ASR_Explanation: It governs end-to-end data flow, processing, storage, and archiving requirements, directly shaping the system’s data management and infrastructure architecture.

requirement: The “prototype” version of the DigitalHome System (as specified in this document) must be completed within twelve months of inception.
ASR_Explanation: This is a project timeline constraint and does not directly influence the system’s structural or design decisions.

requirement: The development team will consist of five engineers. The DigitalHomeOwner Director will provide management and communication support.
ASR_Explanation: This describes team composition and management support, which affects project planning but not the software’s architecture.

requirement: The development team will use the development process specified by the Digital HomeOwner Inc.
ASR_Explanation: This is a project management/process requirement and does not impact the system’s architectural structure or quality attributes.

requirement: Where possible, the DigitalHome project will employ widely used, accepted, and available hardware and software technology and standards, both for product elements and for development tools.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains technology and standards choices, impacting interoperability, vendor lock-in risk, and the overall system architecture.

requirement: The cost of DigitalHome elements (sensors, controllers, server, tools, etc.), for this project should be minimized.
ASR_Explanation: It drives technology and component choices as well as deployment and integration trade-offs to meet strict cost constraints.

requirement: The DH system will be tested in a simulated environment. There will be no actual physical home and all sensors and controllers will be simulated. However, the simulated environment will be realistic and adhere to the physical properties and constraints of an actual home and to real sensors and controllers.
ASR_Explanation: This is a test-environment requirement and does not impose constraints on the system’s runtime architecture.

requirement: Major changes to this document (e.g., changes in requirements) must be approved by the Director of the DigitalHomeOwner Division.
ASR_Explanation: This is a governance/process requirement for document change control and does not influence the system’s architectural design.

requirement: The home system shall require an Internet Service Provider (ISP). The ISP should be widely available (cable modem, high speed DSL), such as Bright House or Bellsouth FastAccess.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes an external network infrastructure constraint, affecting the system’s connectivity, deployment, and integration architecture.

requirement: A DH System shall have the capability to establish an individual home web server hosted on a home computer. This server will provide interaction with and control of the DH elements, storage of DH plans and data, ability to establish and maintain DH User Accounts, and provide backup service for user account information, user plans and a home database.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives key architectural decisions around hosting topology, security boundaries, data storage management, and backup infrastructure.

requirement: The DH Gateway device shall provide communication with all the DigitalHome devices and shall connect with a broadband Internet connection.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the gateway’s communication interfaces, network protocols, and system topology.

requirement: The Gateway shall contain an RF Module, which shall send and receive wireless communications between the Gateway and the other DigitalHome devices (sensors and controllers).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the inclusion and integration of a core RF communication module, directly affecting hardware/software partitioning and protocol architecture.

requirement: The Gateway device shall operate up to a 1000-foot range for indoor transmission.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts hardware and network architecture choices (RF components, antenna design, power amplification) to meet the 1000-foot indoor range requirement.

requirement: The system shall include digital programmable thermostats, which shall be used to monitor and regulate the temperature of an enclosed space.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives hardware integration and control system architecture, impacting sensor interfacing, real-time control loops, and communication protocols.

requirement: The thermostat shall provide a reading of the current temperature in the space where the thermostat is located.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic sensor-reading feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The controller part of thermostat shall provide a “set point” temperature that is used to control the flow of heat energy (by switching heating or cooling devices on or off as needed) to achieve the set point temperature.
ASR_Explanation: This describes basic controller functionality and does not drive high-level architectural structure or major crosscutting concerns.

requirement: The sensor part of the thermostat has a sensitivity range between 14ºF and 104ºF (-10ºC and 40ºC).
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level hardware specification for sensor selection and calibration that doesn’t drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The system shall include digital programmable humidistats, which shall be used to monitor and regulate the humidity of an enclosed space.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates hardware–software integration and control subsystem design by specifying sensor interfaces, communication protocols, and real-time feedback loops.

requirement: The humidistat shall provide a reading of the current humidity in the space where the humidistat is located.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic sensor input function with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The humidistat shall provide a “set point” humidity that is used to control humidifiers and dehumidifiers achieve the set point humidity.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines basic control functionality without imposing system‐level architectural constraints or influencing key architectural drivers.

requirement: The system shall include magnetic alarm contact switches which shall be used to monitor entry through a door or window when the switch is active.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates integrating magnetic door/window sensors, impacting the hardware interface layer, event-driven processing, and communication protocols.

requirement: The system shall include security sound and light alarms, which can be activated when DigitalHome senses a security breach from a magnetic contact.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of real-time event processing and hardware integration modules for sensors and alarm outputs.

requirement: The system shall include digital programmable power switches which shall be used to monitor the current state of an appliance (e.g., a coffee maker is off or on).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the system’s hardware integration and interface architecture by mandating digital power switch components and associated monitoring protocols.

requirement: The system shall be able to use a power switch to change the state of the appliance (e.g., from “off” to “on”).
ASR_Explanation: This simple on/off switch function is a low-level implementation detail with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The DigitalHome System shall allow a web-ready computer, cell phone or PDA to control a home's temperature, humidity, lights, security, and the state of small appliances.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of a secure, scalable, multi-platform client-server architecture with diverse communication protocols.

requirement: The communication center of the DH system shall be a DH home web server, through which a user shall be able to monitor and control home devices and systems.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines the core system topology and communication hub (web server) and therefore directly drives the high-level architectural design.

requirement: Each DigitalHome shall contain a master control device (the DH Gateway Device) that connects to the home’s broadband Internet connection, and uses wireless communication to send and receive communication between the DigitalHome system and the home devices and systems.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines a core architectural component (the DH Gateway) and its network connectivity and communication protocols, shaping the system’s topology and interfaces.

requirement: The DigitalHome shall be equipped with various environmental controllers and sensors (temperature controller-sensors: thermostats, humidity controller-sensors: humidistats, contact sensors, security sound and light alarms, and power switches).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the hardware integration and communication architecture by mandating support for diverse sensors and controllers.

requirement: Using wireless communication, sensor values can be read and saved in the home database.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the choice of wireless protocols, communication middleware and database integration impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: Controller values can be sent to controllers to change the DH environment.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces cross-component communication and real-time control interfaces, impacting system integration and messaging architecture.

requirement: The DigitalHome programmable thermostat shall allow a user to monitor and control a home’s temperature from any location, using a web ready computer, cell phone, or PDA.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives key architectural decisions around network connectivity, security, cross-platform support, and backend infrastructure.

requirement: A DH user shall be able to read the temperature at a thermostat position.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic sensor-read functionality with minimal impact on system architecture.

requirement: A DH user shall be able to set the thermostat temperatures to between 60°F and 80°F, inclusive, at one degree increments.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward business rule for input validation and does not drive high-level architectural choices.

requirement: Up to eight thermostats shall be placed in rooms throughout the home.
ASR_Explanation: The limit of eight thermostats is a minor scalability detail that doesn’t drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The thermostats may be controlled individually or collectively, so that temperature can be controlled at different levels in different home spaces.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s control layer and device coordination architecture by requiring support for both individual and grouped thermostat management.

requirement: A single thermostat shall be placed in an enclosed space (e.g., a room in the house) for which the air temperature is to be controlled.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level deployment detail for a single sensor per room and does not drive high-level system structuring.

requirement: For each thermostat, up to twenty-four one hour settings per day for every day of the week can be scheduled.
ASR_Explanation: This scheduling limit is a domain-level detail that doesn’t drive high-level architectural structure or key cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: If a thermostat device allows a user to make a manual temperature setting, the setting shall remain in effect until the end of the planned or default time period, at which time the planned or default setting will be used for the next time period.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized scheduling rule for override behavior with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A thermostat unit shall communicate, through wireless signals, with the master control unit.
ASR_Explanation: It drives key architectural decisions around communication interfaces, wireless protocols, and system integration.

requirement: The system shall support Fahrenheit and Celsius temperature values.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor implementation detail (unit conversion) with negligible impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system shall be compatible with a centralized HVAC (Heating, Ventilation and Air Conditioning) systems: gas, oil, electricity, solar, or a combination of two or more. The system shall adhere to the standards, policies and procedures of the American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers [ASHRAE 2010].
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around hardware interfaces, integration layers, and compliance with ASHRAE standards.

requirement: The DigitalHome programmable humidistat shall allow a user to monitor and control a home’s humidity from any location, using a web ready computer, cell phone, or PDA.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a distributed, networked architecture with secure remote access interfaces and cross‐platform client support.

requirement: A DH user shall be able to read the humidity at a humidistat position.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level sensor interface requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A DH user shall be able to set the humidity level for a humidistat, from 30% to 60%, inclusive a 1% increments.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a low‐level UI/configuration detail (input validation and control logic) that doesn’t drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Up to eight humidistats shall be placed in rooms throughout the home.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the maximum number of sensor endpoints and thus drives the design of the sensor network topology, addressing scheme, and processing capacity.

requirement: A single humidistat shall be placed in an enclosed space (e.g., a room in the house) for which the humidity is to be controlled.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed hardware placement requirement and does not impact the high-level system architecture.

requirement: If a humidistat device allows a user to make a manual temperature setting, the setting shall remain in effect until the end of the planned or default time period, at which time the planned or default setting will be used for the next time period.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local state‐management and scheduling detail that does not drive new architectural components or patterns.

requirement: For each humidistat, up to twenty-four one hour settings per day for every day of the week can be scheduled.
ASR_Explanation: This scheduling detail is a straightforward functional constraint and doesn’t drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: A DigitalHome system shall use wireless signals to communicate, through the master control unit, with the humidistats.
ASR_Explanation: Mandates wireless communication, affecting the system’s network topology, protocol choices, and master-unit–humidistat integration.

requirement: The DigitalHome security system consists of contact sensors and a set security alarms.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic functional feature description and does not impose architectural constraints or affect the system’s overall structure.

requirement: A DigitalHome system shall be able to manage up to fifty door and window contact sensors.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the architecture’s scalability and resource management by defining communication, processing, and storage requirements for up to 50 sensors.

requirement: A DigitalHome system shall be able to activate both light and sound alarms: one sound alarm and one light alarm subsystem, with multiple lights.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates separate sound and light alarm subsystems and a hardware‐abstraction/event‐driven design, directly impacting module decomposition and integration.

requirement: When a security breach occurs and a contact sensor is set OPEN, the alarm system shall be activated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward event-driven functional behavior that does not impose major architectural constraints or drive high-level design decisions.

requirement: The DigitalHome programmable Appliance Manager shall provide for management of a home’s small appliances, including lighting units, by allowing a user to turn them on or off as desired.
ASR_Explanation: It defines core device-control functionality affecting the system’s hardware abstraction, communication protocols, and control module architecture.

requirement: The Appliance Manager shall be able to manage up to one hundred 115 volt, 10 amp power switches.
ASR_Explanation: This capacity constraint drives the system’s scalability, concurrency model, and hardware interface architecture.

requirement: The system shall be able to provide information about the state of a power switch (OFF or ON), indicating whether an appliance connected to the power switch is OFF or ON.
ASR_Explanation: This simple I/O requirement for reporting a binary switch state does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The system shall be able to change the state of a power switch (OFF to ON, or ON to OFF), in turn changing the state of an appliance connected to the power switch.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic functional requirement that doesn’t impose significant architectural constraints or drive major design decisions.

requirement: If a user changes the state of power switch device manually, the device shall remain in that state until the end of the planned or default time period, at which time the planned or default setting will be used for the next time period.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the control system’s state management and scheduling architecture by introducing manual override persistence and timed reversion logic.

requirement: DigitalHome Planner shall provide a user with the capability to direct the system to set various preset home parameters (temperature, humidity, security contacts, and on/off appliance/light status) for certain time periods.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a scheduling service and device-control integration layer to support time-based automation of home parameters.

requirement: DigitalHome provides a monthly planner.
ASR_Explanation: This monthly planner feature is a standard functional requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: For a given month and year, a user shall be able to create or modify a month plan that specifies for each day, for up to four daily time periods, the environmental parameter settings (temperature, humidity, contact sensors and power switches).
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward CRUD/scheduling feature that doesn’t impose major architectural constraints or cross-cutting design trade-offs.

requirement: A user shall be able to override planned parameter values, through the DH website, or if available, through manual switches on household devices.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system architecture by necessitating real-time synchronization and conflict resolution between web interfaces and local device controls, including security and connectivity considerations.

requirement: For a given month and year, in the past two years, DigitalHome shall be able to provide a report on the management and control of the home.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard report-generation feature with a fixed two-year data window that can be handled within the existing data storage and query infrastructure.

requirement: The month report shall contain daily average, maximum (with time) and minimum (with time) values of temperature and humidity for each thermostat and humidistat, respectively.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement for reporting content and does not drive major architectural decisions beyond standard data aggregation and storage.

requirement: The month report shall provide the day and time for which any security breaches occurred, that is, when the security alarms were activated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed reporting requirement that can be fulfilled by existing logging and report-generation components without altering the system architecture.

requirement: The month report shall provide a section that indicates the periods of time when the DH System was not in operation.
ASR_Explanation: This requires integrating or extending the system’s monitoring/logging infrastructure and data storage model to capture, persist, and query DH System downtime intervals for the monthly report.

requirement: Displays of environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, contact sensors and power switches) shall be updated at least every two seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This update-frequency requirement imposes real-time performance and scheduling constraints affecting data acquisition, processing pipelines, and network/resource allocation.

requirement: Sensor (temperature, humidity, contact sensor, power state) shall have a minimum data acquisition rate of 10 Hz.
ASR_Explanation: Imposes real-time performance and throughput constraints affecting hardware selection, data pipelines, and concurrency architecture.

requirement: An environmental sensor or controller device shall have to be within 1000 feet of the master control device, in order to be in communication with the system.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint impacts the system’s network topology and communication subsystem architecture by dictating device placement and hardware range requirements.

requirement: The DigitalHome System must be highly reliable with no more than 1 failure per 10,000 hours of operation.
ASR_Explanation: This reliability requirement drives the need for fault-tolerance, redundancy, and high-availability architectural designs.

requirement: The Digital Home System shall incorporate backup and recovery mechanisms.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of data storage, redundancy, and disaster recovery strategies across the system architecture.

requirement: The DH System will backup all system data (configuration, default parameter settings, planning, and usage data) on a daily basis, with the backup time set by the DH Technician at system set up.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the data storage/backup subsystem, scheduling mechanisms, and recovery architecture.

requirement: If the DH System fails (due to power loss, loss of internet access, or other software or hardware failure), the system recovery mechanism shall restore system data (configuration, default parameter settings, planning, and usage data) from the most recent backup.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s fault-tolerance and availability architecture by mandating backup, storage, and automated recovery mechanisms.

requirement: All DigitalHome operations shall incorporate exception handling so that the system responds to a user with a clear, descriptive message when an error or an exceptional condition occurs.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes a cross-cutting global exception-handling architecture to ensure consistent, descriptive error messages across all system operations.

requirement: Although there are no specific safety requirements, high system reliability is important to insure there are no system failures in carrying out user requests. Such failures might affect the safety of home dwellers (e.g., security breaches, inadequate lighting in dark spaces, inappropriate temperature and humidity for people who are in ill-health, or powering certain appliances when young children are present).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around fault tolerance, redundancy, monitoring, and graceful degradation to ensure high system reliability.

requirement: Upon installation, a DigitalHome user account shall be established. The DigitalHome web system shall provide for authentication and information encryption through a recognized reliable and effective security technology, such as Transport Layer Security.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives foundational security architecture choices for authentication, encryption protocols (e.g., TLS), and key management.

requirement: Log in to an account shall require entry of an account name and a password.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a core authentication mechanism, impacting the security architecture, credential storage, and integration with identity management.

requirement: The development of the DH system shall use methods and techniques such as documentation of requirements, design, and code; use of abstraction, information hiding and module independence in system design; and use of IEEE standards [IEEE830, IEEE1008, IEEE1016, IEEE1028] and the HomeOwner Coding Standard [HO4710].
ASR_Explanation: It enforces high-level design principles (abstraction, information hiding, module independence) and standards compliance, directly shaping system modularity and maintainability.

requirement: Although the product produced under this document will be a “prototype” version, all modules and components of this prototype version shall be designed and implemented in such a manner that it may be incorporated in a fully specified commercial version of the DigitalHome System.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates a production-quality, modular and extensible design for future commercial integration, directly shaping system architecture.

requirement: All system documents (Software Requirements Specification, Architectural Design Specification, Module Detailed Design, Module Source Code, and all Test Plans) shall be up-to-date, use the Homeowner document format [HO2305] and reside in the HomeOwner Document Archive at completion of the project.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation/process requirement and does not directly influence the system’s structural architecture or major design decisions.

requirement: HomeOwner has designated object-oriented development, using UML 2.0, as the preferred method for development of software for HomeOwner products. Exceptions to this rule must be approved by the CIO.
ASR_Explanation: This is a process-level constraint on modeling notation and methodology, not a decision that directly affects the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: The DigitalHome System shall provide users with online documentation about the DigitalHome system installed in their home. The user documentation shall include an FAQ section, a section that explains how DH parameters are set and sensor values are read, and a section that describes how to use the DH Planner.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation/content requirement that does not drive the system’s architectural structure or component design.

requirement: The RLCS software shall send to and receive data from the field device I/O cards through I/O driver software.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the hardware‐driver integration layer, shaping the system’s module boundaries and abstraction architecture.

requirement: All external systems shall retrieve RLCS status from a server outside the RLCS network (outside a firewall). A single data file will include the following data elements: Status, Customers, Access, Signs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the network and security architecture by mandating a DMZ/outside‐firewall deployment and secure data‐exchange mechanism.

requirement: All external systems will access RLCS data through a one-way output only interface via a Department of Transportation ISSC resource (possibly a WAN connection) via a firewall.
ASR_Explanation: It defines critical network topology and security controls (one-way interface, firewall) that directly shape the system’s integration and security architecture.

requirement: A secure remote dial-in interface through a firewall via a dial-up modem shall allow connection into the RLCS network via a remote computer equipped with the application software, and with a user logon authorized for remote access.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives key network and security architecture decisions around firewall configuration, dial-up access points, and remote authentication mechanisms.

requirement: The system shall have a Graphical User Interface (GUI) that allows the operator to view system status, issue commands to change device status, configure the system, export log data, and generate reports.
ASR_Explanation: This defines a major presentation‐layer component, driving decisions on UI frameworks, service interfaces, and data flow between GUI and backend.

requirement: The RLCS software shall have a logon screen for the GUI.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only specifies a GUI element and does not drive key system structure or component interactions.

requirement: The logon screen shall request user name and corresponding password.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI functional detail with no significant impact on system-wide architectural decisions.

requirement: The logon screen shall activate command control for the user if the user requests it and has authorization.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a functional detail within the existing authentication/authorization UI workflow and doesn’t drive new high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Command control shall be from only specified workstations.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement creates a new trust boundary and mandates endpoint-based access control and authentication mechanisms in the system architecture.

requirement: If command control is enabled by another user, and the logging in user is of higher security, the logging in user shall be requested to accept or deny command control.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the security and session-management architecture by adding role-based command-control flows and user-interaction handling.

requirement: If another user is logged in with command control and the new user takes command control, the other user is notified.
ASR_Explanation: Requires real-time notification infrastructure and session management changes to alert users upon control transfer.

requirement: The GUI shall indicate the current date and time, user’s name, and workstation location name. The GUI shall also show other users currently logged in the other units within the RLCS network.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐level requirement (GUI display details) with no major impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall provide an option that allows the system user to issue commands that monitor and control opening and closing events.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level feature affecting GUI design only and does not drive system-wide architectural decisions.

requirement: Based on the user’s security level, the control option shall provide the user with the appropriate level of control.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s authorization layer and access-control architecture to enforce security-level based permissions.

requirement: The control option shall provide the user with the capability to set the operational status of failed devices.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional requirement affecting only the device-management module’s behavior, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: The RLCS software shall display information about active overrides: Which are active, and which devices have no currently active 'rules protection' against erroneous opening/closing.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-level functional requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall provide a display of the I-15 Reversible Lane Control System facility geographic area, including a layout of the mainline I-15, SR-163 freeway area in the same geographic area as the I-15 Reversible Lane. Screen information includes: Facility status, Current direction of traffic flow, Customer eligibility, Current facility boundaries, Device sensor status using dynamic icons for all field device sensors and controllers.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives architectural decisions around real-time data integration, geographic mapping frameworks, and dynamic icon rendering in the UI.

requirement: For alarm status, the GUI shall also issue an audible alarm, and the icon shall be different from the okay status for that device. The visual alarm shall include a change of color for the affected device.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI implementation detail for audible and visual cues and does not drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: There shall be an option to turn off the audible alarm permanently or temporarily for a given period.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized feature affecting alarm management logic and UI controls, without driving major architectural changes.

requirement: The alarm icon shall change to the normal status icon automatically when the alarm condition is removed. Alarm conditions shall be configurable on the screen.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI behavior requirement without major impacts on system architecture.

requirement: When a device status has been overridden, on the screen it shall appear with different color from the normal and alarm status colors.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI styling requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Status information shall continue to display when no user is logged on to the workstation and shall continually be updated every 2 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a system‐level background service with a 2-second update scheduler running at startup even without user sessions, impacting core module and startup architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall provide an option for “Configuration” that is only accessible by the RLCS Software user with System Administrative privileges. It shall display and allow modification of all database tables with the exception of log tables.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces role-based access control in the GUI and data-access layers, impacting security design and system layering.

requirement: When the system administrator modifies the database tables, the GUI shall analyze the data before storing in the database and notify the system administrator of any conflicting or redundant entries.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a real-time validation and notification mechanism that affects the UI–database interaction layer and requires event-driven architectural support.

requirement: The configuration option shall allow a security level and password to be assigned to each defined staff member.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts the identity and access-control architecture by requiring credential management, security-level enforcement, and secure storage of passwords.

requirement: User security levels shall be assigned at the command level, device, mode, workstation, and system function.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a cross-cutting security architecture for fine-grained access control enforcement across commands, devices, modes, workstations, and system functions.

requirement: The configuration option shall also allow user accounts to be changed remotely in the field units.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s security and remote-management architecture by requiring secure, authenticated user account changes in field units.

requirement: When an operator is making changes on the system, the GUI configuration screen shall display to the user which device, controller, or workstation in the RLCS network will be affected by the changes.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a UI-level functional detail with no broad impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The option to configure device rules shall require an additional login password for that option.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement adds a new authentication layer for the device rules feature, impacting the security and access-control architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall allow devices to be added and removed from the display without requiring programming effort.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a plugin-style, dynamically configurable UI framework to support runtime device addition/removal.

requirement: The GUI shall allow the facility map to be modified without requiring programming effort.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a data‐driven, plugin‐style GUI architecture to enable runtime map editing without code changes.

requirement: The system shall display information logs and provide the capability to export the logs in common ASCII text for importing to commercial database, spreadsheet, or reporting programs. The following logs are required: Device Command Log, System Operation Command Log, Problem Work Order Log, Alarm Log, Operator Daily Diary Log, Maintenance Log, System Operation Schedule log.
ASR_Explanation: Requires designing a modular logging and export subsystem with standardized ASCII output formats and interfaces for multiple log types.

requirement: The GUI shall provide the ability to display the status of one device at the detail level, showing all sensor data for that device.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI detail‐view requirement with no broad impact on the system’s high‐level architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall provide the ability to display the current status of a category of devices at the detail level, showing all sensor data for all devices of the same category.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around data aggregation, real-time streaming, and UI/backend scalability to support high-volume sensor data display.

requirement: The user shall be able to retrieve historic reports from the COTS reporting system based on date range and report name.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward COTS reporting retrieval feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system shall display the appropriate emergency contact information in the event of an alarm condition.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI presentation requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall display a separate window requesting confirmation of the command upon receiving a command either from the user at the keyboard, or from a scheduled sequence of commands.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user interface detail that does not impose significant architectural changes.

requirement: The GUI shall allow the operator to acknowledge an alarm and have the option to silence the audible portion of the alarm for a configurable number of seconds or permanently for that device only.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the alarm management architecture by requiring a device-specific suppression service, UI integration with the audio subsystem, and configurable persistence handling.

requirement: The GUI shall include diagnostic screen providing the user with the capability to diagnose failed devices at the sensor level.
ASR_Explanation: This is a GUI-level feature change with no major impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall provide a screen for authorized users to change system mode.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic GUI feature requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall provide a screen for describing system features, functions, database tables, and fields.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI feature specification with minimal impact on high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The RLCS shall monitor all field device sensors, and shall process operator requests for changing field device status.
ASR_Explanation: It defines core real-time monitoring and command processing modules, impacting component decomposition, communication infrastructure, and latency/scalability constraints.

requirement: Any operator or system command, which changes the state of field control devices, must be checked for integrity at multiple levels in the RLCS.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the introduction of cross-cutting, multi-tier integrity checks, impacting module boundaries, communication protocols, and security infrastructure.

requirement: The RLCS software shall monitor, display, and update the database with the status of all system field elements. Any change in device state shall be reported on the screen not later than 2 seconds from the time it occurs.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes strict end-to-end latency and real-time event-driven design constraints on the data acquisition, messaging, storage, and UI update architecture.

requirement: During ‘degraded’ mode, the system shall monitor device sensors at the frequency rate stored in the database to take effect only during ‘degraded’ mode. In general, the system shall monitor the status of all field devices at the frequency specified in the System Control Parameters for that mode.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the design of the monitoring and scheduling subsystem, requiring dynamic database-driven configuration to handle mode-specific polling frequencies.

requirement: The system shall control all system field elements to device sensor level for those device sensors that may be controlled.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for hardware abstraction layers and real-time control and communication modules in the system architecture.

requirement: Each device control command shall check the current status of all closure devices in the system and shall abort if any closure control device status is unknown.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates system-wide device state monitoring and abort logic, impacting the command-processing pipeline and fault-handling architecture.

requirement: Each command (at the device sensor command, device command(macro), or system operational command (super macro) level) shall only be executed when a valid or good status exists for all device sensors. An authorized user shall be able to log in and issue device status requests and control commands from specified computers in the network.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the authentication/authorization services, network access controls, and sensor state‐validation mechanisms.

requirement: The current status for all devices shall be maintained at each controller unit.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the architecture of distributed state management and synchronization by requiring each controller to maintain real-time device statuses.

requirement: Each status received from device sensors shall be checked against alarm conditions for that device sensor and the status will be updated to indicate an alarm.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine sensor‐data processing requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Critical alarms shall be generated when one or more of these conditions are met: closure device changes from a known state to unknown state, closure device changes from legal state to illegal state, control system Integrity verification indicates a verification failure, user logs in any of the field units, command to override a device has been issued anywhere in the system, communication failure within the RLCS network, computer in the RLCS network goes down, power failure at any controller or workstation, cabinet ID is changed, DCUs are in manual mode, Watch Dog timer failure.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a distributed, real-time, fault-tolerant alarm/event processing architecture.

requirement: Warning alarms shall be generated when one or more of these conditions are met: security sensor activation at either the FCU or DCU, Air pressure, Temperature in cabinets, and Voltages are outside the limits of established thresholds as stored in the database.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates real-time, event-driven processing with threshold evaluation against database values, impacting data flows, performance, and monitoring architecture.

requirement: If a critical alarm occurs during opening or closing operation, the system shall present the operator with possible actions that can be taken in order to complete the operation. If overriding a device status is needed in order to proceed, the system shall determine if the operator has high enough security and provide advise on how to proceed.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system architecture by mandating integrated alarm handling, role-based authorization checks, and dynamic UI workflows for operator guidance.

requirement: In order for a command to be processed from any workstation or controller, the MCU field device shall be in the “Auto” mode.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local functional constraint on device state management and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The system operator shall be able to override any device and continue with a system operational command sequence. To ‘override’ a device means to set the status to a normal value even if the device is not functioning in order to continue with a sequence.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates architectural support for an override mechanism, impacting control flow, fault tolerance, and device state management.

requirement: The process of overriding a device status shall not affect the status of any other device.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces strict state isolation, impacting module boundaries, concurrency control, and data consistency mechanisms.

requirement: Each control command that is processed must be validated against the secured safety rules (stored in non-volatile memory) for the command.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a secure validation layer with access to non-volatile storage, impacting module boundaries, storage interfaces, and performance/latency design.

requirement: Commands are only forwarded from superior units to inferior ones. This prevents a lower level unit from changing the state of a device which is controlled by either a higher level unit, or by a peer unit. The TSU is superior to the FCUs which are superior to the DCUs.
ASR_Explanation: Imposes a hierarchical messaging constraint affecting component interaction, communication protocols, and security boundaries across system layers.

requirement: If a status from any device is not received upon request, the system shall automatically request the status again.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the communication layer’s retry and fault-tolerance mechanisms, impacting the overall resilience architecture.

requirement: Failure to receive a valid status after a configurable number of retries shall be considered a device failure.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s retry logic, failure detection, and configurable error-handling architecture.

requirement: When each workstation and control unit (workstation or intelligent controller at the FCU or DCU) comes online, the system shall identify it and all its associated device sensors.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a dynamic device discovery and registration mechanism, impacting the communication and service registry architecture.

requirement: The RLCS software shall initialize each control unit and device sensor as it is identified.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level initialization detail that does not drive or change the high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The RLCS software shall be designed to allow for future changes to the roadway without requiring programming effort. Updating non-volatile memory-based tables shall be sufficient to accommodate future changes to the roadway.
ASR_Explanation: This drives a table-driven, configurable architecture with non-volatile memory interfaces, impacting data model and modularity decisions.

requirement: The system shall generate log files as follows for reports: Device Command Log, System Operation Command Log, Problem Work Order Log, Alarm Log, Daily Diary Log, Special Event Log, System Operation Schedule Log.
ASR_Explanation: This logging requirement introduces cross-cutting concerns for storage, performance, and component design, necessitating a dedicated logging framework and retention strategy.

requirement: The RLCS shall execute stored operational control command sequences based on the current system mode of operation and the schedule for each sequence.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a scheduling and mode‐management subsystem with persistent storage and execution orchestration in the architecture.

requirement: The RLCS shall present scheduled command operations to the operator at the GUI for confirmation prior to executing the command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard GUI confirmation flow that affects only the presentation layer and not the overall system architecture.

requirement: At any point in an opening or closing sequence, the sequence shall be halted if: a device fails to report completion of the current sequence step within the response time window allotted for the step, or the status of a closure device, which was previously opened at the current entrance, changes to ‘unknown’ or ‘closed’, without an operator-initiated command, or the status of a closure device, which was previously closed at the current entrance, changes to ‘unknown’ or ‘open’, without an operator-initiated command.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the real-time monitoring, fault-detection, and safe-shutdown mechanisms, impacting the event-driven and error-handling architecture.

requirement: At any point in an opening sequence, the sequence shall be halted if the status of a closure device for the opposite direction of travel changes to ‘unknown’ or ‘open’.
ASR_Explanation: This safety interlock requirement impacts the control architecture by mandating real-time monitoring and event-driven halting of the opening sequence upon sensor status changes.

requirement: To resume an opening or closing sequence after a halt has occurred, the operator shall be able to issue a command to resume if the offending device status can be corrected within a configurable time period as defined in the database and in non-volatile memory.
ASR_Explanation: Requires persistent state retention and configurable timeout logic, impacting the system’s state-machine and storage architecture.

requirement: The RLCS shall store, process, and retrieve all data necessary to operate the application software as well as generate current and historical reports of system operations, and export system status data to an external server data store. The RLCS shall also store, process, and retrieve all data necessary to secure the system from unauthorized use. A commercial off-the-shelf database management system shall be used for this function.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates core data persistence, processing, reporting, security storage patterns and the use of a COTS DBMS, which drives major architectural decisions.

requirement: The RLCS application software shall update and read database tables to support system operations.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic CRUD requirement with no unique constraints or quality-of-service demands that would drive architectural decisions.

requirement: The RLCS application software shall update and read password and device rule data in encrypted format.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes encryption and key management requirements on the data access layer, impacting storage architecture and security services.

requirement: The Personnel Security Level entity stores information about the five attributes used to restrict access to the RLCS: Command Level, Device, Mode, Workstation, and System Functions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard domain data entity definition with no major impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Command levels are of three types: ‘Status Only’, ‘Control’, and ‘Override’. Users with ‘Status Only’ command level security may not issue any control commands at any level (device, macro, or super macro). ‘Control’ allows a user to issue control commands, and is a higher level of security. ‘Override’ allows a user to temporarily change the status of a device in the database for a configurable period of time in order to allow a command sequence to continue.
ASR_Explanation: Defines critical role-based access control tiers impacting the system’s security and authorization architecture.

requirement: The system shall employ a one-way hash function as an aid to maintaining the integrity of the data and software in the field. The hash value returned by the function shall be at least 128 bits in length. The MD5 algorithm is acceptable for this purpose.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the choice and integration of a cryptographic module (MD5-based hashing) affecting security architecture, performance, and component selection.

requirement: At each time, one or more of the above item types, listed under ‘Control Unit Non-Volatile Memory’, is created or modified, a UTC date/time stamp shall be updated. The update of the time stamp shall be the last step in the process which builds the time stamped code/data section.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes ordering and durability constraints on non-volatile memory updates, affecting the design of the persistence/transaction management architecture.

requirement: The system shall also, for each control unit in the system, produce a table of the returned ‘one-way hash function’ (Message Digest) values, of each of the ‘Control Unit Non-Volatile Memory’ items. The returned ‘Message Digest’ values shall be stored as hexadecimal characters. The appropriate ‘Message Digest’ table shall be maintained in non-volatile memory in each system control unit.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates integration of the hashing module and non-volatile memory layout for control units, impacting component design and storage architecture.

requirement: The system will provide for periodic verification that current, recomputed ‘Message Digest’ values, for each unit in the system, correspond with ‘record’ values computed by the development process. The periodic evaluation shall occur at least once a day. The ‘Message Digest’ value verification results shall be recorded in the system log. A verification failure shall cause an alarm condition for the affected control unit. If the failure occurs in checking the non-volatile memory items, the system shall prevent the affected unit from being used in control sequences.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing scheduled integrity‐check services, logging and alarm mechanisms, and fail‐safe unit disablement that impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system shall provide for ‘Message Digest’ verification requests for a given unit by operator command.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates integrating a cryptographic module for digest generation/verification, impacting system security, performance, and interface architecture.

requirement: For system login purposes, the hash function shall also be used to encrypt user passwords.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the choice and integration of cryptographic algorithms and key management, impacting the system’s security architecture and data storage design.

requirement: To change device command rules on the production system, the System Administrator must upload a new database version after testing the rule changes in the Simulator environment.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the deployment architecture by requiring database versioning, migration scripts, and a staged rollout pipeline for rule changes.

requirement: The system will use data exported from the RLCS database to create and format a variety of reports. A commercial off-the-shelf reporting tool shall be used for this function.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the selection and integration of a COTS reporting tool and the data export pipeline, impacting system architecture.

requirement: The external server data store containing RLCS status for use by external systems shall be updated once per minute.
ASR_Explanation: This data‐refresh frequency can be met with a simple scheduled task and doesn’t drive major architectural choices.

requirement: The field device status information logging to the database shall be 2 seconds, but can be configurable within the database to more than 2 seconds by the user.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data ingestion and storage architecture by imposing configurable logging intervals that affect database performance, throughput, and resource allocation.

requirement: The field device status information display update frequency shall be 2 seconds, but can be configurable within the database to more than 2 seconds by the user.
ASR_Explanation: This performance requirement drives architectural decisions around data polling intervals, communication protocols, and system scalability.

requirement: The RLCS shall receive device status information from devices sensors within 2 seconds of the status information being issued by the device sensor.
ASR_Explanation: This two-second latency requirement imposes strict real-time communication and processing constraints that will drive choices in network protocols, data handling, and system distribution.

requirement: Field devices shall receive respond to commands from the RLCS within 12 seconds of the command confirmation being issued by the operator using a keyboard (or other input device).
