ASR_Explanation: This 12 second response-time constraint imposes performance and communication latency requirements that will drive the system’s network design, scheduling, and resource allocation.

requirement: The RLCS shall support multiple users logged on, up to the limit of the number of users defined in the database.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the system’s concurrency and scalability design, requiring appropriate session management, connection pooling, and resource allocation.

requirement: Not including device and network response times, requests from the GUI for status updates shall not exceed 2 seconds to update the GUI display.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates architectural decisions for asynchronous data handling, caching, and UI rendering optimizations to guarantee sub-2-second updates.

requirement: Not including device and network response time, requests from the GUI for device status changes (control commands) shall not exceed 2 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This 2-second response constraint drives architectural decisions on client-server communication protocols, concurrency models, and performance optimization.

requirement: The facility map on the screen shall refresh every 2 seconds but can be configurable within the database to more than 2 seconds by the user.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the real-time data refresh mechanism, performance/scalability considerations, and configuration management architecture.

requirement: The RLCS notification to the operator workstation of any critical alarms shall occur within 2 seconds of alarm detection, and shall occur whether or not an operator is logged on to the system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a real-time notification subsystem with persistent connections and low-latency messaging infrastructure.

requirement: The field units (controllers) shall continually monitor device status, controller status and the control system integrity and send the status to the central computer in the TMC every 2 seconds or less.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces strict real-time latency and throughput constraints that will drive the selection of communication protocols, scheduling mechanisms, and system scalability design.

requirement: The RLCS shall detect alarm conditions within 2 seconds of occurrence.
ASR_Explanation: This performance constraint imposes low-latency, real-time processing requirements that will drive the design of the event-detection and system architecture.

requirement: At a minimum of every 60 seconds, the system shall check the current date and time against a list of scheduled events for the current mode to determine if any event should be executed.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a periodic scheduling mechanism that impacts the design of the system’s timing infrastructure and resource management.

requirement: The RLCS shall support at a minimum the 4 daily ‘normal’ mode open and close scheduled operations plus at least the same number of ‘emergency’ and ‘maintenance’ mode scheduled events.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design and capacity requirements of the scheduling/event‐handling subsystem to meet concurrency and throughput needs.

requirement: The database retrieval and update response time shall not impact any other performance requirements such as the GUI response time or monitoring and control responses.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates architectural strategies for performance isolation and resource allocation to prevent database operations from degrading GUI or control response times.

requirement: The operator shall be able to store and retrieve previously created report results from the RLCS for a minimum period of 60 days, but configurable for up to one year.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts storage architecture and data lifecycle management, requiring scalable retention, archival, and retrieval mechanisms.

requirement: The raw data used to create reports shall be kept in the RLCS for a minimum period of 13 months prior to backing up to tapes or other secondary storage media.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts storage capacity planning and data lifecycle/backup architecture by mandating a 13-month retention period before secondary archiving.

requirement: Report processing shall not impact any other performance requirements such as the GUI response time or monitoring and control responses.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates architectural mechanisms for resource isolation and priority scheduling to ensure report processing doesn’t degrade GUI or control response times.

requirement: Report response time shall be determined by the database resources allocated to the reporting function. Depending on user needs for fast report response times (such as for ad hoc reporting), database extracts may be created for reporting purposes only.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around database provisioning and ETL processes for separate reporting infrastructure to meet performance and scalability targets.

requirement: RLCS system components will communicate via the private communications networks established by Department of Transportation District 11 prior to the development of the RLCS. Any operator or system command, which changes the state of field control devices, must be checked for integrity at multiple levels in the system. Valid checksum algorithms must be employed to check the integrity of messages between units.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement influences the communication architecture and security layers by mandating multi‐level integrity checks and specific checksum algorithms across the network.

requirement: The RLCS must support the following data transfer performance goals: The number of kbits transmitted for a single polling event of all device sensors is estimated to be: 205 x 200 x 8 x 1/1000= 328 kbits. The transmission rate required to transfer this data in 2 seconds is: 328 kbits/2 sec. = 164 kbits/s. At a 30 second polling interval, the transmission rate drops to 10.9 kbits/s.
ASR_Explanation: This throughput requirement dictates network technology selection, communication protocols, and data buffering strategies, impacting overall system architecture.

requirement: The data processing and security, and reporting functions of the RLCS application software shall be implemented with commercial off-the-shelf software.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes COTS component selection and integration constraints that shape the system’s modular design, extensibility, and vendor lock-in considerations.

requirement: The RLCS shall incorporate a database to store, process, and retrieve all data necessary to secure the system from unauthorized use. The processing code at the FCU and DCU controllers shall be resident in non-volatile memory.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around secure database integration and the non‐volatile memory layout for controller code.

requirement: The MD5 algorithm shall be used to secure application data and software in the controllers and the application server.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying MD5 as the security algorithm directly impacts data protection design, component integration, and overall security architecture.

requirement: The RLCS must demonstrate the ability to function continuously without needing to be reset or rebooted due to an RLCS error for at least 30 consecutive days.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes stringent availability and fault-tolerance requirements affecting system reliability architecture, error handling, and resource management.

requirement: Valid checksum algorithms must be employed to check the integrity of messages between units.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the design of communication protocols and security modules by requiring integration of specific checksum algorithms for message integrity.

requirement: The RLCS must be built with redundant capabilities to ensure uninterrupted operation. The RLCS will function in a degraded mode as specified.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts system availability, requiring redundant components, failover mechanisms, and degraded-mode support in the architecture.

requirement: The current configuration information for each processing unit in the field shall be duplicated in a database at the TMC level.
ASR_Explanation: Duplication of field unit configurations to a TMC-level database drives the design of data replication, synchronization strategies, and underlying network and storage architecture.

requirement: The RLCS must be available 24/7, 365 days per year. If there is a failure, recovery time must be no greater than 10 minutes, and total yearly uptime must be at least 99.99% (.01% downtime, or approximately 50 minutes per year).
ASR_Explanation: Requires high-availability architecture with redundancy, automated failover, and rapid recovery mechanisms.

requirement: The RLCS shall support remote system administration and maintenance of the system.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of secure network interfaces, authentication modules, and remote management components in the system architecture.

requirement: The RLCS shall utilize an open architecture that is modular and scaleable. The system will be scaled up to a maximum of two additional DCU controllers, each with the number of devices currently at DCU Location 1, plus four additional CMS, and twenty additional contact closures.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It mandates open, modular, and scalable architecture with capacity planning for additional controllers and devices, driving key system-level design decisions.

requirement: Wherever possible open systems standards for hardware, software, software development tools, and communications shall be used.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains technology and tool choices to open standards, impacting interface design, interoperability, and vendor independence in the system architecture.

requirement: The FSW shall process science data from the camera and relay it to the Spacecraft Control Unit (SCU) in the form of CCSDS Source Packets.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the data processing pipeline, CCSDS packetization, and the interface between FSW and SCU.

requirement: The FSW shall receive commands from the SCU that establish the current instrument state and camera mode.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a critical interface and messaging protocol between FSW and SCU, impacting the system’s component architecture and communication design.

requirement: The FSW shall transmit detailed housekeeping data to the SCU in the form of CCSDS Source Packets.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a CCSDS packetization layer and telemetry interface, impacting the design of data handling and communication modules.

requirement: The FSW shall receive a time message from the SCU and synchronize the XCP local copy of the spacecraft clock.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s communication and time-synchronization architecture by mandating a real-time clock update mechanism from the SCU.

requirement: The FSW shall control the heaters on the telescope tube and on the thermal baffles.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine hardware-control function and does not impose any high-level architectural patterns or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The FSW shall read the Telescope Alignment Monitor (TAM).
ASR_Explanation: It defines the hardware-software interface and real-time data acquisition pipeline for the TAM, impacting module boundaries and communication architecture.

requirement: The FSW shall maintain primary and alternate FSW configurations in EEPROM.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of non-volatile memory architecture with dual-bank (primary/alternate) configuration storage and failover handling.

requirement: The primary FSW configuration in EEPROM shall be software locked to be read-only.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the firmware storage architecture by mandating secure, read-only locking of EEPROM-held configuration, affecting update and boot mechanisms.

requirement: The XCP bootstrap software shall autonomously switch to the alternate FSW configuration in the event the primary configuration fails to boot.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the bootloader’s redundancy and failover architecture by mandating autonomous detection of boot failure and dynamic configuration switching.

requirement: The FSW shall contain an EEPROM-resident file system on which an object file containing a software patch can be loaded.
ASR_Explanation: Defines EEPROM-based file system requirements for patch loading, impacting storage subsystem design, memory mapping, and software update mechanisms.

requirement: The FSW shall allow a complete software build to be loaded to the alternate FSW location in EEPROM and the XCP commanded to boot the alternate configuration.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the bootloader design, EEPROM memory partitioning, and update/fallback strategy impacting system startup architecture.

requirement: The EEPROM section containing the bootstrap shall be software write locked prior to flight and include a checksum that is compared against the stored data as part of the Built-In Tests (BIT) executed on system startup.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the secure‐boot and firmware initialization architecture by mandating EEPROM write‐lock mechanisms and checksum‐based BIT integration.

requirement: The DRAM shall include Error Detecting and Correcting (EDAC) memory capable of detecting and correcting single bit errors and detecting double bit errors.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the memory subsystem architecture by mandating ECC/EDAC hardware, affecting DRAM module selection, data paths, latency, and overall system reliability.

requirement: Both single and double bit EDAC events shall cause an interrupt, and the software shall log information about the error including the affected address and increment the EDAC error count.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the interrupt handling and error-logging architecture at the hardware/software boundary, affecting driver and system design.

requirement: Multiple bit errors (two or more) detected by the EDAC shall cause the software to reset the processor by discontinuing strobing of the watchdog timer, which causes the watchdog timer to reset the board when it times out.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts system fault-tolerance and reset-control architecture by specifying EDAC-driven watchdog reset behavior.

requirement: A low priority software task called the Memory Scrubber shall run when no other tasks are executing, stepping through DRAM and reading each location.
ASR_Explanation: This describes a simple idle‐time background task that doesn’t alter core architectural structures or interfaces.

requirement: When a word is read by the Memory Scrubber that contains a single-bit error, this routine shall rewrite it to correct the problem, and the problem shall be logged to EEPROM through the interrupt mechanism.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the memory subsystem and logging mechanism, requiring interrupt-based error correction and EEPROM integration.

requirement: Multiple bit errors uncovered by the Memory Scrubber shall result in a reset, even if the memory was not currently in use for processing by the FSW.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the fault-tolerance architecture for memory error handling and system reset management.

requirement: Errors shall be reported in XCP housekeeping telemetry.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the system’s telemetry/logging architecture by requiring integration of error reporting into XCP housekeeping data flows, impacting component interfaces and performance design.

requirement: If an unrecoverable error occurs (such as an uncorrectable memory error), the XCP shall reboot via watchdog timer.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the system’s fault-tolerance architecture by mandating integration of watchdog-based reboot logic for unrecoverable errors.

requirement: Detectable exceptions that cause a watchdog reboot shall be recorded to EEPROM.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the fault-management architecture by mandating a persistent logging mechanism to EEPROM, affecting memory driver design and reboot sequencing.

requirement: A heartbeat message from XRT to the SCU shall serve to indicate “aliveness” of the XRT Instrument to the Spacecraft.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a periodic liveness-check protocol requiring dedicated timing, messaging middleware, and fault-detection mechanisms in the system architecture.

requirement: The FSW design shall structure its housekeeping (HK) packets such that the 230-byte constraint is not violated.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains packet serialization and data structure design, impacting communication module interfaces and memory allocation.

requirement: The FSW shall not compress any packet that must be recognized and processed by ITOS.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the design of the data‐processing and communication pipeline, specifically how the FSW interfaces with ITOS and manages packet handling.

requirement: Any packet that must be processed by ITOS shall not be segmented.
ASR_Explanation: This no-segmentation constraint impacts the network I/O architecture, requiring specific packet handling, buffer sizing, and protocol-layer design.

requirement: The FSW design shall avoid any time-consuming setup or configuration as part of its nominal operation.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system startup architecture by driving choices for initialization strategy, pre-configuration, and module coupling to meet performance constraints.

requirement: The FSW shall provide for small dump packets if going down the RT link, and larger ones if going to the Solid State Recorder (SSR).
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of the data‐handling subsystem by requiring distinct packetization and routing logic for RT link versus SSR storage.

requirement: The FSW shall be capable of receiving telecommands as listed in Appendix C, and execute the corresponding actions and parameter changes.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the telecommand handling subsystem, defining communication protocols, component interfaces, and data flow for command execution.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to select and upload bias algorithms for bias calculation via command.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for a modular, dynamically loadable algorithm framework with uplink command handling and security validation.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to select and upload mean row correction algorithms via command.
ASR_Explanation: This requires a runtime plugin framework for dynamic algorithm uploads and selection, impacting module interfaces, command handling, and memory management.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the CCD DAC values via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local functional detail using existing command and hardware‐interface layers without requiring new architectural elements.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable baseline correction via command.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates dynamic configuration of the onboard processing pipeline via command, affecting module interfaces and state management.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable mean row correction via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor configurable feature requiring only a command flag and conditional processing change, with no major architectural impact.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the upper and lower limits for housekeeping parameters via command.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a dynamic parameter management service impacting the command-processing and telemetry-thresholding architecture.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the housekeeping period via command.
ASR_Explanation: This only affects the implementation of the housekeeping scheduler’s parameter handling via existing command infrastructure, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable command echo via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor toggle in the command-processing component and does not alter the system’s overall architectural structure or interfaces.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable stripchart mode via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine command‐handling enhancement that can be accommodated within the existing FSW command/telemetry framework without altering the system’s high‐level structure.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to upload and execute sequencer programs via command.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the FSW architecture by requiring dynamic code loading/execution infrastructure with associated security, memory management, and command interface design.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to upload and store memory via command.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the communication protocol and onboard memory management architecture for reliable uplink and storage.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to download memory via command.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the architecture of the command/telemetry interface and memory management within the flight software.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to reset command counters via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a small-scale functional detail (a specific command interface) and does not drive major architectural structure.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the state to AUTO, MAN, or RED via command.
ASR_Explanation: It defines new state transitions and ground command interfaces, impacting the core state‐management and command‐processing architecture.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to open or stop opening the camera door via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine command‐handling function that fits within the existing FSW command/control architecture without requiring new structural changes.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to open or stop opening the camera pressure relief valve via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional interface requirement with minimal impact on high-level system structure.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set configuration information for SAA handling via command.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the command processing architecture by adding a dynamic configuration interface, parameter storage, and validation subsystem.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable the Telescope Alignment Monitor (TAM) and set its parameters via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard command interface requirement that fits within the existing telemetry/command framework without driving new architectural structure.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable the Thermo-Electric Cooler (TEC) and set its parameters via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional requirement for a command interface and device driver that doesn’t drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable the tube and baffle heaters and set their parameters via command.
ASR_Explanation: This simply adds specific heater enable/disable commands into the existing ground command interface and driver layer without altering the overall architectural structure.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable data compression via command.
ASR_Explanation: It adds a runtime-configurable compression feature in the telemetry pipeline, impacting performance, interfaces, and module design.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable time synchronization with the spacecraft via command.
ASR_Explanation: This merely adds a command to toggle an existing time‐synchronization service and does not drive major architectural changes.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to turn on or off the CCD test LED via command.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional detail handled within the existing command‐processing and device‐driver framework with no new architectural impact.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the buffer interrupt row count via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to execute a command at the VxWorks shell via command.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates integrating a VxWorks shell interface into the command/control subsystem, impacting module partitioning, communication protocols, and security.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to upload sequencer program lookup tables via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to upload and set bad pixel and bad row tables via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the centroid algorithm parameters and thresholds via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set event thresholds and upper level discriminators via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set timing mode parameters and thresholds via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to force the TAM to be read immediately via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the period for reading the TAM via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the TAM's boresight calibration offsets via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to close, open, or lock the sun shutter via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to abort the current mode and reset the sequencer and FIFO via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to execute Ramp DACs mode for I&T purposes only via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the TEC to automatic cooling or heating mode, manual cooling or heating mode, or turn it off via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the telescope tube heater temperature set point via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the thermal baffle control heater's control parameters via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable TAM correction via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to enable or disable the DACs via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to calculate and dump mean row correction rows via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to set the running mean length for the FPC via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to clear or set the raw image flag via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to dump bad pixel and bad row tables via command.

requirement: The FSW shall allow the ground to select the FPC's active bias map via command.

requirement: Track the movement of lapsed quantities in an area, including: which permit and well obtained the lapsed quantities, where the lapsed quantities are located, why the quantities are lapsed (i.e. expired, cancelled, retired), what MFL zone the lapsed quantities are in.
ASR_Explanation: Requires new geospatial data handling and domain‐model extensions for tracking lapsed quantities, impacting data storage and service integration.

requirement: Track the relocation of active water use within the SWUCA.
ASR_Explanation: This requires geospatial-temporal data modeling and real-time tracking infrastructure, impacting data storage, integration, and scalability decisions.

requirement: Report on the history of irrigation water conserving credits, including the balance, how much earned, and how much used, and drought quantities. Know who uses them and who has accumulated them, and be able to report on an individual basis, by a geographic area, or aggregate the data.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard reporting feature and does not impose cross-cutting architectural constraints beyond typical data aggregation and filtering capabilities.

requirement: Track and report on the history of permitted data, such as use type, irrigated acres, and pumpage (one use would be to know whether expired permits have had quantities reallocated).
ASR_Explanation: Requires designing temporal data storage and audit-trail mechanisms for permit history, impacting database schema and reporting architecture.

requirement: Track and measure alternative source projects: surface water or stormwater projects, reclaimed water (reuse water), augmented surface water (reservoirs, harvesting of high flows), ASR (Aquifer storage recovery), desalination, conservation.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: Tracking and measuring multiple water-source projects dictates the data model, integration interfaces, and analytics modules, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: View the spatial impact of a person's application with other active applications on-line so the applicant or evaluator has a visual of who else is available to compete for quantities with. Also include application and permit data on-line, either with scanned documents or access to database. The historical data should also be available, but viewing restrictions for certain legal documents has to be followed. Be able to query by geographic area or permit number.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the overall architecture by requiring GIS integration, document management, secure role-based access, and spatial/query indexing decisions.

requirement: Create heat map indicating who was over-pumping, who was not over-pumping, or who was using their water credits.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data visualization feature with no major impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Heat map for water quality. Add the District WUPnet sentinel wells on this map - those are the District wells that have been in existence the longest time and we have the best data with.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/data‐mapping enhancement that doesn’t introduce new architectural concerns.

requirement: Report on the permits that have been relocated by a permit holder.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard reporting feature that can be implemented within existing data access and reporting components without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: Track the impact that water use has to the set MFL levels. In particular, compare the affects of the new or modified use to the actual flow and level of the water body or aquifer to make sure the actual level will not go below the MFL level. Types of things to check are: new applications, renewals, or modifications; relocated uses; change of use type; lapsed quantities; retired permits.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for real-time data integration, hydrological modeling and rule‐based threshold checks, impacting core system architecture.

requirement: Track net benefit changes. Some things involved are: relocated permits, permits with a change of use, old and new locations, lapsed quantities, reclaimed water, projects, amount of net benefit gained or lost from the change, new permits that came from an older permit due to a new benefit gain.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the core data model and event‐driven processing pipeline to support complex, longitudinal permit benefit tracking.

requirement: Report the net benefit amount in MGD for: change of permitted location or use, land use change, use of lapsed quantities, water withdrawal credit (replacement, reclaimed water).
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-specific reporting feature that does not impose new architectural constraints or alter the system’s structural design.

requirement: Mark those permits that have a land use change so the quantities can be relocated to the public supply utility.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized business‐rule filtering requirement without implications for the overall system architecture.

requirement: Create layer that includes 'cones of depression' for the MFL information, which will indicate the area of influence to an MFL.
ASR_Explanation: Adds a new spatial data layer requiring updates to the GIS architecture, data model, and spatial indexing for performance.

requirement: Report on any water use amounts sold or given from one permit to another permit, especially reclaimed water.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized reporting feature affecting data queries and schema in the reporting module but not the overall system architecture.

requirement: For mitigation of MFL impacts, report on the improvement percentage amount, where it was located, the cost, how the decrease was obtained and what impacts it had.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed reporting requirement that only affects the report‐generation component and does not drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: Report on how much water was made available through the District's water resource development projects. Elements of the report should be: source of benefit (aquifer or surface water), the locations of the alternative sources, what permits are using the quantities generated by the projects, and what project they are getting the water from.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward reporting feature that doesn’t impose significant constraints on system architecture beyond normal data retrieval and presentation.

requirement: Report on the permit duration (To track expiration dates for those looking for someone to compete with).
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard reporting feature for tracking data expiry and does not impose major architectural constraints.

requirement: Should allow comparison to adjacent districts GIS layers to permit GIS layer to access adjacent data.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the spatial data model and GIS service interfaces by introducing adjacency queries, affecting system modularization and performance.

requirement: Report compliance on a permit (not wells). Crop reports on permits (not wells).
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward reporting feature with limited impact on high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Report on competing applications (including ones that are in modification or expiring).
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for new data aggregation and reporting services, impacting the system’s data architecture, integration points, and analytics infrastructure.

requirement: Supply the Regulatory requirements of MFL data: what water levels are in relationship to MFL so you know who has to mitigate or how much excess flow there is. Need the historic and current levels so applicant knows how much to mitigate or what is available.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of data ingestion, storage, and processing pipelines for historic and real-time MFL levels to support regulatory compliance and mitigation calculations.

requirement: Be able to determine the availability of alternative use supplies, such as reclaimed water and desalination.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for external data integration and resource‐availability modeling, affecting the system’s integration and data management architecture.

requirement: Report the Reservation from use amounts allocated to certain participants.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard reporting feature that can be implemented within existing data access and UI layers without altering the system architecture.

requirement: Track ownership of wells or permits over the life of the withdrawal point so the relocated quantities can be traced from owner to owner. Quantities that are relocated will have certain restrictions placed on them, as well as any quantities that remain on the original permit. For permits that have multiple withdrawal points, we need the ability to track ownership of specific withdrawal points over time.
ASR_Explanation: Requires temporal data modeling and audit/event‐sourcing capabilities to track ownership and restrictions over time across multiple withdrawal points.

requirement: Be able to look at permitted quantities, actual quantities (pumpage), and the resources it uses (Water Management DB).
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data‐retrieval feature against an existing DB with minimal impact on the overall architecture.

requirement: Ability to track requested and permitted quantities.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine business logic requirement affecting data modeling and storage rather than core architectural structure.

requirement: Identify when a standby permit is reactivated due to the loss of alternative quantities.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional trigger that can be handled within existing modules and doesn’t drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Have a heat map that shows permit information for specific time periods. You should be able to input a geographic area, select the time period(s) you want, and select one of two views: permits issued or active permits in that time period, and produce the map show the locations. On a high-level view, you need to be able to see the locations with the predominate use type reflected on the map itself, and include a graph or chart showing the total number of permits, predominate use shown by %, and the time period involved. If you click on it, bring up the permit details, such as name, permit number, issue date, expire date, use type, wuca, and average quantities.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement demands geospatial data services and real-time spatial/time-based filtering which will dictate the choice of spatial database, caching, and mapping architecture.

requirement: Automate some of the requests that Jim Whalen receives. The majority of request are related to permitted and pumpage quantities, both at the permit level and well level. They are usually sorted temporally or geographically.
ASR_Explanation: This requires adding an automation and scheduling layer for dynamic queries on permit and pumpage data, impacting the system’s service orchestration and data-processing architecture.

requirement: Ability for public utilities and internal staff to find existing permits in or near their service area so they can spatially see what water is available, or may become available, when they plan for their future growth and water resources. They will need to view the use type (interested mainly in agricultural and mining), owned acres, and the current permittee's contact information.
ASR_Explanation: This FR impacts the architecture by requiring geo-spatial data storage and indexing, spatial query performance, and mapping/GIS integration.

requirement: Analyze permits using a soils type GIS layer. Have a second soils layer based on the soil types indicated on the permittees compliance to conditions.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces GIS integration with multi-layer spatial data management and map-rendering requirements, impacting data services and UI architecture.

requirement: Ability to have printable and customizable maps.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the map rendering architecture by requiring a customization API and a print/export pipeline (e.g., vector-to-PDF/raster conversion and styling engine).

requirement: Identify when the District or another governmental agency purchases land, a link should be available to show if there is a permit on that area so it can be retired.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐linking feature within the existing domain model and does not impose new architectural constraints.

requirement: Need to know how water use changes when land use changes.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the inclusion of geospatial modeling and dynamic analytics capabilities, shaping data storage and processing architecture.

requirement: Have an easy way to query data by various fields: issue date, expiration date, use type or other commodity, predominate use, pumpage reports, permitted quantities, permit status (active/delete/app/permit).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data model, indexing strategy, and query API design to support dynamic, performant filtering across multiple fields.

requirement: Ability to pull a well package that provides a view of water use at a particular time that is then plugged into models used to generate water use amounts. The data would include wells, land elevation, aquifer, and total and cased depths.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data‐retrieval feature with no significant impact on core system architecture.

requirement: The ability to associate permitted quantities, pumpage quantities, and use code data at the well level.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data‐modeling detail (schema/association at the well entity) and does not drive major structural or architectural changes.

requirement: Track changes in land use and how it affects water availability.
ASR_Explanation: Requires geospatial and time-series data handling that impacts database design and integration with external hydrological models.

requirement: Need historical data for Water Use Permits including their spatial representations (polygons).
ASR_Explanation: Requires temporal geospatial data support, affecting database choice, schema design, and spatial indexing.

requirement: Must be able to track trends in land use and water use changes. These include several means by which a proposed new withdrawal that impacts an MFL waterbody can achieve a 'Net Benefit,' including the provisions for relocated, lapsed, and water withdrawal credits.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data model and analytic service architecture for trend tracking and net-benefit credit management.

requirement: Ability to track water use over time and negotiate reasonable new water use based on water use pumpage trends.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives design of time-series data storage, trend-analysis analytics, and negotiation workflow integration, impacting data architecture and scalability.

requirement: Track by withdrawal source – what aquifer or USGS water body are general and Individual permits pumping from.
ASR_Explanation: This feature requires extending the domain model and database schema and integrating with external USGS/aquifer data sources, impacting data storage and service interfaces.

requirement: Have map be able to show quantity of pumpage. Hover over or click a point in the map and see the average rolling 12, peak month, and max month compared to the pumpage of the well. Also get the cumulative quantities for an area you enter manually.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/data‐visualization enhancement using existing mapping and data services without requiring major architectural changes.

requirement: Provide the ability for applicants or District staff to compare the percentage a permit is over-pumping, based on use type, against the regional average for the same use type. The region would be the area that fell within the buffer distance entered, and the time of interest would also be entered.
ASR_Explanation: It requires spatial-temporal data querying and dynamic statistical aggregation, impacting data storage/indexing and performance optimization.

requirement: Need to know what aquifer is being pumped from. There are permitting constraints in ETB MIA that no additional water from the Floridan Aquifer can be pumped, but it is allowed from Intermediate aquifer.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the core data model and business logic by enforcing aquifer-specific source tracking and permit constraints.

requirement: Select water use data temporally or with geographic overlays, which can be from another layer or graphics on the page. Also be able to place it in an Excel spreadsheet.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data filtering and export feature that can be handled within existing GIS and reporting modules without impacting the overall architecture.

requirement: Select water use based on predominant use, use types, and regions they fall in. The predominant use would be agricultural, industrial, mining & dewatering, public supply, and recreational. Each predominate use is further broken down into more detailed categories, such as melons or citrus for the agricultural predominate use. This needs historical data for trend analysis.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the data storage, retrieval, and analytics architecture by requiring scalable classification models and historical trend analysis.

requirement: Generate monthly reports for Board Packet that shows the difference in quantities permitted every month for specified geographic areas (i.e., counties, MIA, etc.).
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine batch reporting feature that can be implemented within existing data processing and reporting frameworks.

requirement: Establish relationships between inputs (i.e. rainfall) and pumpage data.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward analytical function that can be implemented within existing data processing components without altering the system’s architecture.

requirement: Show the intensity of water use in a geographic area on a color-coded map.
ASR_Explanation: This affects architecture by requiring integration of a GIS mapping engine and data processing pipelines to render color‐coded geospatial overlays efficiently.

requirement: Identify different types of water use, such as groundwater, surface water, and re-use.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic classification function without constraints or quality attributes that would drive architectural decisions.

requirement: Know how much water has been permitted and how much has been used based on land use over time.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data architecture by necessitating time-series storage, GIS integration, and analytics pipelines for water permitting and usage tracking.

requirement: Support extraction or reporting of data for ad-hoc geographic areas by using spatial queries such as graphics or polygon buffering. The ability to aggregate the data must be there and any type of polygon should be able to be used.
ASR_Explanation: Requires a spatial data management layer with indexing and geometry processing to support arbitrary-polygon queries and aggregation at scale.

requirement: Track aquifer level changes, lake level changes, rainfall level changes, and stream flow changes by area over time (i.e. monthly).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a scalable time-series data storage and ingestion architecture, influencing database selection, schema design, and indexing strategies.

requirement: Create layer that shows the Developments of Regional Impacts (DRI) and their status. This layer will be plugged into the new model for population projections and water supply demand projections.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s layered architecture by introducing a new data visualization and integration component for population and water demand models.

requirement: Identify what lakes are stressed, when they became stressed, and if/when they came off stressed list.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional query requirement for tracking status changes over time and doesn’t drive any major architectural decisions.

requirement: Track pumpage oddities. Two examples are: A. Permittees that have consistent pumpage readings even when some of the data is missing. Use this information to predict whether they are in compliance in spite of the missing data. B. Permittees that enter zero on their pumpage reports.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates a data ingestion and analytics pipeline with anomaly detection and predictive modeling capabilities, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: Track permittees not submitting conditional/pumpage reports, and generate a map showing their locations.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces GIS/map service integration and report-tracking pipelines, impacting data storage, service orchestration, and UI architecture.

requirement: Obtain report of those out of compliance of permit conditions by over-pumping their allowable quantities. Show how much over pumping is done, and have option to select by time period or by area. Need to break down to a permit-by-permit basis, but also show the whole thing on a map.
ASR_Explanation: This requires integrating geospatial mapping, time-series filtering and high-performance reporting which impacts data storage, query engines and the UI mapping component.

requirement: Need a protocol to assess how the actual flow and levels (AFL) compare to the established minimum flows and levels (MFL), including general trending information and whether waterbody-specific criteria are being met.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a real-time data ingestion and analytics pipeline with configurable rule evaluation for thresholds and trending, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: Use system to publish a chart or graph that compares the MFL to a running average of AFL's. This should reflect how the AFL's are moving along the MFL line and indicate how different resources impact the MFL. The resources to include are pumpage, new projects, and rainfall.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around data aggregation pipelines, visualization framework selection, and API design for resource-impact analytics.

requirement: Ability to add estimated quantities for unmetered permits, which is found in the Water Estimates Database.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD enhancement (adding fields to the Water Estimates Database) with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Provide access to population data in system that are aggregated to appropriate geographic areas (services areas, counties, etc.).
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of geospatial data models, storage/indexing strategies, and service interfaces to support aggregated population queries by area.

requirement: The ability to access crop report information spatially and temporally.
ASR_Explanation: Requires spatio-temporal data modeling and indexing, impacting database design and query infrastructure.

requirement: Link between Water Use Permits (WUP), Environmental Resource Permits (ERP), and Well Construction Permits. One use would be as a means of notifying appropriate District staff when pertinent land use changes.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces cross-module integration and event-driven notifications requiring messaging infrastructure and data model adjustments.

requirement: Calculate water quality trends by geographic area.
ASR_Explanation: It requires geospatial and time-series data storage and processing pipelines, driving choices in database, indexing, and analytics architecture.

requirement: Compare the population reported to the Bureau of Economic and Business Research (BEBR) report.
ASR_Explanation: This simple data comparison requirement has minimal architectural impact, fitting within standard data retrieval and processing layers.

requirement: Ability to provide external customers a means to query and view application information and locations by entering spatial, temporal, or data specific information. Current applications satisfying the criteria would be viewed immediately; future applications would be e-mailed.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for spatial-temporal indexing and hybrid sync/async delivery (real-time view vs. email) impacting data storage, query services, and integration architecture.

requirement: Create a consistent grid to analyze data through time, including population, water use, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a spatio‐temporal data model, storage/indexing strategy, and ETL pipeline for consistent grid-based analysis.

requirement: Use GIS layers to analyze how salt-water intrusion is affecting water quality, etc. Layer would need to be updated either quarterly or annually.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a geospatial data management architecture with periodic ETL pipelines and scalable GIS storage/analysis components.

requirement: Develop a link between pumpage, water quality, and water levels (both of MFL's and other water bodies). The permittee has to stop pumping when certain levels are reached: augment lake when water levels are too high; pump water when chlorides concentrations too high; pump more out of well to pond & from pond to golf course; use more ground water when allowed to, when also q's for surface water.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for real-time sensor integration, threshold-based control logic, and orchestration across pumping and water-quality systems.

requirement: Need tool to be able to estimate water use (now being done in SAS). To do this estimate, you need to be able to query the water use data with various variables, such as time period, use types, withdrawal type (groundwater or surfacewater), and geographic areas. Once the initial query is complete, you need to be able to refine the resulting data set.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data‐query and filtering feature that doesn’t drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: Capture crop report data and link it with the corresponding use quantities, so you can observe changing crop patterns, see where land has shifted out of crops, and track water use per planted acre per crop to see patterns in water use by geography.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the data architecture by requiring a scalable geospatial data model and ETL/integration pipeline to link crop reports with water-use metrics.

requirement: Report compliance on a permit (not wells). Crop reports on permits (not wells) (I think this was a requirement to report this data some how at a well level).
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor report-filtering change that won’t require significant architectural modifications.

requirement: Report the Reservation from use amounts associated with some surface water.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine reporting feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Report the per capita daily water figures for the permitted amount, reported amount and adjusted number for per utility.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional reporting requirement that does not drive any core architectural decisions.

requirement: Provide the ability to obtain data necessary to determine and access the population estimates for an area or on a permit-by-permit basis. Needs to accommodate how it is determined now and how it will be determined under the SWUCA 2 rules.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a flexible data model and rule‐driven service layer to support both current and SWUCA 2 population estimation methods.

requirement: Access well construction data for domestic wells using locational data, such as GPS or section, township, and range.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for geospatial data storage, indexing, and query capabilities in the architecture.

requirement: The external users need to be able to click on an area and obtain data for the surrounding area, such as lapsed quantities, historical use, and adjacent permits. Need to support decision-making on the applicant's part, especially when their request does not meet the MFL requirements.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of spatial data services and APIs, impacting database architecture, GIS integration, and performance considerations.

requirement: Make the following property appraiser data available so you can improve the ability to project service area populations in inter-Census years in several ways: provide a more discreet and up-to-date count of housing units in a utility service area; help determine when a DRI is completed; match commercial properties by the connection data provided by utilities; use lot sizes, where available, to determine its role in household water use compared to other variables in research projects.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives architectural decisions around data ingestion, integration, and storage schemas to support timely analytics on housing units, development projects, and lot-level metrics.

requirement: Need quick access to currently published information to base decisions on.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes low-latency and data-freshness constraints that drive architectural choices for caching, replication, and real-time update mechanisms.

requirement: Replicate the DB2 data daily in Oracle.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the data integration architecture by requiring a replication strategy, tooling, scheduling, and consistency mechanisms between DB2 and Oracle.

requirement: Support consistency in decision-making among various users.
ASR_Explanation: Ensuring consistent decision-making will require a shared rules engine or centralized decision service and governance infrastructure.

requirement: Hire a metadata librarian to manage data associated with the Water Use Tracking project.
ASR_Explanation: This is a staffing/resource requirement and does not drive any software architectural decisions.

requirement: The value you enter should be the actual data value, not any code number associated with it.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data entry/display validation detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Have consistent way to report on permit status and withdrawal status.
ASR_Explanation: This consistency requirement drives the design of a shared reporting component/API and impacts data models, component interfaces, and integration patterns.

requirement: Develop and make readily accessible metadata for the data (i.e. need to know the time frames when the data is considered to be good).
ASR_Explanation: It requires schema extensions and metadata services for temporal validity, impacting data storage and retrieval architecture.

requirement: System has to be user-friendly.
ASR_Explanation: It drives choice of UI frameworks and architectural patterns to ensure usability, impacting the system’s overall structure.

requirement: Ability to customize queries.
ASR_Explanation: It demands a flexible query builder in the data access layer, affecting component design and extensibility.

requirement: A process needs to be established so corrections to the source data sets can be initiated, i.e. when errors are discovered when running models.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the data pipeline and error‐handling architecture by introducing correction triggers, auditing, and version control.

requirement: The same datasets need to be used by all departments or sections of the District.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the data architecture by requiring a shared storage/integration layer and governance model to ensure consistent dataset usage across all departments.

requirement: Need to receive job-specific training and support.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement pertains to user training processes and does not impose constraints on the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: The data has to be available in real time.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts system architecture by imposing real-time data processing and low-latency streaming or in-memory solutions.

requirement: Clear documentation that lends to the defensibility of system for when permits are denied or modified.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of audit‐logging and document‐management subsystems to ensure defensible, traceable permit decision records.

requirement: Could have wizards to help users perform tasks.
ASR_Explanation: This UI pattern only affects the presentation layer’s navigation flow and doesn’t drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Have a decision tree for the help desk personnel that will receive questions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a procedural/training requirement and does not impose technical design constraints or require changes to the system architecture.

requirement: Clear hours of availability need to be established.
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational scheduling requirement that does not drive major architectural structure or technology choices.

requirement: System has to have the flexibility to handle different scenarios, or 'what-if' questions, or 1-10,2-10, and 5-10 drought events.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for a modular, configurable scenario engine and dynamic configuration management to support arbitrary drought-event simulations.

requirement: Need on-line help and training.
ASR_Explanation: This usability-focused requirement can be met without driving major architectural changes.

requirement: Need training material for in-house training.
ASR_Explanation: This is an internal training/material requirement and does not drive any system‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: Need to be able to save (or download) the results of queries into an outside system.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts the integration architecture by requiring export APIs, data transfer mechanisms, and associated security and formatting considerations.

requirement: Has to manage the metadata, and metadata has to be FGDC compliant.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of the metadata schema, validation components, and storage/retrieval architecture to ensure FGDC compliance.

requirement: Update interval protocols for updating different data types.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the architecture of the data update scheduler, synchronization mechanisms, and performance/caching strategies.

requirement: Populate missing data in the database, especially permits and pumpage reports.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data migration/population task and does not drive changes in system structure or architectural patterns.

requirement: The results from queries need to be consistent and reliable over time.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the data and system architecture by driving choices around consistency models, replication, caching, and fault-tolerance mechanisms.

requirement: The tools the system will use to access the data need to be better than what is available now.
ASR_Explanation: The requirement is too vague and non-specific to drive concrete architectural decisions about data access tools.

requirement: The data has to be accurate.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for robust data validation, consistency and quality assurance mechanisms across storage, processing, and integration components.

requirement: Application must fit into statutory time frames for evaluating permitting applications, so there must be a quick turn-around time.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes performance and scalability constraints that will shape architectural decisions around concurrency, caching, and resource provisioning.

requirement: Reasonable refresh rates.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes performance and latency constraints that drive decisions on data delivery mechanisms (polling vs. push), caching, and real-time architecture.

requirement: Need to establish response rates if it will be on a web page.
ASR_Explanation: This performance-related NFR will drive architectural decisions around infrastructure sizing, caching, and front-end/server-side optimization.

requirement: Data to support requirements must exist in the database, and be readily available.
ASR_Explanation: It drives key decisions in the persistence layer—database selection, schema design, and caching/replication strategies to ensure data availability.

requirement: Get compilable versions of code stored in SourceSafe.
ASR_Explanation: This is an implementation detail of source control integration and build processes with minimal impact on the high-level system architecture.

requirement: Do not use SDE log files.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint affects the logging and error‐handling architecture by prohibiting use of SDE log files and requiring alternative logging mechanisms.

requirement: Be consistent in naming and terminology conventions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a coding standard for terminology consistency that affects code readability and maintainability but does not drive architectural structure.

requirement: A web application is preferred. If ArcMap, has to be able to run in Citrix ArcView.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates key deployment architecture decisions by requiring a web-based solution or Citrix-compatible virtualization for ArcMap.

requirement: If toolbars are written, must be in .dll format.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes a plugin-style architecture with dynamic linking and platform-specific (.dll) module loading for toolbars.

requirement: Consider the ease of deployment of application.
ASR_Explanation: This affects architectural decisions around deployment automation, packaging, environment configuration, and CI/CD pipeline design.

requirement: Can extend the current District architecture, i.e. Oracle Spatial, if needed.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for a modular, extensible architecture and clear extension points for integrating or replacing the Oracle Spatial component.

requirement: Need technical documentation for maintainability.
ASR_Explanation: This is a process-level maintainability guideline that doesn’t impose direct constraints on the system’s architecture.

requirement: Follow District change management standards.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of deployment pipelines, version control integration, and audit/logging mechanisms to ensure compliance.

requirement: Do not join features based on the shape column.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a low-level data processing constraint that doesn’t drive system structure or architectural patterns.

requirement: System should be written in map.net – either VB or C#.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating .NET (VB/C#) fixes the core technology stack, affecting platform compatibility, tooling, performance, and integration choices.

requirement: Follow District programming standards.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces coding conventions but does not affect high-level system structure or architectural decisions.

requirement: The product runs as a stand-alone application.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates a standalone deployment architecture, impacting module boundaries and dependency management.

requirement: Its user interface uses menus, graphics and sounds.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level detail concern that does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: The product requires the use of a keyboard and a mouse to interface with the user.
ASR_Explanation: This simple input device requirement does not impose substantial architectural constraints beyond standard UI input handling.

requirement: It requires a graphical display of at least 800*600 resolution.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level display constraint that has minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The product uses the Qt graphical library.
ASR_Explanation: Choosing Qt as the UI library drives the core application framework, cross‐platform strategy, and module architecture.

requirement: It must run with all the operating systems that Qt supports.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for platform abstraction layers, cross-compilation/build pipelines and modular packaging to ensure portability across all Qt-supported OS.

requirement: The user must be able to undo and redo their last thousand actions.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the choice of core state-management patterns (e.g., command pattern) and impacts memory/storage architecture for maintaining a 1,000‐step action history.

requirement: The product must count and display the time that the user uses to play.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine timer/display feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The product must count and display the number of the user's actions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional feature that can be handled within existing modules without driving major architectural changes.

requirement: The product must record the score (time and number of counts) of a play associated with the name of a user.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple CRUD persistence requirement affecting only the data model and storage layer, not requiring major architectural changes.

requirement: The product must display a window with all the player's scores.
ASR_Explanation: This UI-specific functional requirement has minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The product generally requires a mouse and a keyboard for input. Other pointing and input devices are allowable, provided they provide similar functions to a mouse and keyboard, namely the ability to move a cursor onscreen to select buttons and the ability to type names.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the UI/input architecture by requiring an abstraction layer to support multiple pointing devices and text‐entry hardware.

requirement: The hardware and operating system must provide an 800x600 screen resolution.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes constraints on the display subsystem and hardware selection, affecting the UI layout and overall system architecture.

requirement: Sound is not required to play the game.
ASR_Explanation: This optional audio requirement has minimal impact on high-level system structure and can be handled within the audio subsystem without driving core architecture decisions.

requirement: The main window shall provide a board and a menu bar.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI layout detail that does not drive core system structure or architectural decisions.

requirement: The board is a rectangular zone where the user could move some blocks. The height of the board game is 5x, its width is 4x: x can't be less than 50 pixels and greater than 100 pixels. The blocks are separated by a margin of 0.1x. There are four square blocks with a side of x. There are four rectangular blocks with a height of 2x and a width of x. There is one block with a height of x and a width of 2x. There is one square block with a side of 2x. The board is black and the blocks are yellow.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI layout specification affecting only the presentation layer, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: Block selection is performed by left-clicking down on a block. The game state becomes 'Block displacement.'
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI interaction detail that doesn’t drive major architectural components or system structure.

requirement: Block deselection is performed by left-clicking up on a selected block. The game state becomes 'Block selection.'
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI interaction detail with no impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Block movement is performed by mouse movement during the 'Block movement' state. The selected block follows the mouse movement without overlapping the other blocks and exiting the game zone. The selected block can't move nearer than 0.05x from the other blocks.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional constraint on input handling and collision logic that affects subsystem implementation but not the high-level architecture.

requirement: The user can cancel a movement (Undo Action) via menu selection. The game displays the block positions at the places where they were before the last movement. The 'undo' action is unavailable if there was no previous movement.
ASR_Explanation: This single-step undo only adds modest state tracking to the existing game model and doesn’t drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The user can redo a movement that has been canceled (Redo Action) via menu selection. The game displays the block positions at the places where they were before the last movement was canceled. The redo action is unavailable if the previous action wasn't an 'undo' action.
ASR_Explanation: Requires incorporation of a command history stack and state management mechanism, impacting core engine layering and memory architecture.

requirement: The end of the game is triggered when the great square is moved at the bottom of the board. All the player statistics are frozen.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local game‐rule implementation detail with no broad impact on system architecture.

requirement: If the number of block movements of the current player is lower than the highest number of block movements recorded in the statistic file, the 'Finish Window with Statistics' is displayed. If not, the 'Simple Finish Window' is displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI presentation rule based on existing data and does not drive any architectural changes.

requirement: The Finish Window with Statistics contains the text: 'You win! Enter your name:', an Edit Box that can contain 20 characters, and a pushbutton with the label 'OK.'
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level UI detail that does not affect the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: When the player clicks on the pushbutton 'OK' in the Finish Window with Statistics, the player statistics are recorded in the statistic file of the software.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local UI event handler triggering a simple file write, not impacting the high-level system architecture.

requirement: The Simple Finish Window contains the text: 'You win!' and a pushbutton with the label 'OK.'
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI detail with no impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: When the player clicks on the pushbutton 'OK' in the Simple Finish Window, the Simple Finish Window is closed and the statistic window is displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI flow for closing and opening windows and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The following statistics are recorded during the game: number of block movements since the start, and time since the start.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple game‐level metrics requirement with minimal architectural impact, achievable via basic counters/timers.

requirement: The game statistics is composed of 10 player statistics.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level functional detail (number of stats) that does not drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: When the Finish Window with Statistics is completed by the player, the statistics of the player (name, block movement number, time passed to solve the headache) is recorded in the statistic file. If 10 player statistics are already recorded, the player statistics of the file with the greatest number of block movements is erased.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward file-based persistence rule with limited scope and minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: If the statistic file was correctly updated, there is no output. If not, like wrong permissions or disk full, an error message is displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level error‐handling detail with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The user could erase all the statistics via menu selection.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD operation that does not materially affect the system’s high-level architectural structure or major quality attributes.

requirement: The Player Statistics Window is composed of a listbox of 10 lines. Each line is composed of the name of a player, the number of block movements, and the time used by the player to solve the headache. These statistics are read from the statistic file of the game.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI‐level feature reading from a file and doesn’t impose any new architectural constraints.

requirement: The Player Statistics Window is displayed at the end of the game or via menu selection.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI display requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The user can open a previously saved game via menu selection. A dialog box is opened: the user could choose a file that contains all the data of the game previously saved. The board game is re-drawn according to the file data. The player statistics are set to the player statistics of the file data.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional feature (file I/O and UI update) with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The user can save the current game via menu selection. If the game was never saved, the processing is identical to the action 'Save as...'. If not, the following internal data are saved into the previous file: the current positions of the blocks, their last 10000 previous positions, the number of the previous movements, and the time passed by the user to solve the headache.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard game-state persistence requirement with no major impact on system architecture.

requirement: The user can save the current game as a new file via menu selection. A dialog box is opened: the user could choose a file that will contain all the data of the current game. The following internal data are saved into the file: the current positions of the blocks, their previous positions, the number of the previous movements, and the time passed by the user to solve the headache.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a persistence subsystem with serialization and file‐I/O concerns, impacting the system’s layering and infrastructure modules.

requirement: The user can exit the game via menu selection. If the game is not saved, a dialog box is displayed that asks the player if they want to save the game. Two choices are possible: 'Yes' and 'No.' If 'Yes' is selected, the action 'Save' is processed and the main window disappears. If 'No' is selected, the main window disappears.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI flow requirement with no impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Game menu contains 'Open game...', 'Save Game...', 'Save Game As...' and 'Exit' in this order. Menu selection performs the associated action.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI layout detail and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Action menu contains 'Undo' and 'Redo' in this order. The menu selection is unavailable if the associated action is unavailable. Menu selection performs the associated action.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level functional detail with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Statistics menu contains 'Display' and 'Erase' in this order. Menu selection performs the associated action.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI menu ordering detail that does not influence overall system architecture.

requirement: The Help menu contains 'About.' Menu selection displays the 'About Window' which is composed of the text 'Qheadache 1.0 by Jean-Philippe Brossat jp_brossat@yahoo.fr'.
ASR_Explanation: This is a trivial UI detail with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: There can be only one user per machine.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint affects the system’s concurrency and session-management architecture by enforcing single-user isolation per machine.

requirement: The software must be portable to the Windows OS.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates cross‐platform abstractions and choice of frameworks, libraries, and build/deployment pipelines for Windows portability.

requirement: The system shall provide one-point access to data from the sites in well-documented, and standardized formats.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the need for a unified integration/API layer and data transformation pipelines to aggregate and expose site data in standard formats.

requirement: The system shall deliver consistent high quality data in support of the CEAP watershed-scale assessment from a one-stop data portal to the CEAP clients and, in time, the public at large.
