ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of data ingestion, quality validation, storage, and delivery layers to ensure a scalable, one-stop portal for high-quality data.

requirement: The data system shall consist of a central database management system for the storage and management of data, and a client application to allow users access and interact with the data.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines core architectural components (central DBMS and client application) and their interaction, directly impacting system structure and technology choices.

requirement: The data stored in the database can be viewed and downloaded using graphical and tabular interface tools.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level functional feature (view/download) that does not drive major high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The data system shall serve as a repository where diverse end-users can access, search, analyze, visualize, and report various types of integrated watershed data contributed from the multiple locations.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives key architectural decisions around data integration pipelines, storage schema, indexing and analytics services, and modular front-end visualization/reporting components.

requirement: STEWARDS must have the capability to translate data from location-specific formats into the standardized STEWARDS formats.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a data transformation/integration layer, affecting the system’s overall architecture and data flow.

requirement: Data on STEWARDS will not be available to the public in real time. Access to real-time data will remain at the discretion of each location research team.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing a fine-grained access control and data distribution layer to enforce per-team, location-specific real-time data access policies.

requirement: Annual or more frequent updates from location databases to STEWARDS will be made by the watersheds’ staffs after the locations have completed their quality assurance procedures.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data‐synchronization functional requirement that can be handled by standard ETL/scheduling components without altering the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system shall operate with the following Web browsers: Microsoft Internet Explorer versions 5.0 and 6.0, Netscape Communicator version 4.7, Netscape versions 6 and 7, and FireFox.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates UI architecture and technology choices for cross-browser compatibility, including support for legacy browsers.

requirement: The system shall operate on Certified and Accredited servers running the current corporate approved versions of operating systems as appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes infrastructure constraints on OS and hardware platforms, affecting deployment environment and compatibility.

requirement: The Data Management portion of the system shall permit user access from the corporate Intranet and, if a user is authorized for outside access through the corporate firewall, from an Internet connection at the user’s home.
ASR_Explanation: This drives network and security architecture, requiring firewall traversal, DMZ deployment, and authentication mechanisms for internal and external access.

requirement: The Data Research and Reporting portion of the system shall provide public access to reviewed and approved data sets.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s architecture for security, access control, API design, and data exposure to public users.

requirement: The web application is to be platform independent on the client side wherever possible. Therefore, the web applications will be implemented to run on the server side as much as possible.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates client-server logic distribution, impacting deployment architecture, scalability, performance, and technology selection.

requirement: It is required to test the application using different platforms, connection speeds, screen settings, colors/graphics, and browsers.
ASR_Explanation: This is purely a testing (QA) requirement and does not impose design or structural constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: The application codes generated during prototyping may not run properly when re-hosting the data system to a non-Windows OS or transferring the data system to another location.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates cross-platform compatibility, affecting technology selection and OS abstraction layers in the architecture.

requirement: The database structure should be as complete as possible during the design stage but there should be a room for modification without a large overhaul during later phases.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data architecture by mandating an extensible schema design to support future modifications with minimal overhaul.

requirement: Data uploading and management will likely be done as infrequently as once a year. This fact greatly limits the complexity of the user interface and the learning curve needed for completing this task.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR only constrains UI complexity and usability without driving core architectural structure or technology choices.

requirement: The system shall use the current corporate standard Microsoft SQL Server database engine.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes a key technology constraint on the data layer, shaping deployment, integration, and scalability decisions.

requirement: The system shall be in compliance with all Accessibility, Web Design, and Security policies applicable.
ASR_Explanation: It drives key architectural decisions around UI frameworks, security controls, and overall system design to meet accessibility, web design, and security standards.

requirement: A testing plan will be documented during the Design phase. The testing plan will be based on user roles, modules or use cases, required tasks and expected outcomes.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement concerns test documentation processes and does not impose constraints or decisions on the system’s architecture.

requirement: A tutorial will provide a quick start, a walk-thru of major system features, and further reference source for the complete system features.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user documentation/training requirement and does not drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: An online hierarchical and cross-linked help system in HTML will describe and illustrate all system functions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation UI requirement that does not drive core system architecture decisions.

requirement: An online form will enable users to request help. Frequently asked questions will be screened for the FAQ pages.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI/feature requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The user's guide (or user manual) will contain sufficient information and instructions required to access and use the data system. It will include: overview of the system features and architecture, instructions for accessing the system, and samples of screens, where appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation requirement and does not drive or constrain the system’s architectural design.

requirement: The standardized data structure will facilitate database development, storage, and maintenance; metadata development, storage, and maintenance; and support database functionality, including tabular and spatial data queries, evaluation, visualization, and transfer (downloading) to off-site locations.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the overall data architecture by dictating standardized schemas, database technology selection (including spatial support), metadata management, and data transfer mechanisms.

requirement: The database management system should be stable and secured, have high performance (in terms of speed and efficiency/effectiveness), and be easy to maintain.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts key architectural decisions around database selection, security design, performance optimization, and maintainability strategies.

requirement: The team will elicit user requirements from a cross-section of user class members to accurately and completely describe the expected uses and functionality of the system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a process-oriented requirement for requirements gathering and does not impose constraints or shape the system’s architecture.

requirement: Establish a database in support of CEAP that will house the collective data assembled or generated during research activities. The database will support a variety of data types and formats, including but not limited to: spatial data - vector, raster, imagery, and tabular; tabular data – static and time-series; spreadsheets; documents; reports; photographs; and URL links.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the selection and design of a multi‐model database architecture to handle diverse spatial, tabular, document, and media data types.

requirement: The use of agricultural models (SWAT, AnnAGNPS) is a core element of the CEAP effort. The current scope of the STEWARDS system is to provide measured data for input to the model and for comparison to output.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for an extensible integration layer and data pipeline architecture to interface with external agricultural models.

requirement: Design tools to navigate the individual watershed sites and their data.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional feature (site navigation) that does not impose cross-cutting architectural constraints or drive core technology decisions.

requirement: Summary descriptions of research watersheds, stations, and instruments that are useful in describing the research activities should be accessible.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data‐retrieval function (CRUD) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Provide access to the data via browsing of sites, stations, and instruments; allow for simple queries to individual datasets; provide a metadata search tool to query dataset parameters; and allow for downloading of datasets (full or partial).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the data access layer, metadata indexing/search architecture, and API download endpoints.

requirement: Users may wish to examine time-series data, e.g. stream discharge data, over a user-specified time frame in order to select only those data desired for download. Charting tools in association with the query engine are desirable.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives architectural decisions around time-series data storage/indexing, query performance optimization, and front-end charting integration.

requirement: Provide access to CEAP-related reports, tables, and project documents.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard content access feature with no major architectural impact.

requirement: STEWARDS will provide web-based geographic information tools to allow site-specific data to be viewed within their spatial context. These tools will provide browse and query functionality and support links to download spatial data and their associated tabular data.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional requirement for GIS browsing and download features and does not impose specific architectural constraints.

requirement: Users will be able to examine the data used in the modeling effort, visualize the results in their spatial context, and download the model input data and results.
ASR_Explanation: It drives integration of data access services, GIS-based visualization components, and export APIs, shaping core system modules and technology choices.

requirement: Develop and maintain a metadata database that provides browse and query access to formal descriptions of the database elements. Provide tools for database contributors to create and upload metadata compatible with the STEWARDS metadata database.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates architectural decisions for metadata storage and schema, indexing and query/browse services, and ingestion/tooling integration.

requirement: Develop search tools that query watershed, station, and instrument metadata across the STEWARDS database. Metadata query tools will support query by location, theme, and keyword.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the choice of search/indexing architecture, metadata schema design, and integration with search engines for performance and scalability.

requirement: Provide administrative metrics – user access lists, data download estimates.
ASR_Explanation: Requires architectural decisions around data capture, storage, and analytics infrastructure to support metric collection and reporting.

requirement: Provide user feedback and user support tools.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement can be implemented within existing UI and service layers without driving major architectural changes.

requirement: Conform to USDA web site style guide.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI styling guideline and does not impose any high-level architectural constraints.

requirement: Adopt a metadata standard that is compliant with Federal regulations. The current Federal standards for spatial data are the Content Standard for Digital Geospatial Metadata (version 2.0), FGDC-STD-001-1998.
ASR_Explanation: This decision shapes the data architecture and schema design for metadata, affecting integration, validation, and compliance modules.

requirement: Implement a metadata input tool that supports creating and editing of metadata in the chosen format. The metadata tool will allow for local and web-based input and editing.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the overall client-server architecture by requiring modules for both local (desktop) and web interfaces and their synchronization.

requirement: Implement a web-based browse-and-query tool for searching STEWARDS metadata. Queries by location, theme, and keyword will be supported.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a web UI and backend search architecture—including geospatial, thematic, and keyword indexing—impacting technology choices and component decomposition.

requirement: Implement a database of STEWARDS metadata elements. The database will provide browse and query functionality.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data storage and access architecture, influencing database selection, schema design, and query/indexing strategies.

requirement: The web service will be platform independent as much as possible. Where there is an exception, the software/hardware requirement should be specified for a particular application.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architectural decisions around abstraction layers, technology stacks, and deployment strategies to ensure platform portability.

requirement: Users whose computing environment precludes downloading the data via the internet will have the opportunity to acquire data, reports, model results and other STEWARDS information via alternative media.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement necessitates designing and implementing offline distribution and packaging mechanisms for alternative media, impacting the system’s deployment and distribution architecture.

requirement: The system needs to be able to store hundreds of megabytes of data on demand. The potential for needing Gigabytes of data storage capacity is also in the realm of possibilities.
ASR_Explanation: This storage volume NFR impacts the choice and design of scalable storage solutions and data management architecture.

requirement: All User and Application data as well as software installation and configuration files must be fully backed up week-nightly. Further, secure, off-site storage will be performed on a weekly basis with 24-hour retrieval times.
ASR_Explanation: This backup and off-site storage requirement drives the design of storage infrastructure, data replication, security controls, and disaster‐recovery architecture.

requirement: Application Servers and Database servers must be load balanced at the application level to ensure maximum stability and availability.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s high-availability and scalability architecture by requiring load balancers, clustering, and failover strategies for app and DB tiers.

requirement: The system requires a “back-end” private network environment in order to ensure that end-user operations (on the front-end) are not impeded by database backups, index propagations, or other large back-end data transfers.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives network segmentation and deployment topology by mandating a dedicated back-end private network to isolate heavy data operations from front-end traffic.

requirement: The system will use, where appropriate, the standard hardware and data communications resources provided by the ARS OCIO at the ARS George Washington Carver Center in Beltsville, MD.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the system’s infrastructure and deployment architecture by mandating specific hardware and network resources.

requirement: Data and application backups will be managed through fully supported backup software solutions.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the storage and disaster-recovery architecture by imposing backup software integration, retention policies, and RTO/RPO constraints.

requirement: HTTP and related GIS server applications will be required to support system functionality.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the choice and integration of HTTP services and GIS server architecture, impacting infrastructure topology, middleware, and scalability.

requirement: Commonly supported web browsers will be used to implement a thin-client architecture. The use of Browser plug-ins will be judiciously restricted on an as-needed basis.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the client-server architectural style, browser compatibility requirements, and plugin management strategy.

requirement: Email server applications will be required to support system functionality as a secondary delivery protocol for alerts, data, and other information from the system.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR requires integrating an email delivery subsystem with queuing, security, and failover mechanisms, impacting the system’s communication and scalability architecture.

requirement: Microsoft SQL Server will be required to support system functionality as the primary data storage mechanism for the corporate standard relational database management system.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying SQL Server as the primary database constrains the data layer technology, impacting schema design, integration, deployment, and performance considerations.

requirement: The system will use, where appropriate, the standard software resources provided by the ARS OCIO. This includes, but is not limited to, MS ASP/Java Scripts, C++/C#, MS SQL server and IIS, or the use of PHP/Perl, JB scripts, C++/C#, MySQL server, and Apache server.
ASR_Explanation: This technology stack constraint drives key architectural decisions around platform selection, integration, and deployment.

requirement: The system will make data available in standardized data formats such as tab-delimited text for use on users’ client systems including local installations of ARC GIS software or other off-line software applications.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of data export/transform services and interfaces to generate and serve standardized formats (e.g., tab-delimited) for offline client and GIS integration.

requirement: Data will be subject to quality assurance prior to either initial or recurring uploading to STEWARDS. Initial population of STEWARDS will require that transition filters be developed jointly between the STEWARDS team and the individual watersheds.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the high-level data ingestion architecture by requiring built-in QA processes and transition filter modules for initial and recurring uploads.

requirement: Local watershed staff will be responsible for performing necessary quality assurance/quality control (QA/QC) procedures prior to data being uploaded to the STEWARDS database. Measurement methods and QA/QC procedures established by the CEAP Quality Assurance Team will be used by local data producers and recorded in the data provided to STEWARDS.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the architecture by driving the design of the data ingestion pipeline, validation modules, user roles, and audit‐logging for QA/QC workflows.

requirement: Quality assurance checking will be carried out at two different levels – local site level and central site level. Guidelines for quality assurance, including parameter-specific ranges, threshold values, quality flags etc., will be determined.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a multi-level QA framework with configurable rule engines and distributed vs. centralized processing.

requirement: Basic information regarding individual watersheds will be made available to STEWARDS by local watershed sites. Data will be reformatted to be compatible with the STEWARDS data schema and CEAP modeling requirements.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the data ingestion and transformation architecture to ensure schema compatibility and CEAP modeling integration.

requirement: Protocols for data imports from local sites via input files with different formats will be developed and implemented jointly between the STEWARDS team and the local watersheds. The standard exchange file format will have been jointly determined first. A single import filter will be written to parse the standard formatted files from the local watershed sites.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data ingestion architecture by mandating a standardized exchange format and corresponding import filter design.

requirement: Time-series data and other data will be collected by local watershed staff, processed through local and CEAP QA/QC procedures, and uploaded to the STEWARDS database on an annual basis using the filter developed during the initial population of the database.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the annual ETL/data ingestion pipeline, including QA/QC workflows, scheduling, and filter integration.

requirement: The central database will allow users to access the data of individual watersheds, sub-watersheds on a station-by-station basis, or a collection of stations if the entire data set is desired. Multiple levels of data aggregation of time-series data (daily, monthly, or yearly) will be supported. Export data should be available in terms of data volume and output format (csv, dbf, shp, grd, pdf).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data architecture and impacts storage schema, aggregation services, and multi-format export components.

requirement: The user interface will be simple and consistent, using terminology commonly understood by the intended users of the system. The system will have a simple interface, consistent with industry standard interfaces, to eliminate the need for user training of infrequent users.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI usability guideline that affects design details of the presentation layer rather than driving major architectural decisions.

requirement: User testing will be used to ensure the user interface is clear (simple, commonly understood vocabulary, intuitive to use without training), complete (users can perform all functions from the interface), and consistent (buttons and wording are the same throughout the system).
ASR_Explanation: This is a usability validation requirement and does not impose structural or technology constraints on the system’s architecture.

requirement: No extra hardware interfaces are needed. The system will use the standard hardware and data communications resources provided by the ARS OCIO at the ARS George Washington Carver Center in Beltsville, MD.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes deployment and integration constraints by requiring use of existing ARS OCIO hardware and network resources.

requirement: The system will include a warning message when a low transmission speed is detected, and a non-graphical interface option will be available.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a network‐speed monitoring component and an abstracted UI layer to support both graphical and non-graphical interfaces.

requirement: The system will use the standard software resources provided by the ARS OCIO. This includes, but is not limited to, MS ASP/Java Scripts, C++/C#, MS SQL server and IIS, or the use of PHP/Perl, JB scripts, C++/C#, MySQL server, and Apache server.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the technology stack and platform choices, directly shaping the system’s architectural design.

requirement: The system will use the communications resources provided by the ARS OCIO. This includes, but is not limited to, HTTP protocol for communication with the web browser and the web server and TCP/IP network protocol with HTTP protocol.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates the network and communication stack requirements (HTTP over TCP/IP via ARS OCIO), affecting integration, deployment, and security architecture.

requirement: The data system shall show no visible deterioration in response time as the number of persons increases. Response times seen by end users for querying metadata should be on the order of a few seconds or less. Response times seen by end users for retrieving the actual images may take much longer, anywhere from a few minutes to several hours. If the user requests a large image with a short response time, it is acceptable for the ARS data system to advise the user that the target response time cannot be met.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the scalability and performance architecture—requiring caching, load balancing, and asynchronous processing to sustain consistent response times under load.

requirement: The data system shall load as quickly as comparable productivity tools on whatever environment it is running in.
ASR_Explanation: This performance requirement will drive architectural decisions around system initialization, resource loading strategies, and environment-specific optimizations.

requirement: Data on the server should be protected from power loss but data in transit from server to requester could be lost. Such failures will be monitored and the uploading process will be repeated.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of durable storage and fault-tolerance mechanisms (e.g., write-ahead logging, UPS support and retry logic) to ensure data persistence under power loss.

requirement: STEWARDS will have reasonable controls consistent with ARS OCIO practices, in compliance with agency, Dept and Govt regulations. STEWARDS security requirements will have four primary components: authentication, confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
ASR_Explanation: Drives security architecture decisions for authentication, encryption, integrity validation, and availability to ensure regulatory compliance.

requirement: STEWARDS will follow industry best practices for authentication, using single-sign-on systems like Microsoft Active Directory to perform authentication.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the authentication subsystem design, requiring integration with Microsoft AD SSO and associated identity‐management infrastructure.

requirement: STEWARDS will use the user classes described to define boundaries of information sharing to ensure confidentiality as appropriate. Any data that should be viewed by a restricted audience must be protected with appropriate security features.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the security architecture by enforcing role-based access controls and data classification with appropriate protection mechanisms.

requirement: Extensive data validation and review will be performed both before data are uploaded to the system and as part of the upload process. The system will need policy and procedures protecting the data from intentional or unintentional modifications, and to ensure accurate data are made available.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s data integrity and security architecture by mandating validation pipelines, audit logging, and protection mechanisms at both pre-upload and upload stages.

requirement: The system must be available to the intended audience 24 hours per day, 7 days a week with, 99% availability and a tolerance of -5% (not less than 50% of working hours in any week).
ASR_Explanation: This 99% uptime requirement drives high-availability architecture including redundancy, failover, monitoring, and capacity planning.

requirement: This database will be built for a particular system and may not be portable but results to queries will be portable between many environments.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the choice of database technology and design of the data access layer to ensure engine-specific optimizations while providing portable query outputs.

requirement: Implementation of the application software/code and design of database structure should be flexible enough for the necessary change in the later phase.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for a modular, extensible architecture and adaptable database schema to support future changes.

requirement: Availability is defined here to mean the ability to use the system during its intended period of operation as defined in SCR-4 above.
ASR_Explanation: This is merely a definition of availability and does not impose any architecture-level constraints or influence design decisions.

requirement: The software will be used to search multiple web sites for streaming videos and torrents and return these results to the user.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a modular adapter layer for searching and aggregating across multiple external sites, impacting system integration and scalability.

requirement: The system will use an active internet connection to search multiple websites for streaming videos or torrents.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing an extensible network integration layer for crawling and retrieving streaming-video and torrent sources.

requirement: The system will aid the user in locating a specific video or genre of video by searching all these websites with just one query.
ASR_Explanation: This requires a federated-search integration layer across multiple external video sites, impacting API design, data normalization, caching, and performance architecture.

requirement: The system will provide tools to help when navigating through search results to save time and effort.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI/UX feature request with no significant impact on the system’s high-level structure or core architectural drivers.

requirement: The system will provide a simple interface and a more efficient way of finding the video the user requires.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the choice of UI framework and the design of the search/indexing and caching layers to meet usability and performance goals.

requirement: The system will provide the user with various options and tools to help make their search more relevant to the content that they need.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine feature-level requirement affecting the UI and search module without constraining the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system will contain databases of the different websites that will be used within the search, and these databases can be easily updated to provide a wider searching range.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for an extensible, modular data storage and update mechanism, influencing database schema design and integration architecture.

requirement: The main feature of the system will be a search engine for finding the location of torrents and streaming sites for videos on the internet.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives core architectural decisions around web crawling, indexing, search performance, scalability, and data storage.

requirement: The user can specify whether to search for torrents, streaming videos, or both.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/search‐logic filter change and does not require altering the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The results will be divided into the types that were specified using different tabs, and the results will be orderable by different categories, such as name, size, site, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level requirement for tabbed views and sorting that does not drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: The user will have the option to filter out videos containing certain content depending on age restrictions (e.g., adult content).
ASR_Explanation: This requires extending the content delivery pipeline with metadata classification, policy enforcement layers, and user preference management.

requirement: The user will have the option to filter out or only search certain websites.
ASR_Explanation: This adds UI options and filter logic in the search module but doesn’t drive major architectural changes.

requirement: The system will provide the facility for users to store their favorite videos, so that they can come back to the video at another time.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD feature (a user‐favorites table and related endpoints) with minimal impact on high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: There will be two levels of user: general users (who use the front end) and system developers (who can edit which sites the system will search).
ASR_Explanation: Introducing distinct user roles necessitates designing an authentication/authorization layer and access-control mechanisms that impact the system’s security architecture.

requirement: The system will be portable and work across different operating systems including Microsoft XP and Vista, Mac OS X, and a range of Linux platforms.
ASR_Explanation: This portability requirement drives key architectural decisions around cross‐platform frameworks, abstraction layers, and build/deployment strategies.

requirement: The system will work with different web browsers, for example Internet Explorer, Firefox, and Safari.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts front-end architecture by driving choices for UI frameworks, compatibility layers, and cross-browser testing strategies.

requirement: The hardware needed for the system will be a reasonably up-to-date computer that is connected to the internet via a modem.
ASR_Explanation: This is a generic deployment environment constraint with minimal influence on software structure or major architectural decisions.

requirement: Torrent search will share the same search bar with the streaming search.
ASR_Explanation: This requires a unified search aggregator and shared UI component, impacting service boundaries and data flow in the search architecture.

requirement: Database of torrent sites can be updated via the internet.
ASR_Explanation: This drives architectural decisions for remote data synchronization, network interfaces, security, and update workflows.

requirement: There will be a tick box to allow the user to choose to include torrent searching in the search.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI preference toggle that only affects a flag in the existing search module without changing the core architecture.

requirement: Query will retrieve the number of seeds and peers, size of the file, date posted, and a link to the webpage itself.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a standard data-retrieval requirement with no significant impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: If no results are found on a search it will display a message 'No results were found for this search.'
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI feedback requirement with no significant impact on system architecture.

requirement: Results will be arranged in size/date/alphabetical order by clicking on the column headers.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI feature (column sorting) with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: There will be a page button for the user to navigate the results.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI pagination control with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Streaming search will share the same search bar with the torrent search.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level consolidation of search inputs and doesn’t drive major backend or system architectural changes.

requirement: Database of video hosting and video linking sites can be updated via the internet.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of remote communication, data synchronization, and security layers for updating the database over the Internet.

requirement: There will be a tick box to allow the user to choose to include video host searching in the search.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI enhancement with a simple search filter addition and no significant architectural impact.

requirement: There will be a tick box to allow the user to choose to include video link searching in the search.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI toggle controlling search filter logic and doesn’t mandate any fundamental architectural changes.

requirement: Query to video hosting sites will retrieve full video name, length, date posted, and a link to the video itself.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐retrieval function with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Query to video link sites will retrieve the show’s name, episode name, and a link to the webpage itself.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward external API data‐retrieval requirement with no broad architectural impact beyond normal integration.

requirement: If no results are found on a search it will display a message 'No results were found for this search.'
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI feedback requirement with no impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Results will be arranged in length/date/alphabetical order by clicking on the column headers.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI-level sorting feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: There will be a page button for the user to navigate the results.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI navigation detail with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The user interface will consist of one main screen, allowing all the functionality to come from this one screen.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It’s a UI layout constraint that doesn’t drive major system‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The software will use hyperlinks to allow the user to open websites in their default web browser.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI feature with no significant impact on system structure or component interactions.

requirement: Communication standards that will be used through the software will be PHP or other such languages to query the different servers that the websites use.
ASR_Explanation: This is an implementation‐level technology choice (programming language) rather than an architecturally significant constraint affecting system structure.

requirement: Query times will take no longer than 5 seconds to any website.
ASR_Explanation: This performance NFR drives architectural decisions around caching, load balancing, CDNs, and network design to ensure sub-5 second responses.

requirement: Sending the hyperlink to the default browser will take less than 1 second.
ASR_Explanation: This performance NFR impacts the architecture of the browser-launch subsystem, requiring optimized process invocation and IPC strategies to meet the sub-1s requirement.

requirement: Loading the program will take less than 10 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes performance constraints that will influence architectural decisions around caching, resource allocation, and system optimization.

requirement: Any torrent result with 0 seeds will not be displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple data‐filtering rule at the application layer without broad architectural impact.

requirement: Sorting results should take less than 0.1 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This strict 0.1 s latency target drives key architectural choices around data structures, indexing, caching, and compute distribution.

requirement: A results page will display 100 results.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level pagination detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Any torrent result with a rating of less than 1 will not be displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple business‐rule filter applied at query or UI level and does not drive any architectural changes.

requirement: Our development team must thoroughly investigate each website within our database each month, to ensure that no illegal or harmful content is exposed to our users.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a scheduled content-scanning pipeline with database integration and reporting, impacting system modules, scheduling infrastructure, and compliance workflows.

requirement: We don’t maintain any user data or host any content.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint on not storing user data or hosting content directly impacts data flow, storage architecture, and compliance-related component design.

requirement: A full legal review of the software should be undertaken before the site is available to the public.
ASR_Explanation: This is a process compliance requirement and does not impose any direct constraints on the system’s architecture.

requirement: The system will stay within all the legal requirements, as the software is only listing links for video streams and video download, and will not host any videos.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around compliance controls, content filtering, and logging to ensure legal requirements are met.

requirement: The software includes a filter option which allows the user to set parental controls over the software, enabling the user to search for videos without getting explicit content.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional feature that can be implemented within existing modules without imposing new architectural constraints.

requirement: The software includes a favorites option, giving the user the option of storing links to their favorite videos and returning to these websites after restarting the software.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple persistence feature for user data that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The user can run multiple searches and keep different search results stored at the same time using tabs.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts state management and resource allocation by requiring concurrent, isolated search sessions with persistent result storage.

requirement: Within the search results display, the user will be told the name of the video, the location of the video (which website), the size of the video, the rating of the video, and comments on the video.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level display detail without substantial impact on system structure or key architectural decisions.

requirement: The user can decide how to sort the search results using the column headers at the top.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard presentation-layer sorting feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Users of the tool shall be classified into two types. The first type, general user, shall only be permitted to utilize a limited set of the capabilities of the tool. The second type, administrator, shall be permitted all the privileges of the general user plus those identified in Table 2. The administrator is permitted all the privileges of the general user in addition to the privileges to configure the system and modify fixed data and diagnostic settings.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces role-based access control, impacting the security architecture, module interfaces, and permission management components.

requirement: The automated diagnostic tool shall input sensed data in real time from data acquisition devices and the HVAC unit under diagnosis as well as from data files containing previously acquired data, though not simultaneously. In addition, the tool shall input fixed data provided by the user. Sensed and fixed data used by the tool in performing automated diagnostics shall be stored to permanent storage for post-analysis by the diagnostic process and viewing of the results.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of core data‐ingestion interfaces, real‐time processing pipelines, and persistent storage subsystems.

requirement: The tool shall input sensed data in real time from data acquisition devices and the HVAC unit under diagnosis. The absolute time of input will be associated with each sensed data item for its complete lifecycle, including permanent storage. The time will be determined at the instant the acquisition of the sensed data is complete.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the real-time data ingestion pipeline, timestamp synchronization mechanisms, and persistent storage schema.

requirement: The tool shall permit sensed data to be input from an electronic file, though not simultaneously with sensed data from external acquisition hardware. When inputting sensed data from an electronic file, only one file shall be used containing all necessary sensed data. The file shall be an ASCII text file containing only columns delimited by a single tab character or single comma. The file shall begin with a required header.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only specifies a low‐level input file format and affects the parser module design, not the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: Each sensed data item will be qualified as to its certitude during the acquisition process. Sensed data will be considered in doubt if acquisition of the data was not possible (for example, if an error occurs during communication with the acquisition hardware), or the data is outside the range of possible values. Otherwise, the sensor value will be deemed accurately acquired.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates adding data validation and certitude‐classification modules to the sensor acquisition pipeline, impacting component design and data flow.

requirement: The software will deem sensed data in doubt if acquisition of the data is not possible, due to communication failures, for a default number of successive samples. The default is dependent on sensor type. The number of successive samples for a given sensor type shall be modifiable by the administrator.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data acquisition and fault-handling architecture by mandating per-sensor configurable thresholds and an administrative configuration component.

requirement: Sensed data used in or supporting diagnostic analyses shall be saved to permanent storage.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the design of the persistent storage subsystem, impacting storage technology selection, capacity planning, durability, and data retrieval strategies.

requirement: The occurrence of missing data shall be indicated in permanent storage by a unique and obvious place holder or indicator, e.g., a null value or sequence of characters that cannot be confused with non-missing data.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data storage representation requirement affecting database schema design rather than driving high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Sensed data input to the tool shall be stored to permanent storage with a precision of three significant figures.
ASR_Explanation: This precision constraint is an implementation detail for data handling and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Sensed data input to the tool shall be validated with respect to its expected range of values. Sensed data shall be assigned the ranges listed in Table 5 by default.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed input-validation requirement that does not drive high-level architectural structure or major design decisions.

requirement: The administrator shall have the capability to modify the expected range of sensed data against which the data will be validated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine administrative configuration feature with minimal impact that can be handled by existing configuration modules.

requirement: The engineering units of sensed data input to the tool shall comply with the units listed in Table 6 for the associated physical parameter measured prior to input to the diagnostics.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data validation/detail requirement for input formatting and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The tool shall provide the capability to convert sensed data not provided in the units listed in Table 6 to the required units.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional requirement (unit conversion) that can be implemented within existing components without driving major architectural changes.

requirement: The tool shall associate with each continuous parameter one or more tolerances each corresponding to a different sensitivity level for the tool.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional data‐modeling requirement for the parameter module and does not drive high‐level architectural changes.

requirement: Any and all errors associated with obtaining, validating, converting, or storing the sensed data shall be logged as described in subsequent specifications and selected errors shall be reported to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a cross-cutting error-handling and logging infrastructure, impacting module interfaces, data flow, and storage components.

requirement: The automated diagnostic tool shall detect and identify certain performance and operational faults in chiller and cooling tower subsystems of HVAC units following specified algorithms. In addition, the tool shall be designed to permit expansion of diagnostic analysis to additional subsystems. The faults identified and their causes and sources, shall be saved to permanent storage for subsequent analysis.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates an extensible modular architecture for adding diagnostic subsystems and a persistent storage layer for fault data.

requirement: The tool shall monitor performance and operational parameters listed in Table 7 for chillers and cooling towers and identify the specific faults in the specific fault categories listed in the table.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of real-time data ingestion and processing pipelines with a rule-based fault detection engine, impacting scalability and reliability.

requirement: With each fault identified in Table 7, the tool shall associate text describing the possible problem, causes, and a recommended fix.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional mapping requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The tool shall be designed to permit the addition of diagnostic algorithms for other subsystems of HVAC units.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a modular, extensible architecture with defined extension points/interfaces for adding new diagnostic algorithms.

requirement: A diagnostic algorithm shall not process if any input data to the algorithm is missing. The user shall be notified if a diagnostic algorithm cannot process due to missing data.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed algorithm-level validation and user notification rule that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Any and all errors associated with failures of the diagnostic algorithms shall be logged as described in subsequent specifications and selected errors shall be reported to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This cross-cutting error-logging and user-reporting requirement drives the choice and integration of the logging infrastructure and error-handling architecture.

requirement: The tool shall monitor the unit under diagnosis, either in real time or batch mode, for an indefinite period of time or until input data are no longer available, and log diagnostic results as they are determined.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of continuous real-time and batch processing pipelines with indefinite runtime and persistent logging, impacting processing architecture, resource management, and storage infrastructure.

requirement: Diagnostic results that are saved to permanent storage shall be accompanied in storage by the absolute time of occurrence, the identity of the unit to which the diagnostic applies, and the identity of the diagnostic.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data persistence detail (timestamp and identifiers) that does not drive major architectural design decisions.

requirement: The administrator user shall have the capability to adjust the sensitivity of the diagnostic analysis in order to increase or decrease the probability of fault detection.
ASR_Explanation: This requires a configurable diagnostic engine with runtime-adjustable sensitivity thresholds, impacting component design and performance management.

requirement: The automated diagnostic tool shall display and record diagnostic results. Diagnostic results to be displayed to the user will be done so in a simple, graphical and textual format. Diagnostic results shall also be saved to permanent storage for subsequent retrieval, display, and analysis.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional requirement (UI display and data persistence) with no major impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The administrator shall have the ability to specify which diagnostic results will not be reported in any manner, i.e., not detected.
ASR_Explanation: This requires a configurable filtering layer in the diagnostics/reporting pipeline, impacting event‐handling and configuration subsystems.

requirement: The display of each diagnostic result for fault conditions will include a description of the fault, potential causes of the fault, the location of the fault, and the absolute time of occurrence.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/data-reporting detail that doesn’t drive high-level architectural structure or component boundaries.

requirement: The area on the main, systems, and condition windows sensitive to the users selection device, typically the mouse, shall include the line surrounding the item of interest over the entire width of the window. This includes the diagnostic indicators.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐layer UI behavior/detail and does not influence the overall system architecture.

requirement: The windows listing diagnostic results shall increase in height as needed to display entries up to a maximum of ten entries. Once the window has reached maximum size and there are additional items to display, the window shall deploy a scroll bar to permit the user to scroll through the additional entries. In this event, a summary set of diagnostic indicators shall appear above (below) the top (bottom) entry in the display. This set(s) of indicators shall summarize diagnostic indicators not currently appearing on the list above (below) it. An indicator in the summary set shall be active if any similar indicator not appearing above (or below) it is active. The summary set indicators comply with the same specification for activation as other indicators.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation/detail requirement and does not drive high-level architectural structure or component interactions.

requirement: The building, subsystems, and condition windows shall display the current time if the diagnostic tool is operating in real time. If the tool is operating in batch mode, the time displayed will be the time associated with the entry currently being processed in the batched input.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI/time‐display requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: If only a single building is defined, the subsystems window shall become the main window of the application and shall have all the functionality of the main window as described in these specifications.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the application’s window composition and startup flow, requiring dynamic reassignment of the main window and module integration.

requirement: Each window of the diagnostic tool shall display an animated graphical feature indicating the status, whether active or not, of the diagnostic processing.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐layer UI detail (animation) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The subsystem window shall be modal to the building window, the condition window modal to the subsystem window, and the diagnostic history and diagnostic information windows shall be modal to the condition window.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level window management detail with minimal impact on the overall software architecture.

requirement: Significant errors in software operation shall be logged to permanent storage with sufficient detail to guide the administrator and to assist support staff in locating and rectifying the error. In addition, selected errors shall be reported to the user.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a cross-cutting error‐handling and logging infrastructure, including persistent storage and user notification mechanisms.

requirement: Software errors logged to permanent storage shall include the absolute time of occurrence, the name of the software module in which the error occurred, the name of the software function in which the error occurred, and a description of the error.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes detailed cross-cutting logging requirements that drive the design of the persistent logging infrastructure and error-handling components.

requirement: The user interface shall consist of six windows: Main window, configuration window, subsystems window, condition window, diagnostic information window, and diagnostic history window.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI layout requirement that does not drive high-level architectural structure or technology choices.

requirement: These windows shall be organized in a hierarchy enforcing a navigational order on the user.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the presentation‐layer architecture by requiring a hierarchical navigation structure and associated controller framework.

requirement: The main (e.g., initial or default) window shall list buildings monitored and summarizing the current diagnostic status of all contained monitored units.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐layer requirement (UI listing and summarization) and does not impose system‐level architectural constraints.

requirement: The main window shall have main menus entitled “File” and “Status”.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI labeling detail with negligible impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The main menu entitled “File” shall contain the following menu items in the following order: “Configure Diagnostics”, “Logout”, “Exit”.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user interface detail with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The main menu entitled “Status” shall contain the following menu items in the following order: “Start Diagnostics”, “Stop Diagnostics”.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI layout requirement with negligible impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The menu items named “Configure Diagnostics” and “Exit” in the main menu entitled “File” shall be enabled at all times.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI behavior requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The menu item named “Logout” in the main menu entitled “File” shall be enabled only if the current user is presently authenticated as the administrator user. Otherwise, this menu item shall be disabled.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI control rule with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The menu item named “Start Diagnostics” in the main menu entitled “Status” shall be enabled only if diagnostic processing is not currently running. Otherwise, the menu item shall be disabled.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level control behavior for enabling/disabling a menu item and does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The menu item named “Stop Diagnostics” in the main menu entitled “Status” shall be enabled only if diagnostic processing is currently running. Otherwise, the menu item shall be disabled.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI behavior detail and does not drive architectural structure or major design decisions.

requirement: Selecting the “Configure Diagnostics” menu item of the main menu entitled “File” shall display the configuration window, making it the current window, modal to the main window.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level modal behavior detail and does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The configuration window shall permit the administrator user to modify the following parameters: a. The password for the administrator user. b. A toggle enabling or disabling the main window of the tool as a screen saver. c. A toggle enabling or disabling the main window’s position on the screen as the top most window. d. Buildings and subsystems to be monitored and the conditions they will be monitored for. The conditions will be selected from a list provided by the tool. e. The enabled state of individual conditions. Disabled conditions will not be processed or displayed. f. The sensitivity of each condition by selecting a sensitivity from a list provided by the tool. g. Scale factors for transforming the engineering units of sensed input data to the required units. h. The expected range of sensed input data.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user‐interface configuration requirement impacting only preference storage and display, with no significant effect on overall system architecture.

requirement: The configuration window shall display the following non-modifiable information: a. [content not specified in excerpt]
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI display requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The configuration window shall have a button labeled “Save” that when clicked will present the user with a window by which to enter a disk file or database name in which to save the modifiable data in the window. Entering a file name shall cause the permanent storage of the modified parameters and their immediate use by the diagnostic operations after confirmatory responses by the user. If the user does not confirm the request to save, it will not be performed. If the user does not confirm the request to relay the modified parameters to the diagnostic processing immediately, it will not be performed. In either event, the configuration window will remain displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI-level functional requirement that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The configuration window shall have a button labeled “Close” that will dismiss the configuration window and return the user to the main window. If the user has made changes to data in the configuration window, the tool will request confirmation of the user’s desire to dismiss the window. If the user does not confirm the request, the window will not be dismissed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI interaction detail without broad impact on system structure or architectural patterns.

requirement: The configuration window shall have a button labeled “Recall” that will permit the user to recall a disk file of previously saved parameters from which to populate the configuration window. When functioning as a screen saver, the window shall change location on the screen periodically and will continue to be updated by diagnostic results.
ASR_Explanation: It’s purely a UI-level feature (button behavior and screensaver movement) without impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The subsystems window shall list subsystems monitored for a specific building and summarizing the current diagnostic status of all monitored subsystems.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level display requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The condition window shall list the current status of all diagnostics for a specific subsystem.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level diagnostic listing requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The diagnostic information window shall provide a detailed description of the diagnostic.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR is a UI presentation detail and does not impact the high-level architectural structure or component interactions.

requirement: The diagnostic information window shall have a button labeled “Print” that when clicked will display a window listing items to print including the diagnostic information window, the history window, and both the diagnostic and history windows. The user selects the item of interest and clicks the “OK” button to print the selection, an image(s) of the item(s) selected.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI printing feature that does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The diagnostic information window shall include the following information regarding the diagnostic: 1. A description of the possible problem. 2. Potential causes. 3. Fixes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI content requirement and does not impose any structural or quality-attribute constraints on the system’s architecture.

requirement: The diagnostic information window shall have a button labeled “Close” that shall dismiss the window.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI implementation detail with no significant impact on the system’s architecture.

requirement: The diagnostic information window shall have a button labeled “History” that when clicked will display the diagnostic history window and display the previous week’s diagnostic results.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI navigation feature affecting only view/controller behavior without impacting the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The diagnostic history window shall display historical diagnostic results for a specific diagnostic.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI display requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The diagnostic history window shall provide user selections for start times and end times for the history interval. These selections shall be in the form of a calendar control.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI implementation detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The smallest time interval displayed by the diagnostic history window is one week.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI display granularity requirement and does not impact the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The longest time interval that may be displayed by the diagnostic history window is two years.
ASR_Explanation: This retention limit drives storage sizing, indexing and data partitioning decisions for the diagnostic history subsystem.

requirement: The color map of the history window shall indicate confirmed faults with the color red, potential faults with the color yellow, normal conditions with the color green, and periods when diagnostic processing was not active with the color gray. Diagnostic processing should be assumed inactive between periods of time when no confirmed fault, potential fault, or normal condition has been recorded.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement is a UI-specific color mapping detail and does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The vertical axis of the color map of the history window shall indicate hour of the day and the horizontal axis shall indicate the absolute date and the day of week.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI visualization detail specifying axis labels and has minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The diagnostic history window shall have a button labeled “Print” that when clicked will display a window listing items to print including the diagnostic information window, the history window, and both the diagnostic and history windows. The user selects the item of interest and clicks the “OK” button to print the selection, an image(s) of the item(s) selected.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI printing feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The diagnostic history window shall have a button labeled “Close” that will dismiss the window.
ASR_Explanation: This simple UI control requirement has minimal impact on the system’s architecture and does not drive structural or quality-attribute decisions.

requirement: The subsystems window, condition window, and diagnostic information window shall display a title that indicates its specific location or absolute path in the navigation hierarchy of windows. This title shall be a concatenation of the title of the previous window displayed, not including the diagnostic history window, and the name of the entry selected to obtain the current window. The two parts of the title shall be separated by a dash surrounded by a single space.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation detail affecting only the title‐generation logic in the presentation layer, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: The main window, subsystems window, condition window, and diagnostic information window shall display the appropriate time below the title as specified in 3.3.1.3.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI display requirement with no significant impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The main window, subsystems window, and condition window shall have three diagnostic indicators for each item listed.
ASR_Explanation: This UI display requirement has minimal impact on high-level system structure.

requirement: One indicator shall be active when no diagnostic conditions exists. This indicator shall be designated the “OK” indicator and shall appear green when active.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI presentation detail (indicator color/state) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: One indicator shall be active when a potential diagnostic condition exists. This indicator shall be designated the “Caution” and shall appear yellow when active.
ASR_Explanation: This is purely a UI color‐coding detail with no significant impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: One indicator shall be active when a confirmed diagnostic condition exists. This indicator shall be designated the “Not OK” indicator and shall appear red when active.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation detail with no bearing on the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: Only one indicator shall be active at a given time for a specific diagnostic and no indicators shall flash when active.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI behavior constraint and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The diagnostic indicators on the main window and subsystems window shall summarize all diagnostic conditions associated with each entry in the window. In general, an active “OK” indicator indicates no positive diagnostic results currently exist within the building or subsystem, as applicable for the window. An active “Caution” indicator indicates that at least one potential problem currently exists within the building or subsystem, as applicable, and no confirmed problem currently exists. An active “Not OK” indicator indicates at least one confirmed problem exists regardless of whether cautionary or non-positive results exist. Essentially, confirmed problems override potential problem indications which override non-positive results.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local UI diagnostic‐indicator behavior requirement and does not drive system‐level architectural structure or constraints.

requirement: Certain functions of the tool directly available to the user require the user to possess supplementary privileges associated with performing these functions. These supplementary privileges are possessed by the administrator user and are in addition to those of the general user as described in 2.5. The tool determines the type of user by requesting an identifier and password from the user.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the authentication and authorization architecture by requiring role-based access control and secure credential management.

requirement: The tool shall log to permanent storage the occurrence of changes to the fixed data. The log shall indicate the time and date of the change, original value of the item(s) changed, and the new value of the item(s) changed.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the persistence and logging architecture by requiring audit-trail mechanisms, storage schema changes, and performance considerations.

requirement: The ELSS must capture all data necessary for recording the fishing activities undertaken by a UK fishing vessel.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional requirement defining domain data capture without prescribing architectural constraints or impacting system-wide design decisions.

requirement: The ELSS must output the data as an XML file for transmission to the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates an XML-based data serialization and integration interface for the ERS system, shaping the output pipeline architecture.

requirement: The ELSS data must be validated against the UK XML/XSD before transmission from the fishing vessel.
ASR_Explanation: It requires the addition of an XML/XSD validation component in the data transmission pipeline, impacting integration and error-handling architecture.

requirement: The ELSS data must be transmitted at the required times set out below in Section 2.5.1.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes strict time-based transmission constraints, impacting the design of the scheduling and communication architecture.

requirement: Each ELSS data transmission will be acknowledged by a return message from the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system.
ASR_Explanation: This ack-requirement drives the messaging/integration architecture, impacting communication protocols, reliability, and error-handling with the external ERS system.

requirement: The ELSS must provide data capture screens for the entry of the logbook, transhipment and landing declaration data that is required to be transmitted to the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement concerns UI screen details for data entry and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The ELSS must use English (UK) localizations for all UK Electronic Logbook features.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level localization detail that can be handled by existing internationalization frameworks with minimal architectural change.

requirement: All dates and times must be UTC.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data‐format convention that affects implementation but does not drive major architectural structure or component interactions.

requirement: The ELSS may provide the means for recording additional data but this must not interfere with the data capture and submission functions as set out below.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates architectural modularity and isolation layers to support optional data recording extensions without impacting core capture and submission functions.

requirement: The ELSS must provide the ability to print out the ELSS logbook data (including landing declarations) using an onboard printer.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces a new hardware integration and print‐service module, impacting the system’s abstraction layers and driver/infrastructure architecture.

requirement: The ELSS must provide a means so that electronic print files are protected so that they cannot be modified in any way once generated and distributed.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring tamper-proof file formats and integration of digital signatures/encryption and key management.

requirement: The ELSS must provide Electronic Logbook data for transmission to the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system in accordance with the frequency requirements defined below (Section 5.1).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines an external integration point with specific transmission frequency constraints, impacting interface design and scheduling/throughput architecture.

requirement: The ELSS should at least be able to transmit data via email, either by being included in a packaged solution with an email system or by linking with an existing on-board email service.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the system architecture by requiring integration with an email subsystem or on-board messaging service, influencing module interfaces and communication layers.

requirement: All xml documents attached to emails to the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system must be encrypted using PGP.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates integrating PGP encryption and key management into the email processing architecture, impacting security components and data flow.

requirement: The ELSS must permit the Master of the vessel to generate formatted data for transmission to the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for an external integration layer with specific data formatting and transmission protocols to the UK ERS system.

requirement: The ELSS must provide facilities to capture and deliver for transmission corrections to previously successfully transmitted data. Data corrections must not be transmitted piecemeal; the entire report containing one or more corrected items must be transmitted.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the transmission architecture by enforcing atomic batch delivery of corrected data, requiring transaction management and payload packaging design.

requirement: All corrections must be easily identifiable within the ELSS user interface.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level usability requirement for highlighting corrections and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The ELSS must only permit correction messages to be generated and sent for reports sent during a current trip up to the submission of the End of Fishing report for that voyage.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s state management and workflow orchestration by enforcing trip-lifecycle constraints on message generation and delivery.

requirement: The ELSS must provide facilities to transmit deletions to previously transmitted data.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data transmission architecture by mandating support for deletion events and tombstone handling in the streaming pipeline.

requirement: The ELSS must only permit deletion messages to be generated and sent for reports sent during a current trip up to the End of Fishing report for that voyage.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-specific validation rule enforceable within existing message‐processing components without requiring new architectural patterns.

requirement: All deletions must be easily identifiable within the ELSS user interface.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation requirement and does not drive core architectural structure or technology choices.

requirement: The ELSS must be able to receive acknowledgement (RET) messages transmitted from the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the integration architecture by requiring a messaging interface, protocol handling, and dependency management with the external ERS system.

requirement: The ELSS must match each acknowledgement message with the appropriate transmitted data operation, deletion or correction report.
