ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level message-handling detail that doesn’t drive major structural or systemic architectural decisions.

requirement: The ELSS must be able to confirm that a transmission has been successfully acknowledged or display any error message should the ELSS receive a negative acknowledgement message.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional behavior for handling acknowledgements and error messages that does not drive major architectural structure or component decisions.

requirement: This test facility must be used to send test transmissions to establish that the communications between the vessel and the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system are fully operational.
ASR_Explanation: This is a testing-focused functional requirement and does not impose core architectural design decisions.

requirement: ELSS must retain all logbook reports and any corrections on the system at least until the end of each trip, i.e. on submission of the electronic landing declaration or of a transhipment report.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It dictates the data retention and lifecycle management strategy, impacting storage design and automated purge workflows.

requirement: Any ELSS software updates must not impact upon the ELSS’s ability to meet the requirements set out in this document and other test documentation. If it does then the product must be submitted for re-approval, and cannot be deployed by fishing vessels until this is granted and the new version id is published on the UKFA website lists.
ASR_Explanation: This is a regulatory release and certification process requirement, not a driver of the system’s structural or behavioral architecture.

requirement: ELSS security and access controls such that one username/password must be provided for the owner of each vessel; the owner is then able to set up subsidiary users such as the master of the vessel with their own username and password. The username is required to be recorded in each report completed and in each transmission made; the person who has entered the data is required to “sign” the Electronic Logbook data stating that they are aware of the responsibilities/liabilities they are committing to in completing and / or transmitting a report. Each copy of the ELSS installed must be provided with a unique (internal?) number that is automatically entered into each transmission to identify the instance of the ELSS from which the report has been transmitted.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the design of cross‐cutting security services (authentication, authorization, audit/logging, digital signing) and unique instance identification.

requirement: ELSS must only be supplied for use at sea and loaded onto an onboard system and is not to be provided for onshore use by agents or representatives.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes environment-specific deployment and access control constraints that will shape the system’s distribution and deployment architecture.

requirement: Product MUST only permit deletion message to be sent only during a current trip up to the End of Fishing report for that voyage.
ASR_Explanation: Requires maintaining voyage state and enforcing time‐bound deletion permissions, impacting the messaging and workflow architecture.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means to allow deletions to be identifiable via the ELSS user interface.
ASR_Explanation: It requires introducing audit/soft‐delete mechanisms and corresponding UI enhancements, impacting data model, logging, and interface architecture.

requirement: Product MUST submit the whole Electronic Logbook report when sending a correction message i.e. the entire report containing one or more corrected items is to be submitted for re-transmission and not piecemeal corrections.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the communication protocol and data flow design by mandating full-report payload handling, affecting message size, bandwidth, and error recovery.

requirement: Product MUST only permit correction messages to be sent only during a current trip up to the End of Fishing report for that voyage.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-level validation rule enforceable within the existing request‐processing workflow without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means to allow corrections to be identifiable via the ELSS user interface.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level requirement for marking corrections and does not drive high-level architectural changes.

requirement: Product MUST correlate each Acknowledgment message with the outgoing report and operation (DAT|DEL|COR).
ASR_Explanation: This necessitates adding a cross‐component correlation mechanism in the messaging infrastructure for traceability of acknowledgments to outgoing reports.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means of indicating that the report as being successfully acknowledged and for displaying any error message or data provided in the acknowledgement.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the overall communication and error-handling architecture by defining how acknowledgments and related data are propagated and displayed across system layers.

requirement: Product MUST alert the Master if no acknowledgement is received within a time limit as pre-set by the Master.
ASR_Explanation: This timeout-based alerting requirement impacts the system’s messaging, scheduling, fault-tolerance, and monitoring architecture.

requirement: Product MUST provide data capture screens for the entry of the logbook, transhipment and landing declaration data that is required to be transmitted to the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level data capture requirement and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means for fisherman to manually enter required Electronic Logbook and Landing Declaration information.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI data‐entry feature that doesn’t impose significant constraints on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Product MAY provide automated features for populating the Electronic Logbook information (e.g. interfacing with a GPS feature for inserting the location, date, time etc).
ASR_Explanation: This is a feature‐level integration detail (optional GPS auto-populate) that won’t drive or constrain the overall system architecture.

requirement: Product SHOULD provide Man-Machine Interface factors suitable for operation of the ELSS on a moving/swaying platform.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architectural decisions around sensor integration, UI framework choice, and motion-compensation/stabilization logic.

requirement: Product MUST use English (UK) localization for all UK Electronic Logbook features.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s internationalization/localization framework (resource bundles, locale settings and formatting) for UK English.

requirement: Product MUST use UTC for all dates and times.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating UTC for all time data affects data storage, integration, logging and client interactions, shaping the system’s core time-handling architecture.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means to provide a hard copy print out of the Electronic Logbook and Landing Declaration using an onboard printer.
ASR_Explanation: Integrating an onboard printing subsystem and drivers for hard‐copy output impacts the system’s hardware interface and modular architecture.

requirement: Product MAY provide an electronic copy of the Electronic Logbook or Landing Declaration to be transmitted and printed on-shore.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the architecture by requiring dedicated document‐generation and secure transmission/printing modules, driving technology and interface choices.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means so that electronic print files are protected so that they are not able to be modified in any way once generated and distributed.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the security architecture by requiring integration of cryptographic signing/encryption and key‐management into the document generation/distribution pipeline.

requirement: Product MAY provide features to facilitate other print requirements, including: Generation of hard copies of the Electronic Logbook when fishing in 3rd country or Regional Fisheries waters, e.g. NAFO, Faroese and Norwegian waters; Generation of a hard copy Electronic Logbook or landing declaration as a transport or takeover document; Generation of a hard copy for providing regulatory return for Cod and Hake effort reporting.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a document‐generation subsystem (templating/PDF engine, storage, format compliance) impacting core architecture.

requirement: Product MUST enable the transmission of the required reports by an on-board email system eg by generating the data for transmission as xml documents attached to email for the email system deployed to be sent to an email address of the UK fisheries administrations’ ERS system.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the integration and messaging architecture by mandating XML serialization and email-based transport to the external ERS system.

requirement: Product MUST use the Electronic Logbook filename syntax based on the GBRRN attribute i.e. [RSSNumber][YYYYMMDD][999999].
ASR_Explanation: It’s a file-naming convention detail that doesn’t drive or constrain the high-level system architecture.

requirement: Product MUST encrypt the Electronic Logbook XML file using the UK Fisheries public PGP key when sent as an email attachment.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates integrating PGP encryption into the email subsystem, impacting security architecture, key management, and message-processing design.

requirement: Product MAY provide details of other transmission methods supported by the ELSS product which should be considered by UK Fisheries for future release of the ERS.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the modular communication architecture by requiring extensible support and interfaces for multiple transmission methods.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means whereby the system can track whether the submitted Electronic Logbook report has been successfully transmitted to the ERS systems and to track whether an acknowledgment has been received for each transmission.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the messaging and persistence architecture by requiring reliable transmission, acknowledgment handling, and state-tracking mechanisms.

requirement: Product MUST provide for a test mode of operation to enable the fishermen and the UK fisheries administrations to carry out test transmissions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor functional mode toggle (test vs. production) and does not drive major architectural patterns or cross-cutting decisions.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means to ensure that Electronic Logbook is transmitted to the UK ERS at least on a daily basis not later than 24:00 hours even when there is no catch data with the proviso that if the vessel is in port, has no fish on board and has submitted a landing declaration, transmission may be suspended subject to prior notification to the Fisheries Monitoring Centre of the flag Member State. Transmission shall be resumed when the vessel leaves port.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a reliable, event‐driven scheduling and state‐management subsystem for compliant daily transmissions and suspend/resume integration with the UK ERS.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means to transmit the Electronic Logbook at the time of completion in response to a UK fishing administration request.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of an external integration layer for secure, reliable transmission of logbook data to UK administration systems.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means to transmit the Electronic Logbook immediately after the last fishing operation has been completed.
ASR_Explanation: It requires event-driven triggers and a robust network communication layer to ensure immediate logbook transmission upon completion of fishing operations.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means of sending the Electronic Logbook before entering into port.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the communication architecture, mandating maritime-compliant messaging, secure transmission, and offline‐sync capabilities before port entry.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means of sending the Electronic Logbook immediately on departing a port.
ASR_Explanation: This “immediate” send requirement drives real-time, event-driven communication and connectivity considerations in the system architecture.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means of sending the Electronic Logbook at the time of inspection at sea.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the communication and synchronization architecture to ensure reliable logbook transmission over intermittent at-sea connectivity.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means of sending the Electronic Logbook at the time of events defined in Community legislation or by the UK fisheries administrations.
ASR_Explanation: This regulatory-driven requirement imposes real-time/event-triggered integration and scheduling constraints, shaping the system’s communication architecture.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means of sending a transhipment electronic logbook data immediately after the transhipment.
ASR_Explanation: This real-time data transmission requirement impacts the integration and messaging architecture, requiring reliable, low-latency communication and error‐handling patterns.

requirement: Product MUST provide a means of sending a transhipment electronic logbook data immediately on completion of a Landing Declaration.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a real-time, event-driven messaging/integration layer for reliable immediate transmission of logbook data.

requirement: Product MAY use stored zone definitions to provide an alarm for the Master to prepare a report or to generate a report from catch data already stored e.g. exiting zone report.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data storage and event‐driven processing layers by adding alarm triggering and report generation based on stored zone definitions.

requirement: Product MUST retain all logbook reports and any corrections on the system at least until the end of each trip, i.e. on submission for transmission of the electronic landing declaration or of a transhipment report.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data persistence and retention architecture, dictating lifecycle management of logbook reports until trip completion.

requirement: Software updates for a product MUST NOT impact upon requirements described in Council Regulation (EC) No. 1966/2006 and commission Regulation (EC) No. 1077/2008.
ASR_Explanation: Ensuring compliance with EU regulations imposes constraints on the update architecture, deployment process, and validation mechanisms.

requirement: Product MUST provide a unique ID for the master of each vessel; provide a means for the Master to assign a unique ID to each person with computer access; provide a means for each unique ID to authenticate (e.g. with a password or passphrase or token) before accessing the system; associate an Electronic Logbook report with a unique User ID; record a legal acknowledgement from the user entering the data for the Electronic Logbook record.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the security and identity management architecture, impacting authentication mechanisms, user provisioning, audit logging, and legal compliance features.

requirement: Product MUST only to be supplied for use at sea and loaded onto an onboard system and is not to be provided for onshore use by agents or representatives.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the deployment environment and drives architecture for location‐based distribution and onboard access control.

requirement: Systems designed to support OM must offer configuration flexibility so that new data fields, entities, entity types and relationship types may be added to capture information unique to each particular health event.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives a metadata‐driven, dynamically extensible data model architecture to support on‐the‐fly schema and entity configuration.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must support structured data entry for common forms and fields to ensure data integrity, validity, and standardization.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a metadata-driven form and validation framework, shaping the core data model, UI components, and integration architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should support multiple deployment options (e.g., client server, disconnected, and potentially web based).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architectural decisions by requiring support for client–server, disconnected, and web-based deployments, impacting component modularization, layering, and connectivity infrastructure.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should provide the ability for computers in disconnected mode to reconnect to a server to share OM data among other computers that operate in disconnected mode.
ASR_Explanation: This offline/online synchronization requirement drives core architectural decisions around data caching, reconnection handling, and conflict resolution.

requirement: OM data should be synchronized so that all instances of OM applications working from the same server are able to share and use the same data.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the overall system architecture by requiring a distributed data synchronization strategy and consistency model for shared access across multiple application instances.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should be able to electronically record and store data from remote devices that may be uploaded to an aggregating system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the data ingestion and storage architecture, affecting choice of protocols, data models, and scalability.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should be capable of using configurable, domain-specific vocabulary.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a modular, extensible vocabulary‐management component, shaping core configuration and integration services.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the capability to capture demographic data about persons involved in an OM investigation, including: Subject ID, name, address, date of birth, gender, phone number, race, ethnicity, and country of citizenship.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐capture requirement affecting the data model/schema without imposing broader architectural constraints.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the capability to capture data about organizations involved in an OM investigation, including: organization name, location, and contact information.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data‐capture requirement (standard CRUD and schema design) with no major architectural impact.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the capability to capture data about locations involved in an OM investigation, including: name (if applicable), type, street address, city, state, zip code, country, GPS coordinates, and other specific details.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐capture/detail requirement affecting the data model only, with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the capability to capture data about any animals involved in an OM investigation, including: type, age, gender, owner's name and address, color, weight, and species. A Subject ID should also be collected for animals in an OM investigation.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD data‐model requirement with minimal architectural impact confined to extending the schema and UI forms.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the capability to capture data for any object involved in an OM investigation, such as a letter, invoice, food item, or any object that cannot be classified as a person, organization, place, or animal.
ASR_Explanation: This FR drives the design of a flexible, extensible data model and classification mechanism for arbitrary object types, impacting core domain architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the capability to capture data about any conveyance involved in an OM investigation, including: type of conveyance, route taken, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data‐capture requirement that does not impose new architectural structures or patterns.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the capability to capture data about any public or private gathering of people involved in an OM investigation, including: time, location, nature of the event, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives core architectural decisions around data modeling, storage scalability, privacy/security controls, and integration with time-location capture services.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the capability to capture data about any living things other than persons or animals that are involved in an OM investigation, including: type of living thing, and other customizable data collection questions.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for a flexible, metadata-driven data model and configurable form architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the capability to capture an entity’s travel history to support investigations of entities infected, exposed or potentially exposed.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data‐model, storage, and integration architecture by mandating capture, retention, and query of geo‐temporal travel history data.

requirement: When a health event is investigated, it must be assigned an event identifier (Event ID) that is unique within the jurisdiction.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data‐integrity rule for ID generation and does not drive high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: Data describing the health event should be captured, including the reason for the investigation, the category of event, the date the event began, the suspected agent or investigation focus, the geographic area impacted by the event, as well as the event status.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐modeling requirement for CRUD operations and doesn’t impose system‐level architectural constraints.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should have the ability to record the case definition for a health event.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD‐style functional requirement with minimal cross‐cutting or systemic architectural impact.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should have the ability to capture changes to the case definition that occur as the health event evolves.
ASR_Explanation: This requires a versioned, flexible data model and dynamic configuration management to track evolving case definitions.

requirement: Travel history data should include information such as the method of transportation, flight number, departure and arrival dates and times, and the origination and destination locations.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain data detail affecting the data model but not driving any significant architectural decisions.

requirement: Information about each leg of a trip, as well as the parent information about the trip, should be captured.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data-modeling requirement for capturing trip and leg details with minimal impact on the overall architecture.

requirement: Travel history data to be collected for an animal or object should include shipping invoices, animal shelter delivery and adoption receipts, and delivery schedules (including delivery vehicle and driver information).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data integration, storage schema, and subsystem interfaces needed to ingest and manage multiple external documents and delivery details.

requirement: Detailed conveyance data must be collected when relevant to the investigation, including the carrier identifier, the type of conveyance, as well as the make, model, year, and identification number of each vehicle with which the entity was in contact.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives extensions to the core data model and storage layers (database schema, ingestion APIs, and related services) to capture and manage conveyance details.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the ability to avoid capturing redundant entity demographic information.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data-deduplication requirement that can be handled within the existing data-model and service implementation without altering the overall system architecture.

requirement: Case data about the entity should include: a case identifier (Case ID) that is unique within the jurisdiction being reported, the suspected agent, case diagnosis, health status, case status, investigation dates, clinical history, symptom onset date and time, epidemiological links to other cases, and priority.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data-field specification for the case entity and doesn’t drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: There must be a means to update the case diagnosis either manually or automatically if the case definition changes during an event.
ASR_Explanation: It requires integrating a dynamic rules/configuration engine and real-time update mechanisms, impacting the event-processing pipeline and data consistency architecture.

requirement: Epidemiological (epi) data must be collected to assist in the case investigation of events. Standard epi data to be collected includes: onset date and time of symptoms, type of symptoms, risk factors, medical history data, laboratory data, procedure data, and questionnaire responses.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of an extensible, secure data model and integration layer for heterogeneous, privacy-sensitive epidemiological and lab data.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must allow for dynamic, event-specific case investigation data to be captured.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a flexible, metadata-driven data model and dynamic form/UI framework, impacting storage, schema management, and event-processing architecture.

requirement: In the context of a case, all entities exposed to a case must be recorded and linked to the case.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s data model and persistence architecture by mandating entity linkage and traceability within case records.

requirement: Demographic information should be collected about the investigator, including their name, address, and contact information.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a straightforward CRUD/data-model requirement with minimal influence on overall system architecture.

requirement: Both the jurisdiction investigating the event and the jurisdiction reporting the cases and associated investigations must be captured.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the domain model and persistence architecture by requiring entities and relationships to track both investigating and reporting jurisdictions.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should have the ability to classify entities associated with the investigation as investigation controls.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-specific functional requirement affecting business logic/data model without imposing cross-cutting architectural constraints.

requirement: Exposure investigation data to be captured must include information related to exposure levels, type of exposure, place of exposure, length of time the entity was exposed, frequency of exposure, and the entity’s proximity to the source of exposure.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data capture requirement affecting the data model but not imposing any significant architectural structure or constraints.

requirement: Detailed data must be collected about the source of exposure as well as the exposed entity to support contact exposure tracing.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data model, storage strategy, and privacy/security architecture for contact tracing.

requirement: Epi data must be collected to assist in the exposure investigation of health events. Standard epi data to be collected for exposure investigation parallels the data to be collected for case investigation.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the overall data model and storage architecture by extending and reusing case‐investigation data schemas for exposure investigations.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must support capturing dynamic, event-specific data that describes contact between two subjects.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates an event-driven, scalable data capture and storage architecture to handle dynamic, per-contact event data.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should support the monitoring and follow-up activities required when tracking the status of cases and exposed individuals.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for stateful case management workflows, persistent data storage, and real-time monitoring integrations affecting the system’s core architecture.

requirement: Monitoring data should be collected about cases and exposed individuals who are isolated or quarantined because of a health event.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a scalable, real-time telemetry and storage pipeline for monitoring quarantined and isolated individuals.

requirement: Follow-up data should be collected from subjects or their proxies to track symptoms and compliance with recommended treatment plans or prophylaxis.
ASR_Explanation: This requires designing asynchronous workflows with scheduling, notifications, proxy integrations, and scalable data storage for follow-up tracking.

requirement: Follow-up data may be received from take response exams of persons who received a countermeasure that requires such an exam.
ASR_Explanation: It requires extending the data model and designing new ingestion/integration pipelines for follow-up exam data, impacting the system’s data architecture.

requirement: Specimens/samples collected for laboratory testing must be assigned an identifier (Specimen ID) that is unique within the jurisdiction.
ASR_Explanation: This FR simply mandates a unique identifier constraint in the data model, with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: The subject of a specimen/sample collected for laboratory testing must be linked to the specimen/sample by an identifier (Subject ID) that is unique within the jurisdiction.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s identifier generation and data storage architecture to ensure jurisdiction-wide uniqueness and consistency.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to store data about the specimens/samples that are collected for laboratory testing.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data persistence requirement and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Clinical specimen data should include information about the specimen source/site from which the specimen was taken, symptom date of onset, and whether the sample is acute or convalescent.
ASR_Explanation: This simply extends the clinical specimen data schema with additional fields and does not affect high-level system architecture.

requirement: Environmental sample data should include information about the collection method, location (geocoded if possible), source, nature of the sample, quality control data, collection begin and end date and time for air samples, and original volume and volume of concentrate tested for water samples.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data-model specification affecting database schema fields but not requiring changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: Food sample data should include information about the lot number, batch number, manufacturer name, shipping invoice, temperature, sample type, and product storage condition.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data-field requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Bar-coding should be supported for the capture of detailed specimen/sample data to improve the quality and efficiency of data collection.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around barcode hardware integration, scanning middleware and data encoding workflows.

requirement: Chain of custody information for all specimens/samples should be captured.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of your data model, audit/logging framework, and security controls to ensure traceability and compliance.

requirement: Chain of custody information for forensic and select agent samples must be captured, including the person who collected the sample, the location of collection, all people who came into contact with the sample during the preparation for shipment to the laboratory, and the acceptance of the package by the shipper.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of data models, audit/logging services, and identity management for secure chain-of-custody tracking.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to create a laboratory test request for a specimen/sample or group of specimens/samples.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard domain-level CRUD function and does not impose significant high-level architectural constraints.

requirement: Information about batch shipments of specimens/samples that are transferred to test laboratories or other facilities must be collected, including the shipper, shipment tracking number, and the sending organization’s contact information.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐capture requirement that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should be able to support the inclusion of labeling, packaging and shipping instructions, and the shipping manifest with batch shipments of specimens/samples.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the addition of new modules for label/manifest generation, batch shipment workflows, and integration with external shipping systems.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to store laboratory result(s) and link the result(s) to the original laboratory test request.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data persistence and relationship requirement with no major architectural impact.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must store data about laboratory results. Examples of this data include the Specimen ID, Subject ID, test date and time, test type, data for each organization involved in the testing of the specimen/sample, laboratory results and result values, other data such as unit of measure for result value, overall interpretation, and any relevant notes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data storage requirement for CRUD operations that doesn’t impose special architectural patterns beyond a normal database schema.

requirement: If the specimen/sample collection record exists, the laboratory result must be linked to the specimen collection record by the Specimen ID.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data integrity rule enforceable at the application or database layer without driving major architectural decisions.

requirement: If the specimen/sample collection record does not exist, the laboratory result must be linked to the subject by the Subject ID.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple business‐rule for data linkage with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: All levels of granularity of results (e.g., specimen/sample level, assay level) must be supported.
ASR_Explanation: Requires a flexible data model and service/API design to handle multiple result aggregation levels, impacting database schema and system layering.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should capture or be linked to data regarding the prophylaxis or treatment to cases, exposed individuals, or at risk persons, including the person who ordered the prophylaxis or treatment, and the name, date, type, duration, and dosage of the treatment or prophylaxis given.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives extensions to the data model and storage/integration architecture to capture and link detailed treatment and prophylaxis information.

requirement: Contraindication information should be collected to indicate why vaccinations, treatments, or antidotes may not have been administered or why the patient may not have complied with prescribed interventions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data capture requirement affecting the domain model without imposing new architectural constraints.

requirement: If an affected person suffers a negative reaction to a vaccine or prophylaxis that was administered, adverse event data may be collected and used to determine the need for additional interventions or to determine if there is a problem with the pharmaceutical, batch, or the administering facility or person.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts architecture by requiring event-driven data capture, processing, and analytics services to enable real-time monitoring and regulatory reporting.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should capture information such as the date and time of an activity, activity type, who initiated the activity, and contact information to generate activity logs for management purposes.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the logging infrastructure, data schema, storage, performance, and compliance considerations.

requirement: Activity logs, which are tools for investigators to track their actions during a case, should be supported.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces cross-cutting concerns for the logging infrastructure, data storage, performance and audit compliance.

requirement: Activity logs may also provide information needed to support communication with various jurisdictions in the event that the investigation crosses jurisdictional boundaries.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the logging architecture by requiring compliant, extensible storage and retrieval of audit data formatted for multi-jurisdictional reporting.

requirement: Electronic questionnaires must be developed and validated. They will be designed by investigators to collect common data elements, agent-specific data elements, and other customized data elements.
ASR_Explanation: This necessitates a metadata-driven, dynamic form builder and validation framework, affecting modularity, data model extensibility, and UI architecture.

requirement: Electronic questionnaires should provide the capability to accept digital signatures.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring integration of digital‐signature services, certificate management, and signature verification flows.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must provide the ability to control the configuration of and revisions to investigation-specific questionnaires.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a metadata‐driven questionnaire engine with versioning and configuration services, impacting data models, service layers, and UI architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must provide the ability to publish investigation-specific questionnaires and implementation guides.
ASR_Explanation: This requires integrating a content‐management and publishing subsystem for dynamic questionnaires and implementation guides, impacting data storage, UI, and workflow architecture.

requirement: Case investigation should be supported by reusable questionnaire libraries that use common terminology to maximize the efficiency of data exchange.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system architecture by requiring modular, reusable questionnaire libraries with standardized terminology and data exchange interfaces.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should provide a manual or automatic means of updating the status of case records as the case definition changes.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the core data model and workflow engine design by requiring dynamic triggers or interfaces for propagating case-definition changes to record status.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should track the changes made to the status of case records as a result of changes in the case definition.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates an audit/change‐tracking mechanism and schema extensions, impacting data storage, logging, and performance design.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must support dynamically defined associations between entities for the purpose of defining relationships.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives a metadata-driven architecture with a flexible data model and runtime-configurable entity relationship handling.

requirement: Entity-to-epi data links must match the entity to their symptoms, survey questions, specimens/samples collected, laboratory results, and prophylaxis and treatment data.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the overall data model and integration architecture, dictating how entities and epidemiological data are linked and queried across modules.

requirement: Each new case must be able to link an assigned Entity ID to an Event ID within the scope of the investigation.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data-model relationship requirement and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Systems supporting OM require the ability to capture information about possible cases and potential contacts from the identification process through the treatment and follow-up process, as supported by linkages among entities, events, and actions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the core data architecture and service interfaces to support robust, end-to-end entity, event, and action linkages across identification, treatment, and follow-up.

requirement: Laboratory results must be linked to corresponding specimens/samples (including multiple results from one specimen/sample), and subjects when the participating laboratory returns the results. These linkages must unambiguously associate multiple laboratory results to case and contact identifiers.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the core domain and data model design for unambiguous linking and integration of lab results, specimens, subjects, cases, and contacts.

requirement: If the specimen/sample was created from another specimen/sample (e.g., aliquots, and new specimen types created from a source sample), laboratory results for the child specimens/samples must be linked to the corresponding parent specimen/sample.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data model and service layer by introducing parent–child specimen relationships, affecting database schema design and data integrity constraints.

requirement: Each investigation subject may be associated with exposure contacts, including unambiguous links to contacts in other jurisdictions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data architecture by requiring global unique identifiers and cross‐jurisdictional integration for contact relationships.

requirement: Contacts of exposed entities may be traced, investigated, and monitored.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement demands architectural support for tracing, auditing, and real-time monitoring pipelines, impacting data storage, logging, and analysis components.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should be able to create new contacts from existing case records, and should also identify the contact type.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional enhancement affecting existing modules and data flows without requiring major architectural changes.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must support contact exposure tracing by allowing one contact to be linked to multiple cases, and allowing multiple contacts to be linked to a single case.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard many-to-many data‐model requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should be able to produce contact work lists for each investigator to use, and should allow sorting by priority or geography.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine business‐logic feature (list generation and sorting) that doesn’t drive significant architectural changes.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should allow for analytical searches based upon multiple criteria.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for a scalable, indexed search architecture (e.g., choice of search engine, indexing strategy, query optimization).

requirement: Systems supporting OM should have the ability to produce charts, maps, and graphs that illustrate OM data, such as epi-curves and the effect of vaccination or prophylaxis on the number of new cases.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the integration of data visualization libraries and the design of analytics pipelines, impacting UI architecture, data modeling, and performance scaling.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should generate electronic data dictionaries for dynamic data, line lists, activity logs, aggregate data, and call-back lists to assist the emergency response group and investigators.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates integrating a metadata management layer and dynamic schema/report‐generation infrastructure for diverse data outputs.

requirement: Reports generated by systems supporting OM should clearly indicate the number of cases, the number of contacts per case, the number of cases with no known epi-link at the time of diagnosis, the laboratory results, and the number of vaccinations and/or treatments administered.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional reporting requirement without broad architectural implications.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should have the ability to produce pre-formatted queries and reports to allow faster and more accurate reporting, while still allowing the flexibility of ad-hoc reporting.
ASR_Explanation: This can be handled within existing reporting modules without affecting the overall system architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should have the ability to produce individual reports for each emergency team member or investigator.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard reporting feature with minimal impact that can be implemented within the existing architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should have the ability to compare characteristics of exposed and non-exposed (i.e., controls) persons.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of data models, analytical pipelines, and reporting modules to support cohort comparison functionality.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should have the ability to produce lists of action items (e.g., to do lists).
ASR_Explanation: This basic to‐do list feature has minimal impact on overall architecture and can be handled within standard UI and data storage patterns.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must have the ability to print questionnaires for multiple uses, including taking to the field, use during phone interviews, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard printing feature that can be handled within a reporting/printing module without impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to aggregate data. Examples of aggregated data to be supported are: number of cases, number of contacts per case, and number of vaccinations and/or treatments administered.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of scalable data aggregation and reporting architecture, impacting database schema, ETL pipelines, and analytics infrastructure.

requirement: Health event data should be aggregated into a centralized data store (i.e., data warehouse) designed specifically to support analysis of events over time.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates adding and integrating a centralized data warehouse and ETL/streaming pipelines, impacting the overall data storage and processing architecture.

requirement: Data should be accessible for use with commonly available analytical tools (e.g., SAS, SPSS, EPI-INFO, MS Access, MS Excel, Crystal Reports).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data architecture and integration layer to provide the necessary export formats, connectors, or APIs for external analytical tools.

requirement: Contact information for key response partner organizations should be stored in a local instance of a public health directory for associating to a health event.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward CRUD/data-model requirement with limited broader impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to accept data from other partner systems supporting OM.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of integration layers and data interchange protocols, impacting system interoperability and interfaces.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to create and send messages for laboratory test requests.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional requirement for messaging lab requests and doesn’t drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should be able to receive, parse and process messages for laboratory test request responses.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces messaging integration concerns requiring dedicated infrastructure for reliable receipt, parsing logic, and processing workflows.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to receive, parse and process messages for laboratory results.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the integration and processing pipeline, dictating messaging protocols, data transformation components, and throughput requirements.

requirement: Laboratory results should be linked to laboratory test requests, which are linked to subjects.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward domain relationship requirement affecting data modeling but not driving systemic architectural decisions.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should be able to exchange messages for laboratory results with systems supporting surveillance, early event detection (EED), and other preparedness areas.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s integration architecture, mandating messaging protocols, data models, and interoperability interfaces.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to exchange messages for confirmed, probable and suspect cases, and the other case classifications that are noted in the PHIN message implementation guide, with systems supporting surveillance, early event detection (EED), and other preparedness areas.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates cross-system messaging interoperability and interface design according to PHIN standards, impacting the integration and messaging architecture.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must exchange messages for investigations and for exposure contacts.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of cross‐system messaging infrastructure and integration patterns (protocols, data schemas, reliability).

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to receive, parse and process messages for countermeasures that have been administered.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of the messaging infrastructure and parsing/processing pipeline, impacting integration, scalability, and data flow architecture.

requirement: Countermeasures that have been administered must be linked to the entity that was administered the countermeasures.
ASR_Explanation: Requires extending the data model and service interfaces to enforce referential integrity and traceability between countermeasure records and their target entities.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to create and send messages for countermeasure administration requests.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the messaging infrastructure and integration protocols for countermeasure administration requests.

requirement: Systems supporting OM must be able to exchange aggregated data. Examples of aggregated data to be supported are: number of cases, number of contacts per case, and number of vaccinations and/or treatments administered.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the integration architecture by mandating standardized aggregated data models, exchange protocols, and interoperability mechanisms.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should be able to provide the aggregate data necessary for health event monitoring to systems that support response tracking, such as the number of suspect cases, number of persons under isolation or quarantine, and the number of patients receiving countermeasures.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of cross‐system integration, data aggregation pipelines, APIs, and scalability to support real‐time health event monitoring and response tracking.

requirement: Mapping interfaces and data dictionaries must be clearly defined and included in data exchanges to indicate and describe both standard and customized fields.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of data schemas and API contracts, impacting integration consistency, versioning, and governance.

requirement: Message components should be grouped by observation type (e.g., laboratory, symptom, exposure, risk, treatment) by systems supporting OM.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the message schema and data model, requiring architectural changes to payload structure and component grouping across OM-supporting systems.

requirement: Systems supporting OM should support multiple file formats for import and export, such as databases, spreadsheets, messages, and text files, among others.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the architecture by requiring an extensible adapter framework and data transformation pipelines for multi-format import/export support.

requirement: Data exchange should support analysis and information sharing of possible health events at all levels of public health (e.g., national, state, local).
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It drives architectural decisions around interoperability, scalability, data standards and security for distributed public health information systems.

requirement: It is required that vocabulary standards be used when exchanging data.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates data model and interface design for interoperability, influencing the integration and service contract architecture.

requirement: Policies and procedures for communicating information to appropriate stakeholders should be clearly defined.
ASR_Explanation: This is a governance/process requirement and does not directly drive software architecture structure, components, or quality-attribute decisions.

requirement: Policies regarding data synchronization should be defined to support multiple deployment options.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the architecture of data replication and synchronization mechanisms across different deployment topologies.

requirement: Configuration management protocols and personnel should be identified to support multiple deployment options.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the deployment and operational architecture by defining configuration management strategies, tooling, and team responsibilities for multiple environments.

requirement: Protocols and personnel should be identified to support the set-up and configuration of laptops and other field devices used in OM investigations.
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational support requirement for processes and staffing, not impacting the software’s architectural structure or quality attributes.

requirement: Processes and personnel should be identified to support agent-specific deployment packages, including syndromic grouping libraries and vocabulary sub-sets, which will be used to efficiently collect data specific to the particular health event being investigated.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a modular, plugin-based deployment architecture and configurable packaging pipelines to support agent-specific libraries and vocabularies.

requirement: Policies and procedures should be in place for determining when follow up and isolation and quarantine monitoring should be done as a part of a focused countermeasure administration and response effort, rather than as a function of contact exposure tracing.
ASR_Explanation: This is a business policy/process requirement and does not impose technical or structural constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: Systems and data supporting OM must be protected from sabotage, corruption and unauthorized access, and must be available subsequent to a catastrophic event.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of security controls, encryption, backup/restore, redundancy and disaster‐recovery mechanisms for high resilience.

requirement: Privacy requirements ensure that sensitive information is not accessible to unauthorized uses.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of secure data handling layers, encryption, access control, and auditing, impacting the system’s overall security architecture.

requirement: All software will be maintained under a version control system, and must include facilities for easy (nearly automatic) inclusion into the Gemini software environment.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of the build/deployment pipeline and infrastructure components for version control integration and automatic inclusion into the Gemini environment.

requirement: Observing astronomers shall have no privileges as far as the direct control of the telescope is concerned. They shall not be able to send control commands directly but they must be able to enquire about the status of the telescope or any subsystem at any time.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the access-control and interface segregation architecture, separating command execution modules from status-monitoring components.

requirement: Traditional interactive operation shall normally be replaced by operation via an automatic sequencer.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing an automatic sequencer in place of interactive operations drives the architecture of control flow, component interfaces, and orchestration mechanisms.

requirement: A certain degree of interaction shall be provided, meaning in this case that the user will interact with the scheduler program, rather than with the control programs directly.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s layering and module boundaries by mandating a dedicated interactive scheduler interface separate from control components.

requirement: Observing commands will normally be submitted via the User interface to a queue for later execution. It must also be possible to break and resequence this queue.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates an asynchronous command queue with dynamic break and resequence capabilities, affecting the overall messaging and process-flow architecture.

requirement: Operations staff will be able to enable direct interactive operation, but this shall not be considered as the normal operation mode.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces a secondary interactive mode requiring architecture-level support for mode switching, access control, and alternate processing paths.

requirement: Operations staff shall have privileges to access all commands and maintenance procedures in case of problems. This includes direct control of physical units.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the system’s security and authorization architecture by mandating privileged access controls and direct physical-unit control interfaces.

requirement: Operations staff shall have access to operation tables in update mode, while observing astronomers will have access to them only in read mode.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a role‐based authorization component in the security and data access architecture.

requirement: Software development and maintenance staff need the highest privilege in order to be able to modify everything in the system. Nevertheless strict configuration control guidelines must be followed to prevent interference with ongoing normal operation.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the security and configuration-management architecture, including RBAC, audit trails, and controlled deployment workflows.

requirement: Software development and maintenance staff usually work at the test level for the part of the software under test. Other parts of the Gemini 8m Telescopes may also need to run in test mode to support integration tests.
ASR_Explanation: Requires architectural support for test-mode operation and simulation interfaces across telescope subsystems, impacting module design and integration configuration.

requirement: Parallel access to all the mounted instruments shall be provided, though only one instrument has access to the telescope beam (active instrument).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes concurrency and resource-arbitration constraints, driving the design of scheduling, locking, and hardware‐interface layers.

requirement: Regardless of the status of an inactive instrument, it shall not be possible for any of its permitted actions to adversely impact the active instrument.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR demands strict isolation and fault containment between active and inactive instrument modules, influencing component boundaries and interaction patterns.

requirement: Visitor instruments must be capable of operating as servers, responding to commands from the upper levels of the system.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates a client-server architecture requiring visitor instruments to host server endpoints, impacting component distribution, communication protocols, and security.

requirement: The visitor instrument interface should support acquisition of status information of instrument by other systems, capability to enter preprogrammed observing sequences, and capability to offset the telescope position and focus.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of real-time, cross-system communication interfaces and synchronization mechanisms, impacting the system’s modularization and protocol architecture.

requirement: Any subsection of the whole Gemini 8m Telescopes system should be accessible and controllable from any single point (but of course with protection ensuring security and safety).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a centralized control and access architecture, impacting network topology, subsystem integration, security, and safety mechanisms.

requirement: Monitoring shall exist both in the form of automatic displays of status information at different locations, and in the form of explicit access to the required status information from any point.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a distributed monitoring infrastructure and impacts deployment topology, data flow, and system instrumentation.

requirement: Monitoring shall not affect the performance of ongoing observations.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a non-intrusive, low-overhead monitoring architecture to ensure observation performance remains unaffected.

requirement: Normally executed with the sequencer by providing a computer executable program, in order to make efficient use of the Gemini telescope.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the integration and execution flow between the sequencer and the program‐execution module, impacting system decomposition and interfaces.

requirement: Interactive operation is supported, but always through the Observatory Control System (OCS).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement centralizes all interactive operations through the OCS, impacting subsystem boundaries and interface design.

requirement: There is a visual user interface to the OCS to allow for changes to the viewing program.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the choice of UI architecture and its integration with back-end services, impacting layering and technology stack decisions.

requirement: It is possible to have more than one station participate in the observing.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s architecture by requiring support for concurrent multi-station communication, synchronization, and scalability across distributed components.

requirement: The observing program must be fully automated, requiring very little human interaction during the observation. The Gemini software must include a sufficiently rich programming environment to make this feasible.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for an embedded scripting/automation framework and extensible runtime in the system architecture.

requirement: There should be a full telescope simulator to enable the astronomer to test observing programs for completeness, errors, and functionality.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing a full telescope simulator mandates defining new subsystems, interfaces, and performance constraints that will shape the overall system architecture.

requirement: All control software must provide support for simulated use within the virtual telescope.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the introduction of a simulation subsystem and abstraction layers in the control software, impacting module boundaries and system integration.

requirement: There is a requirement for software to assist in object selection both within an observing program and across observing programs, in order to optimize observing efficiency.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates designing a scalable scheduling/optimization engine and associated data access layer to enable efficient cross-program object selection.

requirement: There must be software to support flexible scheduling, both manually and via a scheduler, allowing for the interleaving of observing programs in a manner that is transparent to the individual observing programs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement necessitates a dedicated, pluggable scheduling subsystem and resource‐management architecture to support dynamic and manual program interleaving.

requirement: It must be possible to queue all of the observing that is possible with the currently available instruments. It should be possible to resort the queue so that the next observation to take place comes to the front of the queue.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard scheduling feature that can be handled within the existing queue management component without broader architectural changes.

requirement: All software should be developed to permit remote operations. There should be no conceptual difference between software working on-site and remotely.
ASR_Explanation: Mandates a distributed, network-enabled architecture ensuring identical behavior, connectivity resilience, and session management for both on-site and remote use.

requirement: All observing facilities should work both on-site and off-site. It should be possible to do full operations remotely.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a distributed, networked architecture with secure remote-access and data-synchronization capabilities across on‐site and off‐site facilities.

requirement: Team observing, with multiple observers at different sites should be supported.
ASR_Explanation: Requires a distributed, scalable architecture with real-time synchronization and network communication to support multi-site observers.

requirement: It must be possible to restrict specific operations to specific remote sites. The method used to restrict such operations should be independent of the operations themselves, and dynamic.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a dynamic, decoupled policy enforcement layer, impacting security architecture and module boundaries.

requirement: Despite the obvious limitations introduced by the link bandwidths available at the different locations, the system shall be totally transparent to local or remote use.
ASR_Explanation: This transparency requirement drives key architectural choices around distribution, caching, and network performance optimization across varied bandwidth links.

requirement: Security of operation shall be considered and might imply different operation levels and privileges at different sites.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring configurable, site-specific access‐control and privilege‐management layers.

requirement: Remote operations software must be considered from the beginning in the Gemini 8m Telescopes software design, to avoid redesign later.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates a distributed, modular, and secure architecture to support remote telescope operations from the outset.

requirement: Remote control means that the function normally associated with a local Telescope Operator, that of entering telescope control commands, would be available from a remote site. In practice remote control will be restricted to the Gemini 8m Telescopes Enclosure and Gemini 8m Telescopes Control Facility.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces a distributed control architecture with secure remote connectivity, impacting networking, security, and system modularity.

requirement: Remote observing means that users shall be able to observe from a remote site such as Cerro Tololo, Hilo or even home Institutes. This possibility has to be provided on the Gemini 8m Telescopes.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the overall system architecture by introducing distributed control, network connectivity, latency management, and security considerations for remote operation.

requirement: Remote monitoring is a requirement for the Gemini 8m Telescopes operation and will complement service observing, making it friendlier for users.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing distributed data streaming, secure remote access, and integration with telescope control systems, impacting network and infrastructure architecture.

requirement: Remote access to the Gemini 8m Telescopes and its instruments is required for monitoring and diagnostic purposes. This must be possible from the Gemini 8m Telescopes base facility.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the network, security, and system integration architecture for remote control and monitoring infrastructure.

requirement: The observing program must be automated, requiring little human interaction during the observation. The Gemini software must include a sufficiently rich programming environment to make this feasible.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architecture to include an extensible automation framework and embedded programming environment, impacting module interfaces and system extensibility.

requirement: This programming environment should be available both to the astronomer, for developing the program, and to the observer, for review and adjustment of the program.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces multi-role access and review functionality, impacting the system’s authentication/authorization design and UI module architecture.

requirement: The programming environment should allow for the communication of special notes, instructions, and comments from the astronomer to the observer, possibly involving multimedia techniques.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI/communication feature and doesn’t impose new architectural constraints beyond routine module or service integration.

requirement: To achieve efficient service observing, queue observing, and flexible scheduling, it should be possible to carry out observations automatically, in accordance with predefined sequences of exposures.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates a core workflow and scheduling subsystem, driving the decomposition into orchestration, queue management, and device-control modules.

requirement: Interaction shall be allowed at the desired level (for example, only on error conditions, any time the user is allowed to break a sequence, etc.).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the control‐flow and event‐driven architecture by dictating how and when user interrupts or error‐condition interactions are handled.

requirement: Flexible scheduling requires the possibility to change telescope scheduling quickly, exchanging observing programs. This can be achieved only with the help of appropriate scheduling software, which must advise the operations team on the best choice to make which is compatible with the relative priorities of the various observing programs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a dynamic scheduling engine and its integration with priority management and user advisory interfaces, significantly shaping system components and interactions.

requirement: The syntax of control flow commands is to be consistent across the system, whether accessing workstation software or IOC software.
ASR_Explanation: This drives a unified command interface and parser design across subsystems, impacting overall system architecture and module interactions.

requirement: All subsystems must respond to a common set of commands to test operational status, inquiries as to version, perform self-tests, etc.
ASR_Explanation: Enforcing a shared command protocol drives the design of subsystem interfaces and middleware for uniform status and self-test handling.

requirement: The support structure for communicating commands must be reliable, with a uniform ACK/NAK protocol adopted across all systems. Timeouts must be supported at approximately 500 msec.
ASR_Explanation: This reliability and timing constraint dictates core communication protocol architecture and cross-system integration design.

requirement: Handshaking of commands between IOCs must occur within 100-200 msec, signaling acceptance of each command.
ASR_Explanation: Imposes strict sub-200 ms latency requirements on IOC communication, driving choices in protocols, buffering, and infrastructure.

requirement: For commands allowing delayed replies, timeouts for that reply must also be supported.
ASR_Explanation: This non-functional timeout requirement impacts the design of the command-processing and scheduling architecture, including timer management and resource allocation.

requirement: Peak control information within the system is expected to be 100 TPS. This assumes bridging between communication sections, to isolate traffic in relevant sections only.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the communication architecture, requiring segmented traffic bridges and capacity planning to sustain 100 TPS throughput.

requirement: Data from detectors must be stored in the most effective method permitted by available technology.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives critical decisions on storage technology, data architecture, and system performance optimizations.

requirement: For focusing and related activities, maximum acceptable detector readout time is about 0.1 sec, though only a portion of the detector may be read during that time.
ASR_Explanation: This sub-0.1 s detector readout time NFR drives data pipeline architecture, hardware selection, and real-time processing concurrency decisions.

requirement: For mosaicked, large optical detectors, a full readout of the detector must be done in about 2 or 3 minutes.
ASR_Explanation: This performance constraint drives the design of high-throughput data pipelines, hardware selection, and parallel readout architecture.

requirement: There must be the capability of providing multiple, simultaneous access to data. The LAN must support a transfer rate of 20-40 Mbits/second.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts network architecture and concurrency design by imposing specific bandwidth and simultaneous data access requirements.

requirement: Data is normally acquired as uncompressed data, but may be compressed using a loss-less compression technique for transmission from the Gemini system or across the system LAN.
ASR_Explanation: This lossless compression requirement impacts the design of data transmission protocols, network bandwidth planning, and the inclusion of compression/decompression modules.

requirement: Data from all instruments and detectors is stored as compressed data, using a standard format. There is a first level of storage within IOCs, to secure data in the event of link failures.
ASR_Explanation: This influences the system’s storage architecture by requiring localized IOC buffering, compression, and standardized data formats for reliability and interoperability.

requirement: Data is transmitted between Gemini and home Institutes using a FITS format and contains all header information provided with the data.
ASR_Explanation: Specifies the standard data interchange format and required metadata, impacting data serialization, validation, and interface design.

requirement: The system data capacity is capable of retaining 7 days of data produced by the largest instrument, the last 3 days of which must be available interactively from hard disk or similar medium.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the storage subsystem design and data lifecycle management, requiring tiered storage and performance planning.

requirement: The system must allow for fast transmission of rough images every 0.5 sec. This may be assisted through the use of data-loss compression techniques (e.g. JPEG, MPEG, etc).
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s streaming architecture, requiring choices around compression algorithms, throughput/latency optimizations, and network resource allocation.

requirement: There is the need for transmission of images matching the original resolution. This high-quality transmission must require less than 20 sec, and can only be assisted with loss-less compression.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives selection and integration of high-throughput networking, lossless compression components, and end-to-end performance guarantees in the system architecture.

requirement: A system of privileges must be established at each operating level of the system. These privileges should be determined in a simple manner during logging into the system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of authentication and authorization components, affecting security architecture, module boundaries, and access control flows.

requirement: Protection against accidental interference is to be implemented using an Access Mode Allocation system that dynamically identifies and assigns resources as needed. The allocation system must ensure that the system cannot remain deadlocked with respect to this resource allocation.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the concurrency and resource management architecture by mandating dynamic access mode allocation and deadlock‐avoidance mechanisms.

requirement: Procedures must be implemented for convenience and system integrity, to simplify and codify common tasks such as telescope start-up and shutdown, telescope system self-testing, instrument start-up and shut-down, instrument self-testing and self-diagnosis, configuration and reconfiguration, dynamic reconfiguration of observing configuration, and the control software should know what subsystems are installed and their status at all times.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of the system’s lifecycle management and dynamic reconfiguration framework, including component registry and health-monitoring services.

requirement: The Gemini software should have no hard restrictions on the number of simultaneous users, but should allow for policy decisions that do restrict the amount of simultaneous access.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s concurrency and scalability architecture by requiring dynamic, policy-driven access control.

requirement: Every command must be accepted/rejected within 2 sec and before the corresponding action occurs.
ASR_Explanation: This 2 sec acceptance latency constraint drives architectural decisions around request handling, queuing, and resource provisioning for performance.

requirement: Status display update must be within 4 sec at the local stations.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a strict 4 s latency requirement that affects communication protocols, data pipelines, and real-time processing architecture.
