requirement: Requests of subsystems for status information must be answered within 5 sec and be possible in maintenance level operation.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes strict latency and maintenance-mode availability constraints that drive high-level design of subsystem interfaces, resource allocation, and concurrency management.

requirement: All software bugs should be logged and then fixed as soon as possible after detection. The goal is to have restart conditions occur only on hardware failure.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of the system’s error-handling, logging, recovery, and fault-tolerance architecture.

requirement: Fault recovery, exception handling, fail-safe checks, etc. should be used to improve reliability.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architectural decisions around fault-tolerance patterns, global exception handling, and fail-safe design layers.

requirement: The telescope and instrument software shall contain built-in test (BIT) facilities to verify Gemini 8m Telescopes system and Gemini 8m Telescopes software performances.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes inclusion of built-in test frameworks and diagnostics interfaces, impacting module structure and system integration design.

requirement: Every Gemini 8m Telescopes software module shall have corresponding test specifications to check normal operation of releases, to be used both for acceptance tests and as an on-line test procedure.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the inclusion of test interfaces and instrumentation in the system architecture to support automated acceptance and online testing of every module.

requirement: The Gemini 8m Telescopes control software shall also provide for execution of self-test sequences of the Gemini 8m Telescopes system and subsystems. These shall automatically exercise all subsystems present in a given operational configuration.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates an extensible test orchestration layer with dynamic subsystem discovery, impacting modularity and component interfaces.

requirement: Regression tests should be a part of every Gemini 8m Telescopes software package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a CI/CD/process requirement for packaging and testing, not a change to the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: Subsystems must notify the user when faults occur. This notification must be specific as to origin and problem. The notification must also be capable of being electronically logged.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of cross-cutting error-handling, notification delivery, and logging framework integration across all subsystems.

requirement: Should a subsystem fail, predefined procedures must exist to redefine the environment in such a way that operation can restart with the remaining equipment.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts system resilience by requiring architectural support for failover, redundancy, and dynamic reconfiguration.

requirement: In case of computer hardware failure concerning the user station equipment, it shall be possible to transfer control from one user station to another via a simple software reconfiguration procedure.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s architecture by mandating built-in support for dynamic failover and modular reconfiguration across user stations.

requirement: In the case of IOC failure, there shall be a procedure to replace faulty cards and/or assemblies. If it is possible to observe with that particular IOC in a failed state, then it must be possible to reconfigure the system to do so.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s fault-tolerance and dynamic reconfiguration architecture to ensure continued operation and maintainability.

requirement: All communication shall be based on the use of standard communication protocols, where retry procedures are applied as part of the protocol.
ASR_Explanation: This mandate shapes the communication layer design by enforcing specific protocol choices and built-in retry/fault-tolerance mechanisms.

requirement: There should be no restrictions imposed by the software on users. Only policy decisions (permissions, access privileges, etc.) should prevent any user from accessing any part of the Gemini system from any local or remote station.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint drives the design of a decoupled access-control policy engine and influences module boundaries, security layers, and deployment topology.

requirement: Similar functionality should be presented to the users using similar user interfaces. However, user interfaces should clearly reflect access modes and operating levels.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the front-end architecture and component framework by enforcing a consistent UI design and dynamic rendering based on access modes and operating levels.

requirement: Commercial packages, off-the-shelf public domain software, and standards are to be used whenever feasible.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the architecture’s component selection and integration strategy, affecting modularity, interoperability, and dependency management.

requirement: Existing external software will be integrated with the Gemini software. The interfaces involved in this integration are considered part of the Gemini software system.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts system boundaries and interface design by introducing external dependencies that will affect coupling, scalability, and maintainability.

requirement: All Gemini software is to be developed using standard methodologies and development environments. One of the goals of Gemini software is that all components be easily (preferably automatically) combined into an integrated system.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the system’s modular decomposition, interface standards and automated integration/build pipeline architecture.

requirement: Gemini software developers should maintain accurate change logs showing software modifications as they are applied to the system software.
ASR_Explanation: It requires architecture-level support for an audit/logging subsystem to capture, store, and manage change events, impacting module design and data storage.

requirement: Gemini software developers should adhere to a standard method for the reporting and recording of errors from both internal and external sources.
ASR_Explanation: Mandates a unified error‐handling and logging framework as a cross-cutting concern, influencing middleware, component interfaces and framework selection.

requirement: Gemini software should be developed in evolutionary fashion, using the CVS version control system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a development process requirement (choice of VCS) and does not affect the system’s structural architecture.

requirement: All Gemini subsystem packages should include as part of the software both a simulator module for inclusion in the virtual telescope, and user interface modules for the user interface environments that the subsystem will be operating in.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the subsystem modular structure, integration points for simulation, and UI deployment interfaces, directly affecting the system’s architectural decomposition and runtime integration.

requirement: All Gemini software is to be fully documented, internally with appropriate comments, and external documentation. External documentation must include Unix-style man pages.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation-quality requirement that does not impose constraints on the software’s architectural structure.

requirement: All Gemini subsystem packages must provide modules for the testing and diagnosis of the subsystem.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates subsystem package structure and cross-cutting test/diagnosis modules, impacting component boundaries and interfaces.

requirement: All instrumentation control software must provide full access to all instrument functionality.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the overall system modularization and API/interface architecture to ensure comprehensive instrument access and control.

requirement: All Gemini software must be version labeled, both in source and binary form. The version information is to be retrievable from executing software via control commands.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts build and deployment processes for embedding version metadata but does not alter the core architectural structure or component interactions.

requirement: All hardware subsystems must provide a software simulation module that responds in reasonable fashion to commands directed at that hardware.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the architectural design of subsystem interfaces and layering by requiring integrated simulation modules for all hardware components.

requirement: Gemini subsystem should be as self-contained and autonomous as possible, thereby decreasing the functional width of the interface to the rest of the Gemini system.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the system’s modular architecture by enforcing low coupling and narrowly defined interfaces for the Gemini subsystem.

requirement: No subsystem package should make any assumptions about the surrounding environment beyond that provided in the interface specifications.
ASR_Explanation: This enforces strict modular boundaries and interface-based design, directly impacting system coupling, portability, and maintainability.

requirement: Control information on all controlled variables must be provided by all subsystems on request. No request for information shall produce a delay of control activities or locking, even if the corresponding equipment is not available or faulty.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes real-time, non-blocking information delivery with fault tolerance across subsystems, driving communication, caching, and availability architecture choices.

requirement: All such information is available to the Gemini 8m Telescopes software and is capable of being available to all users of the Gemini 8m Telescopes, subject only to restrictions with respect to updating. It must also be possible to restrict user access to such information.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates designing authentication/authorization layers and data access controls, impacting the system’s security and information architecture.

requirement: Detector data must be acquired and stored in the most effective way technology will allow; effectiveness should be evaluated in terms of cost, space requirements, longevity, and speed.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives high-level decisions on data ingestion pipelines, storage technologies, and infrastructure trade-offs to meet cost, capacity, durability, and throughput constraints.

requirement: Intermediate storage of raw data in memory on different nodes and in different formats should be kept to a minimum. However, there must be at least two copies - one to secure data as acquired and one to do assessment of data quality on-line.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the in-memory data distribution and replication strategy across nodes, directly shaping the system’s storage and processing architecture.

requirement: The link chosen to transfer data should represent as small a bottleneck as possible for data acquisition.
ASR_Explanation: It directly influences network topology and bandwidth provisioning decisions critical to system performance and scalability.

requirement: Astronomical data will have to be transported between GEMINI and the home institutes of visiting astronomers in FITS format.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates support for the FITS data format and associated transfer protocols, impacting data serialization, storage, and interface design.

requirement: TV data concerning site monitoring and voice need to be capable of being available at all operations facilities.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a distributed streaming and network infrastructure to ensure real-time TV and voice data availability at all facilities.

requirement: Other astronomical information such as that coming from sky field monitors, autoguider cameras and sky monitoring devices such as cloud and seeing monitors shall also be capable of being available.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the integration architecture by necessitating an extensible data‐acquisition layer and device-adapter framework for diverse monitoring sources.

requirement: Operation of telescope, instruments and subsystems can be carried out at three different levels: observing, maintenance, and test.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a mode-based architecture supporting distinct observing, maintenance, and test states with corresponding state management and access controls.

requirement: Privileges and protections are also important parameters to define user operations. Protections must also be enforced among users and the operational software should indicate clearly to users the current operation level and check the compatibility between subsystems in different modes.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement shapes the security and access-control architecture, mandates operational mode indicators, and drives inter-subsystem compatibility and enforcement mechanisms.

requirement: The capacity of the system can be expressed in terms of nodes, which is defined as the number of workstations, or in terms of users, which is defined as the sum total of users at all the nodes. Each node will have the capability to run at all operation levels.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s scalability and resource distribution architecture to support variable node counts and total user load with full operational capabilities per node.

requirement: The Gemini 8m Telescopes control software shall allow simultaneous operation of up to six active control nodes and up to two more monitoring nodes (one local and one remote) without appreciable degradation of performance.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a scalable, distributed concurrency and performance requirement that will drive choices in system topology, resource management, and inter-node communication.

requirement: The Gemini 8m Telescopes computers and software shall be capable of coping with the load of 10 active nodes, should the case arise.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts system scalability and resource management, requiring architectural provisions for load distribution and concurrency control across 10 nodes.

requirement: There must be automatic procedures to implement startup and shutdown of the telescope and instruments. These must allow startup and shutdown of instruments independently of the telescope and without affecting the telescope operation.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a lifecycle orchestration layer and modular component architecture to manage independent startup/shutdown without cross-component interference.

requirement: Reconfiguration procedures must exist, to change the observing environment. The definition of the observing environments must be dynamic, i.e. feasible during operations without the need to restart everything.
ASR_Explanation: It requires the architecture to support dynamic runtime reconfiguration of observing environments without restart, affecting module management and state consistency.

requirement: The operational software should know which subsystems are installed and operational at any given time.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a dynamic subsystem discovery and registry mechanism, impacting modularity, service binding, and runtime configuration.

requirement: The user interface should not be seen as a package linked to a specific computer. It should be network transparent so that it does not matter where it is being run.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a distributed, network-transparent client-server architecture affecting deployment topology, communication protocols, and technology choices.

requirement: The user interface tools shall be based on standards, which will be portable across different computer hardware platforms (Portable User Interface Toolkit).
ASR_Explanation: This non-functional requirement drives the architecture for selecting and designing an abstraction layer or toolkit to ensure cross-platform portability of the user interface.

requirement: Standard interfaces to the control electronics shall be available, in the form of standard bus systems, a standard set of interface cards to be used on all the subsystems and instruments, and a standard software skeleton running on the control electronics.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates the system’s interface architecture, impacting modularity, integration strategies, and technology choices across all control subsystems.

requirement: The standard software must be adequate for the real-time requirements and must offer drivers to the standard electronics to be used on all the Gemini 8m Telescopes software subsystems and instruments.
ASR_Explanation: Imposes real-time performance constraints and hardware driver support requirements that drive key architectural decisions.

requirement: Links between electronics interfaces and main processors must meet the requirements imposed by the data specifications.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the selection and design of processor‐to‐electronics interfaces, bus protocols, and data throughput architecture.

requirement: The Gemini 8m Telescopes software must interface to the external software and clearly the interfaces are fully part of the Gemini 8m Telescopes software.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines critical external integration points and interface boundaries, impacting system modularity and component interactions.

requirement: It shall be possible to monitor the quality (image quality, spectral resolution, signal to noise, etc.) of the astronomical data as it comes in.
ASR_Explanation: Requires designing real-time data ingestion and monitoring modules that impact the overall processing pipeline and resource allocation.

requirement: Standard reduction procedures should be available for basic on-line calibrations of the observed data.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates real-time calibration modules in the data‐processing pipeline, impacting system decomposition and performance architecture.

requirement: Quick-look data processing should be provided on the Gemini 8m Telescopes, with procedures suitable for fast on-line data preprocessing.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the design of a high-throughput, low-latency data processing pipeline and resource allocation for real-time preprocessing.

requirement: Quick-look should be usable within exposure sequences to provide results and feedback parameters to the control software in a programmed way, without the need for manual intervention.
ASR_Explanation: Integrating automated quick-look analysis into exposure sequences imposes new real-time data flow, interface, and timing constraints on the control software architecture.

requirement: Near-line processing should be available for simple data reductions required for data integrity validation.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates adding a near-line processing tier and associated data flow and storage integration, impacting the system’s runtime architecture.

requirement: Off-line pixel processing for full data reduction should also exist at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site, but does not have any interface to the Gemini 8m Telescopes software.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates a separate offline processing subsystem and storage deployment, impacting system decomposition and data pipeline integration.

requirement: The output format of the Gemini 8m Telescopes data must be compatible with the GEMINI archive requirements.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of data serialization and export modules to comply with external archive schemas, shaping the data-handling architecture.

requirement: On-line interactive access to the data archiving system should exist, so that access to this database is possible for Gemini 8m Telescopes users.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates an interactive online access layer, impacting the system’s tiered architecture, network protocols, security and scalability.

requirement: Computer access to star catalogues is also required, so that an automatic selection of candidate guide and standard stars can be made.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the data‐integration layer and external interface components for accessing and processing star catalogues.

requirement: The Gemini 8m Telescopes software must be able to interface with all commercial software packages available on the Gemini 8m Telescopes and integrated into the Gemini 8m Telescopes operation.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for a flexible integration architecture and interface abstraction to support all commercial software packages.

requirement: The LAN shall support the majority of the Gemini 8m Telescopes system internal communication needs. This LAN must be capable of dealing both with the data bandwidths required (at peak and on average) and with the required response times and synchronization needs.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes stringent bandwidth, latency, and synchronization constraints that will drive the LAN topology, hardware selection, and communication‐protocol architecture.

requirement: LAN and WAN interfaces shall be homogeneous and shall be based on standards which allow migration on different media, should they become required during the Gemini 8m Telescopes project life.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates standards-based, media-agnostic network interfaces, directly shaping the system’s modular design, technology selection, and future-proofing architecture.

requirement: A clear hierarchical model must be implemented, supporting separation of logical and physical layers, e.g. ISO/OSI model.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces a layered architecture with separation of concerns, directly impacting component organization, interfaces, and overall system decomposition.

requirement: Network redundancy should also be considered in the design phase as a way to increase reliability and security, in particular for control information.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the network and system architecture by requiring redundant communication paths and failover mechanisms for control data.

requirement: Access from any user station will make user stations in principle identical and software configurable as the user station of this or that subsystem.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives a uniform, configurable client architecture impacting deployment, subsystem integration, and UI modularity.

requirement: Computers used at the Gemini 8m Telescopes site, particularly in the test phase when they are outside the control room and near the subsystem under test, shall be checked against altitude and humidity specifications for the Gemini 8m Telescopes site.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes environmental constraints on hardware selection, deployment, and enclosure design, impacting the system’s physical architecture.

requirement: Computer hardware must be able to run the Gemini 8m Telescopes software environment (operating systems, Gemini 8m Telescopes software) and provide compatibility in data format (identical internal data representation).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains hardware, OS, runtime environment, and data representation, directly shaping platform selection and system integration.

requirement: Common development and implementation tools must be both available and supported.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the overall toolchain architecture by driving decisions on integration, build, deployment, and environment infrastructure.

requirement: Identical network access must be supported.
ASR_Explanation: It influences network and infrastructure design to ensure uniform connectivity, security, and performance across all client environments.

requirement: Local processing power must be such that telescope and instrument control does not represent a significant overhead in the whole process of executing an observing program.
ASR_Explanation: It drives hardware provisioning and software architecture for real‐time control, concurrency, and resource allocation to meet performance targets.

requirement: The choice of a scalable hardware architecture with computers at various performance levels should solve the problem of adequate on-line data assessment.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key decisions on hardware infrastructure and scalability patterns, directly affecting system performance and capacity.

requirement: Remote operation must include remote monitoring from the Gemini 8m Telescopes base facilities, together with access tools for diagnostic and test use.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of a distributed system with remote monitoring/diagnostic interfaces, network topology and security considerations.

requirement: Individual instruments must be able to run fully independently.
ASR_Explanation: This drives a need for a modular, decoupled architecture supporting independent deployment, runtime isolation, and inter-instrument communication.

requirement: Telescope software at the two telescopes must be maintained to be identical in the upper layers (even if hardware should differ).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement enforces a common layered software architecture with hardware abstraction, driving decisions on modularization and interface design.

requirement: Additions of new instruments should aim, as a goal, at introducing no modification to already operational parts. Modifications should be confined to the operational procedures and should not affect the bulk of the existing software.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives a modular, plugin-based architecture with strict encapsulation to ensure new instruments integrate without altering existing components.

requirement: Switching to different configurations must be possible at any time with appropriate procedures.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the architecture by requiring dynamic configuration management, necessitating runtime reload mechanisms and stability controls.

requirement: There must be easy procedures to reconfigure the system when subsystems are modified or removed.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the modular architecture by requiring dynamic reconfiguration mechanisms and loose coupling between subsystems.

requirement: The number of main packages of software must be kept to a minimum to facilitate maintenance, but compatibly with the need to have the right degree of modularity.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the modular decomposition and packaging strategy, directly impacting maintainability, coupling, and cohesion at the architectural level.

requirement: Commercial and public domain packages should be used whenever possible.
ASR_Explanation: It affects component sourcing and integration architecture by imposing licensing, compatibility, and maintenance constraints.

requirement: Existing software packages should be reused wherever possible.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR will shape component selection and dependency architecture by enforcing use of existing packages and impacting system modularity.

requirement: Existing software expertise should be consulted whenever possible.
ASR_Explanation: This is an organizational process guideline with no direct impact on system structure or architectural quality attributes.

requirement: All software which does not directly control specific hardware must be written as machine independent, portable code.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates platform abstraction and technology choices—impacting module boundaries, interfaces, and deployment architecture for cross‐platform portability.

requirement: To allow for expansion and maintenance, Gemini 8m Telescopes standards must be defined for the on-line software and the development environment.
ASR_Explanation: Defining coding and development environment standards shapes the system’s modularity, technology choices, and maintainability at the architectural level.

requirement: On-line version control must be implemented. That is, the version control system must be available to recover/restore versions at all times.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the architectural design for high availability, redundancy, and disaster-recovery mechanisms in the version control subsystem.

requirement: At boot time, the Gemini 8m Telescopes software shall check the consistency of versions of all the various software components.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s initialization architecture by requiring a startup-time version-consistency checker and associated dependency management.

requirement: Table-driven software should be used whenever possible, to avoid unnecessary compilations.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates a data-driven architectural style, affecting component design to support runtime configurability and reduce compile-time dependencies.

requirement: Changing system constants, such as arcseconds/bit for an encoder, shall not require recompiling but will be updated as part of system startup, and, for some constants, will be modifiable during operation.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the design of the configuration management and initialization subsystems to support dynamic loading and runtime modifiability.

requirement: System status parameters will be maintained to an extent that will allow restarting the system and regaining the previous state.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of state persistence and recovery mechanisms, impacting storage choices and fault-tolerance patterns.

requirement: Strict checking should be applied on this to preserve maintainability and reconfiguration of the system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives architectural decisions around rigorous validation mechanisms and modular configuration infrastructure to ensure maintainability and reconfigurability.

requirement: All subsystem software is to include modules to aid in the maintenance and testing of the subsystem. For example, each subsystem is to include a simulator that provides a reference behavior for that subsystem. Simple mechanisms should exist for replacing a subsystem with its simulation.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates designing each subsystem with replaceable simulation modules and test harness interfaces, affecting module boundaries, interfaces, and deployment configuration.

requirement: Each subsystem should have a background task running whenever that subsystem is operational, performing such tasks as checking power supply levels, temperatures, performance, correct responses to commands. The OCS is to be notified of any detected problems.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the introduction of cross-cutting monitoring tasks, scheduling constraints, inter-subsystem communication and centralized fault-reporting interfaces.

requirement: Each subsystem should provide a module for fully exercising all subsystem components, both hardware and software. This module is executed automatically during start-up and on demand through the defined interface. Problems are to be automatically reported to the OCS via the defined interface.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the inclusion of diagnostic modules, startup sequencing, and standard interfaces, directly shaping the system’s architecture.

requirement: There are also software modules for testing the subsystem as an integrated portion of the entire system. This software would be executed on demand during maintenance operation level.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the system’s architecture by mandating integration points and deployment of on-demand maintenance testing modules.

requirement: Quantitative maintenance requirements will be allocated to the system, subsystems, and each component. The requirements must be achievable and stated in such a way that verification is permitted.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the system’s modular decomposition and component interface design to ensure maintainability metrics are allocable and verifiable.

requirement: All equipment shall support a programmed adjustment and maintenance interval of 30 days or longer.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a scheduling and monitoring subsystem to track, enforce, and report on periodic maintenance intervals.

requirement: All Gemini Software must be designed with human engineering requirements under consideration.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the UI and interaction-layer architecture, dictating choice of frameworks, component modularity, and design patterns to satisfy ergonomic usability constraints.

requirement: The Gemini system must be self-monitoring to invoke safety monitoring to prevent risk to people or damage to equipment. The software should be able to quickly bring the Gemini system to a safe state upon notification of such danger.
ASR_Explanation: Requires a real-time safety-monitoring and emergency shutdown subsystem, impacting system modularity, fault-tolerance, and resource allocation.

requirement: Subsystems must be able to detect such danger and report it appropriately. In the event that the risk persists, subsystems must be able to move themselves into safe states to protect people and equipment.
ASR_Explanation: It requires safety-critical architectural patterns for hazard detection, reporting, and automatic transition to fail-safe states.

requirement: Safety protection must be applied whenever there is the risk that the actions of the control software could endanger people or cause damage to any Gemini 8m Telescopes subsystem.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical requirement drives the high-level architecture by mandating dedicated safety subsystems, redundancy, and fail‐safe interlocks.

requirement: The Gemini 8m Telescopes software shall be able to bring the Gemini 8m Telescopes system quickly to a safe state upon detection of danger. Safety aspects shall be analyzed during the functional specification phase of the software.
ASR_Explanation: This safety-critical requirement drives architectural decisions around real-time hazard detection, fault tolerance, and emergency shutdown mechanisms.

requirement: Security must be provided in order to both prevent accidental mix-up of commands from different users on different parts of the system and to prevent intrusion from the wide area network into the Gemini 8m Telescopes.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of authentication, authorization, encryption, and network isolation mechanisms, shaping system and network architecture.

requirement: A system that is operating in Engineering/Maintenance mode must ignore directives from other systems, though status information should still be provided for use by other systems.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a mode-based control flow layer in the architecture to block incoming directives while still exposing status interfaces.

requirement: There should be security preventing the intrusion into the system by unauthorized users, or users at unauthorized access levels.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design and integration of authentication/authorization components and security layers across the system architecture.

requirement: All systems are appropriately interlocked. This interlock must not depend on any software for reliable operation.
ASR_Explanation: Mandates hardware-based fail-safe interlocks separate from software, driving a hardware–software partitioning in the system architecture.

requirement: The software must be strictly modular, i.e. the functionality of a subsystem should correspond to that which belongs to that subsystem and only to that, so that software for different subsystems can be installed and maintained independently of all the rest.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates clear subsystem boundaries and deployment isolation, driving the component decomposition and coupling/cohesion in the overall architecture.

requirement: It also important that there are no undesired interactions between subsystems. This may be enforced either at the client/server interface or at the message system level.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint affects the system’s layering and interface/messaging architecture by dictating how subsystems interact and enforce boundaries.

requirement: The security and safety of the system should be guaranteed even in the event of failure of any component, including the higher-level software.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a fault-tolerant, secure-by-design architecture with redundancy, isolation, and fail-safe mechanisms to maintain security and safety under component failures.

requirement: Data redundancy is also a requirement, to prevent a single failure from causing the loss of collected data. The goal is to minimize the effects of single-point errors throughout the system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of distributed storage, replication mechanisms, and failover strategies to eliminate single points of failure.

requirement: The Telescope Control System should be capable of detecting and invoking parallel operation as it is responsible for control of all of the telescope and enclosure subsystems.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the system’s concurrency architecture by requiring detection, scheduling, and coordination of parallel operations across subsystems.

requirement: The Gemini 8m Telescopes software has to be developed according to a structured and formally defined development model.
ASR_Explanation: This process-oriented requirement governs the development methodology rather than imposing constraints on the system’s architecture.

requirement: The development environment for the Gemini 8m Telescopes software consists of the computer hardware and system software (operating system, languages and tools) chosen optimally to support the development model.
ASR_Explanation: This is a development‐process/tooling requirement and does not impact the system’s structural or runtime architecture.

requirement: Test procedure methods have to be defined in the Software Test Plan (STP), while test plans shall be written for all individual software packages and modules comprising the Gemini 8m Telescopes software.
ASR_Explanation: This is a test‐process documentation requirement and does not drive core architectural structure or design decisions.

requirement: A formal release system should exist at package and module level, which should be checkable on-line by the operational procedures for consistency. Every system must thus be able to supply its current version upon request.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of your deployment and versioning architecture by enforcing standardized packaging, metadata interfaces, and runtime version discovery.

requirement: All software is to be developed using typical modularization and standardization techniques. In particular, each module's environment is strictly defined by its interface to other components. No module can rely upon information outside of this interface.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates strict module boundaries and interface contracts, directly shaping system decomposition, component interactions, and coupling/cohesion.

requirement: The on-line databases can be considered part of this interface, but are only accessible through their defined interfaces.
ASR_Explanation: It defines strict component boundaries and access constraints that shape the system’s layering and module coupling.

requirement: A measure of fault rates should be done during commissioning to establish baseline rates for system reliability monitoring.
ASR_Explanation: It requires embedding telemetry and monitoring infrastructure into the system architecture to capture, store, and analyze fault‐rate metrics.

requirement: There are to be recovery procedures to restart after error failure.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates fault-tolerance and recovery mechanisms, impacting the design of error-handling, state management, and restart subsystems.

requirement: During science planning, there should be validity and feasibility checks to help ensure effective and efficient use of the telescope. Where appropriate, these checks should also be performed during operation.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates integrating validity and feasibility check components into both planning and operational workflows, impacting module decomposition, interfaces, and performance constraints.

requirement: The system should be constantly monitoring active subsystems to be sure they are operating correctly before sending commands to each subsystem. This monitoring should continue on inactive subsystems.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for architecture-level health-check and monitoring infrastructure (heartbeats, fault detection) across all subsystems, impacting reliability and performance.

requirement: All telescope and instrument parameters are kept in an on line database to permit easy implementation of table-driven applications.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a centralized online database for all parameters, driving decisions around data persistence, schema design, and application architecture.

requirement: All telescope, instrument, and detector control information is to be available at any operation level.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for a scalable, real-time data distribution and middleware architecture to ensure control information is always available at every operation layer.

requirement: Access times to the database are to be in the range of 2-3 msec per access.
ASR_Explanation: This strict performance requirement drives architectural decisions around database technology selection, caching strategies, and infrastructure provisioning.

requirement: Asynchronous writes are to be supported, allowing for concurrent operation.
ASR_Explanation: Supports asynchronous writes will drive choices in concurrency models, I/O frameworks, and thread or event‐driven architecture.

requirement: Time-access critical information is available in memory.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates an in-memory data storage and caching architecture to guarantee low-latency access, influencing memory management and overall system design.

requirement: There is to be a consistent and logical (i.e. name based) access method.
ASR_Explanation: This is a naming convention guideline that affects implementation details rather than core architectural structure or qualities.

requirement: The database must support both remote access and distributed data.
ASR_Explanation: It affects data and network architecture by requiring a distributed database topology with replication, consistency, and remote access protocols.

requirement: The internal (within the IOC) implementation of this database is to be based on EPICS.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying EPICS as the internal database framework dictates the IOC’s software architecture, module interfaces, and integration approach.

requirement: All operational capability found in on site operations is extended to remote operations, with some degradation in performance resulting from WAN bandwidth considerations.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the distribution and deployment architecture and performance design to support remote operations over WAN links.

requirement: There must be some form of security to control access to system features, possibly restricting some operations to specific remote sites.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of an authentication/authorization subsystem and network access controls for feature-level and site-specific security.

requirement: All additional data that is not required on line (configuration information, detailed documentation, operation logs, etc.) are stored in a relational DBMS.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the data persistence architecture and DBMS selection for off-line data, affecting storage scalability and integration.

requirement: One criteria is that sufficient information be recorded during an observation to recreate the sequence of events that occurred during the observation. This requires that all input and output data be logged appropriately.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of the logging and storage infrastructure to ensure full auditability, retention, and performance.

requirement: Automatic operation is the normal mode of operation. The observation is performed through a preplanned program requiring little or no interaction with the observer.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the need for an automation/scheduling framework and minimal-interaction user interface, impacting core system structure.

requirement: Interactive operation. Science planning and program changes are accomplished through interactive operation. Is is possible to enter interactive operation from automatic operation to handle exceptional conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the core control-flow architecture by mandating dynamic mode-switching state management between automatic and interactive operation.

requirement: Normal operation is possible at all operation levels (observing, maintenance, and test) and applies to the following conditions (where appropriate) on any subsystem: Observing, Stand-by, Maintenance and testing.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a unified state-management architecture to handle observing, standby, maintenance, and test modes across all subsystems.

requirement: Overall performance of the Gemini telescope is defined as the percentage of viewable time during which exposures have been taken. To improve this performance, all possible concurrencies in system operation should be used.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the system’s concurrency and parallel processing architecture, impacting thread management, component coordination, and resource scheduling.

requirement: Best use of concurrency occurs when using the Sequencer.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the concurrency architecture by mandating a Sequencer-based design pattern for optimal performance.

requirement: There are start-up and shut-down procedures that must exist at many different levels: Cold start-up should take about 5 minutes, warm start-up about 1 minute, telescope start-up about 4 minutes, instrument start-up 2 minutes or less.
ASR_Explanation: The specified start-up time constraints will drive architectural decisions around initialization sequencing, concurrency, and resource management across system layers.

requirement: There must be a way to shut down all subsystems (hardware and software).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a global shutdown orchestration layer affecting hardware interfaces, software modules, and error‐handling flows across the entire architecture.

requirement: All start-ups and shut-downs are to be automatically logged with time stamps, to allow for statistics on system availability.
ASR_Explanation: Requires the design and integration of a logging/monitoring subsystem to capture and store lifecycle events, impacting observability and data storage architecture.

requirement: System logging information should include all important events, properly time-stamped and indexed. The goal is to be able to recreate the steps in a observation from the system logs.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of cross-cutting logging infrastructure, including storage, indexing, time-synchronization, and performance/scalability trade-offs.

requirement: It must be possible to log engineering data at up to 200 Hz rates for short periods of time. This data must be available to external software packages for analysis.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of a high-throughput data acquisition, buffering, storage, and external integration architecture.

requirement: Long-term logging of engineering data must be possible at slower (1 Hz or less) rates, into a common format (baselined as SYBASE).
ASR_Explanation: It drives the persistence architecture, mandating Sybase-based long-term time-series logging and affecting database schema, storage scaling, and ingestion pipelines.

requirement: All errors should be reported using a common, system-wide procedure. There are three types of errors: Fatal, Serious, Warning.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a centralized, cross‐cutting error‐handling subsystem, shaping module interfaces, dependencies, and logging infrastructure.

requirement: Besides the time-stamp, error logging should provide enough information to trace the condition back to its apparent source, both in equipment and in event sequence.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the selection and design of the logging infrastructure, data schema, and traceability mechanisms across equipment and event sequences.

requirement: There should be tools available to extract error (and other) logging information by subsystem component, time-sequence, previous events, and so on.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of a centralized, extensible logging framework and storage solution, impacting cross-cutting concerns and system observability architecture.

requirement: The Gemini Control System formally distinguishes alarms from errors. Errors result from failures to successfully complete commands, while alarms represent asynchronous failures.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates separate architectural paths for synchronous command errors versus asynchronous alarms, impacting the design of error-handling and event-management components.

requirement: There are two types of alarm conditions. The first are automatically monitored alarms, which exist as long as the errors persist and are then automatically cleared. The second type of alarm require human acknowledgment to clear.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the alarm management architecture by requiring separate state-handling, persistence and UI/acknowledgment workflows.

requirement: In addition to start-up procedures, there must be well-defined recovery procedures for any subsystem that has become inoperative.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s fault-tolerance architecture by requiring specific recovery patterns and components for subsystem restart and state restoration.

requirement: Command retries must be included in the system for most common timeouts or no-response conditions. These retries should occur automatically in the command handling to avoid unnecessary error conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the system’s error-handling architecture by requiring built-in retry logic, timeout management, and idempotency support in the command processing pipeline.

requirement: The performance of error-mode recovery is specific to the subsystem and is defined in the Functional specification for that subsystem.
ASR_Explanation: This performance-related NFR for error-mode recovery will drive architectural decisions on subsystem fault-handling strategies, resource allocation, and timing constraints.

requirement: Failure conditions should not cascade. That is, failure of one subsystem should not affect other, working, subsystems, including communication links.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the overall system’s fault-isolation and resilience architecture, dictating how subsystems are decoupled, monitored, and fail independently.

requirement: All Gemini supported software is to be developed using a formally defined model. The Ward/Mellor approach to developing real-time systems is used.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying the Ward/Mellor formal modeling approach directly constrains the system’s architectural design, tooling, and process for real-time software.

requirement: The upper levels (User-interface and OCS) are assumed to not require a real-time operating environment. However, the operating environment at these levels is expected to provide sufficient performance for both human interaction and communications.
ASR_Explanation: It drives key platform and performance decisions for the UI and communications layers, impacting OS choice and concurrency architecture.

requirement: Real-time support is required at the IOC level.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates choosing real-time OS/middleware and designing scheduling, concurrency, and I/O architecture to guarantee deterministic latency at the IOC level.

requirement: The development operating system is UNIX System V, Release 4, using only POSIX compliant calls to the operating system.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the target platform and dictates the system abstraction layer design by limiting available OS services to POSIX-compliant calls.

requirement: The GUI systems are to use X-windows, version 11, release 5 (X11R5) or release 6 (X11R6). The principle language for developing GUIs is Tcl/Tk.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes specific technology stack constraints (X11R5/R6 and Tcl/Tk) that directly influence the GUI layer’s architecture, dependencies, and deployment environment.

requirement: The GNU software tool suite should be used where possible.
ASR_Explanation: This is a build/toolchain preference and does not drive the system’s fundamental architectural structure.

requirement: CVS is the recommended version control system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a development tool choice affecting project process but not the software’s architecture.

requirement: The IOCs will use the real-time operating system VxWorks (from Wind River Systems).
ASR_Explanation: This mandates the RTOS platform, impacting system layering, hardware drivers, real-time scheduling, and middleware integration.

requirement: The communication interface between the IOCs and Unix workstations is to be IMP.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the IPC protocol between IOCs and Unix workstations, impacting middleware choice and overall integration architecture.

requirement: IOC software development is to be done using a common development environment, as specified in the Software Programming Standards (SPS).
ASR_Explanation: This is a development process/tooling standard and does not impose direct constraints on the system’s architecture.

requirement: The host workstation operating system is Unix, currently baselined as Solaris 2.3 or above.
ASR_Explanation: This OS baseline requirement constrains the deployment platform and drives compatibility, integration, and low‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The installed target IOC operating system is VxWorks, with EPICS as the interface.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates the real-time OS and middleware stack, impacting platform selection, scheduling design, and module integration with EPICS.

requirement: Communication hardware independence is accomplished by using the DARPA TCP/IP communication protocol over LAN's and the WAN.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating DARPA TCP/IP for hardware abstraction dictates the core network stack design and interoperability architecture.

requirement: The software interface between workstation and IOC is to use DRAMA's IMP protocol for all control communication, using SDS as the command structure.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying DRAMA’s IMP protocol with SDS command structures dictates key communication and integration patterns, impacting the system’s networking and component interaction architecture.

requirement: Interprocess communication on the host workstations is through the same IMP protocol.
ASR_Explanation: Using a single IMP protocol for IPC dictates the system’s communication middleware and integration architecture.

requirement: Communications between real-time components is to based on the EPICS Channel Access protocol.
ASR_Explanation: This protocol mandate shapes the system’s integration layer, network topology, and real-time component interaction design.

requirement: Data communications are typically through IMP/SDS.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying IMP/SDS for data communications drives the choice of middleware layers, network protocols, and integration topology.

requirement: All communication hardware must meet the data flow requirements and software protocols defined in this document.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint on communication hardware and protocols directly shapes system architecture by dictating hardware selection and data flow patterns.

requirement: The control LAN is based on Ethernet IEEE 802.3.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying IEEE 802.3 Ethernet for the control LAN dictates hardware interfaces, networking protocols, and topology, directly shaping the system’s connectivity architecture.

requirement: The detector LAN is also based on Ethernet, at least for the development systems. A solution meeting the performance requirements of the final system is to be decided later.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the network infrastructure and performance constraints that will drive key architectural decisions on topology, protocols, and technologies.

requirement: The backbone LAN cannot be Ethernet because of bandwidth requirements. A FDDI system is recommended for the backbone LAN.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying FDDI over Ethernet dictates the backbone network technology and impacts system communication infrastructure and performance.

requirement: Interlock connections must be provided for all critical subsystems. The software interface to the interlock system is defined in the Instrument Control System Infrastructure Work Package Definition.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes critical safety and interface constraints that shape the system’s modular structure and hardware‐software integration.

requirement: Wherever possible, Gemini software is to take advantage of existing software. However, all existing software is to be evaluated in terms of the specifications given here.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the architecture’s component integration and dependency management strategy by mandating evaluation and reuse of existing software.

requirement: All commands contain the same general structure: Identification (Command_ID, Source, Target) and Instruction (Opcode, Parameter set).
ASR_Explanation: It defines the command message schema impacting interface definitions, protocol design, and serialization layers.

requirement: All Gemini systems must respond to generic commands such as GetVersion, GetStatus, GetState, GetID, GetConfiguration, SetStatus, SetConfiguration, RunTest, SetLogging, OpenRoute, CloseRoute, ResetRoute, TestRoute, AwaitItem, SetCallback, ClearCallback, LocateItem, GetValue, PutValue, StartStream, StopStream, SetEvent, ClearEvent.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement enforces a standardized command interface that shapes the system’s API boundaries, communication middleware, and module interaction architecture.

requirement: Within the OCS, all commands are represented as ASCII strings. Fields within the command are labelled and nesting is permitted using braces.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the command message format and parsing strategy, directly impacting the system’s communication protocol and data-handling architecture.

requirement: The most important requirement is the stability of the system on a high-level user application level, which is a consequence of the availability of basic network and terminal functionality.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around system resilience, fault tolerance, and network/terminal integration.

requirement: System failures caused by user operation or network malfunctions have to be avoided.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for a fault-tolerant, highly available architecture with robust error handling, retry policies and redundancy for network resilience.

requirement: Dead ends in dialogs have to be addressed by a special help system and contextual understanding has to be provided.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates introducing and integrating a contextual understanding component and special help subsystem into the dialog-management architecture.

requirement: The system must guide users throughout the interaction process quickly and effectively and it has to be designed to avoid cognitive overloads caused by information overflow.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR influences the UI architecture by driving choices in componentization, information flow control, and performance optimizations to support rapid, cognitively light interactions.

requirement: The accesspoint has to be controllable through different modalities.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for interface abstraction and modular adapters to support multiple control modalities, impacting overall system architecture.

requirement: A switch between modalities must not affect the system operations, consequently there should be no need to re-initiate the dialog status.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the architecture by mandating a modality-agnostic session/state management layer to preserve dialog status across input modes.

requirement: It should be possible to switch the current context without losing perspective in case the system does not estimate the context properly.
ASR_Explanation: Requires architectural support for robust context/state management and persistence to enable seamless context switching without loss.

requirement: There is an urgent need for extensibility of the software system.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts core architectural patterns by requiring modular decomposition and plugin/extension mechanisms.

requirement: The monolithic implementation which is used at the moment does not support modelling of upcoming functionality requests and it is very difficult to maintain and extend.
ASR_Explanation: It drives a need to evolve the system’s architecture from a monolith to a modular/service-based design to improve maintainability and extensibility.

requirement: All application interfaces for process communications must be carefully designed.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the definition of interface contracts, communication protocols and integration patterns, impacting system modularity and interoperability.

requirement: Better development tools to handle the speech system on an abstract level are required to improve the process of extending its rule database.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s modular architecture by requiring well-defined abstraction layers and extension interfaces for tool-driven rule database updates.

requirement: Context engines need to be implemented rather than interfaces and the method of choice are neural networks.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates using neural network–based context engines instead of simple interfaces, impacting component design, technology stack, and infrastructure planning.

requirement: New tools are needed for training purposes and should be able to derive input from rule databases which are used at the moment.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR requires new integration components and data‐flow architecture to connect training tools with the existing rule databases.

requirement: The solid user interface requires high standards in ergonomics and robustness.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives UI architecture choices, dictating framework selection, ergonomic design patterns and robustness/error-handling strategies.

requirement: The system needs to have a 'subtle notion' of its services to guide users quickly and effectively.
ASR_Explanation: This UI/UX guidance can be handled within the front-end design without imposing new architectural constraints.

requirement: The terminal proactively offers its services, i.e. must be able to attract and help people.
ASR_Explanation: It requires an event-driven, context-aware recommendation and notification subsystem, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: It must be self-explainable and understand users' needs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for AI-driven user modeling and adaptive UI components, affecting system modularization and service integration.

requirement: It must also be able to handle context specific dialogs.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a context-tracking dialogue management component, impacting module structure and state management architecture.

requirement: Dialogs which it doesn't understand clearly should be handled properly to limit user frustration.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of the dialog management and error-handling subsystems by requiring built-in fallback strategies to manage misunderstood user inputs.

requirement: Context switches must be supported at any state of the session and the system should offer alternatives and rank them by guessing its likelihood.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the overall session management and ranking subsystems, necessitating architectural provisions for dynamic context handling and probabilistic alternative selection.

requirement: The graphical and speech user interface need to complement each other.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architecture of the UI layer by mandating integrated frameworks and synchronization mechanisms for graphical and speech interfaces.

requirement: The information flow must be compatible with average user capabilities to avoid cognitive overload.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the UI/information architecture and component design for progressive disclosure and data flow patterns to manage cognitive load.

requirement: The graphical user interface model presents information in an abstract two dimensional way and supports virtual characters.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the choice and layering of the UI framework and rendering engine to support abstract 2D visuals and virtual character integration.

requirement: The following adaptivity specifications are limited to single user sessions.
ASR_Explanation: This limitation impacts the architecture of session-state management and storage by restricting adaptivity to single-user contexts.

requirement: Contextual understanding is an example and support of context switches without explicit requests are another.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of context-tracking and state-management components for seamless context switches.

requirement: The interaction process should be as natural as possible and the development should ultimately enable natural language understanding.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates integrating NLP/ML engines and data pipelines, impacting component selection, service orchestration, and infrastructure design.

requirement: The system should be able to detect people automatically and doesn't need to be approached by a subject.
ASR_Explanation: This FR impacts the system architecture by requiring integration of automatic people‐detection algorithms, sensor selection, and real‐time processing pipelines.

requirement: It should be able to initiate the first step and react adaptively because it can recognize people and distinguish humans from other life, e.g. dogs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a computer-vision/ML subsystem and real-time adaptive control loops in the overall system architecture.

requirement: Speaker independent voice recognition is essential in public space applications.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the audio processing and machine-learning pipeline architecture, including hardware selection, noise-cancellation modules, and real-time inference strategies.

requirement: The terminal is designed to support classical input-output channels like typing or reading, and complement them with speech and visual capabilities.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of input/output abstraction layers and multimedia subsystems for integrating typing, speech, and visual interfaces.

requirement: Visual and audio surveillance enable user detection and language understanding through face and speech recognition.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the integration of real-time ML-based vision and audio processing pipelines, impacting component selection, data flow, and infrastructure.

requirement: Mode switches which are not initiated by the system are only possible through input channels.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement constrains the mode‐switch mechanism and input interface design, impacting component boundaries and interaction flows.

requirement: Output presentation is determined by the system and the user has no direct influence in this case.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level presentation constraint without significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: If desired the user can override default settings, but because of privacy issues, certain output channels are preferred.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the design of the configuration management and output-routing subsystems to enforce privacy-preferred channels.

requirement: Simulation and testing will provide further insight into the integration process and give more practical hints on usability, before new features materialize.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates designing a modular, testable architecture with built-in simulation environments and integration hooks to validate usability early.

requirement: The system functionality should be extensible and able to be integrated into the simulation environment to interface existing and new features at the same time.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives a modular, plugin-based architecture with well-defined integration interfaces for existing and new features.

requirement: The simulation environment should provide an abstract high level scripting language thus providing an interpreter.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the overall architecture by mandating integration of a scripting engine, defining module boundaries and execution/runtime model.

requirement: To ensure extensibility of the software system, an open architecture has been chosen.
ASR_Explanation: Choosing an open architecture dictates the system’s modular structure, interfaces, and extensibility mechanisms, driving high-level structural and integration decisions.

requirement: All functionalities will be implemented using components or even distributed components in a client-server oriented approach.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates a client-server, distributed component architecture affecting system decomposition, communication protocols, and deployment topology.

requirement: The implementations of the context engine with near natural language capabilities, the multiple modality support functions and the speech and graphical interfaces are heavily component based.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates a component-based architecture for core engines and interfaces, directly shaping system modularization and interface layering.

requirement: The authoring tool should allow easy modification of the behaviour of user interfaces.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a modular, extensible UI architecture (e.g. plugin‐ or script‐driven) to separate and configure behavior independently.

requirement: The authoring tool should support easy development of adaptive user interfaces.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the system architecture to include a flexible, extensible UI adaptation framework (model-driven composition, rule engines, dynamic modules).

requirement: The authoring tool should allow the functional requirements specification of the next generation of consumer products to be developed in a much faster and efficient manner.
ASR_Explanation: This is a high-level functional productivity goal with no clear cross-cutting concerns or technical constraints impacting the system’s architecture.

requirement: The authoring tool provides a set of standard widgets.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional feature that doesn’t impose major structural or cross-cutting constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: The authoring tool provides for each project a project repository containing a font definition, a colour palette, bitmap images and strings.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward content‐storage requirement that doesn’t drive high‐level structural or technology decisions.

requirement: The authoring tool allows the user to manage a palette and its colours.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI-level feature (palette CRUD) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The authoring tool allows the user to import bitmaps from an external source.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine I/O feature (file import) with limited impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The authoring tool allows the user to simulate a user interface on a PC.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific functional feature without broad structural or platform‐level impact on the overall architecture.

requirement: The authoring tool provides a complex widget containing a menu structure and a navigation function.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI widget specification and does not drive high-level architectural structure or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The authoring tool should provide a menu editor.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI feature with limited impact on the system’s high‐level architecture.

requirement: The authoring tool allows the user to generate C code for a target platform, e.g. a DVD player.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts system architecture by requiring a modular, extensible code‐generation engine with platform‐specific abstraction layers.

requirement: The authoring tool should be able to assist the user in the authoring process by a wizard.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR describes a UI wizard feature and does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: The tool offers the UI developer a more flexible way of developing a UI (off-line adaptivity).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for an offline-capable UI adaptation engine with caching and state-management layers, impacting the overall component architecture.

requirement: The authoring tool should be able to support specification, simulation and code generation of UI aspects related to these i/o peripherals.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the modular architecture by requiring integration of specification, simulation, and code‐generation engines with the UI and I/O peripheral interfaces.

requirement: A Multimodal user interface for the authoring tool is a possible requirement for future versions.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the architecture by requiring new multimodal interaction components and extensions to the presentation layer and integration middleware.

requirement: Simulations are a way to get early feedback on a UI that is being developed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a development process/tooling requirement for early UI feedback and does not impose structural or quality constraints on the system’s architecture.

requirement: Optimal Video performance on most popular video signals.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around codec support, hardware acceleration, and optimized processing pipelines for varied video signals.

requirement: Environmental Specifications Compliance.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains hardware and system architecture choices (enclosures, cooling, materials) to meet environmental tolerance and compliance standards.

requirement: Event Driven Approach.
ASR_Explanation: Choosing an event-driven approach drives major architectural decisions around asynchronous messaging, component decoupling, scalability, and infrastructure.

requirement: The firmware should allow extensions of the functionality.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for a modular, plugin‐style architecture with well‐defined extension points and stable interfaces.

requirement: The software structure of the firmware should be suitable for adding a ‘UI-interpreter’ (to interpret and visualize the UI code generated by the UI Editor).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the firmware’s modular, layered design and extension points to support integrating a UI-interpreter.

requirement: The UI interpreter (=VM) should show a “start menu” (defined in the UI by the UI Editor) when the system is powered up.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic UI feature with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The UI interpreter should interpret the downloaded UI code to navigate through the menus and perform defined actions.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a dynamic interpreter component for runtime UI code loading and navigation, impacting component modularity and extensibility.

requirement: The UI interpreter should control the interference between the defined UI and the environmental conditions, such as overruling user-defined UI elements and actions by an “emergency” UI and behavior.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a runtime priority-based UI override mechanism integrating environmental conditions, impacting core UI architecture, event handling, and safety design.

requirement: System Configuration Wizard.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a guided configuration component that impacts module initialization, persistence design, and UI-layer integration.

requirement: Font authoring modalities.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific feature-level requirement (font authoring options) that doesn’t drive major architectural structure or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The UI Editor should allow the user to create/modify menus.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI feature affecting only the menu management component with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: The UI Editor should enable the user to associate an event to an action.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI feature that doesn’t drive or alter the overall system architecture.

requirement: The UI Editor should also dispose of a facility to associate an event to more than one action.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine enhancement of the existing event‐handling subsystem that can be implemented without major architectural changes.

requirement: The UI Editor should provide a compile step to generate code on the development platform.
ASR_Explanation: It requires integrating a compile/code-generation pipeline into the editor’s core architecture and build process.

requirement: The Editor also allows downloading the generated code in the embedded Vetronics system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system architecture by necessitating an integration layer and deployment protocol for downloading generated code into the embedded Vetronics hardware.

requirement: The UI Editor should be a user friendly and powerful development tool.
ASR_Explanation: This is a high-level usability NFR without concrete constraints that would drive architectural decisions.

requirement: Attention for visualization and simulation issues (WYSIWYG approach, e.g. Visualization of the designed UI, menus, icons, Simulating navigation etc.).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts architecture by requiring a real-time UI rendering and simulation layer, dictating choice of frameworks, layering, and component integration.

requirement: The Windows guidelines should be followed.
ASR_Explanation: Conformance to Windows guidelines will drive the choice of UI frameworks and high-level architectural patterns for the application.

requirement: Attention for solutions that speed up the development of the UI (e.g. investigate the use of drag-and-drop tools such as in the context of action definition).
ASR_Explanation: Selecting drag-and-drop UI tools will drive the choice and integration of the UI framework and code-generation approach, impacting layer boundaries and maintainability.

requirement: Attention for general usability issues and user interface design.
ASR_Explanation: This is a broad usability/UI guideline without specific constraints or trade-offs that would drive or alter the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: Plug-In-approach of the program: all functionality could easily be ‘Plugged’ into the editor.
ASR_Explanation: Requires defining extension points and a plugin framework, influencing modularity, deployment, and runtime loading mechanisms.

requirement: The UI Editor should support a component-based approach where all functionality could easily be ‘Plugged’ into the editor.
