ASR_Explanation: Enables a modular plugin framework, impacting component boundaries, extensibility interfaces, and overall system decomposition.

requirement: The generated User Interface is only off-line adaptive.
ASR_Explanation: This UI adaptation detail impacts front-end implementation choices but doesn’t drive the overall system architecture.

requirement: The running Vetronics application can demonstrate “intelligent” behavior to some extent.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for AI/ML module integration, data pipelines and adaptive behavior infrastructure, impacting component boundaries and system scalability.

requirement: There is a need for a kind of decision algorithm (=agent) in order to decide whether the UI is more important than the system/environment data or vice-versa, depending on the actual content.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a new decision-agent component, impacting component decomposition, data flow, and real-time UI vs system-data prioritization policies.

requirement: It is possible that in dangerous conditions the environmental data force the defined UI to be overruled by some built-in UI elements (e.g. warnings and error messages).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a real-time, safety-critical UI override mechanism and cross-component event handling that shapes the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: The UI-Editor itself enables us to create and modify the User Interface off-line and then reload it.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a dynamic UI module with offline editing, serialization, and runtime reload capabilities, impacting component decomposition and persistence architecture.

requirement: In the definite program we can implement wizards in order to guide the user through several tasks or warn him for contradictory situations.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI guidance feature (wizard pattern) that can be handled within the existing presentation layer without impacting core architecture.

requirement: These self-learning wizards will recognize the user’s behavior and identify occasions to interfere and help.
ASR_Explanation: This requires integrating real-time behavior‐analysis and machine‐learning components, significantly affecting data flows, service decomposition, and infrastructure choices.

requirement: The UI-Editor should contain the possibility to define protocols, identify several types of communication ports.
ASR_Explanation: This feature requires designing a flexible, plugin‐based architecture and extensible metamodel to support dynamic protocol definitions and multiple port types.

requirement: Currently, support for a touch screen is provided in the UI-Editor.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI input feature (touch support) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The UI-Editor must have a simulation module incorporated so we can try the complete system off-line on our development computer before loading the generated code into the Vector System.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates adding a simulation-engine subsystem into the UI Editor, affecting component boundaries, integration points, and runtime environments.

requirement: An important result of the simulation may be the ability to certify the generated UI for use in life critical conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a safety-critical, certifiable UI architecture with rigorous validation, traceability, and compliance controls.

requirement: The architecture is considered as a very open framework (the base application) where we can easily plug in all Plug-Ins we need.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the overall modular, plugin-based framework design impacting component boundaries, extensibility mechanisms, and integration architecture.

requirement: The intelligent adaptive flight deck must assist the pilot in establishing and maintaining situation awareness by presenting the right information in the right format at the right time.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the need for real-time, context-aware data processing and adaptive UI frameworks impacting system architecture.

requirement: The intelligent adaptive flight deck must assist the pilot in detecting and correcting errors by comparing the overall system’s goals, the pilot intentions, and the actual and predicted flight state.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the inclusion and integration of intent‐inference, flight‐state prediction, and error‐detection modules, shaping the system’s high-level decomposition and real-time data flows.

requirement: The intelligent adaptive flight deck must assist the pilot in decision making by proposing optimized alternatives.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for real-time AI/ML decision-support modules, data integration pipelines, and interactive UI components in the system architecture.

requirement: The intelligent adaptive flight deck must assist the pilot in carrying out some simple actions by comparing the active procedures with the pilot’s actions.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of real-time procedure monitoring and comparison modules and their integration with the user interface.

requirement: The first prototype should be able to solve conflicting hazardous situations in an optimized manner.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes real-time conflict-resolution and optimization constraints, requiring dedicated architectural components for hazard detection and decision-making.

requirement: In the case of self-adaptation, the pilot shall always be offered the opportunity to overrule the adaptation.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a human‐in‐the‐loop override mechanism, affecting control flow, UI interfaces, and safety architecture.

requirement: The adaptive interface shall adapt to the situation and the pilot’s state and task.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates designing modules for context detection, pilot state monitoring, and adaptive UI logic, impacting component decomposition and real-time data flow.

requirement: The adapted constituents are modality, display configuration, information presented, level of detail, and timing strategy.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for an adaptive middleware or UI architecture to manage modality switching, display layouts, content detail levels, and timing strategies.

requirement: The adaptation should leave the pilot in command, and should be non-intrusive.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the adaptation mechanism’s design to preserve pilot control and ensure non-intrusive integration, impacting system architecture.

requirement: The consequences of the adaptation on the user interface itself should be intuitive, and support visual momentum.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI design guideline rather than a top-level architectural concern.

requirement: The second prototype shall incorporate an auditory warning signal, augmenting the visual channel.
ASR_Explanation: Requires integration of an audio output subsystem and hardware support, impacting event processing and system I/O architecture.

requirement: The warning signal will be the result of the integrated warning system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior without significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The multi-agent adaptive intelligent flight deck system will be programmed in Java, using JACK Intelligent Agents [tm].
ASR_Explanation: Specifying Java and the JACK agent framework dictates the runtime environment, middleware integration, and component interaction architecture.

requirement: All user interfaces (displays, controls) are programmed in OpenGL.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating OpenGL for all UIs drives the graphics subsystem architecture, rendering pipeline, and platform abstraction choices.

requirement: No efforts are being conducted to generalize the real-time simulation software for product simulation purposes.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for a modular, extensible architecture with abstraction layers or plugin frameworks to support generalized real-time product simulations.

requirement: System Administration support the needs of a large, multiple-branch library system. Specifically, the system must support a library system with 50 locations, 20 million circulations, purchasing and processing over 500,000 items per year. It is highly desirable that searches and reports can be processed during open hours without disrupting other system functions.
ASR_Explanation: This high-volume throughput and concurrent reporting requirement drives architectural decisions around scalability, performance isolation, and distributed processing.

requirement: System Administration shall operate on a Linux or Solaris server.
ASR_Explanation: This OS constraint drives key infrastructure and deployment decisions, impacting middleware compatibility, tooling, and operational architecture.

requirement: System Administration shall be accessible through a web-browser or a Windows-compatible client.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the frontend architecture by requiring both a web-based interface and a Windows-native client, affecting technology choices and API design.

requirement: If web-browser based, System Administration shall be accessible through Microsoft Internet Explorer (v.6.0 and later) and Mozilla Firefox (v.2.0 and later).
ASR_Explanation: Specifying IE6+ and FF2+ support drives front-end architectural decisions around UI frameworks, compatibility layers, and testing strategies.

requirement: System Administration shall be accessible with screen-reading software, screen-magnification software, and other software programs designed to increase accessibility.
ASR_Explanation: This accessibility requirement drives UI framework and component architecture choices to ensure ARIA compliance, keyboard navigation, and other cross-cutting accessibility features.

requirement: System Administration Module shall use a fully relational database back-end.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the data storage architecture by mandating a relational DBMS, affecting DB selection, schema design, and integration patterns.

requirement: System Administration Module shall produce standards-compliant HTML.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI output quality constraint influencing implementation details (templating/rendering) but not driving high-level architectural structure.

requirement: System Administration Module shall provide a development and training environment with the ability to migrate configurations to a production environment.
ASR_Explanation: It requires separate development/training and production environments plus a configuration migration mechanism, impacting deployment and environment architecture.

requirement: User rights and privileges will be controlled through security groups and/or “roles” that allow access control for individuals, workgroups, and arbitrary staff groups.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the security architecture by mandating a role‐based access control model and identity management integration across the system.

requirement: The software developer shall provide complete data specifications for authority records, bibliographic records, order records, item records, hold/request records, and other records maintained or accessed by the System Administration Module.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the data model and database/schema architecture for the System Administration Module.

requirement: The software developer shall provide a thorough high-level description of major processes, including bibliographic record import and export, validation of bibliographic records against internal and external authority sources, and standard reports.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation requirement for developer deliverables and does not impose structural or technical constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: The system shall provide an online, hierarchical, and cross-linked help system in HTML that describes and illustrates all system functions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation/UI requirement that can be fulfilled with standard help‐authoring tools and does not drive core system architecture.

requirement: System runs on a fully relational, SQL-based database system. Ability to run SQL queries against any table in the database. Ability to access database as an ODBC source. All data tables and data storage are fully accessible.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates the database technology and data access interfaces (SQL/ODBC), directly shaping the data layer and integration architecture.

requirement: The system provides real-time processing. For example: pull lists are up to date at time of viewing or printing; system supports live shelf reading and weeding.
ASR_Explanation: This real-time requirement drives an event-driven, low-latency architecture with real-time data synchronization and consistency mechanisms.

requirement: Ability for multiple staff members and patrons to simultaneously access and update patron and item records, including on staff check-in and check-out terminals, on self check-out stations, through SIP2/NCIP2 and similar protocols and APIs, and in OPAC. Depending on assigned privileges, staff can view all patron and item fields; patrons can access only selected fields. Record changes are applied in a reasonable way, with prompts to warn when a record has been changed since it was displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s concurrency control, real-time synchronization, multi‐protocol integration, and fine-grained authorization architecture.

requirement: For any patron record or item record, staff can identify where it is in use (location, user, date and time placed).
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD/reporting feature that can be implemented within the existing data model and UI without major architectural changes.

requirement: Support for individual and shared staff login accounts; access to modules is granted by use of 'roles' or 'privileges' that allow each account to access as many (or as few) modules as needed. Individual logins allow user-level preferences and audit trail.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the security and identity management architecture by defining authentication, RBAC and audit/logging mechanisms.

requirement: System documentation is library-specific and follows standard formats for technical documentation. Documentation is specific to the particular version of the software in use at library. Documentation is web-based, indexed, organized by function, and easily searchable.
ASR_Explanation: Mandates a web‐based, versioned documentation platform with indexing and search, influencing architectural design for documentation hosting and retrieval.

requirement: System upgrades and updates include written guidelines for updating servers and clients. Includes list of new, changed, and removed features.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement addresses maintenance documentation and does not impose constraints or influence the system’s high-level structure or quality attributes.

requirement: System provides access to all configuration files.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the architecture of configuration management, file access mechanisms, and associated security controls.

requirement: System provides full access to all log files. Log files can be reviewed without stopping system. Logs can be enabled, disabled, and set to a specific retention threshold.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This requirement drives the choice and design of a dynamic, hot-configurable logging infrastructure with storage management and performance considerations.

requirement: System provides access to root shell.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring privileged access control, authentication, and audit logging for root‐level shell access.

requirement: System supports creation of custom dashboards that display current and historical data about system performance, record creation and modification, circulation transactions, etc. Administrators can create dashboards and give access to selected users and groups.
ASR_Explanation: It requires architectural support for real-time and historical data storage/retrieval, a dashboard rendering framework, and fine-grained access control.

requirement: System provides full support for SNMP and supports monitoring of system resources, including disk space, CPU load, memory load, system processes, system interfaces and ports. Alert thresholds are configurable. Alerts can be sent via administrative dashboards, email messages, and text messages. Alerts can be sent to unlimited number of recipients via any or all alert methods.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the core architecture for a scalable monitoring and alerting subsystem with SNMP integration, threshold management, and multi-channel notifications.

requirement: Ability to set thresholds on the length of time records are locked and provide, for all record types, a list of records in sustained use/locked condition. Ability from the same console to unlock one or more records.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the concurrency control and monitoring architecture by introducing timer-based lock thresholds, scheduling components, and console integration for unlock operations.

requirement: System provides dashboard of performance monitoring and management tools. Identification of processes with process ID, owner username, IP address (if applicable), CPU utilization, memory utilization, run time. Runaway processes are identified. System status is represented by visual indicators (e.g. green and red lights).
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of real-time metrics collection, alert logic for runaway detection, and the dynamic dashboard/UI infrastructure.

requirement: System provides a server management console including: software shutdown utility, software startup utility, server shutdown utility, server restart utility.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional feature set for a management console and does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: System provides an administrative dashboard displaying: last full and incremental backup; last planned and unplanned system reboot; last software upgrade; current software version; transactions waiting to be processed; size of log-files; current count of records by record type (item, bibliographic, patron etc), database utilization (size, processes running).
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard admin-UI/metrics collection feature that doesn’t drive major changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: System provides a management console displaying workstations running client software; workstation name and IP address; and utilities for managing and killing client sessions.
ASR_Explanation: This requires a real-time management console and session control service, impacting system modularization, communication protocols, and security architecture.

requirement: System provides a circulation dashboard showing key performance indicators such as check-outs per hour, check-ins per hour, holds placed per hour, holds paged per day, etc. Indicators can be limited to a single branch or set to systemwide.
ASR_Explanation: Requires architecting a scalable data collection and real-time aggregation pipeline plus dashboard services to support per-branch and systemwide KPI reporting.

requirement: System provides a single console with access to all configuration files. Read and write permission to individual configuration files can be assigned to users and groups.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of a central configuration console with RBAC enforcement, impacting security controls, module boundaries, and configuration management architecture.

requirement: System provides a dashboard for locating and viewing log files.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around log aggregation, storage/indexing, search infrastructure and dashboard UI integration.

requirement: System provides a single interface for reviewing and controlling scheduled tasks, including staff-scheduled tasks, automated reports, scheduled imports and exports, software updates, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This necessitates a unified scheduling subsystem and integration layer affecting module boundaries, data flows, and extensibility.

requirement: Ability to access and edit email configuration, including a quick menu of common settings (such as masquerading, log retention, bounce management).
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD/UI feature with limited scope and no cross-cutting architectural impact.

requirement: System supports SMTP for email transport.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation detail specifying an email transport protocol rather than an architectural driver.

requirement: System supports restrictions based on business rules, e.g. restrictions on deleting item records that are in checked-out status, or restrictions on deleting bibliographic records with existing holds.
ASR_Explanation: These are routine domain-level business rule validations enforceable within the existing service layer without altering the system’s architecture.

requirement: System provides customizable 'Rules of Suppression' that specify whether patrons and staff can view authority, bibliographic, order, and item records in staff and public (OPAC) interfaces. Records may be visible to specific workgroups only; to all staff and patrons at specific locations; or to all staff and all patrons.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a flexible, fine-grained access control and data partitioning layer, impacting security, data filtering, and performance across the application.

requirement: System allows creation and modification of requesting rules that determine whether a patron can place a hold on an item. Requesting rules may evaluate patron type, current number of holds, current patron account balance, item type, item status, owning location code, and other criteria. Requesting rules also specify whether staff with specific privileges or roles can override specific criteria.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a dynamic rule‐engine architecture for evaluating and overriding multi‐criteria rules, impacting system modularity, performance, and extensibility.

requirement: System allows creation and modification of loan rules that allow or disallow check-out of items, calculate loan periods, and determine renewal limits. Loan rules may evaluate patron type, current number of items checked out, current patron account balance, item type, item status, owning location code, check-out location code, and other criteria. Loan rules also specify whether a specific criteria may be overridden by staff with specific privileges or roles.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a dynamic rules-engine architecture for configurable, criteria-based loan policies and secure staff override capabilities.

requirement: Ability to specify default value, data validation, automatic formatting, and required status for any field.
ASR_Explanation: Standard field-level validation and formatting can be implemented within existing UI/validation frameworks without affecting the overall system architecture.

requirement: System provides capability to perform live incremental and full backups of data and transaction logs. System supports use of third-party backup software such as EMC NetWorker.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the data persistence and logging layers and integration points for third-party backup tools, impacting system architecture.

requirement: System logs data changes (such as record deletions) and provides 'undo' functionality. Ideally, system provides revision control.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a versioning and audit‐logging subsystem that impacts the data persistence architecture, schema design, transaction handling, and performance.

requirement: Ability to cluster servers for failover capability.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the high-availability architecture by requiring server clustering, load balancing, and automated failover mechanisms.

requirement: Patron data is secure in all transfers to and from the system.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates secure communication channels and encryption mechanisms, impacting the design of the network and security layers.

requirement: System administrative staff has full visibility and control of user privileges.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the security and access-control architecture by mandating role-based permissions, administrative interfaces, and auditing capabilities.

requirement: System supports secure protocols, including SFTP, SSL, and SSH. SFTP is supported in both active and passive modes, configurable per vendor.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of the secure communication stack and protocol handlers, impacting core network and configuration architecture.

requirement: MARC bibliographic and authority records can be imported and exported, singly and in batch, all fields or selected fields, to and from vendors including OCLC. Imported records can overlay existing short or full bibliographic records. Imported batches can be maintained and manipulated as selection lists.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the integration and data‐processing architecture by requiring MARC transformation pipelines, overlay logic, vendor interfaces, and batch management capabilities.

requirement: System supports an unlimited number of record sets, with the ability to import and export set members in batch. Record sets can be the basis for batch field updates; can be used as a limiting scope for queries; can be used to delete original records with the ability to review prior to deletion, write errors to a log file, and undo one or more deletions.
ASR_Explanation: This requires designing a scalable data model and batch-processing pipeline with transactional, logging, and undo capabilities.

requirement: System provides a dedicated interface for creating new staff accounts. New staff account creation process provides configurable templates for account administrator use; provides granular privileges for account creation, modification, and deletion.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the design of the user-management and RBAC subsystems, as well as the extensibility of the account-creation interface.

requirement: System provides a dedicated interface for creating new patron accounts. Patron account creation process provides configurable templates for staff use; supports field validation and required fields; provides configurable defaults.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a metadata-driven form/template engine and validation framework in the overall system architecture.

requirement: System supports scheduling of maintenance tasks, reports, and data exports. Jobs can be scheduled in sequence ('start job B when job A finishes') and can be modified or cancelled at any time prior to starting.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the design of the scheduling and orchestration subsystem, requiring dependency management, dynamic updates, and cancellation handling.

requirement: Staff can be given permission to schedule tasks, reports, and data exports. System administration staff can view and manage jobs scheduled by other staff.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of the authorization and scheduling subsystems, requiring role-based access control and multi‐tenant job management.

requirement: System supports administrator-programmable and user-programmable macros and/or keyboard shortcuts. Shortcut keys may be assigned to macros (e.g. 'Insert Field') or to text strings. Macros are centrally managed on server, can be imported from and exported to individual users, and can be restricted for use and/or editing through centrally-managed permissions.
ASR_Explanation: This affects the core application architecture by introducing a macro/scripting subsystem, client‐server storage and sync, and a permissions framework that must be centrally enforced.

requirement: Staff and group accounts are independent from workstations; client install should not be tied to a specific location.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for a centralized identity and configuration service to enable location-independent client deployments.

requirement: Client software installation and updates must be centrally managed, using standard or proprietary network management tools, allowing streaming updates from server. Ability to specify specific clients to be updated. Client software can be managed with VNC and Remote Desktop.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the design of a centralized update‐management subsystem with streaming protocols and integration for network management and remote‐control tools, impacting the core component structure and data flows.

requirement: All client configuration files are server based; configurations can be exported and imported between clients.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates a centralized configuration management service with server‐side storage and client import/export APIs, impacting system layering and communications.

requirement: Ability to fully customize layout and appearance of reports. Ability to display, print, email, or save report to standard formats including CSV and Excel, as well as to customizable formats.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the overall system architecture by requiring a pluggable reporting engine and extensible export/layout customization framework.

requirement: System provides a user-friendly interface for designing queries against all record types. Staff can select fields to query; select values from picklist of possible values; select regular expressions from drop-down menu, and use a full range of Boolean operators. Administrators control staff access to tables and fields.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architecture by mandating a dynamic query‐builder component with UI framework support, query parsing engine, and integrated access‐control enforcement.

requirement: System administrators can create report templates that are available to front-line staff, and can be run as is or modified to the staff person's particular needs.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional feature affecting the reporting module but does not drive core architectural structure.

requirement: System provides fine-grained permissions to allow or disallow staff to run specific reports, and/or to run ad hoc reports on specific sets of data.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s security and authorization architecture by necessitating a robust, fine-grained permission management framework integrated with the reporting engine.

requirement: System reports for each record type: current record number, current number of records, number deleted, and number purged. Record types include patron, bibliographic, item, order, invoice, etc. Access to record numbers is controlled at the user/group level.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a reporting subsystem with real-time data aggregation and integration with the user/group access-control module, impacting component decomposition and security design.

requirement: Ability to define and select four types of printers: receipt printer, standard printer, label printer, and forms printer. All Windows printers are supported.
ASR_Explanation: Defines a core printer abstraction and driver integration layer impacting the system’s modular architecture and plugin interfaces.

requirement: Ability to print to a file on the server, ftp, email, or printer from any part of the application. When applicable, the ability to select record fields and control order of fields when printing.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a cross‐cutting, extensible print/output service layer with adapters for file, FTP, email, and printer targets and configurable field selection.

requirement: The system uses a graphical user interface to display data to the user
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives presentation layer architecture choices, including UI frameworks, client-server interaction patterns, and screen layout design.

requirement: The system can be used fully with a keyboard
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI accessibility requirement impacting component and event‐handling design rather than driving high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: The system can be used fully with a mouse
ASR_Explanation: This UI interaction detail does not have broad architectural impact beyond standard frontend implementation.

requirement: The system supports multiple games, and can load additional games from an external source
ASR_Explanation: This drives a modular, plugin-based architecture enabling dynamic discovery and loading of external game modules.

requirement: The system has support for multiple opening positions that can be applied to all the games
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard domain feature that can be handled within existing modules without requiring architectural changes.

requirement: The system shall support two player turn-based gaming
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a networked client-server architecture with session management and state synchronization for turn-based gameplay.

requirement: The system has an artificial intelligence that the player may play against. The artificial intelligence supports every two player game
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a generic, extensible AI engine and game‐abstraction interfaces, shaping the system’s modular decomposition and integration strategy.

requirement: The system has an embedded AI that takes over from a player when asked to do so
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s modular design and control-flow architecture by requiring dynamic AI component integration and runtime player-to-AI switching.

requirement: The system has files containing implementation of different artificial intelligences that the system can load
ASR_Explanation: It requires a plugin-based architecture with defined interfaces and dynamic loading mechanisms to support interchangeable AI modules.

requirement: The system prompts the user about implementing the new AI's

requirement: Color Chooser: Pointing device driven color selection utility.

requirement: Find a particular number of colors near a given color.

requirement: Administrative interface (update, add, delete colors, add users).

requirement: The keyboard is used to enter and place paint name and paint number searches. The keyboard also provides keyboard-only application functionality when a pointing device is not available.

requirement: Information that the product will collect or store, which need access protections include user specified color palettes and their store of access permissions. Access to this information must be private.

requirement: The store of access permissions to administrative features and access permissions must be secure.

requirement: Implementations of Privacy and security must be on par with industry standards.

requirement: The proposed modular specification of the application lends itself to adaptability, robustness, and reusability.

requirement: Administrative users can give other users administrative access up to their own access level only.

requirement: The Black Start Capability Plan (BCP) is reviewed and updated at least every five years.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement is a procedural schedule for documentation updates and does not materially affect the system’s architectural design.

requirement: The CAISO determines the amount and location of Black Start Generation it requires through contingency studies that are used as the basis of the CAISO’s emergency plans.
ASR_Explanation: Requires integration of contingency analysis and grid simulation modules, impacting component design and data processing architecture.

requirement: The CAISO annually verifies that the number, size, availability, and location of system Black Start generating units are sufficient to meet WECC restoration plan requirements for the CAISO Balancing Authority Area.
ASR_Explanation: This is a compliance-related functional detail and does not drive major architectural decisions or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The CAISO documents the Cranking Paths, including initial switching requirements, between each Black Start generating unit and the units to be started and provides this documentation for review to WECC upon request.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only drives documentation and reporting capabilities for compliance without imposing major architectural constraints.

requirement: The CAISO Black Start planning evaluation process includes consideration of diverse fuel resources, available cranking and transmission paths, communication adequacy, unit capability of maintaining adequate regulation of voltage and frequency, generator protection and control systems, and limited energy resources are selected sparingly.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of control and communication infrastructure, resource management modules, and overall system reliability architecture.

requirement: The planning evaluation process is coordinated, as appropriate, with the Black Start capability plans of transmission owners and neighboring Balancing Authorities.
ASR_Explanation: Mandates cross-organizational integration and process orchestration, shaping interface and data-sharing modules in the system architecture.

requirement: The CAISO annually enters into RMR and Interim Black Start Contracts with generators that satisfy the results of the annual planning evaluation process.
ASR_Explanation: This describes a routine business workflow for contract management and does not impose major architectural constraints.

requirement: The owner or operator of any Voluntary Black Start unit that desires a Black Start generating unit status with the CAISO Balancing Authority shall demonstrate at least every five years, through simulation or testing, that the unit can perform its intended functions as required by the WECC BCP and this testing section.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates periodic simulation/testing capabilities with scheduling and compliance reporting, impacting the system’s reliability, maintainability, and test automation architecture.

requirement: The owner or operator shall submit documentation of the test results and analysis to the CAISO, WECC, or NERC upon any request.
ASR_Explanation: This compliance submission requirement doesn’t impose major architectural constraints and can be handled within existing document-management and reporting mechanisms.

requirement: The owner or operator shall submit any past reports for Black Start tests with the CAISO and WECC.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data‐submission feature with no significant impact on high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: Participants who do not currently test their Black Start capability will be required to test their Black Start capability.
ASR_Explanation: This is a procedural testing requirement and does not drive any fundamental changes to the system’s architecture.

requirement: Within 24 hours of conducting a Black Start test, all Participants will notify the CAISO RT Dispatcher by phone indicating the success or failure of the test.
ASR_Explanation: This is a procedural notification requirement with no material impact on system architecture or design.

requirement: Within 14 days of performing a Black Start test, all Participants will file a letter with the CAISO regarding the success or failure of the test.
ASR_Explanation: This is a business‐process compliance timeline requirement and does not drive any core architectural design changes.

requirement: Testing records shall include the dates of the tests, the duration of the tests, and an indication of whether the tests met the requirements of this Black Start Testing section.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data-recording detail affecting the schema of test records, not a system-level architectural constraint.

requirement: The Black Start unit must have the ability to maintain voltage within emergency voltage limits over a range of loading from no external load to full external load in accordance with NERC reliability standards.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the design of the voltage control and power system architecture to ensure stability and NERC compliance across all loading conditions.

requirement: The Black Start unit must be equipped with governors that are capable of operating in an isochronous mode.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional constraint on governor behavior and does not drive changes in the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: Adequate transmission capacity shall be available to connect the Black Start facility to the source providing station services to other specified generating stations.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the system’s network topology and capacity planning to ensure sufficient connectivity and resilience.

requirement: The Black Start unit must be capable of starting and energizing the applicable transmission path without assistance from the electrical system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a standalone power‐generation and control subsystem and shapes the overall system topology for grid-independent energization.

requirement: The Black Start Test should include key operating aids used in Black Starts such as telephone communications and SCADA, if applicable.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for architectural integration points and communication layers for SCADA and telephony subsystems.

requirement: The following start-up/synchronizing time limits shall apply to the Black Start testing units: Hydroelectric generating units or combustion gas turbines - within 30 minutes; Industrial gas turbines - within 60 minutes; Hot, steam-driven turbines - within 2.5 hours; Other technologies - as negotiated.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes real‐time performance and scheduling constraints on the control system architecture for orchestrating black-start sequences.

requirement: The minimum run time duration (the generation time after being synchronized to the grid) of a Black Start test is 10 minutes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a test-specific runtime constraint and does not affect the system’s overall architecture or high-level structure.

requirement: Submit a CAISO market schedule for any test energy.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives architectural decisions around external integration, data exchange protocols, and real-time scheduling capabilities.

requirement: All Black Start Generating Units must satisfy technical requirements specified by the CAISO.
ASR_Explanation: This regulatory compliance constraint will impact the design and integration of black-start unit modules to meet CAISO technical standards.

requirement: The CAISO shall from time to time undertake performance tests, with or without prior notification.
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational testing procedure requirement and does not drive core system structure or architectural decisions.

requirement: The CAISO shall have the sole right to determine when the operation of Black Start Generating Unit is required to respond to conditions on the CAISO Controlled Grid.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines a centralized authority model, impacting the system’s control-flow and access-control architecture for black start operations.

requirement: The CAISO annually requests Black Start tests from at least one third of all RMR and Interim Black Start units.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-specific business rule affecting selection logic but does not impose structural architectural constraints.

requirement: Black Start units may be tested and/or assessed for the ability to self-provide start-up power for the period of time it takes to complete the start-up process at the generating station and to switch to the applicable transmission path and synchronize to the grid.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a real-time startup simulation and testing module with grid synchronization interfaces and timing constraints.

requirement: Black Start units may be tested and/or assessed for the ability to complete such number of successive starts within such period of time as may be specified in the Black Start section of the unit’s RMR or Interim Black Start contract.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional test requirement and does not drive major architectural structure or quality attributes.

requirement: Black Start units may be tested and/or assessed for the ability to produce the range of reactive power resources required by its voltage support section of the unit’s RMR or Interim Black Start contract.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional test requirement that does not impose any significant cross-cutting architectural changes.

requirement: RMR & Interim BS Unit Owners may request an Availability Test at any time.
ASR_Explanation: This on-demand test request is a routine functional feature that doesn’t impose new architectural structures or constraints.

requirement: RMR & Interim BS Unit Owners must submit a request for an Availability Test using G-213H Black Start Test Report Form and send it to the CAISO Operations Support Test Administrator.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed procedural requirement that does not impose constraints on the system’s high-level structure or key quality attributes.

requirement: If it is needed to test the Unit to generate above its contract MNDC or stated Availability, request this in G-213H Black Start Test Report Form.
ASR_Explanation: This is a procedural testing documentation requirement and does not affect system architecture or design.

requirement: If a unit retests and passes requested MW, correct the Unit’s Availability in SLIC, per the RMR Contract (Article 7.3 [b]).
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine business‐logic rule for updating a data field and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Unit’s restated Availability Limit will not become effective until the Alhambra Generation Dispatcher has been notified.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the event-driven integration and state-transition architecture by mandating notification-based gating of the Availability Limit.

requirement: Prior to the start to the 1st hour of the test, the Alhambra Generation Dispatcher must notify the BS Unit SC via telephone providing enough time to ramp the Unit to full capacity.
ASR_Explanation: This is a manual operational procedure and does not impose significant constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: Prior to the start of the test, the Alhambra Generation Dispatcher must transmit (electronically) an Availability Test Dispatch Notice for RMR units (or phone for Interim BS units) to the RMR Unit Owner, PTO, and CAISO Operations Support Test Administrator.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It drives the architecture of the notification subsystem by requiring multi-channel dispatch integration and workflow orchestration for electronic and fallback phone alerts.

requirement: Prior to the start of the first hour, the BS Generating Unit Operator must ramp to the requested MW of the test.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific functional timing requirement for test operation and does not influence high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The BS Generating Unit Operator must maintain the requested MW for four full hours.
ASR_Explanation: It demands sustained operational availability and stateful performance over four hours, affecting reliability and resource management in the architecture.

requirement: After the end of the fourth hour, the BS Generating Unit Operator must ramp back down or continue in Market Transaction.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-specific business rule for operational logic and does not impose any system-level architectural constraints.

requirement: If ramping occurs during the four-hour timeframe, it will have an undesirable effect on outcome of the test.
ASR_Explanation: This statement specifies a test condition rather than imposing a structural or systemic constraint affecting the overall architecture.

requirement: After completion of the test and prior to 11:00 the next business day, the RMR Generating Unit Operator must submit Ambient Temperature information relevant to the time frame of the test.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine business‐process timing rule and does not impose significant system‐level architectural constraints.

requirement: Ambient Temperature information must be submitted to the CAISO Operations Support Test Administrator from G-203G RMR Test Request Form Availability Test Ambient Temperature Supplement, including four (4) Ambient Temperature Points, along with an average, each in a different hour of the test.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data‐collection function and does not drive any major system‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: If Ambient Temperature information is not provided within the stated timeframe, CAISO reserves the right to pull its own information.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces a timeout-based fallback mechanism for data ingestion, impacting the system’s integration and error‐handling architecture.

requirement: The CAISO Operations Support Test Administrator must determine the BS Unit’s Availability by averaging the actual MWh (from the Unit’s Revenue Meter) output during the four-hour test period, taking into consideration the Unit’s Temperature correction factor.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed business calculation requirement that doesn’t impose high-level architectural or quality-attribute constraints.

requirement: If the Unit’s output is at least 99% of the Requested MW, declare the BS Unit available at the current Unit Availability Limit.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized business rule for unit availability calculation with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: If the Unit fails to satisfy the 99% Availability threshold, set the Availability Limit of the BS Unit to the Availability level achieved during the test.
ASR_Explanation: This is a unit‐level functional rule for adjusting configuration based on test results and does not impose new architectural patterns or infrastructure changes.

requirement: The CAISO maintains a Black Start database that contains all Black Start generators designated for use during a system restoration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data persistence requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Black Start database is reviewed and updated on at least an annual basis.
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational maintenance frequency requirement with minimal impact on the system’s structural architecture.

requirement: The Black Start database includes the name, location, megawatt capacity, type of unit, latest date of test, and starting method.
ASR_Explanation: This simply specifies data fields for the Black Start database and does not drive high-level architectural structure or quality attributes.

requirement: If a Black Start unit did not successfully pass the CAISO Black Start criteria specified in this procedure, an explanation and a plan to address and correct the deficiency shall be provided to the CAISO by the generator owner/operator.
ASR_Explanation: This is a regulatory compliance requirement for reporting and remediation without significant impact on the system’s architecture.

requirement: The results of the testing and the justification for the periodicity of the testing shall be provided to WECC or NERC on request (within 30 days).
ASR_Explanation: This is a regulatory reporting requirement with timing constraints but does not drive foundational system architecture decisions.

requirement: The Real-time Grid Operators are trained at least annually for system restoration including the use of Black Start capable units.
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational training requirement and does not impose constraints on the software system’s architecture.

requirement: The training encompasses the integrated coordination of NERC requirements, WECC requirements, E-501 - System Restoration, E-501A Black Start Capable Units, Black Start Capability Plan, and simulations of full or partial system shutdowns and restoration (including a critique report of the simulation).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the architecture to include a robust simulation engine, regulatory compliance integration, and critique reporting/workflow orchestration.

requirement: As a system simulator becomes available the CAISO will conduct operator training for system restoration and Black Start unit use.
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational training process requirement and does not impose changes on the system’s architectural design.

requirement: The Framework MUST be capable of capturing information from the software tools specified in Table 3.9.3-A. The data captured SHOULD be stored in a centralized data store. The Framework MAY decide whether the information should be stored as Entities or Associates.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It drives key architectural decisions around integration patterns, choice of centralized datastore, and data modeling for Entities versus Associates.

requirement: User SHOULD be able to modify, add, delete or group Entities by navigating to a virtual folder (e.g. /People/).
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward CRUD requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture, affecting only UI and data‐access implementation details.

requirement: The email is intercepted before it is dispatched to the SMTP server. An Associate is created to contain the message body. The recipient name(s) are used to create (if required) Entities under the “People” category. The Associate is then linked to each Entity.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the email processing pipeline and domain model by introducing interception, entity creation, and association persistence concerns.

requirement: The email is fetched from the POP3 server. An Associate is created to contain the message body. The sender’s name is used to create Entities, under the category “People”. The Associate is then linked to this Entity.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional detail with no cross-cutting or quality-attribute impact requiring architectural changes.

requirement: User SHOULD be able to specify when he is to start a research project; i.e. when the web sites he visits subsequently are to be permanently stored. The project name specified by the user spawns a new Entity in the Framework. An associate is created as an archive and is linked to this Entity.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of dynamic entity lifecycle, persistent archival storage, and scalability of the data persistence layer.

requirement: After editing a document in a word-processor, the user invokes a special GUI supplied by the Framework. The GUI allows him to draw arrows to and from (a symbol representing) the current document to any Entity creating relationships between the document and the relevant Entities. The user may optionally specify a descriptive name for the document. The document is automatically saved to disk in a manner that reflects the relationships it has to other Entities. The user may in the future fetch this document by following the relationship paths that he had created.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the document storage schema and retrieval service as well as the GUI–framework integration for relationship management.

requirement: The GUI described in section 3.1.1.5 MAY also be made available when dealing with other types of information as well.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the modularity and extensibility of the GUI layer, necessitating architectural support for reusable components and dynamic data handling.

requirement: The user MUST be able to view the information in the central store in a hierarchical manner that makes sense to him. The Framework SHOULD include a browser that is capable of displaying this hierarchy in a UI similar to that of the Windows Explorer. The browser MAY OPTIONALLY be integrated directly into Windows Explorer, rather than being a stand alone application. This browser MAY integrate the GUI described in section 3.1.1.5 as well.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the UI architecture and component boundaries by requiring a Windows-Explorer-style browser with optional OS integration, impacting deployment and integration strategy.

requirement: The user may want to archive portions of the information-store to removable storage media (CD-ROMs and USB Pen Drives). This SHOULD not mean that such portions would be “forgotten” by the Framework. Some residue MUST be left on the data store to indicate that a portion of data has been archived. The Framework SHOULD inform the user that the Entity has been archived and OPTIONALLY, if he wishes to proceed, to remount the relevant storage device.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the storage subsystem architecture by requiring a removable‐media abstraction layer, persistent metadata residue, and device‐mount management.

requirement: The Framework API should expose at least the following functionality to client processes: Create, delete and modify Entities and Entity groups; Create, delete and modify relationships between Entities; List and access Entities with an API similar to that of a standard file system API; Execute queries for the retrieval of Entities using different criteria.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines the core API surface and data model interactions, impacting module boundaries, layering, and extensibility of the architecture.

requirement: The API that exposes Entity listings MAY be designed to be similar to the System.IO APIs of the .NET Platform which provide directory and file listings.
ASR_Explanation: This is a design style guideline for consistency, not a driver affecting the overall system architecture.

requirement: GUIs associated with the Framework SHOULD be designed such that a user with basic understanding of the Windows 2000/XP operating systems would instinctively be able to interact with them. Therefore, most GUIs MAY need to closely mimic popular Windows applications.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI usability/style guideline and does not drive high-level architectural structure or component interfaces.

requirement: The mean time between failures (MTBF) SHALL exceed 3 months.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes reliability and availability constraints that drive fault-tolerance, redundancy, and component selection in the architecture.

requirement: At this stage of the development, the Framework MAY only operate in single user mode, with no networking capabilities.
ASR_Explanation: This restriction shapes the system’s architecture by eliminating networking modules and defining a single-user, local-only operation boundary.

requirement: The Framework Shell SHOULD NOT utilize more than 40 megabytes of RAM.
ASR_Explanation: It places tight memory restrictions that influence technology stacks, module design, and resource management strategies.

requirement: Any coding conventions to be used during implementation MUST be documented before the beginning of the phase, and strictly adhered to during the phase.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement governs implementation style and maintainability but does not drive or constrain the overall system architecture.

requirement: Project ZNIX SHOULD be available to its potential users in source and binary form, and free of charge. The project MAY be hosted on a popular Open Source distribution website such that the Open Source community is aware of (and contribute to) its existence.
ASR_Explanation: This is a release and distribution requirement affecting deployment and licensing processes rather than core system architecture.

requirement: The website for distributing the project SHALL include provisions for the users to make comments on the product as well as to compile a wish-list of features.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces user-generated content and wishlist features that will impact the system’s data model, service/component architecture, and scalability/security considerations.

requirement: The Framework MUST guard against malicious scripts on remote websites from accessing its information.
ASR_Explanation: Requires defining security boundaries and sandboxing mechanisms (e.g., content security policies, isolation layers) to enforce strict access control.

requirement: The Framework MUST guard against unintended corruption of information by the user himself.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It requires the architecture to include dedicated validation and integrity‐enforcement layers to prevent user-induced data corruption.

requirement: No design constraint has been imposed at this stage.
ASR_Explanation: This statement imposes no constraints or directives and thus does not influence architectural decisions.

requirement: The online help system should be compiled utilizing as much of non-technical terms as possible. Care SHOULD be taken not to produce an exhaustive user documentation as that would defeat the main purpose of the system as expressed in the introduction to section 2.
ASR_Explanation: This is a content style guideline for user-facing help and does not drive core system structure or key architectural decisions.

requirement: A comprehensive Developer’s Guide MAY be compiled to facilitate 3rd party ZNIX-aware applications.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement relates to documentation and does not impose constraints or decisions on the system’s architecture.

requirement: A GUI capable of browsing the information managed by the Framework, similar to that of the Windows Explorer SHOULD be available to the user. The GUI MUST also be capable of allowing the user to specify relationships between disparate Entities as he sees fit.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the UI/data‐access architecture by requiring an Explorer‐style browsing interface and dynamic entity‐relationship management module.

requirement: Provisions SHOULD be made so that at least the following 3rd party applications can take advantage of the Framework for the specified types of information: Outlook Express, Outlook 2003, Address Book, Internet Explorer, Opera, Firefox, Word 2003.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a pluggable integration layer and adapter modules, impacting the system’s modularity and extensibility.

requirement: Targeted operating systems are Windows 2000 Professional and Windows XP.
ASR_Explanation: This OS targeting drives key architectural decisions around platform-specific APIs, compatibility layers, and system library support.

requirement: ZNIX Framework will be licensed under GNU Library General Public License (LGPL). The browser and any other tool released alongside the Framework will be licensed under GNU Public License (GPL).
ASR_Explanation: This licensing requirement affects component boundaries, linking strategies, and distribution packaging to ensure LGPL/GPL compliance.

requirement: All proprietary trademarks, product names and/or logos used on this document are trademarks of their respective owners.
ASR_Explanation: This is a legal/documentation requirement with no direct impact on the software’s architectural design.

requirement: All components associated with Project ZNIX should conform to the following standards: Windows 2000 Logo Requirements. The system SHALL conform to ISO 10646 (Unicode UTF-8) and OPTIONALLY ISO 10646-1 (Unicode UTF-16) standards for character set encoding. Additionally, design of the system MAY yield a new set of standards that ZNIX-aware client processes MAY need to conform to.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes cross-cutting constraints on component interfaces, data encoding, platform compatibility, and client interoperability that must drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: The system design MUST be subjected to the following constraints: Hardware: Intel Pentium III or compatible 1-GHz PC with 256 megabytes of RAM.
ASR_Explanation: This hardware constraint imposes strict resource and performance limits that drive key architectural decisions around memory management and component design.

requirement: The system design MAY assume that all interfaces designed for the 3rd party software listed in Table 3.9.3-A will be compatible with their subsequent releases.
ASR_Explanation: This assumption impacts the integration architecture by dictating interface versioning, dependency management, and long-term coupling strategies.

requirement: The applications listed in this table are assumed to be safe from vulnerabilities. The security offered by these products will form the basis of the security of the Framework.
ASR_Explanation: It defines critical security assumptions and trust boundaries that will shape the framework’s overall security architecture.

requirement: It is assumed that supporting the software listed would be sufficient to serve the intended user-base, considering their popularity on Microsoft Windows based systems.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains platform and third-party software support, directly impacting architecture choices for compatibility, integration, and deployment.

requirement: The Center shall provide the following node information: 1. Node identifier 2. Node name 3. Node jurisdiction 4. Owner 5. Latitude/longitude 6. Type of node (i.e., intersection or bridge) 7. Node type description 8. Number of links

requirement: For each link defined within the Center: 1. Network identifier 2. Link identifier 3. Data type 4. Data type description 5. Delay 6. Travel time 7. Volume 8. Speed 9. Density 10. Occupancy

requirement: The Center shall provide the following information status information about each CCTV: 1. Network identifier 2. CCTV identifier 3. CCTV name 4. Location (latitude/longitude) 5.
