ASR_Explanation: This is a self-contained UI feature that leverages existing map components without impacting overall system architecture.

requirement: The map user shall be able to pan the map in each of the following directions: North, South, East or West.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic UI interaction requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Each link displayed on the map shall be color coded to provide a graphical depiction of speeds. A configuration file shall be provided to specify specific speed values. The color coding shall be as follows: Green - speeds > TBD MPH; Yellow - speeds between TBD and TBD MPH; Red – speeds below TBD MPH
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI mapping feature with simple external configuration and does not impact high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The map shall display the current incidents (as icons) known to the C2C Project.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI feature that doesn’t drive any new architectural patterns or structural changes.

requirement: The user shall be able to click on an incident icon to obtain further information about the incident.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI interaction detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: All current incidents shall be displayed in tabular format with the following information contained in the table: Location, Type of incident (e.g., accident, lane closure), Severity of incident, Incident status, Travel direction, Effected lanes
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/data‐presentation detail and does not impose major architectural constraints.

requirement: The map shall be capable of displaying the following for a DMS: 1. Location 2. Current Message
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic UI/display requirement with minimal impact on high-level architectural design.

requirement: The map shall be capable of displaying the following for a LCS: 1. Location 2. Current Signals
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI functional requirement with no major architectural impact.

requirement: The map shall be capable of displaying the following for a CCTV: 1. Location 2. Status
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI feature (map display) with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The Incident GUI shall allow the user to enter incident or lane closure information without the use of an Center.
ASR_Explanation: This offline-entry requirement drives architecture for local data storage, sync mechanisms, and network-resilience in the GUI.

requirement: The Incident GUI shall allow the user to input the following information for each incident: Location (latitude/longitude), Description, Status, Effected lanes, Detection time, Response time, Estimated time to clear queue, Queue length
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI data‐entry requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The Incident GUI shall allow the user to input the following information for each lane closure: Location (latitude/longitude), Description, Effected lanes, Date, Start time, End time
ASR_Explanation: This specifies a standard GUI data entry form with minimal impact on overall architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall provide a list of previously entered incidents.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level UI feature confined to the presentation layer and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The GUI shall allow the data about an incident to be modified.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic CRUD GUI requirement for incident data with no major architectural impact.

requirement: The GUI shall allow a user to delete a previously entered incident.
ASR_Explanation: This basic CRUD operation has minimal architectural impact and doesn’t drive high‐level design decisions.

requirement: The GUI shall provide a list of previously entered lane closures.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine GUI CRUD feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The GUI shall allow a user to delete a previously entered lane closure.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD GUI operation with no significant impact on system architecture.

requirement: The remote Center Control GUI shall be designed to execute on a public network (e.g., Internet) and transmit equipment requests to the C-2-C software system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives network deployment, security boundaries, and interface design for a public-facing GUI integration with the C-2-C system.

requirement: When the GUI application is initiated, the user shall be prompted for the following information: User name, Password
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI-level login prompt requirement that does not drive major architectural structure or constraints.

requirement: The user shall be provided with the capability to select a network identifier for a device command/control request.
ASR_Explanation: This is an application‐layer feature for request routing via a selected network ID and doesn’t affect the core architecture.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a DMS from a list and provide the following information: Target DMS, Message to be displayed, Beacons On/Off
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI selection and data-entry feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a LCS from a list and provide the following information: Target LCS, Assignment of lane arrows
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI data-entry feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to issue a CCTV switching command: Source (input), Destination port (output)
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It mandates integration with CCTV switching hardware and real-time command routing, impacting module boundaries and communication protocols.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a CCTV from a list and provide the following information: Target CCTV, Device control including: Pan, Tilt, Zoom
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives integration with CCTV hardware and real-time control subsystems, impacting the system’s streaming and device-communication architecture.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a Ramp Meter from a list and provide the following information: Target Ramp Meter, Plan
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI data-selection requirement and does not drive any system-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a HAR from a list and provide the following information: Target HAR, Text to be sent to the HAR
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional UI requirement with no significant impact on system architecture.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a Traffic Signal from a list and provide the following information: Target Traffic Signal, Plan
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-driven functional requirement with no broad impact on system architecture.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a HOV from a list and provide the following information: Target HOV, Plan
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI data‐entry flow for selecting and inputting values, with no major impact on system architecture.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a School Zone from a list and provide the following information: Target School Zone, Plan
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI functional requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a Reversible Lane from a list and provide the following information: Target Reversible Lane, Plan
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI/data-entry function that does not impose significant constraints on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Once an Center is selected, the user shall be able to select a Dynamic Lane from a list and provide the following information: Target Dynamic Lane, Plan
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-level functional requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: For each device command/control status request sent by the Remote GUI, the status returned from the network identifier will be displayed in a scrollable list on the GUI.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed GUI display requirement with minimal impact on high-level architectural design.

requirement: The C2C Server shall execute in a Microsoft Windows NT environment.
ASR_Explanation: This OS constraint dictates the deployment platform and influences technology stack, runtime dependencies, and integration choices.

requirement: A DATEX/ASN runtime library shall be available on any computer communicating to the C2C project.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes a mandatory cross-platform runtime dependency, impacting deployment architecture and technology stack choices.

requirement: The web server application shall use ESRI's ARC Internet Map Server (ARC IMS) product for creating of map images.
ASR_Explanation: Specifies reliance on a third-party mapping server (ESRI ARC IMS), driving choices in integration, deployment, and system architecture.

requirement: The C2C shall execute in a Microsoft Windows NT environment.
ASR_Explanation: This OS constraint dictates platform-specific APIs, runtime dependencies, and deployment architecture decisions.

requirement: The C2C shall be implemented in the C/C++ programming language.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying C/C++ as the implementation language impacts overall system architecture by dictating performance characteristics, memory management, portability, and toolchain decisions.

requirement: The C2C web interface shall be implemented using C/C++ and ESRI ARC IMS.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying C/C++ and ESRI ARC IMS constrains the core technology stack and integration approach, directly shaping the system architecture.

requirement: The Incident GUI shall be implemented using C/C++ and ESRI Map Objects.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating C/C++ and ESRI Map Objects drives core technology choices, library dependencies, and integration architecture.

requirement: The Remote Control GUI shall be implemented using C/C++ and ESRI Map Objects.
ASR_Explanation: This technology mandate dictates the GUI’s platform, libraries, and integration strategy, directly shaping the system’s architecture.

requirement: The C2C shall be able to operate in normal mode. In this mode the C2C receives data from all connected systems, including the Incident GUI, and combines the data into a single data store (database).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the core data ingestion and storage integration pattern, impacting the system’s data architecture and interfaces.

requirement: The C2C shall be able to operate in test mode. In this mode, the C2C performs normal mode operations and also logs activities.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple operational mode toggle with added logging that can be handled by a runtime flag and existing logging framework without architectural changes.

requirement: The BE shall receive LTA or Speed Dump Lag Frames from the Correlator. For a detailed description of the two dump formats see Reference 8, pages 69 to 71. This will most likely be in the form of one or more frames per UDP/IP packet.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the network transport protocol and data framing strategy for high-throughput UDP-based ingestion, impacting the system’s I/O and buffering architecture.

requirement: The transfer shall take place in such a manner that all data needed to perform any Fourier Transform shows-up on a single processor.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the system’s data distribution and processor assignment strategy, impacting the parallel architecture and inter-node communication design.

requirement: The BE shall receive, via M&C, State Count data produced by the Correlator.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines a critical integration interface and messaging flow between the Correlator and BE, impacting component boundaries and communication architecture.

requirement: The BE shall receive, via M&C, Data Valid Counts.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐ingestion detail over an existing M&C interface and does not drive any new architectural decisions.

requirement: The BE shall receive, via M&C, Quantizer Power Measurement Data.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a required integration point and data‐handling interface with the M&C subsystem, shaping component boundaries and communication protocols.

requirement: The BE shall receive, via M&C, Filter Parameters.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional detail about input handling with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The BE shall receive, via M&C, Frequency Shift Parameters.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a new M&C integration interface, impacting component boundaries and communication protocols.

requirement: The BE shall receive, via M&C, Windowing Parameters.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a new external interface to the M&C subsystem, affecting component boundaries and message‐handling flows in the backend.

requirement: The BE shall receive, via M&C, data and parameters specific to the current EVLA Observational Mode needed for processing the Correlator Lag values.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a critical interface and data flow from the M&C subsystem to the BE that will shape component interactions, communication protocols, and system integration.

requirement: The BE shall receive, via M&C, all meta-data necessary to format BE results for delivery to the E2E.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a critical cross-component interface and data flow contract that impacts service boundaries, integration patterns, and protocol design.

requirement: The BE shall provide operational status data to and receive control data from the M&C System. This includes Lag Frame destination addresses and address changes.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the interface and data‐flow architecture by defining protocols, routing, and dynamic address management with the M&C system.

requirement: The BE shall provide error and warning reports to M&C as operating conditions warrant.
ASR_Explanation: Defines an event‐driven error/warning reporting interface to M&C, impacting integration, messaging, and reliability architecture.

requirement: The BE shall provide several optionally selectable levels of printed messages detailing operational parameters at critical locations in the system.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces a cross-cutting logging/traceability concern requiring framework selection and instrumentation that impacts module design and performance.

requirement: The BE shall deliver formatted final results to the e2e System. The BE shall produce all data needed by the e2e System for archiving and further processing. The output is currently expected to be in a form compatible with AIPS++ Measurement Sets.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating AIPS++ Measurement Sets output defines the data serialization format and drives integration and storage architecture decisions.

requirement: The BE shall acknowledge receipt of all data received from M&C.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional protocol detail and does not drive broad architectural decisions.

requirement: The BE shall notify M&C of any detected interruptions of data delivery from the Correlator.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the fault‐notification and monitoring infrastructure by requiring an event‐driven communication mechanism between BE and M&C.

requirement: The BE shall verify successful delivery of output to the e2e.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for reliable messaging/acknowledgment and retry mechanisms in the backend integration architecture.

requirement: The BE shall guarantee safe delivery of all internal messages.
ASR_Explanation: Guaranteeing safe delivery of internal messages dictates the choice and design of messaging infrastructure, retry/acknowledgment patterns, and error-handling mechanisms.

requirement: The BE shall be able to handle lag frames of less than 128 values.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional constraint on frame handling that doesn’t drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The BE shall be able to handle lag sets up to a maximum size of 262,144 values.
ASR_Explanation: This capacity limit directly impacts data structures, memory allocation, and performance scaling decisions in the architecture.

requirement: The BE shall receive incoming data packets from the Correlator to Backend network interface. This network is a part of the BE System.
ASR_Explanation: Defines a critical external interface and data flow boundary that will shape the network and system integration architecture.

requirement: The BE shall verify the successful receipt of incoming data from the Correlator. This includes checking for receive errors and determining that all expected data was received, accumulation of error statistics and comparison against tolerances, and reporting of all out of tolerance conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional data‐validation and error‐reporting requirement that does not drive major architectural structure or patterns.

requirement: The BE shall store input data records in a memory buffer and track buffer locations of all input data until data processing is complete. Report any buffer overflow conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the memory management and buffering architecture, including buffer sizing, tracking mechanisms, and overflow handling.

requirement: The BE shall respond to incoming correlator mode changes, user optional processing sequence and/or parameter changes, and other external inputs that affect the data processing pipeline. Update internal parameter tables and synchronize data processing pipeline with new operational conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s dynamic reconfiguration capabilities, necessitating architecture for parameter management, pipeline synchronization, and state handling.

requirement: The BE shall assemble the received input data into continuous time series (lag sets).
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional data‐processing step and does not drive high-level architectural structure or quality-attribute decisions.

requirement: The BE shall ensure that time series data is correctly ordered and contains valid data values along its entire extent. Compare against tolerances and report all out of tolerance conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional data‐validation requirement and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The BE shall replace all invalid data with zero values.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a low-level data‐validation detail with no significant impact on system architecture or structural design.

requirement: The BE shall keep track of data invalids.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data validation and tracking function that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The BE shall be able to apply normalizations based on reported data invalid counts.
ASR_Explanation: This is an internal data processing detail that doesn’t impose new architectural patterns or structural constraints.

requirement: The BE shall be able to apply corrections based on state count and/or quantizer power measurement data. This is the VanVleck correction.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a new VanVleck correction stage in the data processing pipeline, affecting module boundaries, data interfaces, and performance provisioning.

requirement: The BE shall be able to make time stamp adjustments as required by the observational mode and correlator output parameters. This may arise when recirculation is used.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the design of the timing subsystem and data pipeline to support dynamic timestamp adjustments based on observational modes and correlator outputs.

requirement: The BE shall be able to perform windowing operations prior and subsequent to Fourier Transform. This will be needed for narrow band RFI mitigation. Post Fourier Transform windowing will be applied as a convolution.
ASR_Explanation: It shapes the signal-processing pipeline architecture by adding dedicated pre/post-FFT windowing and convolution stages that impact performance and resource allocation.

requirement: The BE shall be able to apply user selected time domain processes. These processes should be constructed to be chainable (output of any time domain process can be piped to input of any other, including replica of self and Fourier Transform) and repeatable in the chain. No optional time domain processes have as yet been proposed.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a modular, pipeline‐based architecture with pluggable, chainable processing stages, impacting core data flow, component design, and performance management.

requirement: The BE shall be able to perform Fourier Transform the lag set time series. A power-of-two complex-to-complex Fast Fourier Transform with retention of all output positive and negative frequencies will be used. This process must be able to accept as input the output of any of the time domain processes.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating a power-of-two complex-to-complex FFT with universal input compatibility drives core module decomposition, performance tuning, and memory management.

requirement: The BE shall be able to apply user selected frequency domain processes. These processes should be constructed to be chainable (output of Fourier Transform and any frequency domain process can be piped to input of any frequency domain process including replica of self) and repeatable in the chain. No frequency domain processes have as yet been proposed.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a modular, extensible pipeline architecture for chaining and repeatable frequency-domain processes, impacting component boundaries and data-flow design.

requirement: The BE shall be able to sum the frequency domain, spectral results. The amount (time duration) of summation will be controlled by an observational mode parameter obtained via M&C. The BE shall keep track of the number of samples/dumps integrated in each spectral channel. The summation will occur after all optional frequency domain processing, or if none, after the Fourier Transform. Integration for long periods of time is what will throttle the output of the Correlator to a rate manageable by the E2E.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the design of the data-processing pipeline and buffering/throughput management to support long-duration spectral integration.

requirement: The BE shall combine the finished spectra with meta- and auxiliary data to form suitably formatted output data sets. AIPS++ Measurement Sets are the expected entities.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-specific data processing/detail requirement that doesn’t drive high-level architectural structure or patterns.

requirement: The BE shall store formatted output data records in a memory buffer with backup disk buffering. Store data ready for transmission to the e2e System until successful transfer has occurred. Report any errors and buffer overflow conditions that occur.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the buffering and persistence layer (memory vs. disk) and impacts fault-tolerance, error handling, and reliable data transmission architecture.

requirement: The BE shall send output data to the e2e System.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the integration architecture by specifying interfaces, data flow, and coupling between the backend and the end-to-end system.

requirement: The BE shall verify that all sent data was successfully received. Report all errors.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the choice of communication protocols, acknowledgement patterns, and centralized error-handling infrastructure.

requirement: The BE shall monitor data transfer rates from the Correlator and to the e2e. Accumulate data transfer statistics and compare against tolerances. Report all out of tolerance conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a monitoring/telemetry subsystem for real-time metrics collection, threshold enforcement, and alert reporting.

requirement: The BE shall monitor the overall data processing rate. Compare against tolerances and report all out of tolerance conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces a cross-cutting monitoring and alerting subsystem requiring real-time metrics collection, threshold evaluation, and reporting architecture.

requirement: The BE shall trap, flag and repair inf’s, NaN’s, underflows, overflows and other computation errors. Accumulate computation error statistics and compare against tolerances. Report all out of tolerance conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the error-handling and telemetry architecture by mandating fault detection, recovery paths, statistics aggregation and tolerance checking.

requirement: The BE shall periodically or upon request check PID’s and assure that all started tasks are alive and running. Report missing, stopped, defunct and other damaged processes.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a monitoring and fault‐detection subsystem with scheduling, concurrency, and reporting mechanisms that shape the system’s reliability and operational architecture.

requirement: The BE shall periodically or upon request check Backend physical processors and assure that all needed processors are alive and responding. Report all crashed, stopped, or unresponsive processors.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the system’s health-check and monitoring infrastructure, including scheduling, probing, and alerting components.

requirement: The BE shall periodically or upon request check all Backend internal networks and assure that all communication connections are intact and functioning. Report all non-functioning components.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the inclusion of a health‐check/monitoring subsystem with scheduling and alerting, impacting the infrastructure and service orchestration layers.

requirement: The BE shall be able to initiate a processing task on any Backend processor.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s integration architecture by requiring an extensible task‐dispatch mechanism and dynamic routing to heterogeneous backend processors.

requirement: The BE shall be able to signal a kill for any Backend process.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the process supervision architecture by requiring OS-level signaling, permission controls, and lifecycle management.

requirement: The BE shall be able to alter the priority of any of the BE tasks.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional capability that can be added without impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: The BE shall be able to initiate a reboot of any Backend a physical processor.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces hardware control and fault-management interfaces requiring secure, privileged reboot orchestration in the system architecture.

requirement: The BE shall be able to initiate a reboot of any internal network.
ASR_Explanation: This requires high-privilege network management integration and security boundary design, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: The BE shall be able to redistribute internal workload among its processors. This may involve change of destination IP address(es) for the Correlator network.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s architecture by mandating dynamic load-balancing and network reconfiguration capabilities across processor nodes.

requirement: BE processes shall not violate archive data requirements. All processes shall be reversible; the raw unconverted input always being recoverable from the output.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing data storage and processing pipelines to support reversible transformations and archival compliance.

requirement: Input Data Queue – a memory buffer of lag frames. Data entry status queue to track each record in the buffer. The lag frames will contain all information necessary to properly assemble complete lag sets.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level data handling detail (buffer and status tracking) that does not drive high-level architectural structure or patterns.

requirement: Output Data Queue – a memory buffer plus backup disk storage of all processed spectra. These will be converted to output AIPS++ Measurement Set entities prior to transfer to the e2e. Data entry status queue to track each record in the buffer.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of buffering, persistent storage, backup and asynchronous processing components for data flow and tracking.

requirement: Processing Parameters – names, position(s) in sequence, and adjustable parameters for all fixed and user selectable processing pipeline applications.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a flexible, extensible processing‐pipeline framework, impacting module interfaces, sequencing orchestration, and configuration management.

requirement: Processing flags – a table of flags needed to identify various internal conditions relating to error response and processing state.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement describes low-level state and error flag details affecting implementation rather than core architectural structure.

requirement: Metadata – All internally and externally generated data about the processed time series and spectra including invalid data flags, processes applied, coordinates, etc.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a comprehensive metadata management layer, impacting data modeling, storage schema, APIs, and overall system integration.

requirement: Error Report – error number (translatable into text error message), error source, error rates (as applicable), and time stamp.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement for error logging and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Warning Report – warning number (translatable into text warning message), warning source, warning rates (as applicable), and time stamp.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward reporting requirement with no significant impact on high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Failure Report – internal system component (e.g., disk drive, processors, processes, and networks) failure number (translatable into text error message) and time stamp.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the system’s logging and error-reporting framework, including standardized error codes, text localization, and timestamp handling.

requirement: Recovery Report – internal system component (process, processor, network) recovery action result.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This requirement mandates a cross-cutting recovery‐reporting subsystem and impacts logging, monitoring, and component interaction design.

requirement: Status Report – internal system component (process, processor, network) functional state.
ASR_Explanation: It influences the architecture for internal monitoring and health‐reporting, requiring instrumentation and telemetry interfaces.

requirement: The Backend System shall maintain input data fidelity and dynamic range across all processing, manipulation and I/O functions.
ASR_Explanation: It drives data format, processing pipeline and hardware design choices to ensure high-precision data fidelity and dynamic range are preserved.

requirement: The system shall be capable of flagging and marking corrupted data segments and proceeding without interruption or effect on other data. This includes, but is not limited to, partial data, zero data, underflows, overflows, infinities, and NaN’s whether obtained on input or arising during processing.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the design of a fault-tolerant data‐processing pipeline with validation and isolation layers to flag and quarantine corrupted segments without halting the system.

requirement: The BE System shall be capable of accepting an aggregate data input stream from the Correlator of a minimum of 1.6 Gbytes/sec. This must be done simultaneously with the output stream, but not necessarily over the same interconnects. This is an initial deployment specification and will be increased over time.
ASR_Explanation: This throughput requirement drives critical decisions around network architecture, hardware sizing, and data pipeline design to sustain 1.6 GB/s input and simultaneous output.

requirement: The BE System shall be capable of delivering an output data stream to the e2e System of a minimum of 25 MBytes/sec. This includes resends and simultaneous transfer of data stored due to a previous e2e connection outage. This must be done simultaneously with the output stream, but not necessarily over the same interconnects. This is an initial deployment specification and will be increased over time.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the data‐transport architecture by imposing high‐throughput, concurrent streaming, buffering, and scalable interconnect requirements.

requirement: The total processor capability of the BE System shall be (combination of numbers of processors and individual processor speed) sufficient to accomplish all processing tasks while avoiding loss or delay on the input and output data streams.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes critical performance and hardware configuration constraints that directly influence system architecture, scalability, and real-time data processing design.

requirement: The BE System shall have sufficient (amount TBD) memory with sufficient (rate TBD) access speeds to accomplish all processing tasks while avoiding loss or delay on the input and output data streams.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architectural decisions on memory sizing, data buffering, caching strategies, and I/O throughput to ensure lossless, low-latency processing.

requirement: The BE System shall have sufficient storage (memory and/or disk) with sufficient access speeds to meet short duration Correlator bursting demands (level TBD) plus a standby reserve (amount TBD) to meet reliability needs and handle outage recovery demands.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the storage subsystem architecture by imposing capacity, performance, scalability, and redundancy requirements.

requirement: All math/science application software shall take optimal advantage of all language, compiler, and system computational features and resources to reduce run times to the minimum practical level.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architecture-level decisions on performance optimization, resource utilization, parallelism, and compiler/language integration.

requirement: All management software functions shall take optimal advantage of all language, compiler and system features and resources to reduce overheads to the minimum practical level.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around language, compiler optimizations, and system-level resource management to minimize overhead.

requirement: All input and output, and storage and retrieval operations shall take optimal advantage of all system resources to reduce overhead and latency to the minimal practical level.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around I/O subsystems, caching strategies, concurrency models, and resource management to meet performance targets.

requirement: All data processing functions shall be chainable (outputs pipeable to inputs) and repeatable in the processing pipeline in cases where this makes computational sense.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint drives the design of the dataflow architecture, module interfaces, and orchestration mechanisms to support pipeable, repeatable processing pipelines.

requirement: Operating system, message passing and other middle-ware, and programming language(s) used shall follow industry standards and be commonly available and widely used. Availability of source code for the OS will be very important.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the choice of OS, middleware and languages, directly impacting platform selection, interoperability, licensing and maintainability.

requirement: The Backend System shall be self-monitoring. It will be capable of detecting, reporting on and automatically taking action to remedy or lessen the impact of, at a minimum, the following types of abnormal conditions: processor hardware failure, operating system hangs or crashes, computational performance below minimum specifications, computational error rates above maximum specification, internal communications failures, and external (with the Correlator and E2E) communications disruptions.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the inclusion of monitoring, fault-detection, and self-healing components, shaping the system’s resilience and operational architecture.

requirement: The software part of the system shall be able to perform without total system restart due to internal failure between array maintenance windows.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a fault‐tolerant, high‐availability architecture with isolation and redundancy to enable self‐healing without full restarts.

requirement: The hardware part of the system shall be able to perform indefinitely without complete loss of service, except in the event of total failure of primary and backup power.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the hardware architecture by requiring redundant power supplies, UPS systems, and failover mechanisms to guarantee continuous availability.

requirement: The system shall be capable of responding in a loss-less manner to I/O and processing changes arising from Correlator mode changes.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for dynamic reconfiguration, buffering, and state-management mechanisms to ensure seamless, loss-less mode transitions at the architectural level.

requirement: The system shall continue to operate in a loss-less manner in the event of a temporary (time duration TBD) loss of availability of the e2e System.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the system’s resilience and availability architecture, requiring fault-tolerance mechanisms such as caching, queuing, and failover to ensure lossless operation.

requirement: The system shall be able to complete processing of all onboard data, deliver the results to the End-to-End System and maintain availability for immediate resumption of operations once Correlator access is restored.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives choices around offline-capable processing, data buffering/persistence, fault-tolerance and high-availability mechanisms for seamless recovery.

requirement: The system shall continue to operate during the absence of the M&C System until the first encounter of unavailable critical auxiliary data. The system will cache a predetermined amount (TBD) of correlator data after the first encounter of unavailable critical data and complete all requested operations on cached data once the unavailable critical data is obtained.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the high-availability and caching strategy in the system architecture to handle M&C outages and data recovery.

requirement: The system shall be able to sit at idle and resume operations with minimal (amount TBD) delay.
ASR_Explanation: This idle-resume delay requirement drives architectural decisions around state preservation, resource allocation, and wake-up mechanisms to meet performance targets.

requirement: All system processing and interconnect hardware shall be readily accessible for maintenance, repair, replacement and/or reconfiguration.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes serviceability and physical accessibility constraints on the hardware architecture, influencing component modularity and arrangement.

requirement: All systems and application source code shall be available to or on the systems that execute it.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates including and managing source code in the deployment architecture, impacting packaging, storage, and CI/CD pipeline design.

requirement: All software application modules shall be debuggable.
ASR_Explanation: Debuggability is primarily an implementation and tooling concern and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: All software processes shall be killable, restartable, debuggable and testable without affecting normal operations.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives requirements for process supervision, graceful shutdown/restart and observability, impacting runtime management, resilience and deployment architecture.

requirement: Software tools and pre-built applications that do not have source code available shall come with a complete diagnostic package and customer support.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates inclusion of diagnostic interfaces, logging, and support infrastructure, impacting component boundaries and deployment architecture.

requirement: Operating system software shall either have source code available or come with sufficient diagnostics and customer support.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR influences the choice of operating system and its integration, affecting maintainability, diagnostics, and support strategies in the overall architecture.

requirement: I/O, communications, and processing hardware shall be easily expandable, reconfigureable, augmentable and replaceable to meet increasing data transfer and processing demands imposed by EVLA science, Correlator changes, and availability of new hardware.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes a modular, scalable hardware and interface architecture to support future expansion, reconfiguration, and hardware substitution.

requirement: Scalability shall be accomplished in manner that is transparent to processing, communications and I/O software functions with the possible exception of recompilation of executables.
ASR_Explanation: It requires introducing abstraction layers or middleware to decouple scalability from core modules, fundamentally shaping the system’s architecture.

requirement: Scalability shall be accomplished in a manner that is seamless, in that it does not affect hardware modules or software functionality that it meets at interfaces.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes an architectural constraint on how scalability is achieved without altering module interfaces, impacting overall system modularity and deployment.

requirement: The Backend system shall ultimately be scaleable to an extent that it will be capable of handling up to two Gbytes per second per Correlator output channel in real-time.
ASR_Explanation: This throughput requirement drives core architectural decisions around high-throughput data streaming, distributed processing, and scalable infrastructure.

requirement: The Backend System needs a robust security mechanism in place so that unauthorized users are not allowed access. Authorized users are expected to be restricted to software and hardware development, testing, maintenance and operations personnel.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of the system’s authentication and authorization architecture, impacting security patterns like RBAC, IAM integration, and data protection strategies.

requirement: All users of the Backend System must be uniquely identified. This could be done via a username and associated password scheme that would authenticate and authorize the user access to the system and, if applicable, grant the user access to restricted or controlled parts of the system. If a user cannot be identified, they will not be given access. In order to monitor all past access to the system, all attempts to access the system should be logged.
ASR_Explanation: Mandates the design of a security architecture encompassing authentication, authorization, user identity management, and auditing/logging infrastructure.

requirement: Systems operations should be given unrestricted access to all aspects of the system and should have the authority to grant and revoke privileges on a per-user basis. Development, testing and maintenance personnel, on the other hand, require access to some parts of the system, but not all, indicating that an access level is needed that allows privileges to be granted on a per-user and what-do-you-need-to-do basis.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the core security architecture by defining a fine-grained, per-user privilege and role/attribute-based access control framework.

requirement: All users of the system shall login using some form of unique identification. (e.g., username and password)
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring an authentication service, identity management, and secure credential storage.

requirement: All login attempts shall be done in a secure manner. (e.g., encrypted passwords)
ASR_Explanation: This security NFR dictates the design of the authentication subsystem, enforcing encryption, key management, and secure transport protocols.

requirement: A system administrator shall have unrestricted access to all aspects of the system.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes cross-cutting security and access-control requirements that shape the system’s authentication/authorization architecture.

requirement: Each user shall have a set of system access properties that defines the user’s privileges within the system. (e.g., the subsystems a user may control or system tools the user may access).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s authentication/authorization architecture (e.g., role-based access control and security module integration).

requirement: The administrator shall have the ability to create and add a new user to the system.

requirement: The administrator shall have the ability to remove a user from the system.

requirement: The administrator shall have the ability to edit a user’s system access properties.

requirement: The administrator shall have the ability to block all access to the system for all users or selectively by user. (All blocked users with active sessions shall automatically be logged off.)

requirement: The system shall continue operations, although not necessarily at full capacity, on all unaffected resources during partial shutdowns for maintenance, repair and/or upgrade.

requirement: The system shall be able to handle non-real-time operations in a transparent fashion (i.e., as if real-time). Note: non-real-time refers to input data from a source other than the Correlator (defined as real-time).

requirement: Modular design principles shall be employed to the maximum extent possible. Maximal practical use of available “hot-swappable” devices and components shall be made.

requirement: Claim Status – Status of the claim is depends information available in the Claim, if any false data or any mandatory information is available in Claim, then the claims status will change, if the value of status is not “Accepted” then the claim will be stored in quarantine area and can able to precede it further from its status. The system should have a provision to capture the reason suppose if the claim status is “Rejection or Quarantine”.
ASR_Explanation: This is standard domain logic for claim processing and does not impose major architectural constraints.

requirement: Claim Amount – The calculation of claim amount is explained below: If it is a First visit Claim, Claim Amount will capture First Visit Consultation Fee (from VSP Payment terms) + Lab test Amount (from VSP Payment terms) + (Qty of drugs * Retails Price (from Drugs Master)). If Claim is for first follow up, Claim Amount will capture First Follow up Fee (from VSP Payment terms) + (Qty of drugs * Retails Price (from Drugs Master)). If Claim is for second follow up, Claim Amount will capture Second Follow up Fee (from VSP Payment terms) + (Qty of drugs * Retails Price (from Drugs Master)).
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed business rule for claim amount calculation and does not drive any changes to the overall architecture.

requirement: The system captures the thumb impression available in each claim form, and needs to compare it with the same voucher numbers previous visits if available. If the thumb impression are mismatching for the same patient (Client / Partner) on same voucher the system would automatically make a count on it.

requirement: If the claim from same VSP having more than two times of Thumb Impression mismatching, then the system should automatically produce a warning message and the same time the system should In-Activate the VSP.

requirement: The GSM supplementary services [EN 301 515, Index [9]] to be supported are: Calling Line Identification Presentation (CLIP) (M), Connected Line Identification Presentation (CoLP) (M), Call Forwarding Unconditional (CFU) (M), Call Forwarding on Mobile Subscriber Busy (CFB) (M), Call waiting (CW) (M), Call hold (HOLD) (M), Multi Party Service (MPTY) (M), Closed User Group (CUG) (M), BOIC except those to Home PLMN Country (BOIC-exHC) (M), Barring of Incoming Calls when Roaming Outside the Home PLMN Country (BIC-Roam) (M), Unstructured Supplementary Service Data (USSD) (M), Sub-addressing (M), Enhanced Multi-Level Precedence and Pre-emption (eMLPP) (M), User-to-User Signalling 1 (UUS1) (M).

requirement: Coverage probability of 95% based on a coverage level of 38.5 dBmV/m (-98 dBm) for voice and non-safety critical data shall apply.

requirement: The network shall terminate the ongoing VGCS/VBS call if it receives the 3-digit sequence “***” transmitted via DTMF signals.

requirement: The duration of the tone generated by the fixed line dispatcher shall be 70ms ± 5ms, and there shall be a minimum gap of 65ms between each tone.

requirement: The muting and unmuting for VGCS shall be in line with [EN 301 515, Index [4]].

requirement: The network shall send the SET-PARAMETER message with the attribute “D-ATT = T” to the mobile station of the talking subscriber if it receives the 3-digit sequence “###” transmitted via DTMF or the group call SETUP message from a controller terminal.

requirement: When the network has detected the 3-digit DTMF sequence “###” transmitted via DTMF from a controller terminal and if the controller was not previously talking it should indicate its recognition by playing a single DTMF grant tone “#” of duration of 100ms ± 5ms to be sent to that controller terminal only.

requirement: The network shall send the SET-PARAMETER message with the attribute “D-ATT = F” to the mobile of the talking subscriber only if it has received the 3-digit sequence “#**” transmitted via DTMF from all the talking controllers.

requirement: The duration of each tone added in the voice channel, shall be 70ms ± 5ms, and there shall be a minimum gap of 65ms between each tone.
