requirement: EIRENE mobile equipment shall be capable of operating between altitudes of -100m and 1800m, referenced to sea level.
ASR_Explanation: This environmental operating constraint primarily drives hardware component selection without impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: The equipment shall be able to cope with relative humidities of 100% for short periods, although the yearly average is expected to be 75%. The equipment shall also cope with 95% humidity for 30 days in the year.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the hardware and enclosure architecture by requiring designs and materials rated for high-humidity resistance and environmental sealing.

requirement: Operationally caused infrequent and slight moisture condensation shall not lead to any malfunction or failure.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the system’s environmental protection architecture, influencing enclosure design and hardware sealing strategies to ensure moisture resilience.

requirement: All equipment shall not degrade photochemically when exposed to solar radiation of up to 1200 W/m2.
ASR_Explanation: This is a materials durability constraint for hardware and does not drive core software architecture decisions.

requirement: All EIRENE mobile equipment shall be protected against shock and vibration in compliance with standards defined in [prEN 50125] using tests defined in [prEN 50155].
ASR_Explanation: This environmental durability requirement concerns hardware-level design and does not drive the system’s software or high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: All handheld mobile equipment shall be capable of withstanding non-repetitive shocks of up to 3g for up to 100ms under normal conditions and free fall from 0.5m.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It drives the overall system and hardware platform architecture by imposing enclosure, component selection, and mechanical design constraints for shock resilience.

requirement: EIRENE mobile equipment shall be capable of being subjected to both sinusoidal and random vibration.
ASR_Explanation: This environmental vibration requirement affects detailed mechanical design and component ruggedization rather than the high-level system architecture.

requirement: Handheld mobile equipment shall be capable of withstanding continuous sinusoidal vibration: frequency range 5-200 Hz; peak-to-peak amplitude 7.5 mm; peak acceleration 1.5g.
ASR_Explanation: This is a hardware durability specification with no direct impact on the software architectural design.

requirement: The random vibrations to be withstood by mobile equipment shall be 0.25g in all three axes of freedom.
ASR_Explanation: This vibration tolerance is a mechanical hardware constraint and does not affect the software system’s architecture.

requirement: Battery requirements shall be provided without the use of discontinuous reception or transmission (DTX/DRX).
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system architecture’s power management and radio subsystem design by requiring continuous operation without using DTX/DRX.

requirement: Battery requirements shall be met based on full power during transmission and assuming hourly periodic location updating.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s power management and transmission scheduling architecture, driving hardware and firmware design choices.

requirement: All EIRENE mobile equipment shall be immune to external EMC as defined in [ENV 50121 part 4].
ASR_Explanation: It enforces electromagnetic compatibility constraints on hardware architecture, affecting component selection, layout, and shielding design.

requirement: The transmission of EM radiation from all EIRENE mobile equipment shall comply with the radio frequency transmission masks defined in [EN 301 515, Index [35]] for the range of GSM frequencies defined in section 1 of this document.
ASR_Explanation: This RF compliance requirement drives the selection and design of the hardware/RF subsystem (antenna, shielding, transceiver) and thus influences the overall system architecture.

requirement: Where the emission levels defined by [EN 301 515, Index [35]] exceed those stated in [ENV 50121 part 4], the GSM specification shall take precedence.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes regulatory compliance constraints on the emission-control subsystem’s design and specification-selection logic.

requirement: Mobile equipment shall comply with the generic standard for EMC in the industrial environment as defined in [ENV 50081 part 2], except for emissions at GSM frequencies as noted above.
ASR_Explanation: It drives hardware/system architecture decisions for EMC compliance, affecting shielding, board layout, and component selection.

requirement: The emission and immunity standards for the general railway environment and ancillary services as defined in [ENV 50121 parts 1, 2, 3-1, 3-2 and 5] shall be considered.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces EMC/EMI compliance constraints that will drive hardware architecture choices such as enclosure design, PCB layout, and filtering components.

requirement: The environmental and physical tolerance of the EIRENE mobile radio units shall be tested at a facility in accordance with [EN 45001].
ASR_Explanation: This is a validation/testing requirement for compliance and does not drive major architectural design decisions.

requirement: All EMC emission and immunity tests shall be performed in accordance with guidelines defined in the [EN 61000-4] series and in [EN 50140].
ASR_Explanation: It drives hardware architecture choices for PCB layout, enclosure design, shielding, and grounding to ensure compliance with EMC standards.

requirement: Environmental testing procedures shall follow guidelines defined in [IEC 68 part 1].
ASR_Explanation: This requirement governs testing procedures compliance and does not impose constraints on the system’s architectural design.

requirement: Specific environmental test procedures to be followed for EIRENE mobile equipment shall include the following tests as defined in the [IEC 68] series: A Cold; B Dry heat; D Damp heat (cyclic); E Impact; F Vibration; G Acceleration; K Corrosive atmospheres; M Air pressure; N Change of temperature; P Fire hazard.
ASR_Explanation: This is a compliance/testing procedure requirement and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Upon an appropriate MMI action, the Cab radio shall initiate a call to the appropriate controller with ‘Railway operation’ priority.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional flow for MMI-triggered call setup and fits within existing radio/MMI subsystems with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: The calling driver’s functional number shall be passed to the network using UUS1.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level protocol detail (UUS1 signaling) that impacts implementation rather than the high-level system architecture.

requirement: On receipt of a ‘Call other drivers in area’ request, the radio shall initiate a group call using the ‘all trains’ group identification with eMLPP priority level 2.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the call-control subsystem architecture by mandating group‐call initiation with specific eMLPP priority handling.

requirement: Activation of the ‘Railway emergency call’ function shall cause the radio to initiate a Railway emergency call as defined in section 13.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement for a specific call flow and does not drive high-level structural or platform decisions.

requirement: Where there is more than one active cab, the radio connection shall be provided using the GSM Multi-Party service.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the communication subsystem by mandating integration with GSM Multi-Party service for multi-cab radio connections.

requirement: Upon activation of the function ‘Call train staff’, the radio shall determine the appropriate functional number based on the staff member selected and the train number. A GSM point-to-point voice call at ‘Railway operation’ priority shall then be initiated.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts system architecture by mandating integration with radio/GSM subsystems for dynamic number resolution, call initiation, and prioritized voice communication.

requirement: The Cab radio shall be capable of being used as a standard GSM telephone, such that the driver is able to call any valid number subject to closed user group, call barring or other restrictions.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the integration of a GSM telephony stack, hardware module and call‐restriction services, impacting network interfaces and system security architecture.

requirement: The Cab radio shall be able to receive, display and store incoming short (SMS) text messages.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional feature with no broad architectural impact on system structure or quality attributes.

requirement: The Cab radio shall support shunting mode communications as defined in section 14.
ASR_Explanation: It requires extending the communication subsystem architecture to support the shunting-mode protocol and related interfaces.

requirement: When switched on, the radio shall initiate automatic self-testing using the GSM IMSI attach procedure.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s startup architecture by requiring integration of a self-test module with the GSM IMSI attach procedure.

requirement: Upon switch on, the Cab radio shall be registered with a mobile network.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior with limited impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: If registration is not successful an audible and visual indication shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level error feedback requirement with no broad architectural implications.

requirement: Upon registration, the mobile shall be accessible by calling the MSISDN or the Engine or Coach number with which it is associated. This shall require the home network database to maintain this correlation.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates changes to the home network’s data model and registration service to support MSISDN/engine/coach number correlation, impacting database schema and service interfaces.

requirement: Upon activation of the registration function, a USSD message shall be sent by the Cab radio.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the communication layer architecture by mandating USSD gateway integration and real-time messaging support.

requirement: Upon activation of the deregistration function, a USSD message shall be sent by the Cab radio.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level functional detail and does not impose significant architectural constraints or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: Upon detection of the additional on-train functions for equipment physically connected to the Cab radio, a USSD message shall be sent by the Cab radio after activation of the registration or deregistration function.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of event-detection and USSD messaging interfaces in the Cab radio subsystem, affecting module boundaries and communication protocols.

requirement: This procedure shall take place at the installation of the Cab radio. It shall be initiated by an external device or by a member of a maintenance team.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized installation procedure requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: On-train functions for equipment physically connected to the Cab radio shall be registered or deregistered automatically based on a USSD message sent by the Cab radio.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates dynamic service registration/deregistration and USSD-driven event handling, impacting the system’s module management and messaging architecture.

requirement: The sequence of actions required for a mobile originated call to another user shall be as follows: Initiating a call, Indication, Call arbitration, Conversation, Call termination.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional call flow definition without major impact on system structure or quality‐attribute trade-offs.

requirement: The sequence of actions for a mobile terminated call to a driver shall be as follows: Call arbitration, Indication, Answering the call, Indication, Conversation, Call termination.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional call-flow sequence that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The Cab radio system shall provide a means for the driver to terminate established calls which he is authorised to terminate.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional requirement for call control with limited architectural impact.

requirement: It shall be possible to initiate outgoing voice calls in one of four ways depending on the intended recipient(s) of the call: Emergency access, Priority access, Stored number, Dial access.
ASR_Explanation: Defines distinct call-routing pathways (emergency, priority, stored, dial) that shape the high-level call-handling modules and interfaces.

requirement: Emergency access shall be provided to initiate the following call: Emergency call number, Group call, eMLPP priority 0, Functional number.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates specialized emergency call routing and priority handling, impacting the call-processing and QoS architecture.

requirement: Priority access shall be provided to initiate the following: Primary controller, Secondary controller, Power supply controller, Other drivers in area, Other drivers on same train, Chief conductor.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s core access-control and messaging architecture by enforcing priority-based initiation rights among multiple controllers and roles.

requirement: The driver shall be able to initiate a call by selecting a name/number from stored number information in the radio.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI-driven call-initiation feature that can be implemented within existing contact management and telephony modules without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: Facilities shall be provided to support a list of stored names/numbers of up to a minimum of 100 entries.
ASR_Explanation: This small-capacity storage requirement does not drive any significant architectural decisions.

requirement: Stored number access shall be provided to initiate the following calls: Calls to stored numbers, Point-to-point/VGCS/VBS, eMLPP priority 4, Functional number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional detail for call initiation and does not impose broad architectural constraints or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: By default, calls will have a priority of railway information calls (eMLPP priority designation 4). It shall be possible to store a priority in association with a stored number.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the core call-processing subsystem and data model by introducing per-contact priority storage and handling.

requirement: The driver shall be able to initiate a call by dialling any valid telephone number or functional number.
ASR_Explanation: This simply defines a standard call initiation feature via existing telephony APIs and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Dial access shall be provided to initiate the following calls: Calls to other users, Point-to-point/VGCS/VBS, eMLPP priority 4, Functional number.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the call-control architecture by mandating support for multiple call types (VGCS/VBS/point-to-point), eMLPP priority handling, and functional number routing.

requirement: Abbreviated dialling facilities shall be supported.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a feature‐level enhancement to the call handling module that can be implemented within the existing system architecture without imposing new architectural constraints.

requirement: Dialled calls from the MMI shall be point-to-point voice calls unless otherwise entered from the MMI at the time of initiation.
ASR_Explanation: This is a functional behavior detail of call routing and does not impose structural or quality-attribute constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: By default, dialled calls shall have a priority of railway information calls (eMLPP priority designation 4).
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed default configuration for call priority and does not drive any fundamental changes to the system’s architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to receive and manage the following incoming calls: emergency calls, group calls, broadcast calls, point-to-point calls, multi-party calls.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the call-management architecture, requiring support for diverse call types, routing logic, and real-time resource allocation.

requirement: The Cab radio shall arbitrate between calls when: an incoming call is received whilst the Cab radio is in an on-going call; a Cab radio user attempts to initiate a call whilst the Cab radio is in an on-going call.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the call-control module’s state management and concurrency arbitration architecture.

requirement: The Cab radio shall apply the arbitration rules outlined in Table 5A-1 in Appendix 5A.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional rule for message arbitration and does not drive high-level structural or non-functional architectural decisions.

requirement: Once a call has been established the connected parties shall be able to communicate.
ASR_Explanation: It describes basic call functionality without imposing any architectural constraints or structural impacts.

requirement: Replacing the handset shall result in the outcomes listed in Tables 5A-2 and 5A-3.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior requirement for handset replacement outcomes and does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: Calls shall be able to be terminated by either party subject to the requirements shown in tables 5A-2 and 5A-3.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional detail about call termination logic and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Incoming calls to the Cab radio shall be routed to the correct on-train user or device using information contained in the sub-addressing field.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the core communication subsystem’s routing logic and message‐processing architecture by requiring parsing of sub‐address fields and mapping to devices.

requirement: Using the GSM “Manual” network selection procedure, the Cab radio application shall allow the driver to access a prioritised list of authorised networks and shall allow the driver to select a desired network from this list. This function shall not be available if there is an ongoing voice call involving the Cab radio.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the overall system architecture by mandating integration between the network‐selection module, call‐state management, and the UI layer to enforce state‐based access control.

requirement: If directed network selection is implemented, the directed network selection procedure shall be initiated by an external trigger mechanism, which instructs the Cab radio application to select the required network unless a voice call is ongoing.
ASR_Explanation: It requires an event-driven integration between external triggers and the radio app’s call-state manager, impacting component boundaries and messaging flows.

requirement: If directed network selection is implemented and voice calls are ongoing at the time when the external device attempts to trigger a network change, an audible and visual indication shall be given to the driver and network change shall be deferred until the call is terminated or until coverage of the current network is lost.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the architecture by mandating coordination between network‐management, telephony services, and UI subsystems for deferred network changes and driver notifications.

requirement: If directed network selection is implemented, the Cab radio application shall use the GSM “Manual” network selection procedure to instruct the MT to register with the required network.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional detail of network selection and does not drive major architectural structure or trade-offs.

requirement: If automatic network selection is implemented, the driver shall be capable of deactivating and/or re-activating this function using simple MMI actions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI control requirement that does not drive high-level architectural structure or key system partitioning.

requirement: Whilst automatic network selection is enabled, the MMI shall display an indication.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI display requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: In idle mode, if the GSM Service Indicator is lost, the mobile shall give an audible and visual indication.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/notification detail that can be handled within existing service‐monitoring and alert modules without altering the overall system architecture.

requirement: Train-mounted equipment including the Cab radio terminal equipment, MMI and antenna shall comply with all specifications in section 4 and all of those defined in this section, with those defined in this section taking priority.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives hardware architecture decisions by imposing specification compliance priorities that affect subsystem selection and integration.

requirement: The Cab radio shall be capable of operating within a temperature range of -20°C to +70°C.
ASR_Explanation: This environmental constraint dictates hardware component selection, enclosure design, and thermal management architecture.

requirement: The aerial and any other equipment mounted external to the train shall be capable of withstanding extremes of temperature from -40°C to +70°C.
ASR_Explanation: This is a hardware environmental constraint on component selection and materials, not impacting the system’s software or architectural structure.

requirement: The aerial and any other equipment mounted external to the train shall function correctly during rapid temperature fluctuations of up to 3°C/second.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the system’s thermal management and enclosure architecture, dictating component selection and insulation strategies.

requirement: Any equipment mounted external to the train cab shall withstand in-tunnel pressure pulses of 6 kPa (peak to peak) for up to 3 seconds and pressure gradients of up to 100 kPa/s.
ASR_Explanation: This is purely a mechanical/environmental hardware constraint and does not affect the software or high‐level system architecture.

requirement: The “Subscriber Identity Module” (SIM card) shall be physically integrated with the radio set and shall not be able to be removed except by maintenance staff.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes a hardware integration and maintainability constraint that will shape the system’s physical and security architecture.

requirement: Any equipment mounted inside the train cab shall be capable of withstanding maximum levels of sinusoidal vibration: frequency range 5-200 Hz; peak-to-peak amplitude 7mm; acceleration 1.5g.
ASR_Explanation: This vibration resilience requirement drives selection of ruggedized hardware platforms and enclosures, impacting the system’s physical architecture.

requirement: Any equipment mounted external to the train cab shall withstand maximum levels of sinusoidal vibration: frequency range 5-1000 Hz; peak-to-peak amplitude 5mm; acceleration 2.5g.
ASR_Explanation: This vibration requirement drives hardware platform selection and enclosure/mounting design affecting the system’s physical architecture.

requirement: The Cab radio shall comply with draft European standard [prEN 50124 part 1] concerning insulation co-ordination with reference to clearances and creepages.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the physical hardware architecture by constraining PCB layout, component spacing, and insulation materials to meet mandatory safety clearances.

requirement: The Cab radio equipment shall be capable of withstanding the following changes to the main and backup power supplies without interrupting normal operation: voltage fluctuations up to ±15% of nominal supply voltage; 20% over voltage for up to 10s; other transient effects according to [IEC 571 parts 1-3].
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the hardware and system architecture for power regulation, conditioning, and redundancy to ensure uninterrupted operation under specified voltage and transient conditions.

requirement: The Cab radio shall withstand the effects of power supply transients as defined in [prEN 50155].
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates the power subsystem architecture, requiring specific transient suppression, filtering, and component selection to comply with prEN 50155.

requirement: The driver and other in-cab equipment shall be protected against all electrical hazards arising from EIRENE mobile equipment as defined in [EN 50153].
ASR_Explanation: This safety NFR drives the physical/electrical architecture by requiring isolation, filtering, and protective components to meet EN 50153 compliance.

requirement: EMC emission from the Cab radio shall comply with [ENV 50121 parts 1, 2, 3-2 and 4].
ASR_Explanation: It imposes electromagnetic compatibility constraints that drive hardware enclosure, PCB layout, filtering and grounding architecture.

requirement: Emissions from the train mounted antenna associated with the Cab radio shall be limited to those specified by [EN 301 515, Index [35]].
ASR_Explanation: This is a hardware/regulatory emissions limit and does not drive software architecture decisions.

requirement: Any emissions radiating into the driver’s cab and other on-board equipment from the exterior aerial shall meet the requirements defined in [ENV 50121 parts 1, 2, 3-1, 3-2 and 4] to the highest possible degree.
ASR_Explanation: This EMI/EMC requirement drives hardware architecture choices (shielding, filtering, component selection and layout) to ensure compliance.

requirement: The Cab radio shall pass electrical tests as defined in [IEC 571 parts 1, 2 and 3].
ASR_Explanation: This is a certification/test compliance requirement and does not drive the high‐level system architecture.

requirement: If ERTMS/ETCS communications are required, an interface as defined in the FFFIS for EURORADIO [MORANE EURO FFFIS] shall be implemented.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates implementing a standardized external EURORADIO FFFIS interface for ERTMS/ETCS, impacting the system’s communication and integration architecture.

requirement: A standard data interface shall be provided to allow a computer to be connected to the General purpose radio.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a critical hardware-software interface that will drive module boundaries, communication protocols, and integration architecture.

requirement: The General purpose radio shall operate as a standard GSM terminal, supplying mobile services as defined in section 4.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the adoption of the GSM protocol stack and associated hardware interfaces, directly shaping the system’s communication architecture.

requirement: Automatic self-testing of the radio shall use the GSM IMSI attach procedure.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level implementation detail for the self‐test procedure and does not drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: Upon switch on, once the radio is connected to a mobile network, it shall be able to receive all calls made using the MSISDN or appropriate group call number.
ASR_Explanation: This describes standard call reception functionality and doesn’t impose major structural or quality constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to register and deregister a functional number by the user entering his functional number, which is transmitted to the ground along with the subscriber’s IMSI, using USSD.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the USSD gateway integration and backend messaging architecture for transmitting IMSI and functional number data.

requirement: The full environmental and physical specification of the General purpose shall be as close as possible to that of a Commercial-Off-The-Shelf (COTS) GSM mobile whilst adhering to the specifications provided in section 4.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes strict hardware and mechanical design constraints (form factor, environmental tolerances) that drive platform, component selection, and enclosure architecture.

requirement: The General purpose radio shall comply with the core climatic conditions defined in section 4.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates environmental and thermal management requirements for the hardware enclosure, impacting the system’s physical and structural architecture.

requirement: General purpose radios shall be equipped with rechargeable batteries capable of providing a minimum of eight hours operation over the temperature range +18°C to +25°C from a single charge, based on the following cycle: point-to-point calls 20%; group calls 5%; standby 75%.
ASR_Explanation: This battery-life requirement drives hardware selection and power-management architecture to ensure eight-hour operation under specified usage and temperature conditions.

requirement: Changing the battery shall not result in the loss of data stored in the radio.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s storage and power-management architecture by mandating non-volatile or backup power solutions to retain data during battery changes.

requirement: The General purpose radio shall be suitable for use with a car adapter kit.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the system’s hardware and power-supply architecture by requiring specific connector, voltage regulation, and form-factor support.

requirement: A standard data interface shall be provided to allow a computer to be connected to the Operational radio.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s component boundaries and communication protocols by defining a hardware–software interface for integration.

requirement: The Operational radio shall operate as a standard GSM terminal, supplying mobile services as defined in section 4.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a core system capability requiring integration of the GSM protocol stack and hardware, driving key technology choices and system partitioning.

requirement: Automatic self-testing of the radio shall use the GSM IMSI attach procedure.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates integration of the GSM IMSI attach procedure, impacting the radio subsystem’s protocol handling and network interface architecture.

requirement: Upon switch on, once the radio is connected to a mobile network, it shall be able to receive all calls made using the MSISDN or appropriate group call number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine call-handling behavior on network attach and doesn’t impose unique architectural constraints.

requirement: It shall be possible to register and deregister a functional number by the user entering his functional number, which is transmitted to the ground along with the subscriber’s IMSI, using USSD.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s network and security architecture by mandating USSD interface support and IMSI-backed subscriber registration handling.

requirement: Upon receipt of the call establishment request, the radio shall retrieve the stored number for the appropriate controller from the SIM or other storage location.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It dictates the need for a SIM/storage interface component and shapes subsystem boundaries for controller number retrieval.

requirement: Once an appropriate number has been obtained, the radio shall initiate a call to this number with ‘Railway operation’ priority. Any functional number associated with the user shall be passed to the network using UUS1.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of call-control, priority/QoS handling and UUS1 signalling in the system architecture.

requirement: Activation of the ‘Railway emergency call’ function shall cause the radio to initiate a Railway emergency call as defined in section 13.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates integration of a safety-critical emergency call protocol and priority handling within the radio communication architecture.

requirement: The calling user’s functional number, if there is one, shall be passed to the network using UUS1.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level protocol detail (UUS1 signalling) that affects implementation rather than high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The Operational radio shall comply with the basic standards defined for all EIRENE mobile equipment in section 4. In addition, the Operational radio is specified to allow its use in the operating environment experienced on the operational railway (eg shunting and maintenance), with the specifications in this section taking priority over those in section 4 where any discrepancy is identified.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes regulatory compliance and environmental robustness constraints that will drive hardware/software architecture choices and priority handling.

requirement: The Operational radio shall cope with rapid temperature fluctuations of up to 3°C/second.
ASR_Explanation: It drives hardware and thermal-management architecture decisions for component selection, enclosure design, and subsystem integration.

requirement: The Operational radio shall be capable of withstanding exposure to extreme environmental conditions [IEC 721 part 1].
ASR_Explanation: It imposes critical hardware and system‐level design constraints (materials, sealing, thermal management) to ensure environmental robustness.

requirement: The Operational radio shall conform to IP 54 [IEC 529/EN 60529] as a minimum.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the system‐level enclosure and hardware integration design to meet required environmental sealing (IP54) standards.

requirement: SIM cards shall be fixed into the radio such that they can only be removed by the use of a tool.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level mechanical hardware enclosure requirement and does not impact high-level system or software architecture.

requirement: The Operational radio shall be capable of withstanding the following shocks: semi-sinusoidal shocks of up to 5g for up to 100ms under normal conditions; shocks of up to 10g for up to 5ms under exceptional conditions; free fall from 1.0m.
ASR_Explanation: This shock and drop tolerance requirement drives the mechanical enclosure and hardware ruggedization architecture.

requirement: The Operational radio user shall be protected against all electrical hazards arising from the mobile equipment as defined in [EN 50153].
ASR_Explanation: This safety requirement drives major hardware architecture decisions for electrical isolation, grounding, and protective circuitry to comply with EN 50153.

requirement: Operational radios shall be equipped with rechargeable batteries capable of providing a minimum of eight hours operation over the temperature range -10°C to +55°C from a single charge, based on the following cycle: point-to-point calls 20%; group calls 60%; standby 20%.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the power subsystem and hardware/software partitioning for battery selection and energy management.

requirement: Changing the battery shall not result in the loss of data stored in the radio.
ASR_Explanation: Requires architectural support for non-volatile data storage and power‐fail backup mechanisms to preserve radio data during battery swaps.

requirement: The Operational radio shall be suitable for use with a car adapter kit.
ASR_Explanation: It drives hardware architecture decisions for power management and connector interface compatibility.

requirement: To terminate a VGCS/VBS call by DTMF signalling, the 3-digit sequence “***” shall be used.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level call-control signaling detail affecting only the DTMF handling logic, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: In order to minimise the discomfort caused by the DTMF tone added in the voice channel, the duration of the tone generated by the fixed line dispatcher shall be 70ms ± 5ms, and there shall be a minimum gap of 65ms between each tone.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed timing constraint for the DTMF tone generator module and does not drive high-level architectural structure decisions.

requirement: Muting and unmuting shall be in line with [EN 301 515, Index [4]].
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized functional detail (mute/unmute behavior) with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A group call controller who wishes to start talking (except in the case of an originator, a joiner or a re-joiner speaking for the first time) shall indicate his wish, for example by pressing the PTT button, whereupon the 3-digit DTMF sequence “###” shall be transferred.
ASR_Explanation: This PTT-DTMF interaction is a low-level protocol detail that doesn’t drive high-level structural or quality-attribute decisions.

requirement: Any group call controller who wishes to stop talking shall indicate his wish, for example by releasing the PTT button, whereupon the 3-digit DTMF sequence “#**” shall be transferred.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level signaling detail (DTMF sequence on PTT release) that does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The duration of each tone added in the voice channel, shall be 70ms ± 5ms, and there shall be a minimum gap of 65ms between each tone.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes strict real-time audio timing constraints that will drive choices in scheduling, timer resolution, and streaming pipeline architecture.

requirement: Within each GSM-R network, each Train Number shall be unique for the period of the journey.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement imposes a global uniqueness constraint across the distributed GSM-R network, driving the need for an ID-generation and synchronization architecture.

requirement: Every On-Train Function shall be identified by a standard code and shall conform to the list of functions given in Appendix 9A of this section.
ASR_Explanation: The requirement imposes a coding convention and compliance check but does not affect the high-level architecture or system structure.

requirement: All Train Function Numbers and their associated MSISDN numbers shall be stored in the same routing database, which is the database of the GSM-R network in which the train is currently operating.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the data storage location and integration strategy for routing databases across GSM-R networks, impacting the system’s database architecture and roaming support.

requirement: Every On-Engine Function shall be identified by a standard code and shall conform to the list of functions given in Appendix 9A of this section.
ASR_Explanation: This is an implementation‐level naming and conformance rule that does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The Engine Function Number(s) and associated MSISDN numbers shall at any time be stored as an entry in the routing database of the home GSM-R network of the engine.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the design of the routing subsystem’s data storage and integration with the GSM-R network database.

requirement: The Coach Function Number(s) and associated MSISDN number(s) shall at any time be stored as an entry in the routing database of the home GSM-R network of the coach.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates data persistence and integration requirements for the GSM-R routing database, impacting database schema and network routing component design.

requirement: Every Function shall be identified by a standard code and shall conform to the list of functions given in Appendix 9A of this section.
ASR_Explanation: This naming and conformance requirement impacts implementation standards but does not drive or constrain the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: The functional numbers of the Shunting Team Members, Maintenance Team Members and Train Controller (and any associated MSISDN numbers) shall be stored as entries in the routing database of the home GSM-R network.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design and integration of the routing database schema and signaling interfaces within the GSM-R core network.

requirement: Implementation of the EIRENE numbering plan shall not prohibit any authorised caller from using the MSISDN number where known, thus enabling mobiles to be assigned to particular personnel where this is appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: This is a policy-level requirement affecting authorization logic in the numbering module but does not drive major architectural design choices.

requirement: Service areas shall be defined within each railway network.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain‐modeling detail (defining data entities) and does not impose architectural design constraints.

requirement: The numbering of Service Areas for group calls and broadcast calls shall be made in accordance with GSM Technical Specifications [EN 301 515, Index [21] & [4]] and [EN 301 515, Index [22] & [5]] respectively.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces GSM‐compliant numbering schemes, impacting the design of the numbering subsystem and its protocol interface architecture.

requirement: The EIRENE numbering plan shall be standardised to allow interoperability and shall be implemented as a private numbering plan within the GSM-R network.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the core network’s numbering‐plan management and interoperability interfaces within the GSM-R architecture.

requirement: Within the GSM-R network, the user shall be able to dial the following types of numbers: National EIRENE Number (NEN), International EIRENE Number (IEN), MSISDN numbers, Short Dialling Code (SDC).
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional dialing requirement affecting call-processing logic but not driving any high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: In addition, Breakout Codes (BCs) shall be used to allow users within the GSM-R network to access external numbers.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It mandates external-network integration and gateway routing logic, impacting overall system topology and interfaces.

requirement: Access from the GSM-R network to external networks shall be as detailed in section 9.10.
ASR_Explanation: It defines the network boundary and gateway interfaces for GSM-R, impacting the system’s network architecture and security design.

requirement: Every railway network shall consider a number as a National EIRENE Number (NEN) unless the number is preceded by an International Code, identifying another GSM-R network.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized data validation rule for number parsing with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The National EIRENE Number shall consist of three distinct parts: Call Type, User Identifier Number, Function Code.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the data format and parsing rules for identifiers, impacting data modeling, validation, and integration interfaces.

requirement: The International EIRENE Number shall consist of three distinct parts: International Code (IC), National EIRENE Number (NEN), Function Code.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data format specification for an identifier and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: At least one MSISDN number shall be allocated to each mobile station.
ASR_Explanation: This is a business rule on data assignment (ensuring each mobile station has a number) with no major impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The structure of the MSISDN numbers shall comply with GSM Technical Specification [GSM 03.03].
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates GSM-compliant MSISDN formatting, impacting data models, validation logic, and interface protocols.

requirement: It shall be possible for authorised subscribers of fixed and mobile networks to call mobiles using the appropriate MSISDN number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard call-routing and authorization requirement that can be met by existing telephony modules without affecting the overall architecture.

requirement: For certain functions, standardised short codes shall be implemented for mobile originated calls.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement for implementing dial-plan mappings and does not drive major architectural structure or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: Each short dialling code shall consist of four digits.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level data format constraint for validation and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Short dialling codes shall start with the first digit equal to 1 (ie CT=1).
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor input validation rule with no significant impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Standardisation of UIC group addresses is required to provide interoperability between the fixed railway networks within the GSM-R network.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a mandatory addressing standard that will shape the network architecture and interoperability protocols.

requirement: The group address consists of a Service Area (5 digits) and a Function Code (3 digits) and has a Call Type 5.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a low-level data format constraint that does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: Service Area shall be defined on a national basis.
ASR_Explanation: The requirement is a business/domain constraint on scope without direct impact on system architecture or design.

requirement: In network boundary areas, the Service Area shall be allocated on a bilateral basis.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a network topology constraint affecting how services are distributed and routed at boundary areas.

requirement: Function Codes shall be defined within the framework given in table 9-8 on an international basis.
ASR_Explanation: Constrains the data model and integration architecture by mandating use of international code standards.

requirement: Access to other GSM-R networks shall be possible by using a Breakout Code (BC) as part of the dialled number.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the call‐routing architecture by requiring the dial‐plan parser and network interface modules to recognize breakout codes and route calls to external GSM-R networks.

requirement: The GSM-R network shall allow users direct access to public networks, subject to call barring restrictions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of inter-network interfaces and access/control modules for secure call routing to public networks.

requirement: Where access to public networks is allowed, this shall be performed by using an Access Code (AC), defined in table 9-13, followed by the international or national number of the called subscriber as defined in [ITU-T 164].
ASR_Explanation: This specifies detailed dialing behavior without driving high-level architectural structure or cross-cutting infrastructure decisions.

requirement: Facilities shall be provided to prevent unauthorised calls to mobiles either by functional number or MSISDN number from outside the EIRENE network.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of network gateways and security subsystems to enforce access control and filtering for inbound calls.

requirement: In order to provide a consistent international service, it is necessary to ensure that priorities are allocated consistently across all railways. The following allocation of UIC priority levels to eMLPP priority codes is mandatory: Railway emergency (Y, 0), Control-command (Y, 1), Public emergency and group calls between drivers in the same area (Y, 2), Railway operation (Y, 3), Railway information and all other calls (N, 4).
ASR_Explanation: This mandatory UIC-to-eMLPP priority mapping drives the design of call-handling protocols and resource allocation across the system.

requirement: For consistent working on public networks and in international roaming, the use of access classes in a railway network shall comply with the GSM specification.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the communication subsystem’s architecture and protocol stack to ensure GSM-compliant access classes for public network interoperability and roaming.

requirement: Any implementation of Closed User Groups (CUGs) must take account of requirements for interoperability.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes interface and integration constraints that shape the system’s communication protocols and data models to ensure interoperability.

requirement: SIM cards shall contain a list of authorised networks so that networks shall be displayed (or automatically selected if automatic network selection has been enabled) in the following order of priority: home EIRENE network, ‘foreign’ EIRENE networks, non-EIRENE networks.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the SIM’s data storage schema and the network‐selection module design, altering core platform architecture.

requirement: Mobile Stations designed for use in EIRENE networks shall give preference to the GSM frequency band allocated for railway use.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of the radio interface and frequency management subsystem, impacting the system’s network connectivity architecture.

requirement: Railway mobiles shall be provided with cell broadcast message identifiers in order to accept SMS-CB messages.
ASR_Explanation: This is a protocol‐level detail for SMS-CB handling and does not drive high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: In case of encryption, standardised ciphering algorithms shall be used.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the security architecture by determining cryptographic module selection, key management and compliance requirements.

requirement: Mobiles shall be capable of operation using algorithms for all countries in which they need to roam.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives a modular, extensible design for dynamic loading and management of country-specific roaming algorithms.

requirement: The numbering plan to be used with functional addressing shall be in accordance with the numbering plan given in section 9.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed compliance requirement for numbering consistency and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Mobile access to the functional numbering scheme for registration, deregistration and re-registration shall apply the USSD messages and protocols over the air interface as specified in the GSM Follow-me service.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates support for USSD over the GSM air interface, driving choices in protocol stack implementation and network integration.

requirement: For communication over the (Um) air interface, the USSD messages and protocols as specified in the GSM Follow-me service shall be used to manage the following types of functional numbers: Train number, Engine number, Coach number, Shunting team number, Maintenance team number.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating USSD over the GSM Um air interface drives the design of the communication stack and network integration architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to limit user access to functional number registration and deregistration facilities based on each of the types of functional number identified in 11.3.5.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a fine-grained, type-based access control mechanism, impacting the system’s security and authorization architecture.

requirement: Mobile stations shall use the following sequences for the control of the functional number management: Interrogation, Registration, Deregistration, Re-registration, Forced De-registration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional flow requirement for number management and does not drive high-level architectural structure or quality attributes.

requirement: The network operator shall implement the required functionality to validate the information exchanged between the mobile and network on registration and deregistration.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of core security and protocol interfaces, necessitating dedicated validation components and impacting system communication architecture.

requirement: Functionality shall be provided by the system for the operator management of Functional Numbers, including the removal of the relationship between Functional Numbers and MSISDN Numbers.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a routine CRUD/data-model change without requiring new architectural patterns or major structural restructuring.

requirement: The system shall require manual confirmation prior to the removal of the relationship between Engine Number and MSISDN Number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor workflow/UI confirmation detail and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The result of the registration procedure shall be sent back to the mobile. In the event of a failure, an indication of the cause shall be provided. Information on the outcome shall be provided to the mobile according to [EN 301 515, Index [17]] and [EN 301 515, Index [34]].
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional interface requirement (message format and error reporting) that does not drive significant high-level architectural changes.

requirement: In the event of a registration procedure failing owing to the functional number already being registered to another mobile, the Cab radio shall be capable of providing the user with the ability to perform automatically the forced de-registration of the previously registered mobile and the registration of this functional number to the user’s mobile.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a forced de-registration workflow that impacts state management, transactional consistency, and error-handling across the registration subsystem.

requirement: Deregistration shall only be performed by the subscription identified by the MSISDN number which is associated with the functional number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a functional security constraint requiring MSISDN-based authorization checks but does not drive major architectural structure.

requirement: The result of the deregistration procedure shall be sent back to the mobile. In the event of a failure, an indication of the cause shall be provided. Information on the outcome shall be provided to the mobile according to [EN 301 515, Index [17]] and [EN 301 515, Index [34]].
ASR_Explanation: This is a protocol-level interface requirement detailing message contents and error handling, not an architectural constraint.

requirement: Re-registration of on-train functional numbers based on the train number shall be performed every time a train leaves one EIRENE network and enters into another EIRENE network.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates designing mobility management and roaming services to handle dynamic re-registration of train identifiers across EIRENE network boundaries.

requirement: Deregistration of a functional number shall not be carried out until registration of the functional number has been carried out and confirmed as being successful.
ASR_Explanation: It enforces stateful workflow and transaction ordering, impacting service orchestration and consistency mechanisms.

requirement: The exchange of information between EIRENE networks is handled by the GMSCs and shall use the standardised protocol as detailed in section 11.6.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the communication protocols and interoperability interfaces between critical network components, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: In the event of losing correlation between functional numbers and MSISDNs, provision for recovery from such a situation shall be made.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s data integrity and fault‐tolerance architecture by requiring recovery mechanisms for mapping loss.

requirement: The called party functional identity shall be presented to the user initiating a call and the calling party functional identity shall be presented to the user receiving a call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI functional requirement with minimal impact on high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The calling party functional number shall be passed to the receiving mobile using the User to User Signalling supplementary service (UUS1) during call setup.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the call control architecture by requiring support for the UUS1 signaling flow to pass the functional number during call setup.

requirement: If the calling party functional number is not available or if the calling party is not registered then the CLI of the calling party shall be displayed on the receiving mobile's display.
ASR_Explanation: This fallback display rule affects component-level logic but does not drive high-level architectural structure or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The user-to-user information element in the SETUP, ALERT or CONNECT messages, as defined in [EN 301 515, Index [16]], shall be used to transfer the functional number of the calling party to the called party.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed protocol-level functional requirement about message field usage with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: If no valid functional number is available, a fixed length User-to-User Information Element shall be used.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level protocol formatting rule affecting message encoding rather than the system’s overall architectural structure.

requirement: The protocol used for routing of calls shall be Signalling System No 7 (SS7) as defined by the ITU-T. The signalling system suite shall include the Mobile Application Part (MAP).
ASR_Explanation: Specifying SS7 with MAP directly constrains the signalling architecture and impacts protocol selection, interfaces, and interoperability.

requirement: Call setup between EIRENE networks using international functional numbers shall be based on the combination of the Breakout-Code (BC) and International Code (IC) as specified in section 9.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the call‐routing and network‐interface components to handle BC+IC code combinations for international EIRENE call setup.

requirement: The Country Code (CC) followed by the National Significant Number NSN (as specified in ITU-T E.164, Chapter 6.2) shall be used as the Global Title, which forms part of the Signalling Connection Control Part (SCCP) protocol messages and is used for inter-network routing of messages.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the fundamental addressing and routing scheme for SCCP messages, impacting protocol handling and inter-network routing architecture.

requirement: Location dependent addressing may be provided in the following ways: a) cell dependent routing (M); b) using location information from external sources (O).
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing location-based routing modules and integrating external location services, influencing component boundaries and interfaces.

requirement: As a minimum, call routing using location dependent addressing shall be based on the use of short codes in conjunction with cell dependent routing.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the core call-routing subsystem by mandating location-based addressing, short-code management, and cell-dependent routing logic.

requirement: If a more accurate way of location determination is used, then position information shall be provided to the radio system which shall be used to associate the short code with the correct called party subscriber number.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates new integration between the location‐determination component and the radio system, impacting component interfaces and data flows.

requirement: If implemented, the use of location information from train-based systems external to the radio system shall comply with the requirements stated in the enhanced Location Dependent Addressing FRS and IRS.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives integration architecture by mandating compliance with external location-addressing specifications, affecting interface design and data flow.

requirement: Railway emergency calls are defined as those calls of ‘Railway emergency’ priority which are routed to a pre-defined user or group of users due to a railway operational emergency.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional rule affecting call‐routing logic without requiring changes to the high‐level system architecture.

requirement: All Railway emergency calls shall be implemented using GSM VGCS (Specifications [EN 301 515, Index [21] & [4]]).
ASR_Explanation: Mandates GSM VGCS for emergency call handling, shaping the telecom network architecture, protocol stacks, and hardware integration.

requirement: It shall be possible to configure Railway emergency group call areas to contain combinations of cells controlled by one or more MSC(s) within one or more network(s).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the emergency call configuration subsystem and its integration across multiple MSCs and networks, impacting modularization and scalability.

requirement: A Railway emergency call shall be initiated by using the appropriate function code for the required type of Railway emergency call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior requirement with minimal impact on high-level architecture.

requirement: The Railway emergency group IDs required for interoperability are defined in section 9.5. The composition of each group is a matter for national implementation, although all areas shall have a group defined for all mandated Group IDs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement specifies functional identifiers for interoperability without imposing architectural structure or design constraints.

requirement: Each mobile shall store a list of emergency Group IDs in the SIM appropriate to its function (the Cab radio will store Group ID 299 and 599).
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level device configuration detail for SIM storage that doesn’t drive the overall system architecture.

requirement: All Railway emergency group IDs required for interoperability and appropriate to the operation of the mobile shall maintain active status whilst the mobile is powered up.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the session management and resource allocation architecture by requiring persistent activation and monitoring of emergency group subscriptions while powered.

requirement: If the requirement in 13.4.4 is implemented, as a minimum the location information shall be provided by the GSM network (eg current cell or base station serving the mobile).
ASR_Explanation: This affects the system’s architecture by requiring a GSM network integration layer and location‐service components for real‐time cell/base‐station data.

requirement: If the GSM Release 99 capability and the Immediate Setup 2 feature defined in [EN 301 515, Index [6], Release 4] are supported by the network, the network shall set the MSC Release bit in the “Control Channel Description” information element to “1”. Otherwise, the MSC Release bit in the “Control Channel Description” information element shall be set to “0” (zero) [EN 301 515, Index [41]].
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level protocol conformance detail and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: All calls of ‘Railway emergency’ priority must be confirmed.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a guaranteed real-time acknowledgement flow for emergency calls, impacting the messaging infrastructure and fault-tolerance architecture.

requirement: Confirmation of Railway emergency calls shall be implemented using the User to User Signalling supplementary service (UUS1).
ASR_Explanation: Specifying the UUS1 protocol for emergency call confirmations impacts the signaling architecture and dictates integration with the telecom call-control stack.

requirement: After clear down of the Railway Emergency call, the mobile application shall start the confirmation process by automatically originating a call. In order to avoid network congestion the call set up shall be delayed by a random offset.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the call-initiation architecture by requiring a timing/scheduling component with randomized delays for network congestion control.

requirement: Railway Emergency call confirmation messages shall be of eMLPP priority 4 - “Railway information and all other calls”.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes specific QoS and priority handling requirements, impacting the design of call routing and telecommunication integration modules.

requirement: The user information contained in the confirmation message shall be: Cab radio: the engine number or train number (if registered); other mobiles: the user’s functional number (if registered).
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional detail specifying message content and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Confirmation messages shall be sent to a confirmation centre using a defined short code (see table 9-10), which shall be associated with the GSM network.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of the messaging subsystem and its integration with an external GSM short-code gateway.

requirement: In the case of Cab radio, details of the confirmation shall be passed to the train borne recorder if a train borne recorder is connected to the Cab radio.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a runtime-detectable interface and conditional data‐routing module between the cab radio and the train-borne recorder.

requirement: The user-to-user information elements in the following messages, as defined in [EN 301 515, Index [16]], shall be used for the confirmation of high priority calls: SETUP: transfer of confirmation message to confirmation centre; RELEASE COMPLETE: acknowledgement of the confirmation message.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the call control signaling layer by requiring support for user-to-user information elements and integration with the confirmation centre.

requirement: The SETUP and RELEASE COMPLETE user-to-user information element shall be as specified in the [MORANE UUIE].
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level protocol specification detail without broad impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Confirmation centres shall be capable of decoding messages in either format A or B.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s protocol handling architecture by necessitating pluggable or dual-format decoding modules.

requirement: On entering shunting mode, the operational radio shall determine if any shunting group ID is already activated on the SIM.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized functional check on the existing SIM interface for shunting mode and does not alter high-level component interactions.

requirement: If a shunting group ID is activated, the operational radio shall proceed to re-establish the group call.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a specific functional behavior in the call-handling logic and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: If no shunting group ID is activated, the operational radio shall initiate a “regular shunting mode ON” procedure as detailed in table 14-1.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed operational behavior within the existing shunting-mode logic and doesn’t drive new architectural components or interfaces.

requirement: After the “regular shunting mode ON” procedure has been completed, the procedures shown in table 14-2 and 14-3 shall be used to register the shunting leader and shunting members to a dedicated group call.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional procedure for call registration and does not introduce new architectural structures or quality-attribute drivers.

requirement: Two possibilities shall be available to the shunting leader when leaving shunting mode: Release group call; Maintain group call.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the call-control subsystem’s state machine and protocols for handling mode transitions.

requirement: On leaving shunting mode, the Operational radios of shunting members shall follow the procedure shown in table 14-6.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional procedure for a specific mode with no broad structural or cross-cutting architectural impact.

requirement: On entering shunting mode, the Cab radio shall determine if any shunting group ID is already activated on the SIM.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s module boundaries and interfaces by adding SIM-based group-ID validation logic to the shunting-mode control flow.

requirement: If a shunting group ID is activated, the Cab radio shall proceed to re-establish the group call.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the communication subsystem’s architecture by mandating state management and automatic reconnection logic for group calls.

requirement: If no shunting group ID is activated, the Cab radio shall initiate a “regular shunting mode ON” procedure as shown in table 14-7.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed operational behavior for the Cab radio subsystem and does not impose high-level architectural constraints.

requirement: The Cab radio shall exit shunting mode by means of the “Leave shunting mode” procedure as defined in table 14-8.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional detail with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: On entering shunting mode, the terminal shall initially select the common shunting group.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The terminal application shall prevent members of a dedicated shunting team from initiating a new call (unless they are registered as shunting team leader and have left shunting mode using the “maintain group call” function described in 14.2.4).
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed business rule for call‐initiation logic that can be handled within the existing role-based access control and state-management modules.

requirement: Valid group IDs shall be as defined in table 14-9.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level data validation constraint with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Users shall be prevented from joining a dedicated shunting group by the EIRENE terminal application unless a valid functional number for the group has been successfully registered to the GSM-R network by their terminal.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the terminal’s integration with the GSM-R network for registration, group management, and access control mechanisms.

requirement: The link assurance tone shall be of the form specified in subsection 14.4.2 of the [EIRENE FRS].
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level signal-format compliance detail that won’t affect major architectural structures or high-level design decisions.

requirement: The mechanism for the generation of the link assurance signal shall be as specified in [MORANE SM FFFIS].
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation detail for signal generation and does not drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The mechanism for the deactivation of the link assurance signal shall be as specified in [MORANE SM FFFIS].
ASR_Explanation: This is an implementation-level compliance detail referencing an external spec and does not impose major architectural constraints.

requirement: Implementation of direct mode is optional. However, where implemented, the following requirements shall be mandatory.
ASR_Explanation: This optional direct mode requirement influences the system’s modular design and feature‐toggle architecture, affecting component interfaces and deployment configuration.

requirement: Frequency modulated equipment conforming to [ETS 300 086] shall be used for direct mode.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a hardware architecture constraint by mandating FM equipment compliance with ETS 300 086, shaping system design and integration.

requirement: Direct mode equipment shall have a maximum transmit power of 1 Watt.
ASR_Explanation: This transmit-power limit drives hardware architecture choices for RF components, power amplification, thermal management, and regulatory compliance.

requirement: Direct mode equipment sensitivity shall be at least –107dBm.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed hardware performance specification (radio sensitivity) affecting component selection rather than high-level system architecture.

requirement: Direct mode equipment shall be capable of operation in the channels defined in table 15-1.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed physical‐layer channel support requirement that does not drive high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: Direct mode shall operate in simplex mode, a radio link that uses a single frequency for alternate transmission and reception.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the design of the communication subsystem and hardware/protocol layers for simplex radio transmission.

requirement: Voice transmission from direct mode equipment shall be possible only when the Push-To-Talk (PTT) button is pressed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional behavior that does not drive high-level architectural structure or design choices.

requirement: Continuous Tone Coded Squelch Systems (CTCSS) shall be implemented on all direct mode equipment.
ASR_Explanation: Mandates inclusion of CTCSS encoding/decoding on direct-mode equipment, impacting hardware-software partitioning and signal-processing subsystem design.

requirement: The CTCSS tone shall be at a frequency of 203.5 Hz.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low‐level signal specification that doesn’t drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The CTCSS modulation shall be within the limits defined in table 15-2.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level technical constraint on signal modulation limits, not affecting high-level architectural structure.

requirement: Direct mode channel 1 (876.0125 MHz) shall serve as the common access channel.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific radio configuration detail and does not impose architecture-level constraints.

requirement: The presence of the GSM-R network shall be indicated to direct mode users.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI notification requirement with minimal impact on the core system architecture.

requirement: All terminals shall ensure that when GSM-R services are available, the user is prevented from entering direct mode.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the terminal’s communication-control and state-management architecture by mandating service-availability detection and user-interface gating.

requirement: All direct mode equipment shall provide the facility to broadcast an in-band audio shunting link assurance signal as defined in subsection 14.4.2 of the [EIRENE FRS].
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the audio/communications subsystem design by requiring in-band signal generation and integration into the transmission pipeline.

requirement: Where text messaging is implemented in the network, the Short Message Service (SMS) shall be used.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level protocol constraint specifying SMS for messaging, with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system should provide a categorized view of the entire inventory to the Construction Junction staff, allowing navigation from department level down to individual inventory items.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement specifies UI-level inventory categorization and drill-down functionality without impacting the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: The system must display inventory departments and categories in a matrix format with fixed dimensions, able to hold at least 30 tiles at each level.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI layout requirement affecting only the presentation layer’s rendering logic, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: Unused matrix cells must be displayed empty.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI display requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Each matrix cell must display a department or category name, with hyperlinks to subcategories or items as appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐layer UI detail with no impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The items list must contain all Unique and Stock items in the selected department or category, or across all departments and categories when viewing all inventory items.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data filtering requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system must allow filtering of items by item number, donor, department, category, description, attributes, features, and/or details.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard multi-field filtering function implementable within the existing data access layer without major architectural changes.

requirement: Departments and categories must be listed in alphabetical order in each row, from left to right and top to bottom.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI/data-sorting requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The cell in the top left corner of the matrix must always represent a generic item in the selected department and is automatically generated by the system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI/UX functional detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The cell to the right of the top left corner of the matrix must always represent under $5 items in the selected department and is automatically generated by the system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level UI business rule and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system must allow inventory administrators to add, edit, and delete inventory departments.
ASR_Explanation: This basic CRUD requirement for inventory departments is a standard functional detail with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: The following fields are mandatory when adding or editing a department: Name, POS Department Code, Unique Tag.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple field‐validation requirement that does not drive any high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The Department Name and Unique Tag must be unique across the inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This enforces unique-data constraints that drive database schema design, indexing strategy, and concurrency control mechanisms for data integrity.

requirement: The POS Department Code must be at most 3 characters long.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple data-length validation constraint that impacts only field definition/schema, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Unique Tag must be at most 18 characters long.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple input validation constraint with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A Department cannot be deleted if it contains categories and/or items.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine business rule enforceable via database constraints or simple application logic, with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: The system must ask for confirmation before deleting a Department.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user interaction detail (confirmation prompt) with negligible impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The system must allow inventory administrators to add, edit, delete, and move inventory categories.
