ASR_Explanation: This requirement describes standard CRUD operations on inventory categories and has minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The following fields are mandatory when adding or editing a category: Name, Unique Tag, and for Stock Item Category, Price.
ASR_Explanation: This is a field-level validation and data constraint requirement that does not impact high-level architectural structure.

requirement: The Category Name and Unique Tag must be unique across the inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data integrity constraint enforced at the database/schema level and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The Unique Tag must be at most 18 characters long.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple input-validation constraint on tag length and does not drive any major architectural decisions.

requirement: There must be at least one slot available in the Category matrix at the level the new Category will be created to hold the new Category.
ASR_Explanation: This is a functional business rule affecting data validation rather than a cross-cutting or structural architectural concern.

requirement: The available options in the Material, Finish, Color and Features selectors should be the ones defined for the selected department.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local UI/data-filtering rule for selector options and does not drive core architectural structure.

requirement: The fields and available options under the Details section should be the ones defined for the selected category.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects UI behavior for dynamic form rendering but doesn’t significantly impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: When a Stock Item Category is created, a corresponding item is added to QuickBooks POS with the ID set to the Category’s Unique Tag and initial quantity set to zero.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the integration architecture by requiring an event‐driven sync mechanism and ID mapping layer to QuickBooks POS.

requirement: When a Category is deleted, all of its items should be moved to the parent category.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-level data migration rule with limited impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The system must ask for confirmation before deleting a Category.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI confirmation flow and does not impact high-level architectural structure or design.

requirement: Generic categories and Under $5 item categories are generated automatically by the system and cannot be modified or deleted.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the category management architecture and data model by enforcing automatic generation and immutability of specific categories.

requirement: When moving categories, user must select at least one Category to move and a valid target Department or Category.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It’s a localized input validation/business rule with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: User cannot move categories to any Departments or Categories that don’t have enough slots available in the matrix to hold the moved categories.
ASR_Explanation: This is a business‐rule constraint enforceable in the domain/service layer without requiring changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: User cannot move categories to any of their subcategories (cannot create loops in the inventory hierarchy).
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-level business rule for preventing cyclic category moves and does not alter the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: When the option to merge the contents of the selected Categories is selected, the Item Details assigned to the selected Categories are combined and assigned to the target Department or Category.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces complex data-model merging and transactional integrity concerns for bulk category-to-department updates.

requirement: The system should allow inventory administrators to customize the attributes and details available for each inventory item.
ASR_Explanation: Enables dynamic metadata-driven data modeling, impacting database schema design, API flexibility, and UI configuration architecture.

requirement: The Item Attribute Name and Item Detail Name must be unique across the inventory.
ASR_Explanation: It’s a data integrity constraint enforceable via unique database indexes and doesn’t drive high-level architectural design.

requirement: Material and Features attributes can be assigned to any combination of departments or to 'All' departments.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard data-modeling rule affecting schema design without requiring significant architectural changes.

requirement: Color and Finish attributes can only be set to 'All' departments.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor domain validation rule with no significant impact on high-level architecture.

requirement: An Attribute cannot be set simultaneously to 'All' departments and to specific department(s).
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-level validation rule affecting business logic implementation without impacting overall system architecture.

requirement: Details can be assigned to any combination of departments and categories, including 'All'.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the data model and service logic for flexible many-to-many assignments and ‘All’ grouping, impacting schema design and filtering performance.

requirement: Assigning a detail to a department or category automatically assigns it to its subcategories.
ASR_Explanation: This is a domain-level business rule affecting service logic without driving high-level architectural patterns.

requirement: The following fields are mandatory for Item Attributes: Name.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed data-entry validation rule with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The following fields are mandatory for Item Details: Name, Type.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data validation rule with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: If the Type is set to 'Selection', then the selection list must contain at least one item.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic input validation rule and does not affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system should allow users to add items to inventory as part of donation processing or routine inventory maintenance.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD functionality with minimal impact on high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: When an item is added to the inventory, the system updates QuickBooks POS accordingly and promptly.
ASR_Explanation: Requires real-time integration with an external POS system, impacting communication protocols, data consistency, and error‐handling architecture.

requirement: Mandatory fields for unique items: Quantity, Condition, Price, and Description if the selected category is a generic category.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local data‐validation rule for form handling and does not drive high‐level architectural choices.

requirement: For stock items, all fields are pre-set and cannot be modified.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward business rule enforcing immutability on stock item fields, with minimal impact confined to data validation and UI controls.

requirement: For under $5 items, Description is mandatory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple business validation rule with negligible impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system must make pricing recommendations to the user on the item entry screen for unique and stock items.
ASR_Explanation: It requires embedding or integrating a pricing recommendation engine/microservice and real-time data processing into the system architecture.

requirement: Receiving Associates and Pickup and Decon Associates can only add items to the inventory as part of processing a donation.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard role-based access control rule that can be implemented within the existing service and security layers.

requirement: Pickup and Decon Associates can access the screen and enter all item information, but cannot complete the add operation and add the item into the inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This role-based restriction can be implemented in the application or security layer without requiring changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: The item tag should contain: CJ logo, Department name, Category name, Item description, Condition, Size (with units), Price (with indication if item is being sold on consignment), Item number and bar code, Acquisition number, Date received.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation/formatting requirement for item tags and does not impose high-level architectural constraints.

requirement: The system should allow modifying, deleting, splitting, and viewing the details and history of inventory items (Unique and Stock items only).
ASR_Explanation: It requires audit/history tracking and split operations, impacting the persistence and logging architecture.

requirement: All item properties available when the item was originally added to the inventory can be modified, except for the system-generated item number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD update rule and does not drive any major architectural decisions.

requirement: Once set for the first time in the inventory, the item price can only be modified by a Manager, Director or Administrator, must maintain a history and include an explanation for the price change.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of role-based access control, audit-trail/versioned data storage, and change-explanation logging in the system architecture.

requirement: Changes to the item in the inventory should be reflected in the corresponding item in QuickBooks POS.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s integration architecture by requiring real-time or near-real-time data synchronization and interface design with the QuickBooks POS API.

requirement: When an item quantity is adjusted, the system must display a confirmation screen where a reason for the adjustment must be specified by the user.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI workflow requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: If the quantity is decreased, a reason for the change is required. Valid reasons: Scraped, Given Away (free bin), Discarded, Broken, Shrinkage (stolen or lost), Correction (data entry mistake), Other (please explain).
ASR_Explanation: This is a business validation rule that affects UI and data schema but does not drive architectural changes.

requirement: If the quantity is increased, the user must enter a comment describing the reason for the change.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level validation requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: When building the split item list, at least two items must exist in the list, including the original item.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local business rule for list generation and does not affect the system’s overall architectural structure or non-functional qualities.

requirement: New items added to the inventory as part of a split item operation contain a reference to the original item they were split from.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the data model and database schema (and related API contracts) by introducing referential integrity and traceability for split items.

requirement: The system should record the following actions and associated parameters for every item in the inventory and display them on the item history screen, along with action date/time: Item added, Item price set, Item price changed, Item sold, Quantity adjusted, Item split.
ASR_Explanation: Capturing detailed action history for every inventory item requires designing an audit/event‐sourcing layer and impacts database schema and service boundaries.

requirement: The system should assist in the task of pricing a new inventory item by making pricing suggestions to the user when adding to or modifying an item in the inventory.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a pricing-suggestion component (e.g., rule engine or ML service) and associated data pipelines, impacting system structure and integrations.

requirement: Price suggestion is only applicable to Unique items.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward business rule enforceable in the service layer with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: By default, price suggestion considers historical data for items of the same Department, Category, and Condition.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data‐filtering requirement that doesn’t mandate any new architectural components or patterns.

requirement: The following price suggestion configuration parameters are available: Number of months of historical price information to include, whether or not to include item attributes for matching.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local configuration detail for the pricing algorithm and does not affect the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: System displays historical original item price information (lowest, mean, highest), historical sale price information (lowest, mean, highest), and number of items evaluated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐retrieval and display requirement without introducing new structural or non-functional constraints that would drive architectural decisions.

requirement: For stock items, system shows the pre-determined price for this stock item, set when the stock item category was created.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional detail with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: Acquisition records for Drop-Off, Pickup and Decon donations are created in the CRM system. Inventory Management users should be able to view all past and current acquisitions.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s architecture by driving the design of the integration layer and data model for synchronizing acquisition records between the CRM and Inventory Management modules.

requirement: The acquisitions screen should display: Acquisition Number, Type, Donor Name with Zip Code, Primary Contact Name, Primary Contact Phone Number, Start Date, End Date, Status.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level detail concerning field display with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Users should be able to filter acquisitions by Type, Donor and Primary Contact Name, Acquisition Number, Primary Contact Phone Number, Status.
ASR_Explanation: This is standard CRUD filtering functionality and doesn’t introduce significant architectural changes beyond typical query and indexing considerations.

requirement: User should be able to sort the acquisition list by any of its columns.
ASR_Explanation: Standard list-sorting functionality with minimal impact on overall architecture.

requirement: Changes to acquisition information in CRM should be immediately visible in the Inventory Management System and vice versa.
ASR_Explanation: It requires real‐time, bidirectional data synchronization between CRM and Inventory systems, driving choices around integration architecture, middleware, and consistency models.

requirement: CRM should allow the creation of a Drop Off acquisition where only a Zip Code and Donation Location Type or Donor Type are provided.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional requirement affecting business logic/validation but not core system architecture.

requirement: All acquisitions are created in CRM.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates CRM integration for acquisition creation, impacting interface design and data flow architecture.

requirement: The system should provide a link from the acquisitions view in the Inventory Management System to the acquisition creation screen in CRM.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI navigation/link requirement with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Inventory Management users should be granted permission to create new Drop Off acquisitions in CRM.
ASR_Explanation: This is a permissions detail that can be handled within the existing role-based access control subsystem without impacting the overall architecture.

requirement: A donor in possession of a valid acquisition number can proceed to the receiving dock where the items to be donated will be received and processed, and a donation receipt will be generated.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional flow (input validation and receipt generation) with no special architectural constraints.

requirement: The screens used for the acquisition process should simplify and expedite the data entry process, allowing the Receiving Associate to enter only the information needed to generate the donation receipt.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX enhancement request that does not drive core architectural decisions or patterns.

requirement: Additional attributes needed to complete the item information and effectively add the item to the inventory can be added at a second step, after the receipt has been printed and the donor has been served.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts transaction boundaries and workflow orchestration, necessitating a decoupled or event-driven design for staged data entry.

requirement: Pick Up and Decon crews may also initiate the receiving process, entering as much detailed information about the donated items as desired, and generating a donation receipt, but cannot add items to the inventory or complete the acquisition process.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine role-based access and workflow detail that can be handled within existing authorization and process modules.

requirement: The system must record every time a receipt is printed or emailed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level logging requirement with minimal impact on system architecture.

requirement: Completing the acquisition freezes the record in both CRM and the Inventory Management System.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the cross-system integration and transactional consistency architecture between the CRM and Inventory Management subsystems.

requirement: The system should record the total amount of time taken to process each acquisition.
ASR_Explanation: This timing-logging requirement can be implemented via local instrumentation or existing metrics/logging frameworks without altering the high-level architecture.

requirement: The original CRM acquisition data must be kept, not overridden by the values entered here.
ASR_Explanation: This requires data persistence design changes (e.g., versioning or audit logging) to preserve original CRM acquisition values.

requirement: Any item in the inventory may be purchased by a Construction Junction customer. The sale of the item is processed by QuickBooks POS and the inventory must be updated accordingly.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for external system integration and real-time inventory synchronization architecture.

requirement: After a sale in QuickBooks POS, the following item attributes must be updated in the inventory: Sale price, Quantity on hand.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine business logic detail for updating inventory and does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: The sale price must not override the original item price in the inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a business‐logic data integrity constraint with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The item quantity on hand in the inventory must be decremented by the quantity sold.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward business logic rule (inventory decrement) with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The application will provide a reporting mechanism that allows Construction Junction to review and report by inventory, inventory type, donor contact information, and the ability to link inventory to the individual that donated it.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard reporting feature that doesn’t impose system-level architectural constraints beyond typical data retrieval and presentation.

requirement: The system should be able to generate reports based on inventory items, departments, categories, acquisitions, and donors, including reports that correlate them.
ASR_Explanation: This reporting requirement impacts the data model, storage/retrieval strategies, and choice of reporting framework, making it architecturally significant.

requirement: The system should allow Construction Junction Staff to create new reports whenever required to satisfy their changing operational and business needs.
ASR_Explanation: Supporting on‐the‐fly report creation requires a flexible, metadata‐driven reporting architecture and impacts data modeling and module design.

requirement: The system should integrate with Construction Junction’s website and allow customers to view and search CJ’s inventory online.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the integration architecture by defining external API contracts, data synchronization, and search/indexing infrastructure.

requirement: The system should allow for e-Blast flagging, including flagging items for inclusion in e-Blast, adding photographs, and integrating with Vertical Response (or replacement) for email notifications.
ASR_Explanation: Requires integrating an external email service API and media handling for e-Blast flagging, impacting the system’s service and storage architecture.

requirement: The system should allow for e-Commerce integration, supporting online purchases and auctions, and updating inventory accordingly.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of integration layers, API contracts, transaction management, and inventory synchronization strategies.

requirement: The system should implement a membership program, including membership cards, rewards, and integration with CRM and QuickBooks POS.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces new domain modules and external integrations (CRM, QuickBooks POS) that will drive the design of the system’s integration, data flow, and security architecture.

requirement: The system should provide a customer wish list feature, allowing users to be notified when desired items become available.
ASR_Explanation: This feature introduces an event-driven notification subsystem and persistent wish-list storage, impacting component design and integration.

requirement: The system should support scheduling and tracking Pickup and Decon jobs, including map views and automatic reminder/thank you emails.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the overall architecture by introducing scheduling workflows, mapping service integration, background job processing, and email notification infrastructure.

requirement: The system should automatically decrease price of inventory items by a set amount that have remained in the inventory for a set period of time (e.g., 10% per month).
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine scheduled business rule that can be handled by an existing batch/scheduler component without major architectural changes.

requirement: The system should allow tracking of items purchased by customers but left on-site for a maximum seven day period, with automatic notifications and credit adjustments.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s architecture by requiring item‐lifecycle tracking, scheduled notification jobs, and integration with the credit management subsystem.

requirement: The system should allow setting thresholds for specific items or categories to indicate overstock or understock conditions and allow price adjustments accordingly.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates a configurable rules engine and event-driven pricing service to detect inventory thresholds and apply dynamic price adjustments.

requirement: The system should support mobile handheld units or tablets for scanning and updating inventory items.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a dedicated mobile client architecture with device-specific scanning integration, responsive UI design, and synchronization services.

requirement: The system should allow adding a history or back story for items and generate signage containing this information.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a routine content‐management and reporting feature that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The system should store item weight information and generate reports on total weight received, sold, and diverted from landfills.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data‐storage and reporting requirement with no major impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system should allow Construction Junction staff to enter donor ranking/feedback and generate donor statistics reports.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD and reporting feature that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: The application is expected to operate via touch screen user interfaces, minimizing the use of keyboard and mouse.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the choice of UI framework and input-handling architecture to support touch-first interactions.

requirement: The system must be available during normal Construction Junction operating hours for donation/acquisition and sales processing, and beyond normal hours for inventory management and external website users.
ASR_Explanation: This availability requirement drives the need for high-availability architecture with redundancy, failover strategies, and maintenance windows.

requirement: Access to the application by the Construction Junction staff must require user login. Access to application screens and functions is granted or denied based on the profile and role of the currently logged in user.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the security architecture by requiring integrated authentication and role‐based authorization mechanisms across the application.

requirement: The system should record changes to application entities in the form of ‘created user/time’, ‘modified user/time’ fields, and record information about sensitive operations such as deleting items and changing prices.
ASR_Explanation: This audit logging requirement introduces a cross-cutting concern impacting data model design, persistence strategy, and compliance/security architecture.

requirement: The system should perform with consistently and predictably low response times.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts key architectural decisions around caching, load balancing, concurrency, and resource provisioning to meet performance goals.

requirement: A web browser-based interface must be provided for all the Inventory Management functionality.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives a web-based client‐server architecture, impacting UI framework choices, middleware design, and deployment topology.

requirement: Buttons and shortcuts should be easy to click on a touch screen workstation. Extra spacing between rows should be added if necessary.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI layout detail affecting presentation, not requiring changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: All required information fields should be marked with an asterisk (*).
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI presentation detail with no significant impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: During data validation, all validation errors must be reported to the actor and displayed on the screen.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level validation and error-reporting detail with no broad impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: In case of a validation error, the actor must not have to re-enter the values that are valid.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level usability requirement and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system should support hardware interfaces: touch screen monitors, bar code readers, Zebra printers, standard color laser printers, and mobile devices.
ASR_Explanation: Integrating multiple hardware interfaces requires architectural decisions around device abstraction layers, driver integration, and extensibility.

requirement: The Inventory Management System must interface with QuickBooks POS, Salesforce CRM, Vertical Response (or replacement), Construction Junction website, CTI Telephony Integration, MS Outlook, and Google Apps.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the integration architecture by mandating connectors, middleware patterns, communication protocols, and security standards for multiple external systems.

requirement: The user interface implementation should strive to use cross-browser standards wherever possible and avoid any use of browser-specific features.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR constrains the UI architecture and technology stack to ensure cross-browser compatibility, affecting component design and framework selection.

requirement: Constituent and sales history data from Construction Junction’s QuickBooks POS system must be migrated to Salesforce.
ASR_Explanation: This migration impacts the integration architecture by requiring ETL/data‐mapping design and connector development between QuickBooks POS and Salesforce.

requirement: Appropriate procedures for disaster recovery, backups, and business continuity must be developed and documented for the chosen implementation platform.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s high-level infrastructure design by requiring fault tolerance, redundancy, and recovery strategies for availability and continuity.

requirement: The system must be written using industry best practices, and must be built using only Construction Junction approved products and technologies.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It constrains the technology stack and development practices, directly shaping the system’s architecture.

requirement: The following copyright notice must be displayed in all user interfaces: © 2009 - <current year> Construction Junction.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level compliance detail requiring a common footer component but no significant architectural change.

requirement: The TCS shall consist of the TCS workstation Hardware Configuration Items (HWCIs), TCS Computer Software Configuration Items (CSCIs), and additional TCS Support HWCIs and CSCIs.
ASR_Explanation: Defines the high-level decomposition into hardware and software configuration items, shaping system boundary and component architecture.

requirement: All TCS HWCI’s and CSCI’s shall be Year 2000 compliant.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the system-wide date/time data models and interfaces, requiring architectural support for four-digit year handling across all hardware and software components.

requirement: The TCS shall meet the applicable capability and characteristic criteria established by the Operational Requirements Document (ORD) CAF 003-90-I-A for the RQ-1A Predator Medium Altitude Endurance Unmanned Aerial Vehicle/System (MAE UAV) and the Close Range – Tactical Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (CR-TUAV).
ASR_Explanation: It imposes external performance and compliance constraints from the ORD that drive high-level system architecture, component selection, and interface design.

requirement: The TCS shall support 5 levels of UAV interaction: Level 1: receipt and transmission of secondary imagery and/or data; Level 2: direct receipt of imagery and/or data; Level 3: control of the UAV payload in addition to direct receipt of imagery/data; Level 4: control of the UAV, less launch and recovery, plus all the functions of level three; Level 5: capability to have full function and control of the UAV from takeoff to landing.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates core architectural partitioning for telemetry, payload and flight‐control modules, impacting interfaces, real-time processing and security.

requirement: The TCS system shall provide software capabilities and hardware configurations necessary to fulfill the operational tasking requirements across the 5 levels of interaction.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates critical hardware/software partitioning and multi‐level interaction architecture to meet operational tasking requirements.

requirement: The states of operation of the TCS shall include Startup, Operation, and Shutdown.
ASR_Explanation: This simple operational state enumeration is a low-level functional detail with no broad architectural impact.

requirement: The TCS states shall not exist concurrently.
ASR_Explanation: Enforces exclusive state transitions in the system’s state machine, impacting concurrency control and synchronization design.

requirement: Upon application of power the TCS shall enter the Startup State.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic state-transition behavior on power-up and does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: The Startup State shall be comprised of the following modes: Normal Startup Mode and Recovery Startup Mode.
ASR_Explanation: This defines a core system state machine impacting the architectural design of startup and recovery mode handling.

requirement: When the TCS Software is terminated normally the TCS shall enter the Normal Startup Mode of operation upon application of power.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional behavior for the startup sequence and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: When the TCS software is halted due to an unplanned power interruption as well as abnormal program termination, then the TCS shall enter the Recovery Startup Mode upon application of power.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the startup sequence and persistent state‐recovery mechanisms to handle power interruptions and abnormal terminations.

requirement: During startup, the TCS shall determine which of the 5 levels of interaction are achievable by the TCS configuration.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s startup and configuration management architecture by requiring dynamic detection and adaptation of supported interaction levels.

requirement: Levels of interaction higher than those achievable by a particular TCS configuration shall be prohibited.
ASR_Explanation: It requires architecture support for configuration-driven enforcement of interaction levels, impacting module boundaries and control logic.

requirement: The TCS shall inform the operator if the operator attempts to execute a function prohibited as a result of the determined level of interaction.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard access-control feedback requirement affecting UI/authorization logic but not driving a high-level architectural change.

requirement: When executing in the Normal Startup Mode, the TCS shall provide the system functionality necessary to initialize the system to place it in the Operations State within 60 seconds from the time power is supplied and the TCS application is launched.
ASR_Explanation: This performance constraint drives the system initialization architecture, requiring optimized startup sequencing and resource allocation to meet the 60 s requirement.

requirement: Initialization of the TCS HWCIs shall include startup of HWCIs, download of software, startup of CSCIs, execution of Startup FD/L, and establishment of the state of readiness of all interfaces.
ASR_Explanation: This defines the system startup sequence, component dependencies, and interface readiness, impacting the overall architectural design.

requirement: The TCS in the Recovery Startup Mode shall provide the system functionality to resume the Operations State within 45 seconds.
ASR_Explanation: This 45 s recovery-time requirement drives architecture decisions on state persistence, parallel initialization, resource allocation, and performance optimizations.

requirement: Recovery of the TCS HWCIs shall include startup of HWCIs, download of software, startup of CSCIs, and establishment of the state of readiness of all interfaces.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the high‐level system initialization and recovery orchestration, impacting module startup sequencing, software loading, and interface readiness management.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of automatically recording system state data, interface communications and other information necessary to support event reconstruction.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a cross-cutting requirement for a persistent logging and data‐reconstruction subsystem, impacting storage architecture, interfaces, and performance.

requirement: For recovery from abnormal termination, TCS shall prompt the operator to select the type of recovery to be executed: (1) Resume in the Same Modes And Data; (2) Resume in the Same Modes but Review and Modify the Command Data as Necessary; or, (3) Perform a Command Shutdown and Startup Via the Normal Startup Mode.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the recovery/error-handling subsystem and operator interface, impacting state management and control‐flow architecture.

requirement: When in the Operations State the TCS shall be capable of operating in three modes: normal operations mode, training operations mode, and maintenance operations mode.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates a three-state operational mode state machine, impacting module decomposition, UI flows, and context-specific services.

requirement: The Operations State modes shall not exist concurrently.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the system’s state machine architecture by enforcing mutually exclusive operational modes.

requirement: The operator shall have the capability to command the system to the Shutdown State from all modes under the Operations State.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional state-transition requirement and does not impose cross-cutting architectural constraints.

requirement: The TCS hardware and software shall execute periodic Fault Detection/Location (FD/L) while in the Normal Operations Mode and Training Mode, to include a periodic determination of level of interaction.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes cross-cutting real-time scheduling and hardware–software integration for periodic fault detection across multiple modes.

requirement: In the Normal Operations Mode the TCS shall support the following functions: Mission Planning, Mission Control and Monitoring, Payload Product Management, Target Coordinate Development, C4I Systems Interface.
ASR_Explanation: This FR is architecturally significant as it defines the core system modules and their interfaces, driving component decomposition and integration.

requirement: Functions under the Normal Operations Mode shall operate concurrently without precluding or excluding any of the other functions, in accordance with allowable operations as determined by the appropriate levels of interaction.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the system’s concurrency model, synchronization mechanisms, and inter-component resource arbitration.

requirement: In the Training Operations Mode the TCS shall support the following functions: Mission Planning, Mission Control and Monitoring, Payload Product Management, Target Coordinate Development, C4I Systems Interface.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines key system capabilities and external interfaces, driving the modular decomposition and integration architecture.

requirement: Functions under the Training Operations Mode shall operate concurrently without precluding or excluding any of the other functions, in accordance with allowable operations as determined by the appropriate levels of interaction.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architecture’s concurrency model and resource coordination, requiring non‐blocking operation and interaction protocols among training functions.

requirement: In the Maintenance Operations Mode, if computer workstation Human Computer Interface (HCI) is required, the TCS shall support the following functions: Conduct AV maintenance, Conduct payload maintenance, Conduct Data Link Terminal maintenance, Conduct workstation and peripheral equipment maintenance, Perform Fault Detection/Location (FD/L), Perform Software Upgrades, Perform Software Debug and Monitoring.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architectural decomposition of maintenance subsystems and HCI interfaces across multiple modules (AV, payload, data link, software, FD/L).

requirement: Functions, except for software upgrade and software debug, under the Maintenance Operations Mode shall operate concurrently without precluding or excluding any of the other functions in accordance with allowable operations as determined by the appropriate levels of interaction.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates support for concurrent execution and resource arbitration among maintenance functions, impacting the system’s concurrency and scheduling architecture.

requirement: Upon the selection of a shutdown command the TCS shall enter the Shutdown State, which will cause the TCS to be placed in a condition where power can be removed without impacting operations as well as causing damage to the system, and from which restart of the system can be accomplished normally.
ASR_Explanation: This defines a critical system state transition requiring architectural support for safe shutdown sequencing and hardware interface design.

requirement: Shutdown of the TCS shall include storage as well as deletion, as specified by the operator, of mission data files, shutdown of appropriate functions, shutdown of HCIs, and proper termination of all active interfaces.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s lifecycle and resource‐management architecture by defining data persistence/deletion and orderly shutdown of functions, HCIs, and interfaces.

requirement: There shall be no modes of operation in the shutdown state.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s state machine and component lifecycle by enforcing a pure shutdown state with no operational modes.

requirement: The TCS shall have the functionality to allow the operator to generate a UAV mission plan.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a core mission‐planning capability that drives the need for a dedicated planning module, data models, UI design, and UAV control interfaces.

requirement: The TCS shall have the functionality to receive and process UAV mission plans from service specific mission planning systems.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of external interfaces, data formats, and integration architecture for mission plan ingestion.

requirement: The TCS Mission plan shall include all necessary information required to be interoperable with the service specific mission planning systems including the Tactical Aircraft Mission Planning System (TAMPS), Aviation Mission Planning System (AMPS), and Air Force Mission Support System (AFMSS).
ASR_Explanation: It requires defining integration architecture and interface/data model standards to ensure interoperability with TAMPS, AMPS, and AFMSS.

requirement: The TCS shall have the functionality to transmit UAV mission plans to service specific mission planning systems.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates cross-system integration architecture by requiring defined interfaces, protocols, and data exchange patterns for mission plan transmission.

requirement: The TCS shall facilitate automated processing of mission plan data received via C4I interfaces in order to extract the appropriate mission planning data.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of C4I integration interfaces and automated data-processing pipelines, impacting overall system architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall have the functionality to receive and process UAV mission plans from other TCSs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of inter-system communication interfaces, data processing pipelines, and associated security and protocol choices.

requirement: The TCS shall have the functionality to transmit UAV mission plans to other TCSs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s communication architecture, requiring design of network interfaces, data formats, and security protocols for inter-TCS plan transmission.

requirement: A Mission Plan shall include a Flight Route Plan for a selected AV, a Payload Plan for the selected payload, and a Communications Plan.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a modular mission‐planning architecture with distinct Flight Route, Payload, and Communications Plan components and their interfacing.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of storing a minimum of 500 mission plans under unique names to allow for later retrieval.
ASR_Explanation: This storage volume is trivial for standard data‐storage solutions and does not drive major architectural choices.

requirement: The TCS mission planning function shall provide a graphical user interface that gives the operator the ability to define waypoints on a map based display using a pointing device with full keyset redundancy.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a map-based GUI with pointing device support and full keyset redundancy, driving the choice of UI framework, rendering engine, and input‐handling architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to compute the range and bearing between two geographic positions on the map display.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward local computation requirement that does not drive major architectural decisions or affect system-wide structure.

requirement: As an objective, the TCS shall have the capability to import as well as create and modify map display overlays for fire support coordination measures, airspace control measures, and threat identification measures.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the choice and integration of a GIS mapping engine and a modular overlay data model for import, creation, and modification workflows.

requirement: Upon completion of radar cross section analysis and characterization by the Government for each UAV type, TCS shall utilize a UAV signature versus threat lookup table (database) that identities the lethality of the threat to the UAV, and shall provide the capability of displaying aircraft signature versus threat, before and during flight.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for real-time threat database integration, data caching, performance guarantees, and UI display architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall permit dynamic mission and payload retasking during all phases of operational mission execution.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for runtime dynamic reconfiguration and workflow orchestration, impacting modular service design, messaging infrastructure, and real-time scheduling.

requirement: The TCS shall allow the operator to enter as well as review mission plan parameters, including AV flight parameters, payload control parameters, data link control parameters, AV VCR control parameters (if applicable to the selected AV), and AV loiter patterns.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives a modular, extensible UI and data-model architecture to support dynamic mission parameter types per selected AV.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to enter system configuration characteristics in the mission plan, to include selected AV type, AV identification number, selected payload type, ground control authorization information, and required communications pre-set for data links, tactical communications, and C4I data dissemination.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional data‐entry requirement with minimal impact on high-level system structure or architectural patterns.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the system functionality necessary to upload a flight route plan and payload plan (if applicable) to the AV via the selected system data link as well as direct ground connection.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the communications subsystem architecture by necessitating dual data‐link interfaces (wireless and direct connection), affecting network protocols, interfaces, and security design.

requirement: TCS shall provide the capability for the operator to retrieve a mission plan for viewing, modification, as well as deletion at the operator's discretion, and allow the operator to save the mission plan under a different name, for future retrieval.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD requirement that doesn’t impose non-functional constraints or drive novel architectural patterns beyond routine data persistence and UI layering.

requirement: The TCS shall automatically check the validity of the intended mission plan prior to being uploaded including altitude constraints, payload constraints, data link range constraints, airspace restrictions, fuel limitations, threat constraints, data link terrain masking effects, and Loss of Link (LOL) Plan.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a dedicated mission‐validation subsystem and impacts module interfaces, data flows, and real‐time constraint‐checking architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall notify the operator of all discrepancies found during the mission plan check as well as indicate successful completion of the mission plan check.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI notification requirement with minimal architectural impact beyond presentation and error-handling.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to override validation faults after the fault is acknowledged by the operator.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the fault management architecture by introducing new override logic, operator workflows, and audit/security controls in the validation subsystem.

requirement: The TCS shall allow the operator to set the LOL delay timer(s) during mission planning.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI/configuration function for mission planning parameters and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to print waypoint data in alphanumeric format.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only affects data presentation formatting and has minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Flight Route Plan, as a minimum, shall include AV flight path information, Loss of Link plan, AV VCR control tasking (if applicable to the selected AV), and data link control information.
ASR_Explanation: Defines critical data interfaces and structures for flight planning and communications, impacting system modules and integration.

requirement: The TCS shall allow the operator to define the desired AV route in waypoint format, and shall provide the capability to include up to 500 waypoints in each flight route plan.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts data modeling, storage capacity, and performance design to handle up to 500 waypoints per route plan.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to display mission waypoints and flight path graphically.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system architecture by mandating integration of map/rendering components and data pipelines for real-time graphical waypoint and flight-path display.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to enter waypoint data in alphanumeric format.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI‐level functional requirement that does not drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The TCS flight route planner shall include, as a minimum, the following flight planning tools: Weight and balance take off data calculations; Fuel Calculations; Terrain avoidance warning for line of sight flights; Minimum data link reception altitude calculations for line of sight flights; Payload search area information such as: visual acuity range due to atmospheric conditions, diurnal transition periods for thermal imagery, and lunar and solar terrain shadowing; Ability to designate flight corridors and restricted air space.
ASR_Explanation: This set of core planning tools and data flows defines the modular decomposition, interfaces, and integrations that drive the system architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall present to the operator the estimated time of arrival and fuel status at each programmed waypoint of the proposed mission plan.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI feature for displaying waypoint ETA and fuel status without affecting high-level architectural structure.

requirement: At every waypoint and every 5 minutes, the TCS shall compute the AV's flight range based on remaining fuel.
ASR_Explanation: This periodic computation requirement imposes real-time scheduling and performance constraints that will shape the system’s component and thread architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall analyze the flight route plan selected for uplink to determine that the flight constraints of the AV and the limitation of the data link are not violated prior to transmission of the flight route plan to the AV.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates a dedicated pre‐transmission validation component for route and link constraints, impacting module decomposition and data flow design.

requirement: The algorithmic accuracy of all flight planning calculations shall not deviate by more than 10% when compared with a mission flown by a validated 6 degree-of-freedom air vehicle simulator.
ASR_Explanation: This accuracy requirement drives the choice of high‐fidelity algorithms and numerical precision capabilities, impacting component design and technology selection.

requirement: The flight route plan, as a minimum, shall provide the necessary AV commands to autonomously execute a programmed flight and return to a designated recovery area.
ASR_Explanation: This core autonomy requirement impacts the system’s high-level module decomposition, interfaces, and reliability/performance constraints for mission planning and command execution.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the operator with an interactive graphics and map based flight route planning capability.
ASR_Explanation: This FR impacts the system architecture by requiring integration of an interactive graphics engine and map‐based services, influencing UI framework, data flow, and performance considerations.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to create waypoints that define the desired flight path, define AV Altitude and Airspeed associated with the waypoint.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the core navigation subsystem architecture, including data modeling for waypoints, persistence strategies, real-time constraints, and integration with flight control modules.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability of displaying overlays each containing 100 simultaneous icons of known fire support coordination zones, airspace control zones, threat systems, and displaying the threat engagement envelopes with associated radar terrain masking for those threats.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a high‐performance geospatial rendering and data‐processing architecture to support real‐time display of 100 simultaneous overlaid icons with terrain masking.

requirement: A de-clutter capability shall be provided that allows the operator to display only a selected number of the most significant threats.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the addition of a real-time filtering component in the threat processing/UI pipeline, impacting system modularity and performance.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to modify the maximum number of threats displayed, when de-clutter is selected.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level configuration detail with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to display waypoint data in alphanumeric format.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI display feature and does not affect the system’s overall architectural structure or key design decisions.

requirement: The TCS shall have the capability to load a map onto the display, to zoom in and out, to scroll, to print, and to clear a map on the map display.
ASR_Explanation: These standard UI map operations don’t impose high-level structural or non-functional constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the operator with a clearly indicated map scale.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI detail that does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: Upon operator request, the following icons shall be displayed on the map display (when the coordinates reside on the displayed portion of the map): AV position, payload center field of view and footprint, target position and type, TCS position and Ground Data Terminal (GDT) position, Launch/Handover/Recovery site, Flight Plan waypoints.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts the design of the map display subsystem by requiring real-time data integration, layering, coordinate filtering, and icon management.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of displaying the positions of the icons in Lat./Lon., Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM), and Military Grid Reference System (MGRS) formats and provide conversion to multiple spheroid scales.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates the integration of a coordinate‐conversion engine and geospatial library support, affecting modularization and service design.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to graphically display Line of Sight versus terrain profile (DTED).
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions for integrating DTED terrain data, 3D rendering engines, and performance-optimized data pipelines.

requirement: For those payloads identified in Table 3-2, the TCS shall be able to generate payload planning information to be uplinked to the AV separate from a flight route plan.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement adds a new separate uplink dataflow for payload planning, impacting communication interfaces and module decomposition.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the system functionality necessary to generate a payload plan that, as a minimum, shall include the following information: Payload type, Payload commands, Payload mode settings, Payload pointing commands, manual as well as automated, Payload field of view settings, manual as well as automated.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the definition of a core payload planning component with specific interfaces and data flows (manual vs automated modes), directly impacting system decomposition and integration.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to display the payload swath for the selected payload for planning purposes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local UI feature for payload visualization and does not drive major architectural changes.

requirement: While flying two MAE AVs Beyond Line Of Sight (BLOS), the TCS shall provide full control functionality of each AV.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the communication and control architecture for concurrent, reliable BLOS operation of multiple AVs.

requirement: AV specific components used to perform ground based closed loop command and control functions for TCS shall be initialized upon operator selection of a specific AV.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s modular design and runtime initialization mechanism by mandating dynamic loading of AV-specific control components.

requirement: The TCS shall transmit command and control information to the AV via the uplink to the AV, and receive AV telemetry and payload information via downlink from the AV.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the communication subsystem (uplink/downlink protocols, data flow, performance and integration constraints).

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of being interoperable with Predator and Outrider UAVs across the 5 levels of UAV interaction.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the integration architecture by defining required interfaces, protocols, and data models for UAV interoperability.

requirement: The TCS shall have the capability to control and monitor multiple types of AVs.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for an extensible, modular architecture with abstract interfaces to support control and monitoring of diverse AV types.

requirement: TCS shall notify the operator when AV performance parameters are out of limits.
ASR_Explanation: This alerting function can be implemented within existing monitoring and UI components without altering the system’s core architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to pass control of an AV to another TCS, and receive control of an AV from another TCS.
ASR_Explanation: It requires defining secure, reliable inter-system interfaces and protocols to transfer AV control between TCS instances.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to control the AV flight commands as well as to release the AV to an autonomous flight control mode.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing real-time, safety-critical command and autonomy modules with mode-switching interfaces, impacting system structure and intercomponent communication.

requirement: AV telemetry data shall be available to support other TCS functions as required.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a real-time telemetry distribution and integration framework, influencing interfaces, data flow, and performance considerations.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to control the flight of the selected AV in accordance with the specific AV's operational performance capabilities.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives a modular, configurable flight‐control architecture with real‐time performance handling to accommodate diverse AV operational profiles.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to fully control and display the AV's Identification Friend or Foe (IFF).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a dedicated IFF subsystem with secure hardware interfaces and UI integration, impacting the overall system modularity and communication architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to enter AV preset limits which, as a minimum, will include airspeed limits, altitude limits, and fuel limits.
ASR_Explanation: This FR specifies basic input fields for preset limits and only affects UI/data validation, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall allow the operator to control an AV using the LOS as well as SATCOM data links.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the communications subsystem architecture by requiring support for both LOS and SATCOM links, including hardware interfaces, link management, and failover logic.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the necessary system capabilities required for AV flight control BLOS via uplink command to two MAE air vehicles.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the BLOS communication architecture—including protocols, bandwidth, concurrency, and integration for uplink control of two MAE air vehicles.

requirement: The TCS shall support operation of the AV via all flight modes, to include manual controls.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the core flight‐mode management architecture and the integration of manual control interfaces.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to implement an emergency action plan, if supported by the AV, to control the AV during equipment failures.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates fault-tolerant emergency control and failover mechanisms integrated with AV interfaces, impacting system architecture and safety-critical modules.

requirement: The TCS shall provide interactive displays necessary to command the flight of an AV.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions for the human-machine interface layer, real-time data handling, and system integration.

requirement: The TCS shall allow the operator to command the flight behavior characteristics inherent to the selected AV.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of a flexible, real-time command interface and control modules to support varied AV flight behaviors.

requirement: The operator shall have the capability to initiate as well as change, to include as a minimum manual override, flight behaviors by sending the proper control commands to the UAV.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the core flight control architecture by defining real-time command interfaces and safety-critical manual override mechanisms.

requirement: The TCS shall have the capability to command the AV to use the navigation methods inherent to the selected AV.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement necessitates an extender/adapter layer in the integration architecture to abstract and command diverse AV-specific navigation methods.

requirement: The operator shall have the capability to initiate as well as change, to include as a minimum manual override, UAV navigation methods by sending the proper control commands to the UAV.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s control-loop architecture by mandating real-time override paths, safety mechanisms, and command-processing interfaces.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the functionality to control, monitor, and display the operation of the Air Data Terminal (ADT).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the definition of core control, monitoring, and display subsystems and their interaction and data-flow architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the functionality to control the ADT antenna, including its pointing direction and mode of transmission (e.g. omni and directional).
ASR_Explanation: It defines the hardware interface and real-time control requirements, impacting subsystem decomposition and integration design.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the functionality to control the power, transmitter signal strength, and frequencies used by ADT for data link communication.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the communication subsystem and hardware/software interfaces for dynamic power, signal strength, and frequency control.

requirement: As an objective, the TCS shall support an automatic launch and recovery system.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates core control and fault-recovery mechanisms shaping the system’s lifecycle management architecture.

requirement: As an objective, the TCS shall be interoperable with the Integrity Beacon Landing System (IBLS) and the Common Automated Recovery System (CARS), both used by Outrider.
ASR_Explanation: Requires defining and implementing integration interfaces and protocols to ensure interoperability with external systems (IBLS and CARS), impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: As an objective, the TCS shall present sufficient cues to the operator to implement and monitor automatic launch and recovery, and to initiate abort procedures if required.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the human-machine interface and real-time data processing pipelines to deliver operator cues and abort controls.

requirement: The TCS shall allow the operator to initiate the emergency recovery feature of the AV, if the AV has an emergency recovery feature.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates a safety-critical control interface and fault-recovery flow between the TCS and AV, impacting subsystem boundaries and integration design.

requirement: TCS shall provide the capability to monitor specific telemetry elements real-time, and record all telemetry elements for future review and processing.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of real-time streaming ingestion, long-term storage, and processing components to meet performance, scalability, and data retention needs.

requirement: TCS shall provide the capability to monitor AV adherence to the uplinked mission plan, detecting any deviations greater than 10% from projected flight path, and notifying the operator if deviations are detected.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a real-time telemetry ingestion and deviation-detection pipeline with event-driven alerting capabilities.

requirement: TCS shall provide the capability to monitor the status of all AV subsystems reporting status.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates the design and integration of a real-time monitoring infrastructure to aggregate and report health status across all AV subsystems.

requirement: The TCS shall display the AV status, to include but not be limited to the AV location and flight and avionics system status.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates real-time integration of avionics and telemetry data sources with a UI subsystem, influencing module interfaces and data‐flow architecture.

requirement: When the data link is interrupted, the TCS shall present the last known AV status values and the time at which the last values were reported.
ASR_Explanation: Requires local caching of AV status and offline/failover handling in the TCS architecture to support last-known-value display.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of displaying fuel parameters to the operator to include as a minimum, remaining fuel, flow rate, and bingo fuel.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic functional UI requirement with no major impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall compute the estimated position of the AV during Loss of Link (LOL) based upon the last known AV position and anticipated flight path based on current flight mode.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a dedicated dead-reckoning/navigation module integrated with flight-mode management and fault-tolerant data flows, impacting system structure and interfaces.

requirement: TCS shall display a LOL timer to the operator initiating a LOL onset.
ASR_Explanation: This functional UI display requirement has minimal architectural impact, involving only standard timer logic and UI component integration.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of being interoperable with the installed payloads across the 5 levels of UAV interaction.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a flexible, extensible interface and middleware architecture to ensure interoperability across multiple UAV payload interaction levels.

requirement: The TCS will have the capability to control and monitor the AV payload(s).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives key architectural decisions around real-time communication interfaces, integration layers, and payload control/monitoring infrastructure.

requirement: The TCS shall have the capability to receive data from and control payloads on an AV that is being controlled from another TCS.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the communication interfaces, concurrency control, and integration architecture needed for multi-TCS coordination.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the necessary system capabilities required for payload control beyond line of sight via uplink command of two air vehicles of the same type using sequential communication techniques.
ASR_Explanation: This FR drives key architectural decisions around the communication subsystem, protocols, and concurrency design for beyond-line-of-sight multi-vehicle payload control.

requirement: The TCS shall receive, process, and present payload data to the operator so that the status of the payload can be determined.
ASR_Explanation: This core data‐flow requirement drives the high‐level architecture into ingestion, processing, and presentation components.

requirement: The TCS shall permit the operator to control the payload using all methods supported by the payload installed in the selected AV.
ASR_Explanation: Requires a modular, extensible interface layer for dynamic payload driver integration, impacting system modularity and component interfaces.

requirement: The TCS shall provide a graphical user interface display for the purpose of controlling the payload.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a presentation layer and UI framework selection, impacting system layering and integration with control modules.

requirement: The TCS shall provide override of payload automated as well as preprogrammed inputs.
ASR_Explanation: This feature adds new control paths and interfaces for overriding automated and preprogrammed inputs, impacting the core control flow and safety/security layers.

requirement: TCS shall provide the capability to monitor payload adherence to the uplinked mission plan.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for real-time telemetry ingestion and mission plan validation components, impacting the system’s data processing and integration architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall display the current search footprint and the search history of the payload on the map.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the integration of mapping services, data persistence and retrieval mechanisms, and real-time rendering architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall have the capability to simultaneously control and monitor a line-of-sight as well as a BLOS data terminal.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of dual‐path communication subsystems, impacting network topology, concurrency control, and resource allocation.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the system functionality necessary to record data obtained via the data link.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a data ingestion and recording subsystem, impacting interfaces, throughput handling, and storage architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of interfacing with the specified data terminal and issuing data link terminal commands required to establish, control, and maintain the data link with a selected AV.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the communication subsystem and protocol interfaces, impacting component modularization and data‐link reliability.

requirement: Data terminal control shall include, but is not limited to, antenna pointing control, transmitter control, and receiver control.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines core hardware-control subsystems (antenna pointing, transmitter, receiver) that drive major component partitioning and interfaces.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of automatically selecting the proper mode of operation for the selected data terminal.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a core control-flow requirement that drives the architectural design of mode-selection, state management, and modular extensibility.

requirement: As a minimum the TCS LOS data terminal control modes shall include acquisition, autotrack, search, manual point, omni directional, as well as directional modes of operation, if applicable to the selected data link.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines essential operational modes that drive the system’s module decomposition, state‐management design, and interface architecture.

requirement: The operator shall be able to manually override any automatic data terminal control mode selection if desired, except during Emission Control (EMCON) and Hazards of Electromagnetic Radiation to Ordnance (HERO) conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional control behavior exception and does not drive major architectural trade-offs or structural design.

requirement: The TCS shall support a LOS data link and SATCOM data link capability.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the communication subsystem architecture by mandating dual LOS and SATCOM data‐link support, affecting hardware interfaces, network protocols, and redundancy.

requirement: For shipboard operations, the TCS shall provide the capability to switch to a second LOS antenna, if a second antenna is available, when desired (e.g. the currently active antenna is masked by shipboard obstructions).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the hardware interface and control-flow architecture by mandating dynamic antenna selection and fault‐tolerant LOS switching.

requirement: The TCS shall provide the capability to switch to a SATellite COMmunication (SATCOM) antenna, if the selected AV has SATCOM capability, when desire (e.g. the AV proceeds beyond LOS range as well as when LOS is obstructed).
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the communications subsystem architecture by requiring dynamic antenna switching logic, hardware interfaces, and network layer adaptation.

requirement: The TCS shall provide an interactive display for the purpose of controlling the data link terminal.
ASR_Explanation: This is a functional UI requirement for an interactive display and does not impose significant architectural constraints beyond choosing a suitable UI framework.

requirement: The TCS shall provide automatic pointing commands for directional antennas, and shall allow for the manual pointing of directional antennas when desired by the operator.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a real-time control subsystem with separate automatic and manual modes and UI/hardware integration.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of positioning antennas to maintain LOS as well as SATCOM.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives real-time hardware integration and module partitioning for antenna control and communications interfaces.

requirement: The TCS shall incorporate antenna pedestal 3-axis stabilization to compensate for platform (e.g. ship, or HMMWV) motion, if applicable.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the system architecture by requiring real-time control loops, sensor/actuator integration, and mechanical subsystem support for 3-axis stabilization.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of automatically controlling the transmitter and receiver functions of the selected data terminal.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional capability that does not impose high-level architectural constraints or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: The operator shall be able to manually override the automatic function selection of the selected data terminal, if desired.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific functional detail for user interaction that does not drive major architectural structure or technology decisions.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of automatically controlling the transmitter and receiver modes of the selected data terminal.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of hardware abstraction and dynamic control modules for automatically switching transmitter/receiver modes.

requirement: The operator shall be able to manually override the automatic mode selection of the selected data terminal, if desired.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local control function for mode selection and does not impose major architectural constraints.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of automatically controlling the transmitter and receiver frequencies of the selected data terminal.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the design of real-time control modules and hardware interface architecture for dynamic frequency management.

requirement: The operator shall be able to manually override the automatic frequency selection if desired.
ASR_Explanation: This manual override is a UI-level functional detail and doesn’t affect the overall system architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall receive, process, and present status data to the operator so that the status of the data terminal and the supported AV data link can be monitored.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the need for a real-time data ingestion, processing, and presentation subsystem impacting overall module decomposition, communication protocols, and UI architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of monitoring and displaying the signal strength of the received and transmitted signals for the selected data terminal.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized functional requirement for signal monitoring and display, not altering overall system architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall be capable of monitoring and displaying the signal quality of the received and transmitted signals for the selected data terminal.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional feature (monitoring/display) that can be implemented within existing components without impacting the overall architecture.
