ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects maintenance logistics and inventory management rather than driving core software or system architectural decisions.

requirement: The environmental support required by the TCS shall be at least the same as that required for the respective UAV System.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes environmental constraints that drive hardware and system architecture decisions (e.g., materials, cooling, and durability).

requirement: Basing for the system will follow the plan for UAV units and service command echelon requirements as delineated in the ORD.
ASR_Explanation: It defines deployment topology and infrastructure placement constraints for UAV units and command echelons, driving network and physical architecture design.

requirement: The TCS shall meet the deployment criteria for the organic unit to which it is assigned.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes environment-specific deployment and configuration constraints that directly influence the system’s deployment architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall be transported into the theater as an organic component of the operational UAV system being deployed.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes physical integration and deployment constraints on the UAV system’s hardware and interface architecture.

requirement: TCS transportation in theater for Army and Marine Corps systems shall be by ground, air, as well as rail transportable.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes transportability constraints on the system’s physical form factor and modular design across air, ground, and rail platforms.

requirement: For the Air Force, TCS transportation to the theater shall be by air. Within the theater, the USAF GCS shall be capable of being moved around an established air field.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the physical deployment architecture and system packaging constraints for air transport and ground mobility on an airfield.

requirement: The TCS shall be ground transportable.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes physical size, weight, and modular packaging constraints that drive key hardware and system design decisions.

requirement: The TCS shall be air transportable by helicopter (CH-47/CH-53D) and C-130 drive-on/drive-off capable.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the physical hardware architecture, impacting size, weight, structural design, and packaging for air transportability.

requirement: The TCS shall be sea transportable.
ASR_Explanation: Sea transportability imposes constraints on system packaging, hardware selection, and environmental/mechanical architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall be rail transportable.
ASR_Explanation: This is a mechanical transport constraint that does not impact the system’s software or hardware architectural structure.

requirement: The TCS shall be configurable for sea, ground, as well as air transport in 2 hours or less.
ASR_Explanation: This 2-hour multi-mode configurability mandate drives a modular, plugin-based architecture with dynamic loading and efficient configuration management.

requirement: The TCS system shall be capable of being de-configured from sea, ground, as well as air transport and ground-mobile in 2 hours or less.
ASR_Explanation: This rapid de-configuration requirement drives a modular, portable deployment architecture and packaging strategy.

requirement: The TCS shall have an objective capability to be integrated and operated from tactical and command and control aircraft.
ASR_Explanation: This drives a modular, compliant avionics architecture with specific interface standards and environmental constraints for aircraft integration.

requirement: The TCS shall have an objective capability to be integrated and operated from submarines.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes submarine-specific environmental, hardware, and integration constraints requiring changes to packaging, interfaces, and deployment architecture.

requirement: The TCS shall have a capability to be integrated and operated from land based platforms.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the system’s modular architecture and interface design to support deployment and integration on land-based platforms.

requirement: The TCS shall have an objective capability to be integrated and operated from ships.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architecture for platform-specific integration, environmental hardening, and maritime network/connectivity provisions.

ID: (Note: This is a partial extraction due to the extreme length of the document and the number of requirements. If you need the full set
requirement: please specify a range or section
functional/non-functional: or request the CSV as a downloadable file.)
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor functional detail (CSV export/range selection) that does not drive major architectural changes.

requirement: PeaZip’s users can browse and gain access, in various ways, to every area (e.g. storing units, desktop, users documents etc) of the computer system on which PeaZip runs, as well as to included objects (archives and files).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the core file-system abstraction, security/permission model, and cross-platform integration layers.

requirement: By choosing “Filesystem”, users can get access to an object from an object list of objects that is organized according to a functional hierarchy.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional detail about object access and hierarchy navigation, not affecting core architectural structure.

requirement: By choosing “Bookmarks”, users can get access to an object from a list of objects that have been rated by them as favorites.
ASR_Explanation: This bookmark feature has minimal architectural impact and can be implemented within existing UI and data-access modules without structural changes.

requirement: By choosing “Recent archives”, users can get access to an object from a list of objects that have been recently browsed in PeaZip.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI feature (maintaining a recent-items list) and does not impose significant architectural constraints.

requirement: Through “Open path” and “Open archive”, users can get direct access to a path of an object or a file/archive.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor file‐handling/UI convenience feature without major impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Browsing and access to computer system’s objects is provided by navigation bar of the file manager interface, with navigation buttons (back, forward, up) and refresh button.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level navigation feature that does not drive high-level architectural structure or significant technology decisions.

requirement: Users are allowed to manage the way of selection of the displayed in file manager interface objects (files and archives).
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level feature affecting selection controls with minimal impact on the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: “Select all”, selects all objects that are currently displayed at file manager interface.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI feature with minimal architectural impact limited to the presentation layer.

requirement: “Invert selection”, allows inverted selection of objects (all non-selected before the beginning of the function objects are selected and all selected before the beginning of the function objects are deselected).
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI feature with minimal architectural impact, implementable within the existing selection component.

requirement: “All objects…” set of commands, allows selection from the objects displayed on file manager interface, according to: extension, attributes, size and date.
ASR_Explanation: This filtering capability is a standard UI-level feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: “Sort by selection status”, sorts all objects on file manager interface according to whether they are selected or not.
ASR_Explanation: This only affects the UI’s sorting logic and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: Users are allowed to instantly access other main application interfaces (specifically create archive interface and extraction interface), as well as changing view mode for objects (archives and files) displayed in file manager interface.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI navigation and view-mode feature with minimal impact on the overall software architecture.

requirement: “Go to archiving layout”, leads directly to create archive interface.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor UI navigation detail with no broad impact on system architecture.

requirement: “Go to extraction layout”, leads directly to extraction interface.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI navigation flow with no significant impact on system structure or quality attributes.

requirement: “Toggle browse/flat view”, displays all together the objects contained in the current path or in the archive.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It’s a UI presentation toggle that doesn’t impose new structural or systemic architectural requirements.

requirement: “Refresh”, refresh/update of currently displayed content.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI‐level update feature with no significant implications for the overall software architecture.

requirement: Users can use, through PeaZip, multiple computer system (both for storage units and the system itself) management tools.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of an extensible integration layer (plugin framework), cross‐platform abstractions, and security boundaries for system‐level tool access.

requirement: “System tools”, collects system’s disk utilities (clean, defrag, manage, remove), system management tools (control panel, computer management, task manager) and displays environment variables (both for Linux and Windows).
ASR_Explanation: Requires cross-platform OS integration and abstraction layers for privileged system utilities on both Windows and Linux.

requirement: “System benchmark” utility, rates the host system in terms of MIPS (millions of integer instructions per second) and Core 2 Duo equivalent speed in MHz.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing a system benchmark feature requires architectural support for hardware abstraction and low-level performance measurement modules.

requirement: Users can access a group of file management tools, that are not strictly archiving-related. Users can choose from a set of utilities, like: secure file deletion, byte to byte comparison between two files, file checking, file split/join, file information display and hexadecimal preview of the content of a file.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives a modular, extensible plugin architecture with secure low-level file handling and performance-tuned I/O services.

requirement: Secure file deletion, intended for securely remove files and folders from disk, avoiding possible data recovery. This is implemented by multiple successive passes of the deletion procedure over the storage area of the selected for deletion file.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the storage‐layer design by mandating secure wipe algorithms and associated performance and security trade–offs.

requirement: Byte to byte file comparison, which spots exactly what the different bytes are and is not susceptible to collisions under any circumstance.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level algorithmic requirement impacting implementation details (file I/O and comparison logic) but not the overall system architecture.

requirement: Check files, which is useful for finding duplicate files and checking files for corruption when an original checksum or hash value is known.
ASR_Explanation: This file-checking feature can be encapsulated in a standalone module and doesn’t impose overarching architectural constraints.

requirement: Split file and merge split volumes back to the original file.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized file‐processing feature that doesn’t drive high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: Information display for selected (by users) files. Number of files, older and newer object’s date/time, total space occupation, and larger and smaller object’s sizes are presented.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data‐display feature that doesn’t impose structural, performance, or high‐level design constraints.

requirement: Hexadecimal preview, which allows view of the content of a file represented as hexadecimal values.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level feature for displaying file content as hex and does not drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: Users can use PeaZip for decompressing the contents of a selected (by them) compressed archive and extract them in a folder. Name and storage space of the folder in the computer system, are up to users to decide.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic file‐decompression feature that doesn’t impose any high‐level architectural constraints beyond standard file I/O.

requirement: The archive, whose contents are extracted, is neither destroyed, nor deleted and it is not left empty. It retains its content and name and remains saved at the same place of the computer system that was initially stored.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level file-handling detail that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: For the procedure of decompression and extraction to be completed, it is necessary that the selected archive is of one of the supported for extraction by PeaZip formats.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level validation rule for supported archive formats and does not drive architectural structure or major technology choices.

requirement: In case the selected archive is encrypted, user must know and insert password and (if there is one) keyfile in order to have access to the archives contents (for browsing or extracting them).
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional security requirement that can be implemented within existing modules without altering the overall system architecture.

requirement: Users are allowed to compress one or more objects (files or archives) in one or more compressed archive volumes (sizing less or equally to the original ones) in one of the offered for compression by PeaZip formats.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the selection and integration of compression libraries and the modular design for multi-volume archive handling and I/O streaming.

requirement: Users are allowed to update an archive (by adding extra objects-files or archives-to an already existing archive), as well as choose objects that are to be included in an archive.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing an extensible archive management service and storage model to support incremental updates and object selection.

requirement: Name and storage space (in the computer system) for the created archives are defined by users.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement that does not drive major architectural decisions beyond standard storage handling.

requirement: In cases of updating (adding objects) the name of the compressed file which is updated and its storage space remain the same as they were before the update.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates in-place, fixed-size updates of compressed files, impacting compression algorithm choice and storage/subsystem design.

requirement: The objects that are contained in the created archive are not destroyed, are not deleted and are not left empty. They retain their content and name and remain saved at the same space of the computer system where they were initially stored.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional detail about file‐handling behavior and does not drive any high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: Encryption (“lock”) can be used for the created archives by setting password and (optionally) keyfile.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s security architecture by introducing encryption algorithms, key management, and integration into the archive processing pipeline.

requirement: Compression to read-only supported formats is not possible.
ASR_Explanation: This is a minor functional constraint that doesn’t alter the system’s high-level structure or quality attribute trade-offs.

requirement: Users can drag and drop objects (files or archives) between any computer system space and the application. Application’s interfaces that support drag and drop, are file manager interface, create archive interface and extraction interface.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of OS-level drag-and-drop integration, UI framework choice, and file-system/archive abstraction and event-handling subsystems.

requirement: When files and folders are dragged and dropped to the file manager, they get listed in the archive creation interface, allowing fine tuning of the function before confirming or cancelling it.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level behavior requirement (drag-and-drop listing) that can be handled within existing front-end components without impacting the overall architecture.

requirement: Objects that are dragged and dropped to file manager interface while browsing an archive, will be added to the current archive, if the archive format allows modifications.
ASR_Explanation: This requires extending the archive management subsystem with pluggable format handlers and asynchronous UI-to-core modification workflows.

requirement: Dragging an archived object (file or archive) from PeaZip to the system, will extract it to the location (of the computer system) where it is dropped. If dragged objects are contained in a compressed archive, then they are automatically decompressed before being extracted to the location where they are dropped. If the dragged objects are not contained in a compressed archive, they are just copied to the location where they are dropped.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level drag-and-drop feature detail that doesn’t drive high-level architectural structure or patterns.

requirement: PeaZip allows users to encrypt (“lock”) archives during creation or updating, by using password which is demanded by PeaZip (or other applications of the same use) and should be inserted, in case some user wants to gain access to an encrypted (“locked”) archive in order to manage it (e.g. browse it, update it, extract its content).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the security architecture by dictating the integration of encryption modules, key management, and secure password handling within the archive processing components.

requirement: For higher security reasons, PeaZip offers users the ability to use keyfile for archives encryption, in addition to the password.
ASR_Explanation: Introduces new encryption keyfile handling requiring changes to the security architecture, key management modules, and data storage models.

requirement: PeaZip can generate a random password and create a random keyfile.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional feature with no major impact on overall system architecture or design constraints.

requirement: Apart from encryption, PeaZip supports decryption (“unlock”) of encrypted files.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine complementary feature to the existing encryption capability and can be implemented within the same crypto module without impacting the overall architecture.

requirement: PeaZip offers graphic display of the progress of any currently executed through/by it function, through its graphic wrapper, named PeaLauncher. In PeaLauncher’s window, information about the progress of any currently executed function as well as information about its overall results (after the end of it) is displayed.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level progress reporting requirement and does not impose structural or cross‐cutting architectural changes.

requirement: PeaZip allows users to monitor at any time the progress and the status of any of its functions that is being executed, as well as displaying information about the results of those functions on the archive or file for which they were executed, information about the contents of those archives or files and information that have to do with the execution itself (like running time).
ASR_Explanation: Requires designing an asynchronous event-driven mechanism for tracking and reporting task progress and status to the UI.

requirement: Users are allowed to make modifications over offered by PeaZip functions, so as to adapt them to their preference and cover their needs more adequately. In general, available settings pertain to: application language, way of execution of the offered by the application utilities, PeaLauncher, character encoding, supported archiving and compression formats, file and computer system management tools and graphical layout of the application.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement necessitates a modular, plugin-based architecture with dynamic configuration management for UI layout, i18n, encoding, compression formats and execution behaviors.

requirement: “Run as different user”, closes current PeaZip instance and opens a new one under alternative user profile.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts process lifecycle and security context management, requiring architectural support for user impersonation and session state handling.

requirement: “Localization”, quickly changes application’s language (the new selection can be made from a list of supported by PeaZip languages).
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing the application’s resource management and UI framework to support dynamic language bundle loading and runtime switching.

requirement: “Settings”, lead to Settings window (Settings Interface). In Settings Interface, users are able to: change application’s language, define the path for the location of the computer system that is going to be considered by PeaZip as user’s Desktop, choose the way backend command-line applications will run, set policy about PeaLauncher window’s behavior after some function’s termination, have access to options related to character encoding, have choices on recently managed archives history utility, choose a selection of users’ favorite archiving and compression formats to be offered for quick selection and which format should be used as default in create archive interface, configure parameters of the features of archive creation and archive content extraction, choose applications for opening/previewing contents of managed by PeaZip archives, configure parameters of computer system’s files and archives management tools, adapt the application’s graphical interface to their needs, modifying color, icons, fonts, window opacity and size for GUI, and toolbar buttons.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a modular configuration and settings management subsystem—covering i18n, theming, plugin/command-line integration and dynamic UI customization—that impacts core architecture.

requirement: PeaZip’s User Interfaces include: File manager Interface, Create archive Interface, Extraction Interface, Settings Interface, Graphic display of information and currently executed functions’ progress window (PeaLauncher).
ASR_Explanation: Specifying the required UI modules drives the system’s modular decomposition, layering, and choice of presentation/interaction architecture.

requirement: PeaZip supports keyboard shortcuts for its functions, including file management, extraction, creation, and file tools.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level usability enhancement for input handling and does not drive changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: PeaZip can run on any computer system, regardless of the type of operating system used. The only thing PeaZip requires, as far as hardware is concerned, is an x-86 compatible CPU, due to some performance critical sections written in ASM.
ASR_Explanation: This cross-platform NFR drives architecture decisions around OS abstraction layers, conditional builds, and x86-specific ASM integration.

requirement: PeaZip is compatible with: All 64-bit versions of Microsoft Windows, All 32-bit Microsoft Windows(95/98/NT/2000/XP/Vista), All POSIX(all versions of Linux/all versions of BSD/all UNIX-like operating systems).
ASR_Explanation: This cross-platform compatibility requirement drives the need for abstraction layers, conditional builds, and OS-specific modules in the architecture.

requirement: PeaZip is available as source code that is compatible with almost any operating system.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives architectural decisions around using portable languages, cross-platform frameworks, and build systems to ensure compatibility across operating systems.

requirement: All needed software invoked by the frontend is included in PeaZip packages (available under suitable licenses, as open source or royalty free) so no custom package is needed to be installed to make PeaZip work (with the possible exception of some standard gtk/gdk related libraries needed to run PeaZip, Gwrap and Pea binaries, which may be missing in some computer systems, but are well known, trustable and widely available).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the deployment architecture by dictating how dependencies must be bundled and managed within the PeaZip distribution.

requirement: PeaZip is not a web application, thus its installation and use does not require any communication interfaces’ feature. However, connection to the Internet would be considered useful for users, as, through it, they would be able to access on-line help and information about PeaZip.
ASR_Explanation: This optional Internet connectivity for accessing online help is a minor non-functional detail that does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: This application should function even in cases of wrong data insertion or wrong settings. In case of error it should provide users with appropriate help messages.
ASR_Explanation: It affects the overall error-handling architecture and cross-cutting validation and messaging strategies for fault tolerance and user feedback.

requirement: Extraction of the content of encrypted(locked) archives through PeaZip, shouldn’t be allowed without inserting the correct password and (if existing) keyfile for authentication.
ASR_Explanation: This security requirement impacts the design of authentication, key management, and encryption/decryption modules in the system architecture.

requirement: Recovering the password and the keyfile that one PeaZip user has inserted or set to an archive by any other user of the computer system or the application, through the application itself or in general the computer system, should not be possible.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts secure key management and cryptographic storage design, requiring architecture-level decisions on encryption, access control, and isolation.

requirement: No data remains from data that have been selected to be securely deleted should be left in the storing area of the computer system after the end of the procedure of secure deletion.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the storage subsystem design by requiring secure erase mechanisms and data handling processes to prevent residual data.

requirement: Any user should be able to use PeaZip without any specific knowledge or experience over using computer systems, by reading user manuals (help documents, requirements specification document, mini tutorials) and seeing messages and warnings that the application provides with.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the UI architecture by requiring integration of help systems, tutorials, and context‐sensitive messaging for usability.

requirement: The system must be able to capture and manage files where appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the storage and file‐handling architecture, dictating how files are captured, stored, managed, and integrated with underlying systems.

requirement: An actor in the role of course administrator should be able to optionally upload multiple files where he or she finds that such a feature would be useful — such as on a devoted project page.
ASR_Explanation: Enables multi-file uploads, necessitating design decisions around storage architecture, file handling, security, and scalability.

requirement: The system must allow for multiple file upload to be configured on a per-page basis.
ASR_Explanation: This is a feature-level requirement for configurable uploads that does not drive core architectural structure.

requirement: Actors in the capacity of course administrator or professor should be able to enable multiple file uploads on a page of his or her choosing.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a localized UI‐level feature toggle that does not drive major system‐wide architectural decisions.

requirement: The system must be able to capture and organize voice clips that can be used where appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for an audio ingestion, storage, indexing and retrieval subsystem, impacting data models, storage architecture, and media-processing components.

requirement: The system must allow actors to organize their voice clips into a portfolio.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD feature for organizing voice clips with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The system must allow actors to download their voice clips in a flexible format.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for a scalable media‐processing/transcoding component and flexible storage/delivery APIs to support multiple download formats.

requirement: The system must store audio clips in a format conducive to speech.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying an audio format influences storage architecture, codec integration, and the media processing pipeline design.

requirement: The system must allow actors to delete recorded clips.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation with minimal architectural impact and can be handled within existing service endpoints.

requirement: The system must be able to display web feeds, such as RSS 2.0 feeds, for all pages of the course management system where elements such as forum posts, assignment postings, announcements, wiki alterations and other blocks of information that an actor may view are added.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces a cross‐cutting feed‐generation service and API integration across all content modules, impacting system structure and performance considerations.

requirement: Actors in the role of course administrators should have the ability to determine which of these components have feeds and to whom these feeds should pertain.
ASR_Explanation: It requires implementing dynamic feed subscription management and authorization, impacting component interaction patterns and data distribution design.

requirement: The system must allow web feeds to be turned on or off on a per-page basis.
ASR_Explanation: Enabling per-page web-feed toggles drives the need for a configuration/feature-flag subsystem and influences content-rendering and service-integration architecture.

requirement: The system must be able to use search functionality as a way to navigate Moodle pages instead of using hierarchical links.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing search-based navigation requires adding an indexing and query subsystem and altering the UI routing architecture.

requirement: An actor must be able to search through pages in a course.
ASR_Explanation: This simple search functionality doesn’t impose significant architectural constraints or drive high-level design decisions.

requirement: The system must display a search box on every page after an actor has logged in.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI layout requirement with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The system must allow actors in the capacity of course administrator to post grades associated with assignments for a particular actor in the capacity of student.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation with role-based access control and does not drive any major architectural decisions.

requirement: A course administrator must be able to grade an assignment.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic functional requirement for user-role operations with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: The system should display grade information to the appropriate actor.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI/authorization requirement with no major architectural impact.

requirement: The system should maintain a grade history.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the persistence architecture by requiring versioned data storage or audit/history tables to track grade changes over time.

requirement: The system must provide a wiki where actors can collaboratively create networks of documents.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the core content management architecture by requiring linked-document storage, versioning, and real-time collaborative editing support.

requirement: The system must provide a blog engine.
ASR_Explanation: It defines a core content‐management module that will drive decisions on system modularization, data storage, and technology choices for dynamic content delivery.

requirement: The system must provide both e-mail and SMS notifications for pages.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional feature requiring notification integration but doesn’t drive high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: As far as possible, the system should provide a simple, responsive interface.
ASR_Explanation: This will influence the overall solution architecture by driving choices around UI frameworks, client–server interaction patterns, performance optimizations, and component layering to ensure responsiveness.

requirement: The system must be backed up on a configurable schedule.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of scheduling, storage, and recovery infrastructure to ensure configurable backup processes.

requirement: The system should be available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives a high-availability architecture with redundancy, load balancing, failover, and real-time monitoring.

requirement: The system should support at least 1000 concurrent users.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts scalability and performance architecture, driving decisions on load balancing, resource allocation, and concurrency handling.

requirement: The Clarus program shall determine the need for bilateral Clarus Data Sharing Agreements with countries, agencies, states, and regions.
ASR_Explanation: This business-rule requirement can be handled within standard application logic and does not drive major architectural structuring or quality-attribute decisions.

requirement: The authorized representative(s) of the contributors shall be identified.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward user‐role identification detail with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The contributors shall identify and define the types of data and information that will be included in the Clarus Data Sharing Agreement.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives data classification and schema design, impacting database architecture and security controls.

requirement: The contributors shall identify the intended use of their shared data and information.
ASR_Explanation: It requires architectural support for metadata tagging and governance, impacting data schema and access-control design.

requirement: The contributors shall identify the categories of recipients of their shared data and information.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: It drives the design of data classification, metadata tagging, and access‐control components for policy enforcement.

requirement: The contributors shall define the units of measurements of their shared data and information.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates architecture-level data modeling and integration support for unit metadata, validation, and conversion services.

requirement: The Clarus program shall determine how it will provide data and information to contributors.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the data distribution architecture and communication mechanisms for contributor interfaces, influencing API design and integration patterns.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide data and information statistics on its operation to contributors.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing a metrics‐collection and reporting subsystem, impacting data storage, processing pipelines, and deployment architecture.

requirement: The contributors shall inform the Clarus program of changes in authorized personnel/offices.

requirement: The Clarus program shall inform contributors of the 'acceptance and use' of their data and information.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the addition of a notification/event subsystem and impacts the data processing workflow and audit‐tracking architecture.

requirement: The Clarus Initiative Management Team shall undertake joint communications activities and products that will enhance public understanding and dissemination of contributions of the Clarus program.
ASR_Explanation: This is a management and communication objective without technical or design constraints affecting the system’s architecture.

requirement: The Clarus Initiative Management Team shall agree upon the activities and products that will enhance public understanding/communication of the contribution of the Clarus program.
ASR_Explanation: This is a program management and communication planning requirement, not imposing system-level design or architectural constraints.

requirement: The contributors shall inform the Clarus program of known error(s) and modifications in its data and information.

requirement: The Clarus program shall determine its redistribution of shared data from contributors based upon Clarus Data Sharing Agreements.

requirement: The Clarus program shall report successes and failures in data and information transmission to its contributors.

requirement: The contributors shall report periods of data and information outages to the Clarus program.

requirement: The Clarus program shall maintain information about requestors and their access to data and information.

requirement: The Clarus Initiative Management Team shall review and amend data and information sharing and use policies.

requirement: The Clarus Initiative Management Team shall specify the general frequency of policy meetings.

requirement: The Clarus Initiative Coordinating Committee shall nominate technical expert(s) to participate on technical working groups.
ASR_Explanation: This is an organizational governance process and does not impose any constraints on the system’s architecture.

requirement: The requestors shall adhere to the intellectual property requirements of the Clarus User Agreement.
ASR_Explanation: This is a legal compliance requirement with no direct impact on the system’s architectural structure or design.

requirement: The contributors shall adhere to the intellectual property requirements of the Clarus Data Sharing Agreement.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes legal compliance constraints requiring integrated rights‐management and access‐control components in the system architecture.

requirement: The contributors shall have the right to use the Clarus system data and information for purposes delineated within the Clarus Data Sharing Agreement.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security and data‐access architecture by requiring enforcement of usage permissions and auditing controls per the sharing agreement.

requirement: The requestors shall have the right to use the Clarus system data and information for purposes delineated within the Clarus User Agreement.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates incorporating a usage-rights enforcement and access-control policy layer into the system architecture.

requirement: The Clarus program shall have the right to reject the use of data and information provided by the contributors when deemed appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a data validation and governance mechanism in the ingestion pipeline, shaping the modular design and dataflow architecture.

requirement: The Clarus program shall inform contributors of the policies, processes and procedures employed to reject data and information provided by the contributors.
ASR_Explanation: This is a user information/UI requirement for displaying policy content and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The contributors shall provide the Clarus program with a timely notice of their intent to change, alter, replace, or eliminate any shared data and information as specified within this Clarus Data Sharing Agreement.
ASR_Explanation: This is a data‐governance policy requirement and does not impose changes on the system’s architecture or design.

requirement: Any reference in the Clarus Data Sharing Agreement to statutes, regulations and rules shall be a reference to the amended, substituted, replaced or re-enacted statute, regulations and rules.
ASR_Explanation: This is a legal compliance detail about contract wording and does not affect system structure, behavior, or design.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide to contributors the limitation of liability for contributing environmental data and metadata.
ASR_Explanation: This is a legal/policy requirement with no impact on system structure, components, or technical design choices.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide to requesters the limitation of liability for using environmental data and metadata.
ASR_Explanation: This is a legal/content disclaimer requirement with minimal impact on system structure or core architectural decisions.

requirement: The Clarus program shall maintain continuous 24x7 operations.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives high-availability architecture decisions including redundancy, failover, monitoring, and infrastructure design.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide an environment that has uninterruptible power for the Clarus system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s deployment and infrastructure architecture by mandating uninterruptible power for high availability and fault tolerance.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide an environment that has redundant communication for the Clarus system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architectural design for network topology and fault‐tolerant communication channels to ensure high availability.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide network management tools.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the inclusion of dedicated management modules, protocols, and monitoring infrastructure, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide setup support.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide for customer service.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide a trained support staff.

requirement: The Clarus program shall define quality checking rules for environmental observations.

requirement: The Clarus program shall define data retention standards.
ASR_Explanation: Defining data retention standards impacts storage architecture, data lifecycle management, archival/purge processes, and compliance mechanisms.

requirement: The Clarus program shall provide documentation of Clarus standards.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement pertains to project documentation delivery rather than influencing system structure or design.

requirement: The Clarus program standards shall accommodate contributions of new sensor technologies to the Clarus system.

requirement: The Clarus program standards shall support multiple methods of data delivery to users.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a flexible, pluggable delivery layer and impacts system interfaces, protocols, and modularity.

requirement: The Clarus program shall maintain a comprehensive Clarus system test environment.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the deployment architecture by driving requirements for infrastructure provisioning, environment isolation, and configuration management.

requirement: The Clarus program shall test all software changes in the designated test environment before deployment to production.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement governs the testing and deployment process rather than influencing the system’s structural or behavioral architecture.

requirement: The Clarus program shall test all hardware changes in the designated test environment before deployment to production.
ASR_Explanation: This describes a QA process requirement rather than a constraint or driver affecting the system’s component structure, interfaces, or overall design.

requirement: The Clarus program shall operate the Clarus system according to its published IT Security Plan.
ASR_Explanation: This security NFR imposes constraints that will drive the system’s security architecture, access controls, and compliance mechanisms.

requirement: The Clarus program shall operate according to Government IT security requirements as outlined in OMB Circular A-130, Management of Federal Information Resources, Appendix III, Security of Federal Automated Information Resources.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates compliance with federal security controls, shaping the system’s overall security architecture and design decisions.

requirement: The Clarus program shall operate according to Government IT security requirements as outlined in the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Guidelines, Departmental Information Resource Management Manual, and associated guidelines.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions on security controls, compliance frameworks, and system design to meet NIST and government mandates.

requirement: The Clarus program shall operate according to Government IT security requirements as outlined in U.S. DOT Order 1630.2B, Personnel Security Management.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR will drive key security architecture decisions, enforcing specific access controls, encryption, and compliance mechanisms per DOT standards.

requirement: Weather service providers shall be able to use Clarus data to provide localized special weather products.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data ingestion and processing architecture for integrating Clarus data and generating localized weather products.

requirement: Public agency maintenance and construction personnel shall be able to use the Clarus system to obtain environmental conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This standard functional capability doesn’t introduce significant architectural constraints or drive key design decisions.

requirement: Rail system personnel shall be able to use the Clarus system to obtain environmental conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This FR impacts the integration architecture by requiring external interfaces and data flows for real-time environmental sensor acquisition.

requirement: Traffic management personnel shall be able to use the Clarus system to obtain environmental conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional data‐retrieval feature with no specified quality attributes or cross-cutting concerns driving major architectural decisions.

requirement: Transit personnel shall be able to use the Clarus system to obtain environmental conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional requirement without specific constraints or quality attributes that would drive architectural decisions.

requirement: The freight community shall be able to use the Clarus system to obtain environmental conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional capability for data retrieval with no major impact on core architectural components.

requirement: Emergency management and public safety personnel shall be able to use the Clarus system to obtain environmental conditions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional requirement to retrieve environmental data and does not impose new structural or quality-attribute constraints on the system.

requirement: The Clarus program shall maintain information about data providers.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward data‐management requirement (basic CRUD) with minimal impact on high‐level architecture.

requirement: The Clarus program shall maintain metadata about each data provider's network.
ASR_Explanation: Maintaining per-provider network metadata drives the design of a metadata repository, storage schema, and access services, impacting the overall data architecture.

requirement: The Clarus program shall maintain information about data provider redistribution restrictions.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the data model and access control architecture to ensure provider-specific redistribution restrictions are stored and enforced.

requirement: The Clarus program shall maintain information about service providers.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD/data‐model requirement and does not drive any high‐level architectural decisions.

requirement: The Clarus program shall maintain information about service provider communications.
ASR_Explanation: It influences the design of the data persistence layer and communication logging/audit mechanisms in the system’s architecture.

requirement: The Clarus program shall maintain information about service provider access to data.
ASR_Explanation: It requires an audit/logging subsystem for tracking service provider data access, impacting the security, data storage, and system performance architecture.

requirement: The Clarus program shall allow potential weather element data providers to request permission to submit weather information.
ASR_Explanation: This is an onboarding/business logic requirement with impact confined to standard authentication/authorization modules rather than driving core architectural decisions.

requirement: The Clarus system shall accept data only from sources which data sharing agreements have been established.
ASR_Explanation: It requires adding a governance and enforcement layer in the data‐ingestion architecture to validate and authorize sources based on established agreements.

requirement: The user can split only one document at a time.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple functional constraint with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: The compression of the output files requires pdf version 1.5 or above.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the choice and integration of a PDF generation library/version, impacting technology stack and system design.

requirement: The number of digits that can be used for the unique [FILENUMBER] prefix must be 10 or less.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement only affects input validation and data storage constraints, not the overall system architecture.

requirement: Pages number in the page selection must be comprehended.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level clarity requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The compression of the output files requires pdf version 1.5 or above.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the PDF output format to version 1.5+, impacting the choice and configuration of the document generation library in the system architecture.

requirement: The user can mix only 2 documents at a time (not more or less).
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple business rule enforceable in application logic with minimal or no impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The compression of the output files requires pdf version 1.5 or above.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level file format version constraint affecting library choice but not the overall system architecture.

requirement: The number of digits that can be used for the unique [FILENUMBER] prefix must be 10 or less.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed field constraint with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The compression of the output files requires pdf version 1.5 or above.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint impacts the PDF generation component and technology choice by requiring libraries that support PDF version 1.5+ compression features.

requirement: The user can manipulate only 1 document at a time (not more).
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level functional/UI constraint with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Operation system on the computer system.
ASR_Explanation: The choice of operating system dictates platform services, hardware abstractions, and runtime constraints, significantly impacting system architecture.

requirement: Log level must not be OFF (in the Settings panel) in order to work this function.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level configuration constraint for log validation and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: Restart PDFsam application to have the changes applied.
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational behavior requirement with no impact on high-level system structure or architectural design.

requirement: PDFsam is platform independent and it runs on every platform where a Java Virtual Machine is available (requires a JVM version 1.6 or higher).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the choice of a Java/JVM-based architecture and impacts module packaging, dependencies, and cross‐platform deployment.

requirement: Maximum memory usage: 254MB (by default).
ASR_Explanation: Imposes a strict memory footprint constraint that will drive technology, data structure, and caching/memory management decisions.

requirement: Disk space needed: 13.9MB.
ASR_Explanation: The disk space requirement of 13.9MB is trivial and unlikely to influence architectural decisions.

requirement: PDFsam is written in Java and its user interface is written with Java Swing.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying Java and Swing as the technology stack directly shapes platform compatibility, UI architecture, and overall system integration.

requirement: PDFsam is GNU GPLv2 licensed and the PDFsam-console is dual licensed (GPLv2 and LGPLv2).
ASR_Explanation: The GPL/LGPL licensing terms constrain component integration, linking strategies, and distribution packaging, shaping module boundaries and overall system architecture.

requirement: A working Java Runtime Environment version 1.6 or above is necessary.
ASR_Explanation: It specifies a runtime platform constraint, influencing deployment environment and component compatibility.

requirement: The system can check for updates whenever is requested by the user, or automatically when opening the application.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing an update-management service with startup integration, user-triggered workflows, and network/background task considerations.

requirement: PDFsam is designed not to delay the system from other key processes. The response time of the program is direct and the application is considered real-time.
ASR_Explanation: This real-time performance requirement drives architectural decisions around concurrency, resource management, and scheduling to ensure low-latency operation.

requirement: The application must ensure that it leaves untouched the input pdf files. No modification is allowed to these files.
ASR_Explanation: This immutability constraint impacts the file‐processing architecture by requiring read-only access and copy-on-write strategies to preserve original PDFs.

requirement: The application should function even in cases of wrong data insertion or wrong settings. In case of error it should provide users with appropriate help messages.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of cross-cutting error-handling, input-validation, and user-feedback mechanisms affecting the system’s resilience and messaging layers.

requirement: PDFsam does not introduce any security level, since the data it manages is not critical. The users in the application are all equal so there is not the need of any identity management level.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement removes any need for authentication/authorization layers, directly impacting the system’s security architecture.

requirement: This application provides a pleasant and user friendly graphical interface with relatively simple functions. Any user should be able to use PDFsam without any specific knowledge or experience by reading the user manuals (or the help messages that are embedded in the application).
ASR_Explanation: This usability requirement drives the choice of UI frameworks, architectural patterns and the embedding of context-sensitive help features in the GUI layer.

requirement: The project is released under the GNU General Public License. The philosophy of this license implies some basic principles which apply to the project.
ASR_Explanation: This license constraint impacts architectural decisions around dependency selection, module boundaries, and distribution mechanisms.

requirement: The freedom to run the program, for any purpose.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement addresses licensing freedom rather than influencing structural or quality attributes of the system.

requirement: The freedom to study how the program works, and adapt it to your needs. Access to the source code is a precondition for this.
ASR_Explanation: This is a licensing/distribution requirement and does not drive core architectural structure or design decisions.

requirement: The freedom to redistribute copies so you can help your neighbor.
ASR_Explanation: This is a licensing/distribution requirement and does not drive or constrain the software’s structural or technical architecture.

requirement: The freedom to improve the program, and release your improvements to the public, so that the whole community benefits. Access to the source code is a precondition for this.
ASR_Explanation: This is a licensing/policy requirement around source code availability, not a technical design constraint.

requirement: The interface between the CMIB, MCCC, and CPCC shall be Ethernet (IEEE 802.3 compliant) of 100 Mbits/sec or better data rate.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement constrains the network interface protocol and bandwidth, directly impacting component interconnection, hardware selection, and system performance architecture.

requirement: The interface shall be transformer coupled copper twisted pair unless other materials are required for noise (RFI), ground isolation, or physical layout constraints (long distances).
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the physical-layer cabling, EMI/ground isolation, and hardware interconnect architecture and layout.

requirement: Network switches shall be employed to distribute traffic within a correlator rack and where their use will significantly reduce overall network wiring complexity.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the network topology and cabling design within the correlator rack, impacting overall system scalability and maintainability.

requirement: The MCCC-CMIB, MCCC-CPCC, and MCCC-EVLA M&C networks shall be on separate physical interfaces.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the physical network interface allocation and segmentation strategy, impacting hardware architecture and security zoning.

requirement: There shall be a redundant communication path (serial RS-232c or equivalent) between the MCCC and CPCC to provide for remote reboot in the event of a networking or computing failure.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates hardware-level redundancy for fault-tolerant communication and remote reboot capability, impacting system availability and network architecture.

requirement: The interface between the MCCC and external networks (EVLA M&C) shall be Ethernet (IEEE 802.3 compliant) of 100 Mbits/sec or better data rate.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the network interface technology and bandwidth, impacting hardware selection and overall system communication architecture.

requirement: Pathways penetrating the correlator shielded room shall be fiber optic or other low RFI material to meet RFI specifications.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates material and pathway design for RFI compliance, directly impacting the physical enclosure and interface architecture.

requirement: Network routers/switches shall be employed at the MCCC-EVLA M&C interface level (or higher) to protect the MCCC from unauthorized access and irrelevant network traffic.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates network segmentation and boundary controls, directly shaping the system’s security and network architecture.

requirement: The CMIB daughter board shall communicate with the correlator carrier boards via either the PCI or ISA busses. Alternative communication paths may be through a serial or parallel connection as required.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the hardware communication interfaces (PCI, ISA, serial, parallel), shaping the system’s bus architecture and data pathways.

requirement: The CMIB shall be capable of reading a 16-bit identifier from the host correlator board. This identifier will be used to form a unique IP address for CMIB network addressing and allow carry over IP addressing for hot swap modules.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement defines the network addressing scheme and module identification mechanism, impacting system network architecture and hot-swap design.

requirement: The CMIB shall be able to read back the contents of all writeable hardware control registers where meaningful. It is desired that the state of the correlator hardware be available through interrogation across the CMIB bus for monitoring and fault tolerance.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts the design of the CMIB bus protocol and hardware–software interface to support register interrogation for monitoring and fault tolerance.

requirement: The CMIB shall have control of hardware “warm boots” such that an external command from the MCCC to reboot the CMIB shall have an option to force a hardware warm boot.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of low-level hardware control and bootloader interfaces, requiring architectural support for forced warm-reboot commands.

requirement: The carrier board for the CMIB shall have an externally visible indicator (LED or other) that will provide a user with a physical indication of CMIB operational status (red = fault, green = ok).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the hardware architecture by requiring board layout accommodations, component selection, and integration for status indication.

requirement: Where applicable, all computers and peripherals shall be powered though UPS type devices with sufficient capacity for the computers to safely coordinate a system wide shutdown of the correlator hardware in the event of a prolonged power outage. The UPS devices need the ability to signal the CMCS when a power outage has occurred and keep the CMCS apprised of time remaining on backup power.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the deployment and infrastructure architecture by requiring UPS integration and signaling interfaces for high availability and coordinated shutdown.

requirement: All computers within the CMCS system shall have the ability for authorized users to directly access individual systems for maintenance and monitoring through remote logins.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of secure remote access infrastructure, authentication services, and network segmentation.

requirement: Each computer system in the CMCS shall have a hardware based watchdog timer configured to reboot the system in the case of a system hang. Reboots should result in minimal system interruptions with the offending CPU reconfiguring and returning to service autonomously.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the fault-tolerance and high-availability architecture by requiring hardware watchdog integration and autonomous CPU recovery mechanisms.

requirement: The CMIB shall conform to both electrical and physical PC104+ standards.
ASR_Explanation: It constrains the hardware form factor and electrical interface, impacting board layout and mechanical integration.

requirement: The CMIB shall contain 64 Mbytes or greater of SDRAM, IDE hard disk interface, minimum of one serial and one parallel interface, PCI/ISA buses, 100BaseT network interface, capacity to boot and run a generic COTS operating system in a near real-time environment from local non-volatile storage.
ASR_Explanation: This hardware specification establishes critical platform, bus, network and real-time performance constraints that will drive core architectural decisions.

requirement: The operating system/module combination shall be capable of supporting the real-time requirements of the correlator hardware, hardware monitor/control/diagnostics with support for standalone “test bench” operation with simulated control data generation, and the ability to access and upgrade correlator hardware PLD/FPGA personalities through its network connection.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the choice of real-time OS, scheduling and I/O frameworks, standalone simulation support, and network-based FPGA upgrade architecture.

requirement: The MCCC shall be a high availability type general-purpose computer capable of supporting multiple Ethernet interfaces, COTS operating systems, and support server/host services for the CMIB operating system. This computer may exist as a hot swappable or redundant CPU device capable of self-healing where possible.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates high-availability, redundancy, modular hardware design, and multi-OS/interface support that directly influence the system’s architecture.

requirement: The MCCC shall have all required disk and file system facilities installed locally such that the system can boot and run in a stand-alone configuration. This should allow the correlator CMIBs to boot, configure, and run without any communication outside of the correlator M&C network.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates local disk and file system infrastructure for standalone boot and operation, impacting deployment topology and subsystem design.

requirement: The CPCC shall be a high availability type general-purpose computer capable of supporting a COTS operating system and have the ability to accept a large number of external hardware status signals (power, temp, etc) either directly or through external interface hardware. This computer may exist as a hot swappable or redundant CPU device capable of self-healing where possible.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates hardware architecture choices for high availability, redundancy, hot-swapping, self-healing, and external interface capabilities.

requirement: The CPCC shall have all required disk and file system facilities installed locally such that the system can boot and run in a stand-alone configuration. This requirement is to allow correlator power monitoring and control to continue in the event of an M&C network failure.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates local disk and filesystem support for standalone boot and operation, impacting the system’s storage topology and failover architecture.

requirement: The CMCS processors shall be capable of meeting all data processing deadlines and anticipated future requirements.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes performance and scalability constraints affecting hardware selection, processing architecture, and capacity planning.

requirement: The CMCS processors shall be capable of responding to correlator hardware inputs (interrupts) in a deterministic fashion with sufficient performance to avoid data loss, corruption or overflows.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the real-time architecture by driving processor/OS selection, interrupt-handling design, and scheduling to guarantee deterministic performance.

requirement: All lower system error and debug messages shall be present at the MCCC layer. Aside from a networking or CPU failure, It should never be necessary to directly access a CPU to display error messages.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement enforces a centralized logging and error‐handling layer at the MCCC, impacting system layering and inter‐module communication design.

requirement: All system error and debug messages shall be categorized in a logical fashion such that message traffic can be filtered as to content, detail, and message rate. Personnel interested in error messages should be able to easily filter the error message stream.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the logging and monitoring architecture by requiring structured message categorization, filtering capabilities, and rate control.

requirement: All messages passed between CMCS system layers shall have both UTC and wall clock time stamp information appropriate for the message type. Error messages will be stamped with their discovery time, control messages will be stamped with their generation time. Other message internal time stamps can be used as monitor/control parameters as deemed necessary.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes cross-cutting changes to message schemas, middleware, and time‐synchronization services across all system layers.

requirement: Software shall be provided that allows an authorized user full access to all messaging, monitor, and control traffic throughout the CMCS. This software will provide full system access for testing, debugging, and control while the correlator is off line or under the control of the EVLA M&C system.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of a system‐wide control and monitoring interface, impacting security, module boundaries, and communication protocols.

requirement: A Graphical User Interface shall be provided as an interface to the CMCS test software that allows for a convenient and configurable tool to access the CMCS remotely through the VCI.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the introduction of a client–server GUI layer and remote communication protocols, impacting high-level system architecture.

requirement: The CMCS shall be self-monitoring. It will be capable of detecting, reporting on and automatically taking action to remedy or lessen the impact of, at a minimum, the following types of abnormal conditions: processor hardware failure, operating system hangs or crashes, temperature or voltage deviations, computational performance below minimum specifications, computational error rates above maximum specification, internal communications failures, and external (with the EVLA M&C) communications disruptions.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives inclusion of dedicated monitoring, fault-detection, and self-healing components, impacting the system’s reliability and fault-tolerance architecture.

requirement: The software part of the system shall be able to perform without total system restart due to internal failure between system maintenance windows.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the need for a fault-tolerant, high-availability architecture with modular isolation and self-healing to avoid full system restarts.

requirement: The hardware part of the system shall be able to perform indefinitely without complete loss of service, except in the event of total failure of primary and backup power.
ASR_Explanation: This high-availability, fault-tolerance requirement drives architectural decisions around redundant hardware, power backup systems, and failover mechanisms.

requirement: The system shall be able to continue processing of all correlator configuration/control events until the queues of parameters are exhausted and external communications are restored.
ASR_Explanation: This drives architectural decisions around durable message queuing, retry logic, and offline processing for fault tolerance and resiliency.

requirement: The system shall be able to sit at idle and resume operations with minimal (amount TBD) delay.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR impacts the architecture by requiring state management and resource allocation strategies to ensure rapid resume and meet performance targets.

requirement: All system processing and interconnect hardware shall be readily accessible for maintenance, repair, replacement and/or reconfiguration. This excludes items that due to their physical location, are not practical to configure for ready access (i.e. backplanes).
ASR_Explanation: This maintenance/accessibility constraint drives hardware packaging and component placement decisions, impacting the physical architecture for serviceability.

requirement: All systems and application source code shall be available to or on the systems that execute it.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement governs code distribution and deployment practices but does not drive changes in system structure, interfaces, or runtime behavior.

requirement: All software application modules shall be debuggable. They should be organized such that all inputs and outputs can be simulated if necessary.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the modular design and interface architecture by mandating instrumentation, simulation hooks, and test harness support for debuggability.

requirement: All software processes shall be killable, restartable, debuggable and testable with minimal impact on normal system operations.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of process lifecycle management, fault-tolerance, observability, and deployment architecture.

requirement: Software tools and pre-built applications that do not have source code available shall come with a complete diagnostic package and customer support.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR mandates integrating diagnostic packages and support hooks for black-box components, impacting the system’s logging/monitoring architecture.

requirement: Operating system software shall either have source code available or come with sufficient diagnostics and customer support.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the choice of operating system platform and impacts maintainability, diagnostics integration, and supportability architecture.

requirement: I/O, communications, and processing hardware shall be easily expandable, reconfigurable, augmentable and replaceable to meet increasing data traffic and processing demands imposed by EVLA science, Correlator changes, and availability of new hardware.
ASR_Explanation: This drives the need for a modular, scalable hardware architecture with standard interfaces to support future expansion, reconfiguration, and replacement.

requirement: Scalability shall be accomplished in manner that is transparent to processing, communications and I/O software functions with the possible exception of recompilation of executables.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives core architectural decisions around modularization, layering, and transparent scaling across processing, communications, and I/O components.

requirement: Scalability shall be accomplished in a manner that is seamless, in that it does not affect hardware modules or software functionality that it meets at interfaces.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around modular interfaces and scaling patterns to ensure seamless growth without impacting existing hardware or software functionality.

requirement: All users of the system shall login using some form of unique identification. (e.g., username and password)
ASR_Explanation: It drives the inclusion of an authentication module, secure credential storage, and identity management integration, impacting the system’s security architecture.

requirement: All login attempts shall be done in a secure manner. (e.g., encrypted passwords)
ASR_Explanation: This security requirement drives architectural decisions around authentication mechanisms, encryption protocols, and security module integration.

requirement: A system administrator shall have unrestricted access to all aspects of the system.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the security architecture design by requiring a robust role-based access control and privilege management framework.

requirement: Each user shall have a set of system access properties that defines the user’s privileges within the system. (e.g., the subsystems a user may control or system tools the user may access).
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of the authentication/authorization layer and impacts module boundaries, data flows, and policy enforcement across the system.

requirement: The administrator shall have the ability to create and add a new user to the system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic CRUD operation with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The administrator shall have the ability to remove a user from the system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD operation with minimal impact on high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The administrator shall have the ability to edit a user’s system access properties.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD capability for administrative users and does not drive any high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The administrator shall have the ability to block all access to the system for all users or selectively by user. (All blocked users with active sessions shall automatically be logged off.)
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s authentication/authorization architecture by requiring real-time session invalidation and dynamic access control.

requirement: The system shall continue operations, although not necessarily at full capacity, on all unaffected resources during partial shutdowns for maintenance, repair and/or upgrade.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the high-availability architecture by requiring redundancy, resource isolation, and graceful degradation strategies to support partial shutdowns.

requirement: Modular design principles shall be employed to the maximum extent possible. Maximal practical use of available “hot-swappable” devices and components shall be made.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts system decomposition and interface architecture to enable modularity and dynamic hot-swapping of components.

requirement: Complete and comprehensible hardware systems specifications and configuration information shall be readily available.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation and maintenance requirement and does not drive the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: Software system and application code shall be well documented and written in a generally familiar language or languages (preferably not more than two). Software shall be written in a style that is easily readable and using practices that allow for minimal confusion.
ASR_Explanation: This is a coding style and documentation guideline with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: The System shall provide the capability to operate in an Open Mode, within which authorized customers shall be allowed read-only access to requested Data Products. This is the nominal system mode.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the security and operational‐mode architecture by defining role‐based authentication/authorization and read‐only data access patterns.

requirement: The System shall provide the capability to operate in a Degraded System Mode, within which the system generates a subset of products based upon priority and availability of system resources.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the runtime architecture by mandating a degraded‐mode operational layer with resource monitoring and priority‐based processing.

requirement: The System shall provide the capability to notify customers upon the NPOESS Ground System entering and leaving a Degraded Operations Mode.
ASR_Explanation: It requires an event-driven notification subsystem and messaging infrastructure, impacting system interfaces, middleware and reliability design.

requirement: The System shall provide the capability to operate in a Restricted Access Mode, within which only users meeting certain criteria will be authorized access to the data. Criteria will be defined in the TBD Data Denial Plan.
ASR_Explanation: This requires extending the security architecture with a configurable restricted‐access mode and policy‐based authorization enforcement components.
