requirement: Pontis 5.0 will, at AASHTO’s direction, adopt any elements of the look and feel of other existing AASHTOWare/BRIDGEWare applications where appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the choice and integration of UI frameworks and component libraries to ensure consistent theming and design across applications.

requirement: There will be no support for individual personalization of the user interface (skins) other than the availability of certain features that are tied to a user role.
ASR_Explanation: This UI-level theming constraint only affects the presentation layer and does not drive significant architectural structure.

requirement: Pontis 5.0 will be designed to be as straightforward and intuitive as possible, and familiar to existing users. The user interface will provide effective navigation, visual referencing, and task sequencing. Users will have access to on-line help throughout the UI.
ASR_Explanation: This usability-focused UI requirement drives design refinements but does not impose significant architectural structure or core system constraints.

requirement: The application should look and feel consistent in the web and standalone operating environments, therefore it will conform to standard .NET standalone and web application conventions with necessary accommodations for the deployment environment only where these prove necessary and appropriate.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates cross‐platform UI architecture and framework choices to ensure consistent theming, code reuse, and deployment adaptations across .NET web and standalone environments.

requirement: As a general goal, Pontis users should be comfortable operating and using the software after two full days of training. Users who only wish to access and display bridge information should be comfortable operating and using the software after two hours of training. System administrators should be comfortable operating and maintaining the software after user training and one full day of system administration training.
ASR_Explanation: This usability constraint drives key UI/UX architectural decisions to ensure intuitive workflows and minimize training time.

requirement: The system developer shall consider supporting existing custom forms and reports developed in PowerBuilder 9. It shall decide on a development tool for users so that they can continue to customize Pontis and extend its capability.
ASR_Explanation: This drives tool selection and legacy PowerBuilder integration, shaping the technology stack, extension architecture, and integration patterns.

requirement: Pontis 5.0 repository must be able to store at least 50,000 bridges, 30 inspections per bridge, 50 work recommendations per bridge on average, 20 scenarios, 50,000 projects, 150,000 project details, 500 registered users, 25 simultaneous web read-write users, 10 standalone read-write users, 150 simultaneous view-only users, and 2 batch (offline processing) users.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives key architectural decisions around database sizing, indexing, storage capacity, and concurrency scaling.

requirement: Pontis 5.0 shall achieve 98 percent up time during normal times of operation, available for use 18 hours per day, 353 days per year, reserving 6 hours per day and one day per month for routine agency network and infrastructure maintenance.
ASR_Explanation: This high-availability requirement drives architectural decisions around redundancy, fault tolerance, maintenance scheduling, and infrastructure provisioning.

requirement: Pontis 5.0 shall comply with AASHTO application development standards based on the published recommendations of the AASHTO Technical Applications and Architecture (TA&A) subcommittee.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes an external architectural constraint that shapes the system’s structure, design patterns, and technology choices to align with AASHTO standards.

requirement: All source code written by the contractor will be provided to AASHTO, and reasonable effort will be made to develop clearly documented source code which can be maintained after contract by other than the original developers.
ASR_Explanation: This is a contractual/code‐delivery and documentation requirement, not one that impacts system structure or architectural design.

requirement: All modules developed in Pontis 5.0 will support automatic testing except as explicitly distinguished in the technical design. Every module will be delivered with a testing driver module.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the module architecture by mandating built-in test harnesses and design-for-testability interfaces.

requirement: Pontis 5.0 in production is intended to be supported through complete and sufficient on-line help and documentation and equivalent printed documentation, with occasional additional assistance provided through the Pontis Support Center. Pontis 5.0 will also continue to provide field-level help where applicable.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation and support requirement that does not drive core system structure or architectural decisions.

requirement: Pontis 5.0 security is a mix of database security and application security. Overall security is provided by the database and the Pontis 5.0 user authentication mechanisms with more refined security over program capabilities being addressed by the application.
ASR_Explanation: This security NFR drives the overall architecture by mandating authentication modules, database security layers, and fine-grained access controls.

requirement: Authentication will utilize a single sign-on (SSO) approach and application security will be declaratively managed through database or directory entries, rather than hard-wired into the application.
ASR_Explanation: This defines the core security architecture—driving identity provider integration, authorization model and configuration management.

requirement: Database security will need to be insured for the Pontis data stored in the RDBMS; most likely this will be done by using a specific username/password combination and running as particular database roles for each database connection.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the overall system architecture by dictating authentication methods, role-based access control, and secure connection management for the database.

requirement: The application level security controls will be consistent with current Pontis 4.x procedures, except more comprehensive; no exceptional controls are required. A specific requirement addresses field-level data security.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring comprehensive, field-level controls integrated with existing Pontis 4.x procedures.

requirement: For normal users, the configurability of the system via the interface will be limited to changing the parameters used to perform the various analyses and modeling functions. Access to all other configuration parameters, such as module access, will be restricted to Pontis super users or application administrators.
ASR_Explanation: Impacts system architecture by mandating role-based access control and segregation of configuration modules for normal versus super users.

requirement: There are no specific privacy or immunity (antivirus) requirements for Pontis 5.0 identified at this time.
ASR_Explanation: Because the absence of specific privacy or antivirus requirements does not drive any architectural decisions.

requirement: The product likely will be designed for agency/international localization from the ground up with respect to user interface elements, reports, messages, and other application attributes where native language support is necessary.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives core architecture decisions for UI abstraction, resource management, and data handling to support internationalization.

requirement: The system shall be capable of exporting the NBI data in the specified NBI format as outlined in the NBI guideline. The system shall be capable supporting the future NBI coding guide changes.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of a flexible, extensible export module and versioning strategy for evolving NBI format specifications.

requirement: GParted must provide a boot menu with options for loading to RAM only, safe graphic settings, failsafe mode, booting the computer’s operating system, and running RAM tests using Memtest86+.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the bootloader architecture by mandating configurable menu entries, RAM-only loading, safe/failsafe modes, OS chainloading, and Memtest86+ integration.

requirement: GParted must support selection of different keymaps (keyboard layouts) during startup.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI/input feature requiring minor changes to startup configuration and input handling modules without impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: GParted must provide a language selection menu with a wide variety of supported languages.
ASR_Explanation: Requires architectural support for internationalization, including resource bundles, dynamic locale loading, and UI abstraction for multiple languages.

requirement: GParted must provide a desktop interface with access to all program functions, including exit, screenshot, terminal, main GParted window, information, and screen resolution application.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional requirement that can be handled within the existing interface module without affecting the overall architecture.

requirement: GParted must provide a main window from which all supported actions can be performed on selected drives.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level requirement for the presence of a main window and does not impose core architectural constraints.

requirement: GParted must allow refreshing the list of connected devices to recognize new devices added after boot.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized functional detail (dynamic device polling) that can be added without altering the overall system architecture.

requirement: GParted must support undoing and applying pending operations, including undo last operation, clear all operations, and apply all operations.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a command-history mechanism and state management subsystem, affecting module boundaries and operation execution architecture.

requirement: GParted must allow viewing detailed information about the selected device, including model, size, heads, sectors, and cylinders.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI/data‐retrieval enhancement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: GParted must allow creating a partition table on a selected drive, supporting a wide variety of partition table types (default: msdos), and warn that all data will be erased.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates an extensible partition management layer (e.g., pluggable drivers or strategy pattern) to support multiple table formats and integrate data-loss warnings.

requirement: GParted must allow creating a new partition on a selected device, specifying size, position, partition type (Primary, Logical, Extended), file system type, and label.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional requirement that does not impose high‐level structural or cross‐cutting architectural constraints.

requirement: GParted must allow deleting a selected partition.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI-driven operation that can be handled by existing partition‐management components without impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: GParted must allow resizing or moving a selected partition, specifying new size and position.
ASR_Explanation: This is a core functional requirement that can be implemented within the existing partition-management component without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: GParted must allow copying a selected partition to the clipboard for cloning purposes.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized feature change that won’t require fundamental shifts in the overall system architecture.

requirement: GParted must allow pasting a copied partition to unallocated space or over an existing partition, with appropriate warnings about data loss.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed feature-level requirement (UI/UX and partition operation behavior) that doesn’t drive high-level architectural structure.

requirement: GParted must allow formatting a selected partition to a supported file system.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic functional feature affecting only the partitioning/formatting module without driving high-level architectural changes.

requirement: GParted must allow unmounting a mounted partition.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional requirement handled within the existing partition‐management module without altering the system’s high‐level architecture.

requirement: GParted must allow managing partition flags (e.g., bootable, hidden) for a selected partition, with a dialog showing available flags.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized UI and partition-management feature that doesn’t drive changes to the overall system architecture.

requirement: GParted must allow checking and repairing the file system of a selected partition.
ASR_Explanation: It requires integrating file‐system‐specific check/repair modules and privileged OS operations, affecting component boundaries and service interactions.

requirement: GParted must allow labeling a partition, either during creation or later, if the file system supports labeling.
ASR_Explanation: Only minor UI and filesystem labeling command integration is needed, with no fundamental architectural changes.

requirement: GParted must allow taking a screenshot of the desktop or an active window, saving the screenshot to a file.
ASR_Explanation: The screenshot feature is a self-contained UI function using existing windowing APIs and does not impact the core system architecture.

requirement: GParted must provide a terminal utility for advanced operations, including mounting partitions and copying screenshots to devices.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing a terminal utility for partition mounting and screenshot copying mandates new CLI modules, OS-level device integration, and security boundary considerations.

requirement: GParted must provide an information dialog with a list of installed packages and a guide on resizing Windows partitions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI information dialog feature and does not impact the overall system architecture.

requirement: GParted must provide a screen resolution changer, allowing selection of resolution, rotation, and layout for multiple monitors.
ASR_Explanation: Adding multi-monitor resolution, rotation, and layout controls requires new modules and OS-level display service integration, impacting the overall system architecture.

requirement: GParted must display date and time on the desktop taskbar, reading settings from the computer’s BIOS.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: GParted must provide a main menu interface with menu bar options (GParted, Edit, View, Device, Partition) and a toolbar for common functions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI functional requirement and does not influence the system’s high-level architectural structure.

requirement: GParted must display a confirmation dialog warning about potential data loss before applying any changes to partitions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level confirmation behavior and does not drive or alter the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: GParted must allow pasting a copied partition onto an existing partition, with a warning about data loss.
ASR_Explanation: This is a localized functional/UI enhancement that does not affect the system’s high-level architecture or module structure.

requirement: GParted must allow the user to exit the application at any time, with options to reboot, shutdown, or logout.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a standard UI exit flow with system API calls and does not impose significant architectural changes.

requirement: GParted must run on x86-based computers using GNU/Linux, and can be used on other operating systems by booting from GParted Live media.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes platform and deployment constraints—requiring support for x86/Linux environments and a bootable live-media solution, which directly affects system architecture and distribution.

requirement: GParted must not require a powerful processor, graphics card, much RAM, or disk space, and should load and run quickly on most systems.
ASR_Explanation: It drives decisions on lightweight frameworks, memory management, and startup optimizations to ensure low footprint and fast load on diverse hardware.

requirement: GParted must require a CD/DVD drive or USB port for booting the Live version.
ASR_Explanation: This is a deployment constraint on boot media (packaging/distribution) and does not drive core architectural design.

requirement: GParted must support hard disks, USB flash drives, external hard drives, IDE, SATA, RAID controllers, and attached devices.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of the hardware abstraction/driver framework and device detection architecture.

requirement: GParted must depend on certain libraries and tools (e.g., Parted >= 1.7.1, Gtkmm >= 2.8.x, file system tools) for its operation.
ASR_Explanation: This dependency requirement constrains the technology stack and impacts module integration, compatibility, and deployment architecture.

requirement: GParted must not support logical volume management (LVM2) at present.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a constraint to exclude LVM2 support modules, shaping component responsibilities and system dependencies.

requirement: GParted must provide online and third-party documentation and tutorials.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement pertains to documentation and content delivery without affecting the core system’s architecture or structural design.

requirement: GParted must require a working Linux environment for the install version and a working CD/DVD drive for the Live version.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes specific platform and hardware constraints that will drive packaging, deployment infrastructure, and boot architecture decisions.

requirement: GParted must warn users that data loss is a serious possibility and recommend backing up data and using the program with caution.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX notification requirement and does not impose any changes on the system’s architectural structure.

requirement: GParted must not provide any security or privacy features, as it does not run from within an operating system and all users have root access.
ASR_Explanation: It fundamentally dictates the absence of any security or privacy layers, shaping component selection and trust‐boundary design.

requirement: GParted must provide a user-friendly interface accessible from the menu bar and toolbar.
ASR_Explanation: This is a presentation‐layer usability detail with no impact on the system’s high‐level architectural structure.

requirement: GParted must be interoperable, running on both Mac and PC (Linux, Windows, or other operating systems).
ASR_Explanation: It requires cross-platform portability, impacting choice of frameworks, abstraction layers, and build/deployment architecture.

requirement: GParted must be distributed as free software under the GNU General Public License.
ASR_Explanation: This licensing NFR constrains choice of third-party components and distribution mechanisms to GPL-compatible ones, affecting component selection and integration.

requirement: Web Store shall manage customer accounts.
ASR_Explanation: This is a basic functional requirement with no particular impact on the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: Web Store shall manage an online store inventory.
ASR_Explanation: This is a core functional requirement without specific architectural constraints or cross-cutting impacts.

requirement: Web Store shall manage a customer’s Shopping Cart.
ASR_Explanation: It drives architectural decisions around state management, persistence strategy, and scalability for shopping cart data.

requirement: Web Store shall confirm orders.
ASR_Explanation: This is routine business logic for order processing and does not impose new architectural constraints.

requirement: Web Store shall have an unambiguous interface to assist in browsing the categories and products.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard UI clarity requirement and does not drive major architectural structure, technology, or integration decisions.

requirement: Web Store shall use Secure Socket Layer (SSL) for security.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the security and deployment architecture by mandating SSL/TLS protocols, certificate management, and encrypted communication endpoints.

requirement: Web Store shall have an availability of 99.999%.
ASR_Explanation: This high-availability target drives key architectural decisions around redundancy, failover, replication, and monitoring infrastructure.

requirement: Web Store shall allow an optional mirror site for reliability and backups.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the deployment architecture by requiring data replication, synchronization and failover mechanisms for high availability and disaster recovery.

requirement: Web Store shall feature interface for future software enhancement via Plug-ins.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the overall system modularity and extensibility by defining a plugin architecture and extension-point interfaces.

requirement: Initial inventory will be 100 items, expandable to a minimum total inventory of 20,000 items.
ASR_Explanation: This inventory sizing is easily handled by standard data storage and doesn’t drive major architectural choices.

requirement: No software installation will be necessary; the system will be plug and play.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the deployment architecture toward a zero-install, self-contained or browser-based delivery model.

requirement: Account Management (AM) allows users to create, edit, and view accounts information.
ASR_Explanation: Basic CRUD operations for account management are standard functionality and don’t drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Account Management allows users to login/out of the system.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of the authentication and session‐management subsystem, impacting security architecture and identity integration.

requirement: Search Engine (SE) assists the user in finding a specific item in the database.
ASR_Explanation: Enabling search entails adding an indexing/query subsystem (e.g. full-text engine, caching, APIs) that affects service boundaries, data storage, and scalability.

requirement: Product Management (PM) allows sales personnel to manage the product line shown on the web site.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine CRUD feature for product data with no major architectural changes required.

requirement: Shopping Cart (SC) is temporary storage for customers shopping on the web.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts session state management and data persistence design, requiring scalable, low-latency storage or caching layers.

requirement: Purchasing and Payment (PP) is used to approve and transfer payment from buyers when purchasing items in the cart.
ASR_Explanation: It requires secure, reliable integration with external payment gateways plus transaction management and scalability considerations that drive architectural decisions.

requirement: System Administrator shall be able to assign privileges of accounts.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the security architecture by necessitating a role-based access control system and privilege management framework.

requirement: System Administrator documentation shall be provided.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation deliverable requirement and does not influence the system’s architectural structure or quality attributes.

requirement: Sales Personnel shall be able to add, delete, and change descriptions, pictures, prices, and flag items for customers to buy.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD requirement for item management that does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Customer account creation will default as a customer account; administrator can upgrade account privileges.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard role‐based access control detail that can be implemented within the existing authentication/authorization framework without altering the overall architecture.

requirement: Web Store shall operate with Microsoft Internet Explorer version 6 and 7, Netscape Communicator Version 4 and 5.
ASR_Explanation: This legacy browser support requirement impacts the front-end architecture, technology stack choices, and testing strategy.

requirement: Web Store shall operate on an Intel based system with Slackware Linux 2.6 and Apache Web Server.
ASR_Explanation: This platform specification imposes hardware and software constraints that directly shape deployment architecture and technology choices.

requirement: System shall use SQL based database to store inventory information.
ASR_Explanation: Mandating an SQL database defines the data layer design, impacts transaction consistency, scalability strategies, and integration patterns.

requirement: USB interface and drivers are provided by Yoggie Corporation.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying a vendor-supplied USB interface and drivers creates an external dependency that impacts hardware abstraction, integration layers, and platform architecture.

requirement: System must use a SQL based database.
ASR_Explanation: Specifying a SQL database constrains the persistence technology choice and impacts data access layers, schema design, transactions, performance, and scalability.

requirement: Compatibility is only tested and verified for Microsoft Internet Explorer version 6 and 7, Netscape Communicator Version 4 and 5.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR dictates front-end technology choices and compatibility layers for legacy browsers, impacting UI architecture and testing strategy.

requirement: Shall install online help for users via the web interface.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level non-functional requirement for help content delivery and does not drive major architectural changes.

requirement: Shall deliver Operations and Maintenance manual, Users Guide book, and Installation Instructions for the Administrator.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement concerns documentation deliverables and does not drive architectural design decisions.

requirement: Assume the delivery of development, test and evaluate samples of the USB system from Yoggie.
ASR_Explanation: This hardware delivery schedule requirement does not influence the system’s structural design or quality-attribute tradeoffs.

requirement: Assume Yoggie will freeze the baseline of the USB system after delivery.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement constrains system maintainability and upgrade strategies by enforcing an immutable USB baseline post-delivery, impacting versioning and change management.

requirement: Customers will be able to create accounts to store their customer profiles, configure contact information, view their purchase history, and confirm orders.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD user-account feature with no significant architectural constraints.

requirement: Customers will be able to register, log in, and log out of their accounts.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s authentication, session management, and security integration layers.

requirement: Customer profiles will include payment information, such as the ability to store credit card information, and address information.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the data architecture by requiring secure storage/encryption, PCI-DSS compliance, and integration with payment processing systems.

requirement: Products will be stored in multi-tiered categories; a category can contain sub categories or products.
ASR_Explanation: This introduces hierarchical data modeling, impacting database schema design and query/navigation strategies.

requirement: Administrators will be able to update the categories, the products placed in categories, and the specific product details.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD feature for managing categories and products with minimal impact on the overall architecture.

requirement: Customers will be able to add products into the shopping cart.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard CRUD feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Shopping cart will clearly display the number of items in the cart, along with the total cost.
ASR_Explanation: This is a simple UI display requirement with minimal impact on the system’s high-level architecture.

requirement: Customer will be able to add to or remove products from the shopping cart prior to checkout and order confirmation.
ASR_Explanation: Standard shopping cart CRUD feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: Customers will be able to confirm the order after checkout.
ASR_Explanation: This basic post-checkout confirmation is a straightforward functional detail with minimal architectural impact.

requirement: If the order is incorrect, the customer will be able to revise and update their order.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard order‐management CRUD enhancement with minimal impact on the overall architecture.

requirement: Customer will receive a confirmation email with the specific order details.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine email notification feature that can be implemented using existing messaging/email services without affecting the overall system architecture.

requirement: Customers will be presented with an unambiguous interface to assist in browsing the categories and products.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI/UX clarity requirement that does not impose significant architectural constraints or drive major structural decisions.

requirement: Customers will be able to search for products matching their search criteria.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional requirement for product lookup without unique constraints that would drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Interface will be compatible with all major web browsers such as Internet Explorer, Mozilla Navigator, Mozilla Firefox, Opera, and Safari.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives key front-end architecture decisions around technology/framework selection, rendering strategy, and cross-browser compatibility mechanisms.

requirement: System will implement an Application Interface to allow for various plug-ins to interact with the system.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s modular architecture by defining extension points and interface contracts for plug‐in integration.

requirement: Plug-in API will be well documented and specifications will be provided to plug-in developers.
ASR_Explanation: This is a documentation quality requirement and does not impose any structural or design constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: Order database of WebOrder will communicate with the account system through a programmatic interface for the billing operations.
ASR_Explanation: It defines cross‐system integration via a programmatic interface, impacting service boundaries, communication protocols, and data consistency architecture.

requirement: WebOrder will transmit information of items ordered by customers to the Inventory management system through programmatic interface.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the integration architecture by driving choices of communication protocols, message formats, and coupling between WebOrder and the Inventory system.

requirement: Plug-ins interface will be provided.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts system extensibility and modularity by defining plugin integration and extension points.

requirement: The WebOrder system shall send an e-mail confirmation to the customer that the items they ordered will be delivered to the shipping address along with tracking number.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional requirement relying on standard email service integration and does not drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The WebOrder system shall send an e-mail to System Administrator regarding any technical queries from customers or sales people.

requirement: Upon the USB being plugged in the system shall be able to be deployed and operational in less than 1 minute.

ASR_Explanation: Requires integration of digital signature, key management, and audit logging services, impacting the security and data‐flow architecture.

requirement: The VCD viewing system MUST provide an interface that adequately displays various types of data formats contained within a single VCD package.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of an extensible parsing and rendering architecture to support multiple data formats in a single VCD package.

requirement: The VCD viewing system MUST display structural VCD package data contained in the VCD package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine functional requirement for data presentation and does not impose cross-cutting or structural constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: The VCD viewing system MUST display tender-specific data contained in the VCD package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI/display feature that affects implementation details but does not drive core architectural structure.

requirement: The VCD viewing system MUST display economic operator data contained in the VCD package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional requirement to display data and does not drive any system-level architectural decisions.

requirement: The VCD viewing system MUST display structural data of evidence contained in the VCD package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI functionality with minimal impact on high-level architectural structure or technology decisions.

requirement: The VCD viewing system MUST display evidences contained in the VCD package.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the need for core architecture components for VCD package parsing and evidence rendering, affecting system decomposition and technology selection.

requirement: The VCD viewing system MUST display translations of evidences contained in the VCD package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level feature for displaying existing translation data and does not drive high-level architectural structure or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: Member State of this work package SHOULD follow a common standard representation of the different types of data.
ASR_Explanation: It imposes a common data representation standard, affecting data models, service interfaces, and interoperability decisions.

requirement: Any standard representation of the different types of data MUST be agreed upon the pilot partners.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates standardized data representations across pilot partners, impacting data models, integration interfaces, and interoperability in the overall architecture.

requirement: A method for the versioning of the development of this common standard schema SHOULD be found.
ASR_Explanation: It dictates the design of schema management and API versioning mechanisms, impacting compatibility and deployment workflows.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide functionality to collect and process context specific data for evidence that has to be provided manually or semi-automatically by the issuing body, by the economic operator or any trusted third party.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the data ingestion and processing architecture, mandating extensible interfaces and workflows for manual/semi-automatic evidence submissions from multiple parties.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide IT-interfaces to issuing bodies which provide evidences in machine interpretable format.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s integration layer, interface contracts, data models, and communication protocols.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide a user interface to the issuing body to provide context specific data for an evidence in a structured form (e.g. web-interface, XML-file-upload).
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI-level functional detail affecting data-entry components but not the system’s overall architecture.

requirement: Any VCD advanced package MUST follow a common standard schema of context specific data that summarize and structure major information contained within context specific evidences.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes a common data schema constraint that drives the system’s data model, component interfaces, and integration architecture.

requirement: Context specific data MUST be machine interpretable.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of data schemas, serialization formats, and interface contracts to ensure interoperability and machine-readability.

requirement: A semantic definition of each element/class of the common standard schema of context specific data MUST be created and has to ensure a common understanding.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the overall data modeling and metadata management architecture by introducing a semantic layer for schema elements to ensure interoperability.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide a user-interface to the economic operator to initiate the creation of a VCD advanced package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward UI feature that does not impose new architectural constraints beyond standard frontend–backend interactions.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide a user-interface to the economic operator to provide existing VCD packages of other economic operators when specific disclosure options have been activated.
ASR_Explanation: Line 2: This is a standard UI‐level feature for conditional content display and doesn’t affect core architectural structure.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide functionality to merge the following data from several existing VCD packages of other economic operators: Structural VCD package data, Structural data of evidence, Tender specific data, Economic operator data, Context specific data, Evidence, Authorized translation of evidence.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the data integration architecture by demanding heterogeneous VCD package merging, schema mapping, transformation, and consistency handling.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide a user-interface to the economic operator to initiate the re-compilation of an existing VCD package into a VCD advanced package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine UI-driven functional feature with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide a user-interface to the economic operator for uploading an existing VCD package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed UI-level functional requirement (file upload) that does not impose major architectural structure or patterns.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide a functionality to decompose an existing VCD package.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard functional feature affecting the business logic layer without driving high-level architectural structure.

requirement: A VCD system MUST provide a functionality to identify outdated or obsolete evidences.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the data storage and retention architecture by requiring metadata tagging and scheduled processing for evidence lifecycle management.

requirement: A VCD system must provide a functionality to retrieve actual instances of outdated evidences.
ASR_Explanation: It requires a versioned data storage and retrieval layer design to manage and access historical evidence instances.

requirement: The VCD system MUST provide functionality and an interface to the economic operator that enables him to mandate a certain contracting authority to request attestations on demand.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces new service APIs and UI components for on-demand attestation requests, impacting modular decomposition, integration points, and authorization workflows.

requirement: The VCD system MUST provide a functionality that enables National VCD Service Providers to store attestations.
ASR_Explanation: This is a routine data persistence requirement with minimal impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A functionality for referencing to attestations MUST be implemented within the VCD system.
ASR_Explanation: It requires extending the core domain model, database schema and service APIs to support attestation referencing.

requirement: The VCD system MUST ensure that a contracting authority is able to request attestations via the provided reference (including time-restrictions).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the architecture of the attestation service interface, time-based access control, and related data management modules.

requirement: The VCD system MUST provide a functionally to check whether a contracting authority has sufficient rights and has been mandated by the economic operator to retrieve attestations.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the security architecture by requiring integration of access‐control, delegation protocols, and identity/authorization services.

requirement: The VCD system MUST provide a functionality and an interface that enables contracting authorities to retrieve the attestation from the storage.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the architecture of storage schema, API interface design, and security/integration layers for attestation retrieval.

requirement: The VCD system MUST ensure that the attestation that is requested by the contracting authority is still valid at the time of awarding or when the Call for Tender is closed.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates event-driven workflows and integration with attestation services to manage and re-validate document validity at key process milestones.

requirement: The VCD system MUST provide a functionality that initiates a conflict resolution process between economic operator and contracting authority if errors occur or when certain attestation or a translation cannot be delivered on demand.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces a cross-cutting business‐process orchestration for error handling and dispute resolution, impacting the system’s messaging, workflow engine, and integration architecture.

requirement: There MUST be an access policy that is issued by the economic operator that mandates a contracting authority to request attestation from the national VCD service provider on demand.
ASR_Explanation: This mandates integrating a policy enforcement component and dynamic attestation calls to the external VCD service, impacting security architecture and service orchestration.

requirement: A general reference structure and syntax MUST be defined.
ASR_Explanation: Defining a reference structure and syntax establishes system-wide conventions that directly influence component interfaces, data flow, and integration.

requirement: National and local political commitments and service-level agreements MUST ensure that consensuses on roles and responsibilities are built with regard to this solution and in particular the National Service Providers who delivers attestations on demand.
ASR_Explanation: This is a governance and SLA policy requirement that does not impose direct technical or structural constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: There MUST be a service-level agreement between the parties involved (Economic Operators, Contracting authorities, National Service Providers, and Trusted Attestation Service) about the response time of a delivery of attestations and data availability.
ASR_Explanation: This NFR imposes strict performance and availability constraints that will drive architectural decisions around scalability, redundancy, monitoring, and fault tolerance.

requirement: There MUST be a service-level agreement between the parties involved (Economic Operators, Contracting authorities, National Service Providers, and Trusted Attestation Service) about conflict resolution that describe what to do when errors occur or when attestation or translations cannot be delivered “on demand”.
ASR_Explanation: It drives the design of cross-party interfaces, error-handling, monitoring, and fallback mechanisms to meet SLA guarantees.

requirement: Legal systems MUST ensure that context specific data and validation values have an equivalent value to the provision of conventional / traditional attestations. (Scope of mutual recognition).
ASR_Explanation: This NFR drives the design of specialized attestation and validation components and trust frameworks to ensure data equivalence with traditional legal attestations.

requirement: The National Service Providers SHOULD establish validation points in order to approve an economic operator in accordance with Article 52 (1) of Directive 2004/18/EC.
ASR_Explanation: This compliance-driven requirement mandates dedicated validation checkpoints, impacting the design of system workflows, modules, and integration layers.

requirement: The national procurement law MUST ensure that National Service Provider can act as national validation points in accordance with Article 52 (1) of Directive 2004/18/EC for economic operators.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement mandates integration with external national validation services, impacting system integration, security, and compliance architecture.

requirement: Liability issues of the National Service Providers MUST be clarified when they act as national validation points in accordance with Article 52 (1) of Directive 2004/18/EC for economic operators.
ASR_Explanation: This is a legal/regulatory clarification requirement and does not impose direct technical or structural constraints on the system architecture.

requirement: National procedures with regard to Article 52 (1) of Directive 2004/18/EC MUST be mutually recognized and trusted in across borders.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of cross-border interoperability and trust/security frameworks, affecting integration and identity management architecture.

requirement: The tools shall be able to process a set of multiple WARC files at the same time. For this purpose, the tools will allow you to choose/group them by: explicit naming, wildcard name matching, size limitation, number of items.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the system’s concurrency and resource-management architecture by defining how files are batched, filtered, and processed in parallel.

requirement: The tools shall be able to scale to process large collections using distributed processing and data transport.
ASR_Explanation: This scalability requirement drives the architecture to adopt distributed processing, data partitioning, network transport layers, and horizontal scaling infrastructure.

requirement: The tools shall offer best possible performance to correctly process large collections (I/O bound).
ASR_Explanation: This performance NFR drives architectural decisions around high-throughput I/O optimizations—e.g., parallel processing, efficient data pipelines, caching, and scalable storage.

requirement: The tools shall be able to run on multiple machines but will not provide functionality for dealing with hardware failures (out of scope).
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a distributed deployment architecture and impacts decisions on communication, scalability, and fault-tolerance mechanisms.

requirement: Implementation of the tools shall avoid unnecessary technology dependencies and shall not include development of partner-specific integration technologies.
ASR_Explanation: This constraint impacts technology stack and integration architecture by enforcing low coupling, modularity, and avoidance of partner-specific dependencies.

requirement: The tools shall be compliant with Java development environments, by using web services, RESTful API’s, etc.
ASR_Explanation: Mandates core technology and interface decisions by requiring Java-compatible web services and RESTful API integration.

requirement: The tools shall provide logging facilities, for example, to follow command progression, i.e. process duration, output levels, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This influences the design of cross-cutting logging components, framework selection, performance overhead, and storage/analysis infrastructure.

requirement: The tools shall provide enhanced usability by providing easy to adapt shell script wrappers for the major commands.
ASR_Explanation: This usability enhancement is an implementation detail for scripting and does not impact the high-level component or system architecture.

requirement: It shall be possible to operate the tools in modes that restrict their effects to selected collection subsets. For example, select by name, random sampling, etc.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement affects the architectural design of the data-processing pipeline by mandating pluggable filtering/selection modules.

requirement: The workflow system shall have a configurable management strategy for migration from ARCs to WARCs.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing an extensible, configurable migration pipeline component in the architecture to manage ARC‐to‐WARC conversions.

requirement: The migration workflow system will be driven by command line tools and scripts.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system interface architecture by mandating headless CLI modules and scripting support.

requirement: Migration configurations can be built with a Web User Interface.
ASR_Explanation: It necessitates adding a web UI tier with front-end/back-end integration, affecting technology choices, APIs, and system interfaces.

requirement: Active migrations can be monitored, paused, and restarted (a console application) with a Web User Interface.
ASR_Explanation: It requires integrating a web-based UI with the console application, impacting system layering, communication protocols, and real-time state management.

requirement: Pre and post conversion actions will be provided for at each level of the migration, e.g. record, WARC, job.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement dictates the design of the migration engine’s extension points and orchestration pipeline for pre- and post-conversion processing.

requirement: IIPC members should provide us with the default METADATA they want to be included for migration.
ASR_Explanation: This is an operational metadata configuration requirement for migration and does not drive major architectural decisions.

requirement: Deduplication may be run before migration, inside a batch process.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the data migration pipeline by introducing batch-based deduplication logic and related resource provisioning.

requirement: Repackaging filters can be used by the summary module.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level component integration detail and does not drive system-wide architectural decisions.

requirement: The migration workflow shall provide a clear Application Programming Interface (API) to handle the migration process and default configuration.
ASR_Explanation: This defines the migration service’s interface boundaries and influences module decomposition and system integration.

requirement: The migration API will require inclusion of only one header file.
ASR_Explanation: This is a low-level implementation constraint (coding style) with no impact on the overall system architecture.

requirement: A configuration must be provided explicitly, errors should be generated in the absence of a configuration.
ASR_Explanation: This is a standard startup validation requirement and does not drive changes in the high-level architecture.

requirement: The user shall be able to provide metadata related to the conversion that will be stored in the converted files (e.g., institution, context, crawler, collection name, ...)
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data model and file‐storage architecture by mandating metadata capture and persistence in output file formats.

requirement: It shall be possible to request that automatically generated migration metadata be stored in the converted files (e.g., OS/Kernel type, original ARC name, ARC size, ARC digests, conversion timestamp, ARC record offset, ...)
ASR_Explanation: Embedding migration metadata in converted files drives the design of file formats, serialization layers, and storage interfaces.

requirement: The migration workflow shall be able to call external tools and services (e.g., database queries, shell commands, web service).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system’s integration architecture by defining how the workflow engine must support extensible connectors, dependency management, and security boundaries for external tools and services.

requirement: The migration process shall use persistent, opaque, unique, and global identifiers for records access.
ASR_Explanation: This dictates the design of ID-generation and data-access components, impacting database schema, API interfaces, and integration patterns.

requirement: A (default) external tool (e.g., ClamAV) will be used to scan files before conversion (pre-conversion step).
ASR_Explanation: Integrating a default external scanning tool imposes an extensible plugin/component architecture, adds dependencies, error-handling, and security/performance considerations.

requirement: The API should be flexible enough to allow external tools such as JHOVE, DROID to be used for file format identification.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of an extensible plugin/integration architecture and abstraction layer for external format‐identification tools.

requirement: The ARC to WARC migration should be able to run on multiple machines that will be easy to deploy. This may be achieved by using a simple messaging infrastructure.
ASR_Explanation: This impacts the system’s distributed deployment and scalability by requiring a messaging-based infrastructure for multi-machine execution.

requirement: Logging during the migration may be turned on/off at any time.
ASR_Explanation: Requires a runtime-configurable logging subsystem, impacting cross-cutting concerns and the migration service architecture.

requirement: Software checkpoints (such as start-trans, end-trans) may be added during processing for managing atomic transactions (e.g., operations on a file, these must start and complete to be accepted in the migration), except where operating system limitations would prohibit it.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of the system’s transaction management and persistence layers to ensure atomic operations and failure recovery.

requirement: It shall be possible to perform a “dry-run migration”. That is, a blank conversion to generate useful reports (performance issues, bad ARCs, time estimation ...) without writing real WARC data (i.e. simulating only). Dry run is defined entirely by the configuration.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing a dry-run mode requires a simulation layer and configurable execution paths that impact the core data-processing and I/O architecture.

requirement: Duplicate detection shall find and report WARC records with the same checksums. A centralised database will be used to store previously processed records, against which new ones will be compared.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates a centralised database and duplicate-detection service, impacting storage architecture, data flow, and system scalability.

requirement: To validate that the migration succeeded, arc_warc_verify will use the METADATA included in the newly generated WARC files to match records with their corresponding ARC files.
ASR_Explanation: This is a detailed functional verification step that doesn’t drive high-level architectural structure or patterns.

requirement: Core validation shall use payload checksum comparisons.
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a checksum validation layer in the core processing path, influencing module decomposition, data flow, and performance considerations.

requirement: Sampling will be provided where applicable to quickly validate the conversions.
ASR_Explanation: This is a local implementation detail (sampling logic for conversion validation) that doesn’t drive high-level architectural decisions.

requirement: Validation shall use Heritrix’s arcreader to double check that the original ARC record was correctly converted to WARC.
ASR_Explanation: This imposes a third-party dependency on Heritrix’s arcreader, affecting the conversion pipeline’s integration strategy and technology stack.

requirement: It shall be possible to perform a round trip validation: migrate the newly created WARC file back to ARC and compare both checksums.
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the data‐processing pipeline by requiring pluggable WARC↔ARC conversion modules and end‐to‐end integrity verification.

requirement: It shall be possible to repackage WARC files by filtering records based on URL (regular expressions).
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces a URL-based filtering component into the WARC processing pipeline, impacting streaming architecture, performance and memory management.

requirement: It shall be possible to repackage WARC files by filtering records based on MIME-Types.
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward filtering feature that can be implemented within the existing WARC processing pipeline without altering the system’s high-level structure.

requirement: It shall be possible to repackage WARC files by filtering records based on size.
ASR_Explanation: This is a specific file‐processing feature with limited impact on overall system structure or key architectural patterns.

requirement: It shall be possible to repackage WARC files by filtering records based on timestamp (e.g., dates interval).
ASR_Explanation: It impacts the design of the WARC processing pipeline and storage/indexing layers to support efficient timestamp‐based record filtering.

requirement: It shall be possible to repackage WARC files by filtering on any field in the WARC specification.
ASR_Explanation: It requires designing a flexible, high-performance WARC parsing and filtering pipeline, impacting the core data processing architecture.

requirement: Repackaging will allow pre and post record, file and job operations.
ASR_Explanation: It mandates an extensible hook mechanism, impacting module decomposition and orchestration for pre- and post-record, file, and job operations.

requirement: Pre operation can prevent a file or record being processed by returning value.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement drives the design of processing pipelines by introducing pre-operation hooks/interceptors that affect module interaction and control flow.

requirement: Each repackaged WARC file shall include a user-defined METADATA record which will describe the extraction context (e.g., filter used, involved WARC files, ...). Additional metadata records will be added to show the output WARCs as repackaged.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the data model and file-format handling in the WARC repackaging component, driving design of metadata extension and interoperability.

requirement: The migration framework shall provide tools to build reports from WARC files.
ASR_Explanation: This reporting feature is a localized functional requirement that doesn’t drive major architectural structure or cross-cutting concerns.

requirement: It shall be possible to export summaries in various formats (e.g., XML, CSV).
ASR_Explanation: This is a straightforward functional feature with minimal impact on overall system architecture.

requirement: Enhance the WARC browser to display aggregated WARCs summaries in its UI.
ASR_Explanation: Introducing aggregated WARC summaries impacts the data processing pipeline, storage/indexing model, and UI presentation layers, requiring architectural adjustments.

requirement: Enhance the WARC browser to display a manifest of WARC files and their locations.
ASR_Explanation: This is a UI‐level enhancement to list WARC files and locations with no major impact on core system architecture.

requirement: Provide a ‘diff‘ tool to compare WARC sets based on defined criteria (e.g., timestamp, hostname, etc.).
ASR_Explanation: It introduces a high-performance diff engine and data access modules for comparing large WARC sets, impacting system modularity, performance and storage architecture.

requirement: Provide a tool to draw difference graphs between WARC collections.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement introduces a diffing engine and graph visualization pipeline, impacting module decomposition, data processing flow, and UI integration.

requirement: Provide a way to view crawls deltas for quality assurance.
ASR_Explanation: This is a feature-level UI/reporting requirement that can be handled within existing data processing and presentation layers without driving major architectural changes.

requirement: Implement WARC browser server side rewriting.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the core request processing pipeline and server‐side middleware architecture for handling and rewriting WARC content.

requirement: Integrate a proxy mode inside the WARC browser.
ASR_Explanation: Integrating proxy mode modifies the network stack and request-routing components, affecting module interfaces, performance, and security.

requirement: Integrate the full-text search (search-tools project) module with the WARC browser to provide users with WARC indexing/searching capabilities.
ASR_Explanation: This requirement impacts the system architecture by introducing search indexing pipelines, storage layers, query APIs, and integration interfaces within the WARC browser.
